###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<giLM2.1731$Uk.778968@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7dubad$p9e$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
lfirwin@hotmail.com wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>good programmer or even a great programmer.

If you really believe that, then you have a very different standard than I
for what constitutes 'good' programming, or a 'good' programmer.  Anyone who
is not willing to discipline themselves to be *very* detail oriented in their
programming is causing themselves and others a whole lot of extra work and
grief.  I have followed behind too many of them, correcting their sloppy,
malfunctioning work, to think differently.  I'm sorry if this offends anyone,
but it offends me to have to waste enormous amounts of time in correcting what
should have been done correctly to start with, but wasn't because of sloppy
and careless programming by those who "aren't detail oriented".  You can be
certain that computers are "detail oriented".  Their programmers had better
be, if they expect to do it right. :-)

>And if you want to see carelessness in programming, just look at Microsoft....

I totally agree!  Some of their products are so buggy, they border on
uselessness.  Do you admire that? ;-)

>> Please don't make me repeat this again. :-)  The issue *is not* the tool,
>> it is lack of discipline.  A programmer who is careless in any area of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>all be using punch cards or teletype machines....

Using or not using those things has nothing to do with whether or not you
are disciplining your mind to do a very exacting task in the most reliable
way you can.  Saying "I don't want to think about (some aspect of the
programming process)" is a very different issue.  Whether you know it or
not, or whether you believe it or not, human beings are creatures of habits
and patterns of thought.  If you accept "I don't want to think" in one
area of your programming, then you can be absolutely certain that attitude
will also propagate into other areas of you programming to some degree.
Probably at times you least expect it, when under pressure, when it is
least desirable.  This is a demonstrable fact, not an opinion.  Those who
realize these things can use them to their advantage, those who deny them
do so to their detriment.  Sorry if this offends, but it is true, and I
have watched it played out over and over many times over many years.  Never
once have I seen it not to be true.  If you think you have, then you aren't
paying attention. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BRKM2.1700$Uk.763424@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>should know the difference quite well.

I assumed the discussion was about production code.  Surely you don't
suggest any degree of carelessness in production code?

>There is also the fact that one can cover carelessness in one area by an
>appropriate tool.  I am a lousy speller, for example, and so I use a spell
>checker.  The fact that I do use a spell checker has allowed me to type
>faster, as I do not have to as careful while I am typing.

We do not all have the same skills.  But are you telling me that you do
not even want to *think* about spelling as you write?  The comment that I
protested was "When I write code, I'd like to only think about content...".
I don't object to anyone using a code formatter, I would like to have one
myself.  What I object to is the idea "I don't want to think".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e08ng$1d5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <BRKM2.1700$Uk.763424@news3.mia>`

```
I think it depends on *when* the tool is used. There is a major difference
between writing a topdown  module, testing, prettying, then repeating, and
writing a whole system over several days in a sloppy manner then trying to
clean it up a week (or a year) later.

In general, I would agree with your premise: that 99% of style is habitual,
and it is crucial to develop good habits.  Also that developing those habits
will (or should be) reflected in all of your code.

What I disagree with is just about anything specific in the manner that one
uses to get to that point.  Some people go like a bat out of hell as they
write, then tidy and clean up every 10-15 minutes (my tendency).  Others are
very careful as they type. Still others use a variety of tools to assist
them in various phases of producing good code.

The really crucial ingredient, at least in my mind, is not the technique,
but the fact that one is constantly and consciously working on technique.
Presuming that a specific technique is "the best" is the route to
stagnation, IMHO.  Technique is a constant evolution.

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>
>I assumed the discussion was about production code.  Surely you don't
>suggest any degree of carelessness in production code?


<g> *all* code is production code.  No, I do not suggest that at all.  I am
saying that typing quickly and using the compiler or a tool to check the
code is an efficiency measure, not an indication of carelessness.
Or that the carelessness in stage one is countered by carefull checking at
stage two. I never read my code to check syntax, for example.  The compiler
does it much faster.

>
>We do not all have the same skills.  But are you telling me that you do
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>myself.  What I object to is the idea "I don't want to think".


No, I do not think about spelling as I write ... at least not at the
conscious level.  I think about spelling at a conscious level when I am
analyzing the spell checker's mistakes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703B1B7.DDF9AEB8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <BRKM2.1700$Uk.763424@news3.mia> <7e08ng$1d5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
> Or that the carelessness in stage one is countered by carefull checking at
> stage two. I never read my code to check syntax, for example.  The compiler
> does it much faster.
> 

> 
> No, I do not think about spelling as I write ... at least not at the
> conscious level.  I think about spelling at a conscious level when I am
> analyzing the spell checker's mistakes.


Umm, I use the compiler and I use spell checkers.  But spell checkers
show that I typed words that it understood, and compilers show that I
typed commands it understood.

I find that a message which uses the WRONG word is more irritating than
one with a complete misspelling, because I think I understand it and
read on before I discover that the sentence doesn't make sense and I
have to retrack.  In the same way, if my mistake gets compiled into a
program, I have much more work to do in tracking down an error than if
it didn't.  

I don't read my code to check for errors the compiler will catch.  I
read it to check for errors the compiler won't catch!

And I don't read my messages and documentation to find errors the
spelling checker will catch.  I read it for errors the spelling checker
won't catch.
```

 <snippage>...

<G>What you do, is you hire a junior, change the name in the author section,

style training tool.

I have not mentioned it in this thread, but the best re-write tool is a
trainee.

>> important, and depends on ones tools.  I see nothing wrong with using a
tool
>> to end up with the same result in less time.  I think your argument that
you
>> cannot be careless even occasionally because it affects your work is a
>> personal trait, not a universal one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703B46C.1E76C5F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37037E8F.51C33AB0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e08rc$1da$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Of course.  But that means that your regular methodology has also been tuned
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >to be sloppy.

I'm not worried about silly typos.  I'm worried about subtle typos. 
What will bite me is using the wrong variable, having the wrong period,
data overflow, etc.  The compiler won't catch those.

When you write a quick and dirty memo, do you re-read it to make sure
you told people the correct date for the meeting, or do you trust your
spell checker to find silly typos?
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I'm not worried about silly typos.  I'm worried about subtle typos.
> What will bite me is using the wrong variable, having the wrong period,
> data overflow, etc.  The compiler won't catch those.
> 

There is a tip in here:  Don't use variable names that are very close,
the compiler will not pick up the errors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3704DAC7.2822889B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37037E8F.51C33AB0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e08rc$1da$1@news.igs.net> <3703B46C.1E76C5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <37044DD0.C9AE7F12@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the compiler will not pick up the errors.

Many times a shop has naming standards which don't give any options. 
Often they use data dictionaries with different suffixes.   Some shops
have standards to GO TO 1000-EXIT without having tools which catch that
it really should have been 1100-EXIT.  (I hate GO TO EXIT, SECTIONs, &
PERFORM THRUs).

Unless you can guarantee that you never will have to follow someone
else's standards, ...  nevermind ALWAYS, it's worthwhile not to depend
on the compiler to catch errors!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703CABE.2424BF21@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37037E8F.51C33AB0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e08rc$1da$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> Of course.  But that means that your regular methodology has also been tuned
> for speed.
> Do you carefully read all your code before compiling, or do you trust the
> compiler to find silly typos?
>

May I participate?

I can't believe you are discussing this. A good programmer "never" delivers
sloppy code, even if it works. How you get there, is your problem.
I've written thousands of programs (I'm not bragging - it's been fifteen years).
They have to look good. I've also worked with, and trained, quite a few other
programmers. Before even looking at the logic in their programs, I oblige them
to make the code look good. I won't waste time trying to understand an ugly
program. And I've found that the best programmers always are careful about the
way their code looks. The ones that are sloppy with their code, start getting
ever more sloppy with their logic - don't do checks or validations, use
shortcuts, etc. No good.

However, a formatting utility would be absolutely welcome for cleaning up
programs written by other people, before undertaking a mantainence task.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e0vrg$h3n$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37037E8F.51C33AB0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e08rc$1da$1@news.igs.net> <3703CABE.2424BF21@conexion.com.py>`

```

Albert Ratzlaff wrote in message <3703CABE.2424BF21@conexion.com.py>...

>May I participate?

Of course.  The more the merrier.
>
>I can't believe you are discussing this. A good programmer "never" delivers
>sloppy code, even if it works. How you get there, is your problem.

Agreed. Absolutely and 100%.  The claim (that I disagreed with) was that you
had to get there by carefull typing rather than slapping it in any old way
and using a tool to tidy it up and make it pretty.

A secondary claim was made that doing so would turn your brain to mush, to
the point that you would not be able to create a good algorithm either.

>I've written thousands of programs (I'm not bragging - it's been fifteen
years).
>They have to look good. I've also worked with, and trained, quite a few
other
>programmers. Before even looking at the logic in their programs, I oblige
them
>to make the code look good. I won't waste time trying to understand an ugly
>program. And I've found that the best programmers always are careful about
the
>way their code looks. The ones that are sloppy with their code, start
getting
>ever more sloppy with their logic - don't do checks or validations, use
>shortcuts, etc. No good.


I will stack my code against anybody's.  I'd only put it in the top 10-15%
for well written, but I'll bet I'm in the top 2-3% for money made per line
written.  Or hour spent, for than matter.  I've written nothing but fixed
price, I own the copyright, for twenty years now. Most of my income is
royalties.

I'll use any tool I can get to get a workmanlike job out the door in minimum
time. I'll also try every new style that seems reasonable for at least two
or three largish programs.  The style I use when it is important to be
consistent is my own. There are no hard and fast rules: I do not believe in
them.  However there are lots of rules that I haven't found a need to bypass
for five or ten years.

I still feel strongly that I the awareness of the importance of style, and
the conscious practice of a personal style is far more important than any
single specific stylistic rule.  I would grant that all programmers in a
shop need enough consistancy that each is comfortable with each other's
code.

>However, a formatting utility would be absolutely welcome for cleaning up
>programs written by other people, before undertaking a mantainence task.
>
I can see using one in the same manner as I use a word processor.  I simply
do not pay attention to line breaks anymore.  I do not think that dropping
my attention from line breaks, while writing prose, has drastically harmed
my writing ability by inducing laziness.

I see no reason why programming is any different.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37042f2f.357374236@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37037E8F.51C33AB0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e08rc$1da$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Thu, 1 Apr 1999 12:05:28 -0500, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
wrote:

>Of course.  But that means that your regular methodology has also been tuned
>for speed.
>Do you carefully read all your code before compiling, or do you trust the
>compiler to find silly typos?

At risk of being crucified - I develop BY compiler on many occasions.

I write something like:

Perform Open-Files
Perform Process-The-Data
Perform Close-Files

Then I code under Process-The-Data

Perform Read-Master-File
Perform Read-Transaction-File
Perform Update-Master-File

Then I compile.  (This is an over simplification).

Whatever paragraphs are not coded, I code.  I might perform some more
new things in these.  

It's then a game of successive compiles and code writing, compiles
etc.

This is ONE method I have used.  I don't do it all the time, but it
does work and the code that comes out is usually very efficient, clean
and bugless.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37044C93.A52A5280@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

On mite think the a spiel check will help bud it don't.

The bottom line is that despite the fact that you use a tool to assist
where you know you are week, whoops weak, you still try to spell
correctly all the time to ensure that the content makes sense.  How many
times have you sent out a document with 'or' instead of 'of' (like I
just did and corrected because I am checking it myself).

Judson is saying simply in order to write clear code you must think in a
clear ordered manner. 'Crafted' code is almost always more carefully
thought about in a clear ordered manner.  Yes sometimes we all churn
results knowing that they are not the 'best' approach, but we cannot
think of any other way but the convoluted way. Business reality means
that the customer wants a specific result and we are paid to give them
the results.  Even my fast churns have lined up TOs and comments.  Proof
of concept is sometimes thrashed out without formatting but it must be
thrown away before serious development begins and NEVER goes into
production.

Haven't you watched a project being built in a record time only to
languish in user testing for more than twice the time it took to build?
Quality from the ground up, please.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Au4N2.4708$Mk.2457246@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37044C93.A52A5280@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>Judson is saying simply in order to write clear code you must think in a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>production.

Thanks Ken, you expressed that much better than I.  One of the things
which has been often noted in modern literature (since the typewriter,
but especially since the word processor) is that, while the quantity of
literature has gone up, the quality has certainly gone down, sometimes
way down.  There is a very definite penalty which is *always* paid in
quality of creative effort for every decrease in quantity and quality
of thought.  That some are unaware of this does not change the fact to
any degree.  It is not that efficiency tools are a bad thing.  It is
when they are used as a substitute for thoughtful attention that it
becomes detrimental.  Not the tools themselves, but the decrease of
thoughtful attention.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3704DC1E.1397E4A8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7drmfj$e8p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <RxdM2.2125$my4.1019906@news3.mia> <7dtk17$38g$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <l9wM2.3488$wH2.1620954@news4.mia> <7ducqi$r6m$1@news.igs.net> <37044C93.A52A5280@zip.com.au> <Au4N2.4708$Mk.2457246@news2.mia>`

```
> Thanks Ken, you expressed that much better than I.  One of the things
> which has been often noted in modern literature (since the typewriter,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> thoughtful attention.

There's more bad stuff because Sturgeon's law still holds so increased
quantity will produce more at all quality levels.  But word processors
have allowed people to do much more editing than they were willing to do
in the past.  I know of one award winning author who writes out long
hand, and a couple who use typewriters, all of the rest of the best
authors use word processors.

Even my kids were able to print out homework, have me review it, and
then were willing to accept my suggestions and change their work.  When
I was a kid, I worked at typing or printing, not WRITING my papers, and
there's no way I wanted to go through all of the work of printing again
to change a word.  And my course wasn't about printing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>

Howard Brazee wrote in message
>
>There's more bad stuff because Sturgeon's law still holds so increased
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>to change a word.  And my course wasn't about printing.

Why is coding different?  I do not figure out how I am going to organize a
project while I am typing it.  If I am going to work on a design, then I go
for a long walk in the park, or shoot pool for a couple hours ... maybe play
the piano or guitar.

If I am not sure about a particular technique, then I sit down and write
some throw-away code to test the idea.  That code is *always* erased, and I
do not really bother making it particularly pretty.  It is seldom over three
or four hours of work.  I may, alternatively, test the method on some tool
that I decide I want in my library.  I never test a new method on real code.
If I am not happy with the technique, I still throw the code away.

When I am writing production code, there is nothing in it that requires much
thinking.  I'll use tools and methods primarily to make it easy for me to
read it ten years later.  All the tricky stuff was tested and debugged
before I start.  What I want at that stage is primarily good editing,
cutting and pasting, and read at a glance computer instructions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 11)_

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> cutting and pasting, and read at a glance computer instructions.


It isn't.  But I don't take time making things pretty either.  In fact,
prettiness is not my goal.  My goal is clarity, and that is automatic
when I code in a clear manner.  It doesn't take time to write clearly,
especially since I read my code as I write.  I suppose it helps to be a
decent typist for this though.

I'll throw code away, but before I judge it, I have to know what it
does.  Clarity is important at all stages.
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

Michael, I could actually show you about two million lines of COBOL
code I have written in the last ten tears.  I have been a consultant
for 20 years, and most of my programming is new development.  For
at least 15 years I have averaged over 200,000 lines of code per
year.  I know this because I still have much of the original source
code.  Back in the days of coding forms it was much lower of course.
I have kept track of most of the systems I have written over the last
25 years of my 30+ year career, and I conservatively estimate that I
have written at least four million lines of COBOL and at least several
hundred thousand to a million lines in other languages.  I may have
written a million lines of code just for little utility and prototype programs, many of which are in BASIC or C.  I write such
programs on
a daily basis.  Michael, thirty years is a long time. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37078276@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia>`

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia...
>For  at least 15 years I have averaged over 200,000 lines of code per
> year

We believe you Judson. Let me just make a few observations:
Your figure works out to about 1000 lines a day which has 30,000
seconds of worktime. That is 30 seconds per line. Say it takes
5 seconds to type a line (you don't just copy the line, do you?,
because then it does not count as *writing* code), that leaves
25 seconds per line to cover design, debugging, documentation, etc.
That is quite impressive. My own figure is more like 150 lines a
day. That does not mean that I cannot on a good day bang out 1000
lines, but then I have bad days where it is down to 10 lines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9eRN2.9713$Ek.4011488@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote
>>For  at least 15 years I have averaged over 200,000 lines of code
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>25 seconds per line to cover design, debugging, documentation, etc.

Let me describe the process and you can make your own determination
as to whether it is 'writing' code. :-)  I do a *lot* of code reuse.
There are three types of copying and one type of code generation
which greatly assist in my productivity and accuracy.  In every case
the finished product is the same as if I had typed every character
from scratch.  I modify copied code as much as necessary to achieve
this.

The first type of copying is selecting a program similar to the one
I want to write and modifying it.  How much of the code gets modified
depends on the programs, of course.  In general, when starting a new
system you may not get much benefit, because you are using programs
written for other systems.  As more and more programs are written for
that system, you can find more and closer programs to use.  These
are all programs I wrote originally.

The second type is copying a piece of code from another program or
another part of the program I'm writing.

The third type of copying is not code at all, but copying data names
or parts of data names from database definition files or other parts
of a program to construct data definitions or instructions, often
series of moves.  This is as effective for precision as it is for
efficiency.

Years ago I wrote a data dictionary library which permits a lot of
input data validation to be done in online programs by performing
dictionary edit routines.  Many large online programs I write have
sections of code that contain many (perhaps 10-50) sets like this:

      MOVE "data-name" TO hold-name.
      MOVE data-value  TO hold-value.
      PERFORM dictionary-edit
      IF  error
          ...

I copy the field names from the program or database definition to
create an ASCII file of the field names and a few parameters, and
run a BASIC program I wrote which generates that code.  Virtually
guaranteed to be bug-free.

I'm sure virtually all COBOL programmers use at least some of these methods.  The result is a very significant increase in
productivity,
as well as accuracy.  No, I don't actually type every character of
every line of code I produce as it is included in the program.  But
every line of code is exactly as I would have typed it if I had
(except with fewer errors), and I did type the original code at some
point.  The bottom line to me is #1 quality and #2 quantity, which
this helps me to achieve.  If this qualifies as 'not writing' all the
code, then I'm guilty. :-)  But even if you excluded all the code I
have copied as described, the remainder would still be a substantial
fraction of the total, though I don't have enough data to quantify it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3707f623@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <9eRN2.9713$Ek.4011488@news1.mia>`

```
I agree that your method (which many of us use some variant of)
is a very effective and productive way of doing things. I just
think (as others in this thread) that the lines of code so produced
do not count as 'experience'. Experience is somehow connected
to learning and you gain very little experience during a full day
of using your method. You do apply a lot of previous experience.
So, it is producing code, not writing code. Personally, I try to
produce code too, rather than write it when I can. My wife has many
times asked how I can sit a whole day and think hard. I can't.
I (as you) just churn out code without much thought, because
I have tools and boiler-plate stuff, that help me get by without
much effort. Progress is measured in all the things you don't
have to think about. The point is that 3,000,000 lines of
code is not a measure of experience or effort, but just shows
how effective your tools are. Like flying across the continent
is not  a measure of how good I am at travelling, but rather
shows what a good tool I use (a Boeing 757). Everyone following
this NG knows about the 3M lines, but the number itself does
give a mandate. One could argue that because of your method
you don't (as I) know anymore how to program from scratch.
Without your tools you (and I) would be paralyzed. Our advice
to people that do not use (our) tools must always be taken
with several grains of salt, because we are not in their situation.

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote
> Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> >Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm sure virtually all COBOL programmers use at least some of these
methods.  The result is a very significant increase in
> productivity,
> as well as accuracy.  No, I don't actually type every character of
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ic3O2.10625$Ek.4356558@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <9eRN2.9713$Ek.4011488@news1.mia> <3707f623@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
>I agree that your method (which many of us use some variant of)
>is a very effective and productive way of doing things. I just
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>how effective your tools are.

Virtually all the 'boilerplate' code I use is in COPY files, and I
don't count that code each time I use it.

We agree on the importance of efficiency tools and methods.  But I
view code reuse a bit differently than you expressed above.  When
I simply use a library COPY file, I do get experience in using that
particular resource, and the code I write to invoke it would count
toward experience, though the code in the COPY file would not count,
of course.  But if I create 10 systems for people to use, I still
get the same experience in identifying and satisfying client's
needs, whether I write all the code, or copy some of it.  I still
must think about what I'm using.  If you're a pilot, flying over
the desert does give you flying experience. :-)  After all, you
don't just sit and watch the tools and methods work by themselves,
you are actively using them, intently thinking about the process.

And as for code reuse (at least the way I reuse code), you still
identify what code is needed, remember where you have written similar
code, carefully examine it line by line and modify it for the task at
hand.  The thought that goes into it is a significant portion of the
thought used to create the code in the first place, sometimes just as
much, but the physical process of creating it is abbreviated.  It is
not the typing that is the essence of the programming experience, but
the thinking through the logic process, why it is needed, what it does,
how it works, and how it is used, that is the programming experience.
I definitely see this as 'writing' code.  It's not as if you were using
code that you did not write in the first place (possibly many times),
or were using the code without thinking through it's logic again (which
you can't do if you are modifying it :).  It may not have the same
'experience value' as writing the code from scratch, but the value
would be some significant portion of that.  And which experience is
more meaningful, typing code you have written a hundred times before,
or improving your production quality skills? :-)  If I had created only
20 computer systems but had typed them all line by line with no code
reuse, instead of over 60, writing the same routines over and over
again, would I know more or understand programming better?  Just think
of how many more unique problems I have encountered and solved in those
40 additional systems. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ How we create code, was: Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37096562.698982057@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <9eRN2.9713$Ek.4011488@news1.mia> <3707f623@news3.us.ibm.net> <Ic3O2.10625$Ek.4356558@news1.mia>`

```
On Mon, 05 Apr 1999 13:50:32 GMT, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

I would like to add some things to the philisophical discussion this
has become.

I note two things about myself.  

First:  I have times when I am in "work mode" and times when I am not.
These seem cyclical - with a ramp up, a peak and then a rapid
cool-down.  I can code like crazy.  Given specs that I don't have to
pull like teeth, I can develop a system in record time.  However,
sometimes I'm just not "in the mood" and even if I WANT to do it, I
just can't.

Second:  I can't overemphasize the COLLABORATIVE process. (Hope I
spelled that correctly.)  I can sit down with someone, and we can
analyze the best way to do something.  We can weigh the pros and cons
of future maintenance, ease and speed of development, and end user
use.  We can hash out the best algorythm.  I can do this with a
"friendly" and well and an "unfriendly" partner.  Some people become
SO attached to an idea, to a method, or to a plan that the REFUSE to
budge.  May I never be accused of that.  Sometimes I even take the
position OPPOSITE the one I really like.  I argue against my idea
trying to punch holes in it.  Sometimes I argue vehemently FOR my idea
only to finally realize that it is just plain WRONG.  This type of
give and take is essential and leads to a REALLY GOOD end product.  I
have had the joy of continually working with people who, while
PASSIONATE about their positions are willing to realize their own
positions weakness and jump at the chance to use a better way.  This
process is something I really enjoy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e9h2o$51a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Do you count code written by code that you wrote?
If I count listings of programs, all of which I wrote, then I get one
figure.  If I take lines typed, then I get a number about 1/5 of that, maybe
even less.

Copy books are major, but so are generated screens, etc.

I figure about 3-4 million lines counting the former way. Counting only
lines that I actually sat down and "composed", the figure might go a million
for thirty years.

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net>...
>Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
>news:3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bh2O2.10548$Ek.4333778@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e9h2o$51a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>Do you count code written by code that you wrote?
>If I count listings of programs, all of which I wrote, then I get one
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>go a million for thirty years.

There is only a certain specific kind of code that I generate, and it
is only applicable to certain online programs.  Even in those programs
it only represents perhaps 5% of the program code at most.  It is just
simple, repetitive code which is tedious to write.  I do not generate
screens, I create them manually, the screen definitions in the programs
and the actual screen template files we use on mainframes.

If I write COPY files for a system, I count that code only once for
that system.

The 4 million lines of COBOL code really is a conservative estimate.
I could make a pretty good case for between 5 and 6 million COBOL,
plus perhaps 2 million other languages.  I don't have hard data ready
to back it up, but a good estimate, from the kinds and number of
programs I have written, and the way I work, would be at least 2
million lines of COBOL directly coded by hand specifically for the
program, not counting copied code.  I copy very little code in other
languages I have used, mostly various assembly languages, BASIC and
C.

I have said this before, but it is an actual fact that when I work
onsite with a client, sometimes people come to my desk just to watch
me write code, and frequently express amazement at how fast I work.
An old friend, who used to be supervisor over data entry clerks, took
pride in the fact that he was significantly faster than any of his
clerks.  Every time time he comes to my office and sees me coding,
he expresses amazement at how fast I type.  The weird thing is, I
never learned to touch type. :-)  Even weirder, I'm not fast writing
text like this post.  But when I start programming, my mental state
changes or something, and I go like blazes. :-)  I have many, many
times written two thousand lines or more of unique procedure division
code in a few hours.  Even on difficult new code, if I don't write at
least a few hundred lines a day, I am either doing something other
than coding, or ill. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is there a program to nicely reformat COBOL code?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7een6e$rku$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e9h2o$51a$1@news.igs.net> <Bh2O2.10548$Ek.4333778@news1.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>Donald Tees wrote:
---------------large snippage----------.
>
>I have said this before, but it is an actual fact that when I work
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

When something becomes natural to us some have said it's something that has
been repeated many many times. The process begins in childhood when parents
tell us over and over something is hot, or cold as an example.  Next big
push might be learning to ride a bike, or drive a car.  These things become
second nature.   Retyping a 600 page book using WP5.1 on a dos machine made
me very familar with the features of WordPerfect (that I used), and the
placement of the keys. My fingers go there without my direct knowledge it
seems.

Learning the 64 commands of the Univac I did a good job of making that
second nature, too.

As I age, these talents are a blessing.  Where I fall down is failure to
stay concurrent so that my smoose factor has dwindled a great deal.

However, your discussion of the polish, speed, and other factors you all
have acquired in writing COBOL applications based on lines of code per year
would be better expressed in two ways.  $ per year, and system life.  Now,
there's a discussion.  <very big grin>.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Coding speed

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u8WyeCTg#GA.279@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net> <Xx5M2.1598$my4.741170@news3.mia> <7e2s2m$8qs$1@news-1.news.gte.net> <3QJN2.9253$Ek.3806178@news1.mia> <37078276@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e9h2o$51a$1@news.igs.net> <Bh2O2.10548$Ek.4333778@news1.mia>`

```
A lot of it is just what tricks/techniques you pick up along the way, i.e.
experience, such as making good use of edit profiles including software
tabs, learning more of the edit commands that many people never bother to
learn, like shifting and bounds and :TS and :TF and stuff like that. In one
shop I even wrote an edit macro to create a change log or add an entry to
it, I wish I'd kept it somewhere. Just simple tools and things that don't
take much time to create but sure save lots of time with use. :)


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Full Shareware Version of Cbl-Beau and Cbl-Report for Free

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<37272503.14DB10BD@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<7dn4q7$729$1@sparky.wolfe.net>`

```
You may have heard about or participated in a recent discussion 
about merits of automatically beautifying (or formatting) all
Cobol code in an organization to make it look consistent and 
easy to read.

The only thing that seemed to be missing at the time 
of this discussion was a good widely available and 
highly customizable tool that can do a quality job of 
Cobol code formatting for less.

Now you have such tool for free! For limited time
you can download shareware distribution of 
*CobolBeautifier* and *CobolReporter* tools that 
has NO line count or any other limitations!
Download address is:  http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/

CobolBeautifier is the ultimate tool for the beautification job:
- Fully parses the program, like a real compiler.
- Beautification is based on logical program 
  structure, as discovered by CobolTransformer parser.
- Understands 14 dialects of Cobol.
- Can inline and un-inline copybooks.
- Customizable indentation step, max indentation position.
- Customizable positions for PIC, VALUE, USAGE and similar clauses.
- Can generate running line IDs at columns 73 to 80.

In addition to beautification it performs 
important code standardization tasks:
- Add VALUE clause to WORKING-STORAGE section items 
  that have no VALUE clause.
- And END-IF, END-SEARCH, END-EVALUATE, END-PERFORM
  scope delimiting statements to clearly show
  where the scope of statement ends.
- Normalize data item level numbers.
- Add numeric running prefix to section, paragraph and
  data item names.

CobolReporter is a tool that produces reports on your Cobol programs. 
This reports include:
- For every data item: level, PIC, effective USAGE, length in bytes,
  byte offset from the 01 level, byte offset from the start of the section.
- Number of lines, lines of code (LOC), tokens.
- Program-IDs and ENTRYs defined in this file.
- Number of sections, paragraphs, sentences and statements.
- Subsystem invocations: File IO, Sort-Merge, SQL, CICS, Report Write.
- Accept/Display ANSI and Accept/Display Screen subsystem statements used.
- Programs CALLed from the file.
- All EXEC subsystems called from the file.
- All copybooks used in the file, with count of use for each copybook.

These tools are based on CobolTransformer technology 
http://www.siber.com/sct/. You can license CobolTransformer
to build conversion and analysis tools like these or even better.


Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x

1. Copying this FAQ.
This FAQ is copyright 1994-1998 by William M. Klein.
It  may  be  freely  redistributed as long as  it  is  completely
unmodified and that no attempt is made to restrict any  recipient
from  redistributing it on the same terms. It may not be sold  or
incorporated  into  commercial  documents  without  the   written
permission of the copyright holder.
Permission is granted for this document to be made available  for
file  transfer from sites offering unrestricted file transfer  on
the Internet and from the COBOL Forums on CompuServe and Bix.
This  document  is  provided as is, without  any  warranty.  Your
mileage may vary.

2. Where can I get this FAQ?
This  document should be archived at many sites on the  Internet,
including  rtfm.mit.edu -- the archive site for all FAQs.  It  is
also     available     via     e-mail     from     the     author
(wmklein@ix.netcom.com).
An HTML version of the latest FAQ is also available from
     http://www.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm

2.1 Where do I turn if my question isn't answered in this FAQ?
If you have access to the web, then you should go to
http://www.dejanews.com/home_ps.shtml
Then  enter "comp.lang.cobol" as the group and whatever  you  are
looking  for as the subject. The chances are that you  will  find
more  than enough answers already (and many quite recent) to your
question. If you don't find an answer here, then look at 18. What
can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?

3. Where can I get a COBOL compiler?
There  are many vendors who sell COBOL compilers. Almost  all  of
the  mainframe  hardware/operating system vendors,  also  sell  a
COBOL  compiler for their systems. The following are some of  the
vendors providing COBOL compilers for systems where they are  not
the operating system vendor.

3.1 for DOS, Windows, or OS/2?
Acucorp,  CA,  Fujitsu, Liant, IBM, and Micro Focus  all  produce
compilers  for one or more of these environments. Microsoft  used
to re-badge the Micro Focus compiler, but not any more.

3.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
Micro  Focus has a 32-bit development environment for Windows  95
and Windows/NT called NetExpress. A student version of NetExpress
is also available from Micro Focus. Fujitsu's compiler also works
under  both Windows/95 and Windows/NT. Liant has both 16 bit  and
32  bit  development systems for Windows, Win95 and NT.  Computer
Associates' CA-Realia II Workbench Release 3.0 provides a  32-bit
COBOL  compiler and runtime. IBM's VisualAge for  COBOL  is  also
available for Windows/NT.

3.3 for UNIX?
Acucorp,  Fujitsu, Liant, and Micro Focus have products available
across  a  large  number of UNIX platforms.  Some  OEMs  re-badge
and/or re-engineer these products for their own systems too.
Liant used to provide LPI COBOL for Sun SPARC with Solaris 2,  HP
9000  with  HP-UX and Intel-based machines with UNIX SVR4,  SVR3,
and SCO. They no longer make this product.
IBM sells its COBOLSet for AIX.

3.4 for Linux?
AcuCOBOL-85  and AcuCOBOL-GT are available for Linux.  Also,  the
iBCS2 code for Linux should mean that it is possible to get  some
of the i486 COBOL packages for operating systems such as SCO UNIX
to work.
There  were reports that earlier versions of Micro Focus products
worked   under   SCO  UNIX  emulations  under   Linux.   However,
representatives from Micro Focus doubt that this would  still  be
true with their current compiler technology.

3.5 for the Macintosh?
Acucorp  produces a COBOL development system for the Mac  running
A/UX.

3.6 for other environments?
Fujitsu COBOL is also available for IBM MVS and Fujitsu MSP.
Ryan McFarland COBOL is also available for OpenVMS.
AcuCOBOL-85  and  AcuCOBOL-GT are available on a  wide  range  of
environments, including OpenVMS.
Most  major vendors have their own COBOL implementation, or  have
someone else's ported to their platform(s). There are quite a few
available for CP/M and MP/M, and one is even rumored to have been
available for the PERQ workstation.

4. Is there a free COBOL compiler?
Just for the record, no COBOL tools are listed in the Catalog  of
compilers,  interpreters,  and other  language  tools  posted  to
comp.compilers and comp.lang.misc. This probably means that there
are no freely available COBOL compiler sources.
However, there are a number of free compilers that do not include
the  source. For example Fujitsu does offer a FREE COBOL  Starter
Set  for Windows. (See below for a list of various free compilers
and the environments in which they are available.)
Also  several books in the booklists come with a COBOL  compiler.
See section 10 for details.

4.1 for DOS, Windows or OS/2.
There  is  a freely available COBOL compiler for DOS. It  can  be
found  on  many archive sites, named COBOL650.ZIP. You also  need
DPATH30.ZIP. Have a read through Section 8 before you start.
Bob Wolfe has made the compiler available at the Flexus FTP site,
http://www.flexus.com.
It  is  widely  rumored that the sources for  this  compiler  are
available  from  a BBS. This no longer appears to  be  the  case.
Numerous  attempts  have  completely failed  to  track  down  the
sources.
There  is a COBOL701.ARJ archive that contains a version of COBOL

6.50  with a limited number of compiles. It was an attempt  at  a
full  integrated  development environment, including  an  editor.
Unfortunately, no documentation is included.
Also,  it  may  be  possible  to run the  freely  available  CP/M
compiler (see 4.5) under a freely available CP/M emulator.
In  addition,  Fujitsu  is currently offering  a  copy  of  their
Fujitsu COBOL V2 Enterprise Edition which includes PowerCOBOL  to
those who register at
     http://www.adtools.com/

4.2 for Windows/NT or Windows/95?
Fujitsu  is currently offering a copy of their Fujitsu  COBOL  V3
Starter Set to those who register at
     http://www.adtools.com/
The CP/M compiler/emulator combination should work here too.

4.3 for UNIX?
There are no well-documented examples of a freely available COBOL
compiler for UNIX. COBOL 6.50 might run under a UNIX emulation of
a  DOS  system,  however. (For example, VP/ix, SoftPC  or  dosemu
under Linux.)
The  CP/M compiler (see 4.5) should run under a CP/M emulator for
UNIX in a similar fashion.

4.4 for the Macintosh?
Not that I know of.

4.4a for Amiga?
According to HarriottC@cardiff.ac.uk (on March 2, 1998),
There  is  a  freely available COBOL compiler/interpreter  (Amiga
WB2.0+).  GUI/CLI, largely ANSI'85 compliant takes  the  form  of
Microsoft Cobol 2.xx ?)
This is available as postware via Aminet Dev/lang nrcobol_1?.lha

4.5 for other environments ?
There is a freely available CP/M COBOL compiler/interpreter  (NPS
Micro   COBOL).  This  is  available  via  anonymous   FTP   from
oak.oakland.edu  in  /pub/cpm/cobol.  However,   Stefano   Priola
(s70829@galileo.polito.it) comments :
     "I've  used the CPM COBOL ... I think that this compiler  is
     much too old to use or for a student to learn COBOL."

5. Commercial COBOL Products
In order to present an un-biased list of commercial COBOL product
offerings  I've  pulled  in  the  product  overviews  from   each
company's    marketing   information.   For   detailed    product
descriptions, you should probably contact the specific vendor.

5.1.5 COBOL Workbench� v4.0
Workbench  integrates the industry-standard COBOL  compiler  with
development   and   testing   tools   that   promote   programmer
productivity,  satisfaction and quality systems  development.  It
features   powerful   editing,   debugging   and   communications
facilities  as  well  as graphical data  tools  -  all  within  a
powerful  and  easy to use development environment.  Micro  Focus
COBOL   Workbench  supports  the  creation  and  maintenance   of
enterprise-wide  client-server  applications   in   addition   to
offloading  development and maintenance of mainframe  application
to PCs.

5.1 Acucorp
5.1.1 AcuCOBOL-GT
AcuCOBOL-GT  enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured  GUI  COBOL  applications  on  any  of   over   600
platforms,  with full object code portability using a single  set
of   COBOL   source   code.  Programmers  can  create   graphical
applications including floating windows, graphical controls (such
as  entry  fields,  frames,  radio  buttons,  push  buttons,  and
labels),  menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars using COBOL extensions
that are consistent with traditional COBOL syntax.

5.1.2 AcuCOBOL-85
AcuCOBOL-85  is  a  powerful ANSI-85 COBOL compiler  that  offers
exceptionally compact, highly optimized machine-independent COBOL
code.   AcuCOBOL-85  is  portable  across  over   600   different
platforms,  including: AIX, AOS/VS, BSD/OS, DG/UX, Digital  UNIX,
HP-UX,  Linux,  MS-DOS, MPE/iX, Open VMS, OS/2, Solaris,  Ultrix,
UNIX, UnixWare, Windows, Windows NT, and Xenix.

5.2 Computer Associates/Realia
5.2.1 CA-Realia� II Workbench
CA-Realia� II Workbench provides an exciting mainframe-compatible
COBOL  development environment on the PC. CA-Realia II  Workbench
uses  the  power of the PC environment to improve the development
and   maintenance   of   COBOL  and   CICS   applications.   This
revolutionary  technology provides a new and  exciting  graphical
user  interface (GUI) workstation environment that operates under
MS-Windows, Windows 95, Windows NT, and OS/2.
CA-Realia  II Workbench includes a high-speed COBOL compiler,  an
interactive  debugger,  a  COBOL-Intelligent  analyzer,  a   CICS
emulator,  a  COBOL-sensitive editor, and a complete  life  cycle
manager  with  mainframe  connectivity. Its  latest  version  for
Windows  NT  and Windows 95 includes a 32-bit COBOL compiler  and
runtime.  Its  mainframe  options  allow  mainframe-compiled  and
executing programs to be debugged and analyzed under the friendly
Workbench GUI

5.3 Fujitsu Software
5.3.1 Fujitsu COBOL
Fujitsu  COBOL is the highest performing, mission critical  COBOL
available  for  mainframe, UNIX, and Windows  platforms.  Fujitsu
COBOL supports IBM VS COBOL II, Micro Focus, and the latest  ANSI
and  ISO  standards and allows developers to access,  reuse,  and
retarget   their   existing   legacy   COBOL   applications   for
Client/Server  and Internet platforms while fully exploiting  all
the functions of Microsoft Windows 95/NT and BackOffice.

5.3.2 PowerCOBOL�
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers  to  use  their  existing COBOL  knowledge  to  build
scaleable and maintainable state-of-the-art Windows two-tier  and
three-tier  client/server  and Internet  applications  with  data
access to local and remote databases.

5.3.3 NetCOBOL�
Fujitsu NetCOBOL is a revolutionary COBOL compiler that generates
100%  Pure Java Bytecode-based applications/applets from existing
legacy  COBOL programs. NetCOBOL programs execute on any  machine
which  supports  the  Java�  Virtual Machine,  including  Network
Computers.   NetCOBOL  is  the  first  and  only  true   platform
independent COBOL compiler.

5.3.4 PowerBSORT�
PowerBSORT  is a high speed Business SORT, MERGE, and  COPY  tool
for  handling  large quantities of data. PowerBSORT significantly
shortens the time needed to sort, merge or copy data for business
processing.  PowerBSORT provides a record reconstruction  feature
that  creates an output record by relocating and combining fields
from  the input record with self-defined values. PowerBSORT same-
key  feature combines values from multiple records into a  single
record by adding the summation field values when records have the
same  key field value. The same-key feature is used with the sort
or merge functions.

5.3.5 PowerFORM�
PowerFORM  is  a  tool  for  designing  and  programming  printed
reports. These reports can use all fonts supported by the  target
printers  and  can include graphics such as boxes,  shading,  and
bitmaps.  COBOL  applications create  reports  by  writing  to  a
special  print  file, or they can gain greater control  by  using
PowerFORM RTS calls.

5.3.6 PowerGEM Plus
PowerGEM  Plus is a graphical tool for managing the versions  and
histories  of your development resources, including source  code,
screen  forms,  and  documents.  PowerGEM  Plus  makes  resources
available   to  multiple  users  simultaneously,  but   restricts
modification to a single user. Resources are thus protected,  and
modifications  are  traceable. PowerGEM  Plus  keeps  and  tracks
version  and  history statistics for each part of  a  development
project.

5.4 Liant/Ryan McFarland
5.4.1 RM/COBOL Compiler and Runtime System
RM/COBOL's  sophisticated runtime system permits the  maintenance
of  single  source and object code - and the easy  deployment  of
applications  on  a wide choice of open client/server  platforms.
Their  "claim  to fame" is that their compiler generates  objects
that are portable between platforms without recompiling. This  is
why  you need a runtime for the desired platform to interpret the
object

5.4.2 RM/Interface Builder
RM/COBOL developers can now use their choice of industry-standard
tools  such as Microsoft's Visual Basic and Borland's  Delphi  to
develop a true Windows client user interface for Windows or UNIX-
based COBOL application

5.4.3 RM/Enterprise CodeBench
RM/COBOL  developers  can  now  take  full  advantage  of   their
client/server development environments. This new version  of  our
powerful  graphical workbench enables the management of  existing
UNIX- or Windows-based RM/COBOL applications to be handled from a
remote Windows workstation.

5.4.4 RM/Panels
RM/Panels allows you to develop COBOL based applications  with  a
whole new event-driven look-and-feel with true GUI functionality,
while maintaining complete object portability.

5.4.5 Relational Data Bridge (formerly Relativity)
This  product allows you ODBC access to your COBOL data. You  can
update and insert records from ODBC.

5.5 Micro Focus
5.5.1 Micro Focus� Object COBOL v4.0 (32-bit) for OS/2
Micro  Focus  Object COBOL v4.0 is a powerful 32-bit  development
environment,  enabling development and deployment  of  COBOL  and
Object COBOL applications for OS/2.� Micro Focus has brought  the
power of COBOL to the PC with extensive syntax support, including
object  oriented  COBOL, IBM� OS/VS COBOL, DOS COBOL,  COBOL/370�
and  a wide range of other dialects. This release of Object COBOL
extends  the power of previous versions of Micro Focus  COBOL  by
adding   full   support  for  object  oriented  programming   and
exploiting the power of the 32-bit architecture.

5.5.2 Micro Focus� NetExpress V2.0 for Windows NT and Windows� 95
NetExpress  combines Micro Focus Object COBOL� with a specialized
set  of  tools  to  allow  you to develop applications  for  PCs,
distributed environments and the internet. It includes a powerful
integrated   development  environment,  the  Micro  Focus   COBOL
compiler,  a  powerful  debugger, a complete  set  of  tools  for
developing    either   web-based   applications   or    graphical
applications  for  Win32 platforms, full  support  for  accessing
relational  databases, support for creating  ActiveX  objects  in
COBOL and many other features.
A  student  version  of NetExpress V2.0 is  also  available  that
provides  most  of  the  functionality of  the  full  version  of
NetExpress, but with limits on the size of applications that  can
be  developed  and it cannot be used to develop applications  for
sale.

5.5.3 Object COBOL � D e v e l o p e r Suite v4.1 for UNIX
Micro  Focus  Object COBOL Developer Suite v4.1 for  UNIX  is  an
advanced  integrated programming environment for  developing  and
deploying  client  /server  Object  COBOL  and  procedural  COBOL
applications on a wide range of UNIX � operating environments. It
includes:  a flexible, cross-platform COBOL compiler;  a  set  of
powerful  programmer productivity tools; a set  of  Object  COBOL
class libraries; transparent support for large files; support for
DBCS  applications; user interface development tools; new on-line
documentation; the ability to execute COBOL CGI's developed using
NetExpress;  and  run-time  facilities  to  simplify  application
deployment.

5.5.4 Personal COBOL for Windows v3.1, with Object Orientation
Micro   Focus  Personal  COBOL  for  Windows  3.1,  with   Object
Orientation, is an educational version of Micro Focus  COBOL  for
anyone interested in learning procedural COBOL programming or the
new   object-oriented  COBOL  extensions   in   a   Windows   3.1
environment.
Personal  COBOL is ideal for educational institutions,  students,
training organizations and self-learners who need an inexpensive,
yet  full-featured and modern learning tool for  introductory  or
advanced COBOL.

5.6 PCYACC
PCYACC  7.5  is a complete language development environment  that
generates  C, C++, Java, Delphi, and VBS source code  from  input
Language Description Grammars for building Assemblers, Compilers,
Interpreters,  Browsers,  Page  Description  Languages,  Language
Translators,   Syntax  Directed  Editors,  Language   Validators,
Natural  Language  Processors, Expert System  Shells,  and  Query
Languages.  The PCYACC Tool-Kit includes PCLEX, Visual  Debugging
Tools, Object-Oriented Class Library's, and Pre-Written "Drop-In"
Language  engines for virtually every computer  language  in  the
world.
Note the original information stated that support was for the  77
and  90  Standards (which are good years for the Fortran Standard
but  not for COBOL). It is assumed that they actually support the
74 and 85 Standards of COBOL.
Contact them at:
http://www.abxsoft.com/

7. What about COBOL standards?
7.1 What standards exist?
The  current  COBOL standard is the ISO/ANSI '85  standard.  This
replaced the ANSI '74 standard.
There  are  two amendments to the COBOL '85 standard -- intrinsic
functions and corrections.
The  ANSI  (J4) and ISO (WG4) groups are working on  created  the
next Standard. See below for its current status and contents.

7.2 Can I get the standards via FTP?
The standards do not appear to be available via FTP.
     Note:  See  the next section for information on obtaining  a
     copy of the draft of the next COBOL Standard.
This  is probably because they are very large and are copyrighted
by  ANSI  and/or ISO, although the issue of copyright is in  some
doubt.

Whether  or  not  the next COBOL Standard will  or  will  not  be
copyrighted  is  a  matter of discussion  and  argument.  As  the
current  Standard was put into the "public domain" and  the  next
standard is a "derived" work, it is questionable whether  or  not
the  next  Standard  will have an ISO or  ANSI  -  or  any  other
copyright.

7.3 What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard
and what is in it?
NOTE: To see (or download) a copy of the latest draft of the next
COBOL Standard, see
http://www.microfocus.com/Standards/basedoc.htm
First  "9x"  has  become "0x". After the last public  review,  it
turns  out  that there will need to be at least one  more  public
review before it gets approved.
  1.   Major features/enhancements:
       A.   OO gets all the press
       B.   Common exception handling, C-ification (pointers, call
prototypes, typedefs, and everything else needed to use C-type
APIs), user-defined functions, file-sharing/record-locking, 31-
digit numbers, portable arithmetic, National character handling
(MOCS or DBCS but more so), are all some of the "biggies" that
some people are looking for.
      C.  In the category of "old technology" getting a new face lift
and being added to the Standard (as required) see Report Writer
enhancements, VALIDATE feature, and character screen I/O support
via ACCEPT/DISPLAY.
  2.   Little things (and there are too many for me to remember off
     the  top  of  my head) include everything from dynamic  file
     assignment (in the SELECT/ASSIGN statement), assigning multiple
     values via the VALUE statement in tables, sorting tables, hex
     literals, GOBACK verb, apostrophe as quote, bits and Boolean
     support,
          and LOTS more

8. COBOL 6.50
8.1 How do I compile my programs?
It  is  assumed you have installed cobol650.zip in the  directory
C:\COBOL650.  In  install.doc you will find some  information  on
running the compiler.
  1.   Add C:\COBOL650 to the PATH
2.   Run APPEND on C:\COBOL650 :
     APPEND C:\COBOL650
  3.    The  install.doc contained in cobol650.zip  refers  to  a
     program DPATH.COM to be run instead of APPEND. The DOS program
     APPEND seems to work too.
4.   Now you can compile your .cob files as explained in
install.doc.
When  trying  to compile sources in a directory other  than  that
where  the compiler is installed, the compiler terminates without
an  error.  This  restriction is not documented  in  install.doc,
which is probably a result of using APPEND instead of DPATH.
The  compiler accesses drive A:. You should have a disk  in  this
drive.
Peter Mikalajunas adds:
To avoid the need to use drive A:, you should do the following :
subst a: c:\cobol650
When you type A: you will drop into the C:\COBOL650 subdirectory.
The  compiler will behave normally at this point, not  constantly
searching drive A:.
When you are done with a session do the following :
C:
subst a: /D

8.2 How do I link my objects ?
There  is no linker with the COBOL 6.50 compiler. To link objects
you need to use the linker from MS-DOS v3.3 or earlier.
Ralf Laemmel adds :
You  can  use  newer  linkers, especially  from  newer  Microsoft
compiler products, too.
And Peter Mikalajunas has found that :
Tlink  compiled with obj files without complaint, but  the  exe's
were useless. What did work was Link version 5.31.009 which comes
with Visual Basic for DOS. It compiled all obj files I tried  and
the EXEs ran perfectly.
Clinton G. Downing also reports :
The  linker from IBM DOS v2.1 does now work, at least on the PS/2
70. The MS-DOS v3.3 linker works fine, however.
Steve ??? <steve@mado.demon.co.uk> has reported some success with
a linker from the SimTel archives. Look for sl101a.zip.

9. What about OO COBOL?
9.1 ANSI and ISO Work
The  draft  of  the Committee Draft (CD !>!) of  the  next  COBOL
Standard   which  underwent  public  review  in   1997   included
significant  OO COBOL support. Based on the comments received  J4
(ANSI) and WG4 (ISO) or currently revising it (and deleting  some
features.

9.2 Micro Focus
Micro  Focus has an OO COBOL product. It does not conform exactly
to  the  OO COBOL proposal currently being discussed, however  --
the  syntax  is  a  subset of the current  proposal  with  a  few
variations.   Multiple  inheritance,  conformance   and   garbage
collection   are   not   implemented.  Also,   vocabularies   are
implemented  though these are not currently part of the  proposed
standard.

9.3 IBM
IBM offers OO COBOL products for MVS, OS/2, Windows, and AIX. (It
does   not   currently  offer  it  for  OS/400  or   VM).   There
implementation is a subset of s snap-shot of the Standard  as  it
was  in  1995. The syntax is portable across their platforms  and
interfaces with SOM and DSOM.

9.4 Others
I believe that other vendors are at various stages of developing,
testing, and distributing OO COBOL products. For specific product
information,  you  should contact the vendor  (such  as  Fujitsu,
Hitachi, Realia, etc)

10.5 "COBOL For Dummies"
To  install the Fujitsu compiler that comes with this  book,  you
will need to enter a serial number that was accidentally left out
of  the  first  printing of the book. It will install  with  this
number:
 103-2001 1699-03317-70168
The first part of the number should already be in the window,  so
all you will have to enter is
99-03317-70168
Another reported problem with the Fujitsu compiler provided  with
this  book  is linking of programs. The following information  on
the  problem and solution was provided by Thane Hubbell on  March
20, 1998.
  "When Linking you will get a warning message that says:
  No Entry Point
  The program continues to link however. It will not run. Here
  are the steps to get it to Link properly, eliminate the
  error message and run.
  >From the WINCOB window - NOT the WORK FRAME window, just
  the plain WINCOB window, click
  OPTION
  Click
  ADD
  Scroll down to MAIN
  Click
  ADD
  Select the option for "Compile as Main Program"
  Click
  OK
  Now the program will compile and link as a main program and
  life will be just wonderful.
  PS: Thanks to Robert S. Robbins for these instructions, I do
  not know if he still monitors the group, but he deserves the
  credit."
