```
11. Is there a COBOL to C converter ?
Asking  this  question anywhere appears to generate much  general
flaming  and  general  language  wars  and  very  little   useful
information.

No  such beast is listed in the free compilers FAQ, but an ad has
appears  in  the US publication "Programmer's Shop  Catalog"  for
COBOL to C (and PL/I to C) translators. Contact :
Micro-Processor Services,
92 Stone Hurst Lane,
Dix Hills, NY 11746
  Tel: (516) 499 4461
  Fax: (516)- 499-4727
E-Mail: info@mpsinc.com, mpsinc@netusa.net
www: http://www.mpsinc.com
http://www.netusa.net/~mpsinc
A toolset for conversion from COBOL to several other languages is
available.  A  tool  first produces structured  diagrams  (Nassi-
Shneiderman)  from existing source files. Structural  errors  are
identified  and you can edit to correct them. Another tool  takes
those  same  diagrams and produces source code in one of  several
languages  (COBOL,  C,  Ada,  Basic, Clipper,  dBaseIV,  Fortran,
Modula 2, Natural, PL/1, etc.)
The  toolset  is called XperCase by Siemens, and is available  in
the US from:
Boston Technical Distribution Corp.
3 Center Plaza, Suite 440
Boston, MA 02108
  Tel: (617) 248-8989
  Fax: (617) 248-8986
Laurent Sabarthez contributed :
  "Some years ago I was Project Leader on a software project
  termed COBTOC (COBol TO C translation). The company is by
  now out of business, but the rights on this product were
  purchased by NSI (Network Solutions Inc., Herndon, VA, USA -
  Emitt McHenry was Chairman).
  COBTOC is actually a translator generator. It can produce a
  specialized translator for any reasonable COBOL dialect,
  given a dialect description very close to the usual syntax
  notation one can find into any COBOL Reference Manual.
  "semantics" peculiarities are also described in this way.
  Once a translator has been produced in this way, a source
  management module allows automated translation of the COBOL
  source modules. A run-time library is also automatically
  produced as a by-product of the translator.
  The COBTOC user gets a set of C files, each being the
  translation of a corresponding COBOL file. You can get K&R
  C, ANSI C, or common variants like Turbo C. The overall
  structure of the COBOL program is preserved upon
  translation. Identifiers are straightforward transformations
  of COBOL names. Paragraph structure and flow control are
  also preserved, like all name space properties attached to
  I/O and file management.
  The C files are compiled and linked with the run-time
  library, which supports data handling, edition, arithmetic,
  direct I/O, file I/O and transaction management (e.g. CICS).
  Executables are intended to run on any platform supporting
  POSIX C compiling and standard library linkage.
  COBTOC was left by my co-workers and me in an alpha release
  state, mid 1993.
  I don't know the end of the story, but NSI should provide
  more up-to-date information about it.
  There is also a project running to create a COBOL to C
  converter (possibly COBOL to C++ ?) available under the GNU
  license. "
  Another project that can be viewed as either a COBOL to C
  converter or as a compiler, is the "CobCy Project"
For    more    details,    see    the    CobCy    homepage     at
http://www.netsis.it/~asantini/cobcy/.

12. COBOL code generators
12.1 CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation
CA-Telon and CA-Telon PWS (Programmable Workstation) are complete
solutions for designing, generating and maintaining your mission-
critical  COBOL and PL/1 applications. High-level, non-procedural
design  and prototyping, combined with automatic code generation,
result  in  higher  productivity, consistent standards  and  good
programming  practices. With CA-Telon PWS, you can  offload  your
application  development projects from the mainframe and  provide
your   programmers   with  a  complete  application   development
environment on the workstation.
The CA-Telon Design Facility (TDF) provides a "fill-in-the-blank"
approach  for  designing online and batch programs. An  automated
prototyping facility assists in the analysis/design phases of the
life  cycle. CA-Telon generates structured portable  code  for  a
multitude  of target environments and DBMSs. Available for:  MVS,
DOS, OS/2

12.3 ETK
ETK (Easy ToolKit) was developed by SEMA Group in Belgium. It  is
now marketed by TOSC International, Inc :
TOSC International, Inc.
3900 Essex Lane, suite 250
Houston, TX 77027
  Tel: 713 961 1201
  Fax: 713 961 4989
Contact Ted Langlie. TOSC will also soon be on the Internet.
ETK   works   with  a  repository  where  the  programmer/analyst
describes  the  system to be generated in terms of data  domains,
elements,  tables,  relationships,  business  rules,   and   data
mappings.  From  this description ETK generates  complete  COBOL-
based applications that will run on many platforms.

13. COBOL Tools
This  section  documents  some  of  the  add-on  tools  that  are
available  for use with COBOL compilers. Further submissions  are
welcomed,  but  please try to keep them as  free  from  marketing
"hype" as possible.
Note: This section seems to be entirely devoted to tools that run
in  or  are  targeted  at  the PC and/or  Unix  world.  Mainframe
additions are welcome, but will probably be a while before I  can
add them.

13.1 creating GUI's
13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL.
VanGui  consists  of two major components: a design  tool  and  a
runtime  system.  The design tool is a Windows application  which
provides  COBOL developers with the capability to define windows,
populate  those windows with standard Windows and  VBX  controls,
adjust  the properties of those controls and attach COBOL  event-
handling logic to their events.
The  VanGui  Runtime  is  a  Windows .DLL  that  manages  Windows
messages, provides runtime support for the controls, and provides
a COBOL interface to the Windows API.
For more information on VanGui, see 13.1.1 VanGui for RM/COBOL
http://www.liant.com/products/language/rm/ib.html.
For another GUI tool from Liant, see 5.3.4 RM/Panels

13.1.2 From Acucorp
AcuBench

AcuBench  is an integrated development environment for ACUCOBOL.-
GT. Available for the Windows 95 and
Windows    NT   operating   systems,   AcuBench   combines    the
internationally acclaimed ACUCOBOL-GT
(Graphical Technology) compiler and runtime system with a set  of
graphically-based,  GT-optimized  WYSIWYG  screen  Painter,   and
language sensitive source Code Editor.

ACUCOBOL�-GT

ACUCOBOL�-GT enables COBOL programmers to implement  and  deliver
full-featured GUI COBOL
applications on any of over 600 platforms, with full object  code
portability using a single set of COBOL
source   code.  Programmers  can  create  graphical  applications
including floating windows, graphical
controls  (such  as  entry fields, frames,  radio  buttons,  push
buttons, and labels), menu bars, bitmaps, and toolbars
using COBOL extensions that are consistent with traditional COBOL
syntax.

13.1.3 Flexus COBOL spII
COBOL spII allows the COBOL programmer to create GUI or character
mode  screens  using ANSI standard COBOL CALL  USING  statements.
COBOL  spII screen definitions and source programs are 100% COBOL
compiler independent, 100% operating system independent and  100%
text mode to GUI mode independent.
Automatic   screen   conversion  tools   for   many   proprietary
environments  are also available from Flexus. These automate  the
task  of converting screen definitions from proprietary character
mode screens to GUI screen definitions.

13.1.4 CA-Visual Realia
[From CA's Web site:]
CA-Visual  Realia  is a complete Windows development  environment
that  provides COBOL programmers with an easy-to-use set of tools
for creating mission-critical GUI client/server applications. CA-
Visual  Realia  allows you to create Windows  applications  using
COBOL  for  the  business  logic  and  GUI  tools  for  the  user
interface.  CA-Visual Realia combines tools designed for  Windows
user   interface   development  with   powerful   COBOL   program
development tools. Creating a program's user interface can be  as
easy  as  dragging  controls onto forms, and then  modifying  the
control's properties.

13.1.5 Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
PowerCOBOL   is  a  visual,  object  oriented,  enterprise   wide
client/server Rapid Application Development(RAD) tool  for  COBOL
programmers.   The  PowerCOBOL  development  environment   allows
programmers to use COBOL as the GUI scripting language  to  build
scaleable  and maintainable state-of-the-art COBOL-based  Windows
two-tier  and  three-tier client/server and Internet applications
with data access to local and remote databases.

13.2 Sinc Inc. products
13.2.1 FlexGen 4GL Rapid Application Development Environment
A  repository-based, data dictionary-driven  tool  set  and  code
generator. It supports and generates code for RM/COBOL,  Acucorp,
Micro Focus COBOL, Realia (DOS Only), mbp (DOS Only) and runs and
deploys under DOS, Windows, Windows 95, NT, many flavors of UNIX,
VAX/VMS and Open VMS.

13.2.2 Legacy Liberator COBOL Migration Toolset
For   rehosting  proprietary  COBOLs,  converting  them  to  open
systems,  Acucorp, RM/COBOL or MF COBOL. User interfaces  can  be
converted  to  FlexGen user interfaces including the  ability  to
upgrade them to GUI.

13.2.3 Easy Query
A point-and-shoot report writer and query tool.

13.2.4 ODBC Access Tools
Allows Windows ODBC-enabled applications to access COBOL data.

13.3 Siber Systems
13.3.1 CobolTransformer
CobolTransformer  (SCT) is a next generation Cobol  reengineering
and  parsing toolkit (SDK). By using CobolTransformer components,
developers  are  able  to develop Cobol-transforming  (or  Cobol-
analyzing) product or project faster and the resulting tools  are
of much higher quality.

CobolTransformer is a library with API in C++ that includes:
  �    High quality Cobol Parser that parses 12 most popular Cobol
     dialects and that has tremendous error recovery capability.
  �     C++ library that client uses to browse and transform  the
     tree-based Internal Representation of Cobol programs. Definition-
     Use links attached to the Program Tree effectively make it a
     general case Graph.
  �     PrettyPrinter that transforms our Internal Representation
     back into beautifully indented
     human-readable Cobol program.

13.3.2 Cbl-Beau
CobolBeautifier does the following things:
  a.   Beautifully indents your Cobol program.
b.   Renumbers paragraph-names, section-names, data-names
c.   attach an increasing or decreasing prefix or suffix to all
paragraph-names and/or section-names and/or data-names in your
Cobol program. (Based on CobolTransformer.)
A   free   version  is  available  from  their   Web   site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
The   Commercial   version  will  allow  to  specify   your   own
indentation/pretty-printing rules, Program  you  style  rules  in
CobolTransformer and make all programs to comply automatically.
To be available soon.

13.3.3 Mf2Fsc
Mf2Fsc is a Micro Focus COBOL to Fujitsu COBOL converter.
It   converts  Micro  Focus  Cobol-85  to  Fujitsu  Cobol-85  and
beautifies the program at the same time.
A   free   prototype  is  available  from  their  web  site   at:
http://www.siber.com/sct/
A commercial version will be available soon.

!3.4 What about Year 2000 Tools?

13.4.1 CA-Fix/2000�
CA-Fix/2000,   a   mainframe-based  application-at-a-time   COBOL
remediation  tool,  performs rapid automated  date  analysis  and
correction of your COBOL source code to provide the fastest  path
to  Year  2000  compliance. CA-Fix/2000 eliminates  the  tedious,
error-prone  tasks of finding and correcting the date  fields  in
your programs. Because it can correct an entire application in  a
single step, CA-Fix/2000 helps ensure consistent corrections even
when  date  fields  are  passed from one program  to  another  or
records  are  redefined.  CA-Fix/2000 can  determine  which  date
fields  are  at  risk  for failing to handle the  century  change
correctly  and corrects only these at-risk fields,  limiting  the
impact on programs and reducing testing requirements.

13.5 Misc. tools

13.5.1 Flexus WinPrint
COBOL  WinPrint allows the COBOL programmer to make ANSI standard
COBOL CALL USING statements to completely control and communicate
with   the   Windows  Print  Manager.  Forms  may   be   designed
interactively  with the Forms Editor to include bitmaps,  special
fonts,  colors and many other modern features. These reports  may
be viewed on the screen prior to printing or sent directly to the
Print Manager.

14.7 The Acucorp WWW server
This is at http://www.Acucorp.com/.

14.8 The COBOL Foundation
Dave  McFarland,  formerly  of  Ryan  McFarland,  has  begun   an
organization  aimed at promoting COBOL and providing  information
to  and about the COBOL community. Members (including RM, MF, and
IBM)  pay  yearly  dues  and  in  return  are  included  in   the
Foundation's promotion efforts, literature, directories, etc. and
have  their  company  and  product  information  posted  on   the
Foundation's web server. The URL for the server is
http://www.cobol.org/

14.9 The IBM COBOL products WWW server
IBM  has  enhanced  the  COBOL you currently  use  with  powerful
features  to  increase  development  productivity,  simplify  the
maintenance of your legacy code, and provide seamless portability
from your host to your workstation. Whether you're maintaining or
reengineering   legacy  code  or  creating  new   object-oriented
client/server  applications, IBM's COBOL family  offers  you  the
year  2000 ready application development environment designed  to
do the job right.

IBM  COBOL  provides  a  complete offering of  compatible,  cross
platform,  cross product compilers which support OS/2�,  OS/390�,
MVS�,  VM,  VSE/ESA�, AS/400�, AIX�, and Windows�. IBM gives  you
the tools you need to tackle your COBOL year 2000 challenge while
leveraging  your existing applications. IBM COBOL  also  provides
the  tools you need to amplify your program development, enabling
you  to  position your enterprise to take advantage of tomorrow's
technologies.
See http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/

14.10 The Flexus WWW server
Contains all sorts of information, including a copy of the  COBOL
650 compiler. It's at http://www.flexus.com/

14.11 COBOL User Groups
There are a large number of Micro Focus User Groups. Rather  than

can be found at
http://pita.microfocus.com/UserGroup_PC/

15. Information required for the FAQ.
Corrections  and  additions  to  existing  material  are   always
welcome. I'd like to add a section of reviews of different  COBOL
books. If I am sent any reviews I will collate them and add these
to the FAQ. More information on mainframe COBOL products would be
useful.
A section covering COBOL programming could be worthwhile.
Note:  I still have a "folder full" of comments sent to the  last
owner of this FAQ that have yet to be applied. It is my intent to
get them verified and applied as soon as possible.

16. Contributors to the FAQ.
Many  people  have  contributed  to  this  FAQ  in  each  of  its
iterations. In the past an ongoing list of these people have been
included  within  the  FAQ.  At this time,  i  am  stopping  this
practice, although I ,in no way, want to deprecate all  the  work
and  input  that these people have provided. I have continued  to
identify people whom I am quoting (directly or indirectly) and do
still  hope  that everyone who has input for this  document  will
continue to provide it.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.

Wang COBOL Products.

System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x

  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.
```

```
Synkronix, Inc  (www.synkronix.com)

PERCobol� is a compiler to be used to deploy COBOL applications for the Java
environment on any platform. PERCobol enables COBOL programmers to make use
of existing skills and existing business logic to build features and
functions only present in Java.

Standards Supported
PERCobol� is a standards compliant compiler delivering:

IBM COBOL Extensions (VS COBOL II)
HP COBOL II Extensions

X/Open COBOL Definition

EXEC-Java, EXEC-HTML, EXEC-SQL data and capabilities.

Java Bean/Object Support

PERCobol runtimes allow COBOL applications to continue to run as COBOL

Indexed)

PERCobol� Enterprise Edition is a robust COBOL development solution enabling
COBOL programs to execute on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).  PERCobol is a
fully ANSI 1985 X3.23b standard compliant COBOL compiler.  The compile
results will run on any JDK 1.1+ implementation of the Java Virtual Machine
as either an applet or application depending upon how the program is invoked
(applets are subject to applet security restrictions).

PERCobol� includes proprietary Java runtime libraries enable COBOL program
behavior to function consistently across the various Java environments.
These runtime libraries are small allowing for quick downloads across
networks and include support for COBOL data and file types (sequential,
relative, indexed) which are accessible both locally and across networks.

PERCobol� Enterprise Edition also includes debuggers and other tools that
allow COBOL programmers to work with COBOL while executing in a Java
environment.  PERCobol� also includes many value-added programming
extensions which allow the COBOL programmer to access Java API's
significantly increasing the functional characteristics of COBOL programs.

PERCobol� offers significant advantages to its users including new
functionality and the ability to enable the leveraging of current staff and
application investments.  For more information on the advantages of
PERCobol�, please take a brief moment to review the Value Description for
PERCobol� at www.synkronix.com.  In addition, please look for more technical
information on the product itself or take a look at our on-line version of
the PERCobol� Enterprise Edition Users Guide.


NEW COBOL LANGUAGE FEATURES

PERCobol� contains features that allow COBOL programmer�s access to
capabilities that in many cases were previously available only in �modern�
programming languages.   These features are accessed via new COBOL language
syntax extensions. By using these new features available in PERCobol�,
programmers can evolve their legacy applications.  These features include
support for:

GUI language extensions which directly map to the Java AWT (enables the
creation of 100% platform independent graphical COBOL)

TCP/IP Socket language extensions which allow network access to COBOL
programs
Pointers

Multi-threading

Event-driven programming


The PERCobol� compiler and development environment is platform dependent.
The runtimes and resulting programs are platform independent.  In addition
to the platform dependent items, each platform will contain Java classes for
use specifically on the platform of purchase.  These classes include support
for native access on the platform in question.

For more information, please e-mail us at info@synkronix.com or visit our
Web site at www.synkronix.com.

Thank you
```

## Thread: Subscript Out Of Range — 60 msgs

### Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** stevej2352@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Micro Focus Cobol generates a runtime error 153 when a subscript out of range
condition is encountered.  The program loses control of the processing at
this point and an abnormal termination occurs.	Is there a way to detect a
153 after-the-fact and maintain control at the point of error?

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Why? Let it abend so it'll be obvious during testing that it's bad. The
symbolic dump that results on most systems (if the right compile/etc.
options are chosen) should help pinpoint the error quickly. If the
program's poorly written and inadequately tested, then it shouldn't be in
production anyway, but if it is, then the same consideration applies if it
happens in production - the program is invalid and further processing
cannot be trusted, so it should abend and produce a symbolic dump which a
programmer can use to quickly fix the problem.

Obviously this is no substitute for properly written code in the first
place. But IMHO it's the most logical way of handling the impossible. It
would of course be adviseable to have some sort of error-trapping mechanism
in place so that reasonable cleanup can be done, resources released,
subsequent steps not done, doubt cast on results, etc. but this should be
the default anyway after an abend, and whatever recovery mechanism is used,
the abend messages and symbolic dump should still be produced to help
resolve the problem.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC

<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>Micro Focus Cobol generates a runtime error 153 when a subscript out of

>condition is encountered.  The program loses control of the processing at
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Robert M. Pritchett <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> wrote in message
news:OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com...
> Why? Let it abend so it'll be obvious during testing that it's bad.

Agree completely. In fact, one should write the program such as
to MAXIMIZE the chances of it crashing. You often see 'defensive'
programming, where for example the programmer will test for
'>=' rather than '='   just in case it should (by some error elsewhere)
greater although it is not expected to be at this point.
This is bad practice.

Let it crash (or loop, or ...) instead of trying to cover up the errors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<etWhpFYf#GA.239@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Well, I sorta agree and sorta disagree with that part. From a K&P style
book years ago plus experience I learned to always terminate loops with >=
or <= tests rather than = (unless of course you're comparing pointers to
NULL).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
In article <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>,
   "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:
+Robert M. Pritchett <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> wrote in message
+news:OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com...
+> Why? Let it abend so it'll be obvious during testing that it's bad.
+
+Agree completely. In fact, one should write the program such as
+to MAXIMIZE the chances of it crashing. You often see 'defensive'
+programming, where for example the programmer will test for
+'>=' rather than '='   just in case it should (by some error elsewhere)
+greater although it is not expected to be at this point.
+This is bad practice.

What you suggest is far worse.
+
+Let it crash (or loop, or ...) instead of trying to cover up the errors.
+
In the meantime, the program may corrupt untold amounts of data before the 
crash occurs or before anyone notices the loop. And this is a good idea?

Ideally, no program should ever crash for any reason. Best practice is to code 
programs to detect errors before they cause such trouble, and terminate 
gracefully after emitting messages containing details sufficient to enable the 
programmer to determine the cause.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote in message
news:7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net...
> In article <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>,
>    "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ideally, no program should ever crash for any reason. Best practice is to
code
> programs to detect errors before they cause such trouble, and terminate
> gracefully after emitting messages containing details sufficient to enable
the
> programmer to determine the cause.

No, best practice is to code such that errors cause the program to crash.
Then during testing you discover and fix these errors. An error in a
production
program should also cause the program to crash as soon as possible so
we can weed out the errors. the worst thing to do is to catch the error
and continue as if nothing happened.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
In article <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net>,
   "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:
+Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote in message
+news:7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net...
+> In article <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>,
+>    "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:
+> +Robert M. Pritchett <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> wrote in message
+> +news:OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com...
+> +> Why? Let it abend so it'll be obvious during testing that it's bad.
+> +
+> +Agree completely. In fact, one should write the program such as
+> +to MAXIMIZE the chances of it crashing. You often see 'defensive'
+> +programming, where for example the programmer will test for
+> +'>=' rather than '='   just in case it should (by some error elsewhere)
+> +greater although it is not expected to be at this point.
+> +This is bad practice.
+>
+> What you suggest is far worse.
+> +
+> +Let it crash (or loop, or ...) instead of trying to cover up the errors.
+> +
+> In the meantime, the program may corrupt untold amounts of data before the
+> crash occurs or before anyone notices the loop. And this is a good idea?
+>
+> Ideally, no program should ever crash for any reason. Best practice is to
+code
+> programs to detect errors before they cause such trouble, and terminate
+> gracefully after emitting messages containing details sufficient to enable
+the
+> programmer to determine the cause.
+
+No, best practice is to code such that errors cause the program to crash.
+Then during testing you discover and fix these errors. An error in a
+production
+program should also cause the program to crash as soon as possible so
+we can weed out the errors. the worst thing to do is to catch the error
+and continue as if nothing happened.
+
Wrong, wrong, wrong. This approach makes the grossly unwarranted assumption 
that errors will cause a crash, when in fact many errors go undetected for a 
long time before causing a crash. A far better practice is to code so as to 
detect errors immediately, whether or not those errors are of the sort that 
would cause an abend.

Nobody ever suggested continuing on after an error as though nothing had 
happened.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e6v1f$9lr$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
An illustration:

Wage payroll, incentive plans.  One ran for years before the "auditors"
(read wage earners) figured out how they were getting "underpaid".  It took
some time, and a lot of effort to re-calculate all those pay periods for all
of the plans, and pay up.  It was a subtile logic error that caused this and
brought about a virtual plant payroll system so that internal auditors could
review any and all changes. <G>

Warren Simmons
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

```
Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote in message > +No, best practice
is to code such that errors cause the program to crash.
> +Then during testing you discover and fix these errors. An error in a
production
> +program should also cause the program to crash as soon as possible so
> +we can weed out the errors. the worst thing to do is to catch the error
> +and continue as if nothing happened.
> +
> Wrong, wrong, wrong. This approach makes the grossly unwarranted
assumption
> that errors will cause a crash, when in fact many errors go undetected for
a
> long time before causing a crash. A far better practice is to code so as
to
> detect errors immediately, whether or not those errors are of the sort
that
> would cause an abend.

I think we really agree because we both advocate writing the code
so that errors are detected immediately. not necessarily through
a crash. What I mean is that the program should be so 'brittle' that
it resists careless hacking, rather than so 'robust' that it makes it hard
to discover errors immediately.
```

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)

```
In article <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net>,
   "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:
+Doug Miller <dxmixxer@netdirect.net> wrote in message > +No, best practice
+is to code such that errors cause the program to crash.
+> +Then during testing you discover and fix these errors. An error in a
+production
+> +program should also cause the program to crash as soon as possible so
+> +we can weed out the errors. the worst thing to do is to catch the error
+> +and continue as if nothing happened.
+> +
+> Wrong, wrong, wrong. This approach makes the grossly unwarranted
+assumption
+> that errors will cause a crash, when in fact many errors go undetected for
+a
+> long time before causing a crash. A far better practice is to code so as
+to
+> detect errors immediately, whether or not those errors are of the sort
+that
+> would cause an abend.
+
+I think we really agree because we both advocate writing the code
+so that errors are detected immediately. not necessarily through
+a crash. What I mean is that the program should be so 'brittle' that
+it resists careless hacking, rather than so 'robust' that it makes it hard
+to discover errors immediately.
+
+
I agree with your first sentence, but not the last. Rather than being so 
'brittle' as to crash when tampered with, programs should be written in an 
easy-to-maintain style. The 'careless hacking' you are so worried about occurs 
rarely in a structured environment, but is a nearly inevitable consequence of 
the 'old school' programming style of code that is infested with GO TO, 
PERFORM THRU, and SECTIONs in the PROCEDURE DIVISION. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3707905b@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
well, I think we can't get much further along this road.
If the program is strongly structured it is 'brittle' in the sense
that tinkering destroys the structure. 'Easy-to-maintain' is
a subjective thing. I have often heard the statement being
made that a structured program is harder to maintain
because you have to respect the structure. The 'old style'
programs with no structure are easier in that respect.
All my programs are strongly structured and hard to
maintain in the sense that it is hard to screw them up.
They do make for 'safer' maintenance which is what
we are really after; not 'easier'. Maintenance should
be regarded with respect and thought should be put
in it, not 'ease'. But as I said, this is a contentious issue
which I wish to extract myself from, having offered my
2 bits.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37079489.579963672@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
On Sun, 04 Apr 99 14:42:33 GMT, dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Doug Miller)
wrote:


>I agree with your first sentence, but not the last. Rather than being so 
>'brittle' as to crash when tampered with, programs should be written in an 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>PERFORM THRU, and SECTIONs in the PROCEDURE DIVISION. 

Let me give you a real world example.  I have several programs that
are quite complex.  Some of the data needed by the program is located
in an Oracle database - while other information is located in local
disk files.  We are moving more and more information to Oracle.  I
have a subprogram that accesses the data in the different tables - one
program per table presently.  The linkage includes several items.  The
items to be returned are also there.  This program can be called
dynamically - which means that the calling program need not be
recompiled.  However, for various reasons some of the calls are
static.  When a new field is added the copy book for the linkage is
changed, and the programs are recompiled.  As more programs make use
of this method and the same sub programs, the task becomes more prone
to human error.  The subprogram must be run through the pre-compiler,
then compiled, before the main program.  Dynamic calling programs must
be recompiled to obtain the new copybook.

To prevent errors in this order, I built in a failure mechanism.  This
works by using the FUNCTION LENGTH to get the lenght of the linkage
section (a single 01 level) and places this length in a field in the
linkage section.  The called program, on first execution, also does a
Funciton Length on the linkage section and compares this lenght ot the
passed lenght.  If different, it does not execute!  (Causing a
failure, not unlike that which would happen if the database were
down).

This is brittle - and has actually saved me many times.  I always test
the production compiles before moving into produciton - none of these
has failed in production.  This will also help other programmers here
using my routines to ensure that if a change is made, it is made
everythwere it needs to me made, in the right order.  The programs
that must be changed are documented in the copybook, so it is easy to
find and change them.  

I think this is the kind of thing Leif is talking about.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
OO programs seem to be more vunerable this way.  Objects can pass back
generalized errors, and may be designed to handle errors in ways which
are not optimal for your "program".

An all-in-one program, or one with only limited calls is more likely to
figure out what this particular application wants to do with a
particular condition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>OO programs seem to be more vunerable this way.  Objects can pass back
>generalized errors, and may be designed to handle errors in ways which
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>particular condition.

I'll use this note to put in a "plug" for one of the things that I still
think is the WORST parts of the draft new Standard.  (The committee has
heard my complaint - and said that I am wrong - but I still hope that enough
people will object to get them to change their minds.)

In the new Standard, programmers can create "user defined functions"  (these
are NOT a part of OO - but you will see why I think it is related from my
objection).  It has always been my understanding that functions (unlike
called subprograms) are supposed to be (relatively) small pieces of
procedural code that RETURNS A VALUE - and that is all that they change.
UNFORTUNATELY (in my opinion), the draft Standard allows the new user
defined functions to:
 - get parameters passed BY REFERENCE
 - have access to EXTERNAL data
 - use files
 - CALL other subprograms
 - generally cause all sorts of "unexpected side effects"

I know that it is impossible to preclude ALL side-effects (after all you can
pass a (new) pointer data item - for example - as a parameter) but I really,
REALLY wanted the Standard to prohibit user-defined functions from doing as
much as possible OTHER THAN SENDING BACK a returned-value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The BIG difference between functions and subprograms is that functions 
can be used as values in a condition or expression, as for example IF 
FUNCTION SINE (A) > 0 MOVE B TO C. In C-type languages this makes 
programs hard to understand as you can hide an awful lot of processing 
inside an IF statement. But in COBOL it's *much* worse because COBOL, 
being more data-oriented, has more ways of using conditions and 
expressions.

Consider the following cases: (there are even worse examples than these! 
conditions can be coded in the Data Division in the new standard.)

  (1)  IF FUNCTION MYFUNC (A) = 23 OR 65 ...
  (2)  EVALUATE MYFUNC (A)
           WHEN 10  PERFORM P1
           WHEN 22  PERFORM P2  ...
       END-EVALUATE
  (3)  IF B = 1 OR FUNCTION MYFUNC (A) = 16 ...
  (4)  PERFORM P1 UNTIL FUNCTION MYFUNC (A) = 24
  (5)  SEARCH MY-TABLE WHEN FUNCTION MYFUNC (A) = 12 ...

In each case, how often will the Function be invoked? You can't tell  - 
and different vendors vary in their interpretation. If the Function 
produces side-effects such as writing to external files or allocating 
memory, the results can be catastrophic.

John.    Piggott@spc-systems.com

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > I'll use this note to put in a "plug" for one of the things that I still
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> as a function?
```

```
it, or whatever.
```

```
John Piggott wrote in message <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com>...
>The BIG difference between functions and subprograms is that functions
>can be used as values in a condition or expression, as for example IF
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>memory, the results can be catastrophic.

John,
  Your statement concerns me.  There SHOULD be no difference in how this is
done by various vendors.  There has been an (approved) interpretation of the
the current Standard that makes it clear that a function is evaluated ONCE
at the beginning of a statement.  If there are vendors who are doing this -
they are not Standard conforming (and actually, I know of none that don't do
it correctly).

If you need an interpretation number, I will research it and get it for you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed0gu$mnv@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <370AC174.72C8@compuserve.com>`

```
John Piggott wrote in message <370AC174.72C8@compuserve.com>...
>Yes please! I wonder how the "once at the beginning of the statement" rule
would be worded for cases such as:
>
>  PERFORM P1 VARYING I FROM 1 BY FUNCTION MYFUNC (I) UNTIL C1
>

Rather simply, follow the exact same rules as you do for subscripting, for
example,

  Perform P1
     Varying Int from 1 by Table-Element (Int)
       Until C1

In other words, you look at the value of Table-Element (Int) (or Function
MYFUNC(I)) at the beginning of the PERFORM and use that value throughout the
PERFORM VARYING.  This may OR MAY not be what the application logic WANTS -
but it is well defined - and is nothing "new" with functions.
```

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7edmvs$eg6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>John Piggott wrote in message <370AC174.72C8@compuserve.com>...
>>Yes please! I wonder how the "once at the beginning of the statement" rule
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>MYFUNC(I)) at the beginning of the PERFORM and use that value throughout
the
>PERFORM VARYING.  This may OR MAY not be what the application logic WANTS -
>but it is well defined - and is nothing "new" with functions.
>
Unless I misunderstood ...

This appears to be at odds with the draft standard [CD 1.2] and ANS 85,
as well. The PERFORM statement, [CD 1.2] General Rule 7.d says that, if
the identifier is subscripted, the subscript will be evaluated each time the
content (of the identifer) is used in an augmenting operation. I cannot
imagine that this would be different than ANS 85.

I wrote a test program for MF WB 3.2 with ANS 85 flagging. It evaluated
the content of the subscripted identifier with each iteration; rather than
once, at the beginning.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Perform Varying with Subscripted Augment (Re: Side effects)

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7el05l$dn2$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed0gu$mnv@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <370AC174.72C8@compuserve.com> <7edmvs$eg6@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7ej0m3$6mu$1@server.cntfl.com> <370d7d4d.967289893@news1.ibm.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <370d7d4d.967289893@news1.ibm.net>...
>On Thu, 8 Apr 1999 14:57:00 -0400, "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>
[...]
>>... The PERFORM statement, [CD 1.2] General Rule 7.d says that, if
>>the identifier is subscripted, the subscript will be evaluated each time
the
>>content (of the identifer) is used in an augmenting operation. I cannot
>>imagine that this would be different than ANS 85.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>incremented with each iterance of the perform?

The changes to INT were done within the PERFORM statement
and not within the PERFORMed paragraph. See the perform of p1
in the sample code, below.

[For the following discussion, it is assumed that ANS 85 and CD 1.2
are equivalent; that is, I could find no differences that were relevant
to THIS discussion.]

Given the form [from CD 1.2]

PERFORM procedure-name-1
    VARYING identifier-2 FROM literal-1 BY identifier-4
    UNTIL condition-1

the standard says that, if identifier-4 is subscripted, the subscript
will be evaluated each time the content of identifier-4 is used in an
augmenting operation.

Sample ANS 85 code is included, below. This code demonstrates
using variable augment values. The performs of p1 and p2 use
subscripts; the perform of p3 does not.

Although it is clear, from my interpretation of CD 1.2, that the
performs of p1 and p2 are correct, it is not clear that the perform of
p3 is correct. That is, there are no statements in CD 1.2 which require
that the changed content of identifier-4 be used unless identifer-4 is
subscripted. (I expected the perform of p3 to be an infinite loop! It was
not.)

Furthermore, the standard also says that, if identifier-2 is
subscripted, the subscript will be evaluated each time the content
of identifer-2 is referenced for setting or augmenting. This is not
shown.

--------------
      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"s"
       identification division.
       program-id. test44.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01 tabl value "02010102010102010101".
         05 table-element pic 99 occurs 10.
       01 int pic 9(4).
       01 va1-sub pic 99 value 0.
       01 variable-augment-1 value "01020304050607080910".
         05 va1 pic 99 occurs 10.
       01 variable-augment-2.
         05 va2 pic 99 value 0.

       procedure division.
       begin.
           perform p1
             varying int from 1 by table-element (int)
             until int > 10
           display "-------"
           perform p2
             varying int from 0 by va1 (va1-sub)
             until int > 30
           display "-------"
           perform p3
             varying int from 0 by va2
             until int > 10
           stop run.
       p1.
           display int " " table-element (int)
           .
       p2.
           add 1 to va1-sub
           display int " " va1 (va1-sub)
           .
       p3.
           add 1 to va2
           display int " " va2
           .
------------
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform Varying with Subscripted Augment (Re: Side effects)

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00

```
this issue];

"...  Any subscripting or reference modification specified in
condition-1..is evaluated each time the condition is tested."


>
>Sample ANS 85 code is included, below. This code demonstrates
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>not.)

But X3.23-1985 also says, in 6.21.4 (10):

"During the execution of the specified set of statements associated with the
PERFORM statement, any change to the VARYING variable..., the BY
variable..., the AFTER variable..., or the FROM variable... will be take
into consideration and will affect the operation of the PERFORM statement,"


[snip]
>--------------
>      $set ans85 flag"ans85" flagas"s"
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>           .

0001 02
0003 01
0004 02
0006 01
0007 02
0009 01
0010 01
-------
0000 01
0001 02
0003 03
0006 04
0010 05
0015 06
0021 07
0028 08

0001 02
0003 03
0006 04
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com>`

```

John Piggott wrote in message <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com>...
>The BIG difference between functions and subprograms is that functions
>can be used as values in a condition or expression, as for example IF
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[...]
Well-tested programs do not have "side-effects." If a function has
code to write to a file or to allocate memory on each invocation
and it does so, the function is doing what it was programmed to
do. This is not a "side-effect" produced by the function!

In the examples given, additional, unintended, invocations of a
function will necessitate modification of the logic to ensure that
the function is invoked only when intended. This is the same
problem that programmers, using other languages, have faced
for decades. As in those other languages, the results are
catastrophic only if the program is not well-tested.

User-defined functions are a new a powerful tool that, if not
used properly, may create problems. Does that mean it should
be restricted? No! Does it mean that programmers need to be
trained to use it properly? Yes!
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
the environment (has any output at all) other than returning its value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ehbl0$99g$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com> <usJUJnJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```

Robert M. Pritchett wrote in message ...
>I think the term side-effect means that the function changes anything in
>the environment (has any output at all) other than returning its value.
>
Perhaps some might agree; but ...
---------------
"When I use a word," Humpty Dumpty said in rather a scornful tone,
"it means just what I choose it to mean- neither more nor less."

Lewis Carroll (1832-98), English author, mathematician. Through
the Looking-Glass, ch. 6 (1872).
---------------
---------------
side effect (s�d �-f�kt�) noun

A peripheral or secondary effect, especially an undesirable secondary
effect of a drug or therapy.
----------------

If the purpose of the function is to update a master file, and return
status of success or failure; I would not consider the update of the file
to be a peripheral or secondary effect.

If the function also appended a record of the change to a log file, it is
arguable whether this logging would constitute a side effect.

However, in my opinion, if the update and the logging are part of the
design, the logging can not be a side effect. If the logging is not
required by the design, then it is not part of the program and can not
be a side effect. Hence, I stand by my statement that "Well-tested
programs do not have 'side-effects'."
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> If the purpose of the function is to update a master file, and return
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs do not have 'side-effects'."

Side effects are derived from functions performing as directed but make
assumptions on the environment.

for example:

Function will return the next customer id:

Internally it increments a counter to do this function.

What if the programmer calls this function twice because he needs the
next customer number in two places.

Who is right and who is wrong.

Eifel has a prereq that a procedure will update and a function will only
return values:

Generate_next_custumer

move function next_cust() to customer_no

Rules like this limit the side effects of functions.

Ken
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message
<big snip>

There is no "right" and there is no "wrong".  There are several ways of
doing things.

Granted, I could write a function that declared everything global, and
proceeded to change half of my working storage.  Why would I?

As a philosophical point, I have never agreed with the "computer as a
policeman" view.  At least at the programming level, a language should give
me the power to do what *I* want to do, not what someone in management has
decided that I should be allowed to do.  The latter approach to systems
design has a way of producing programs that do not function well.
```

```
A correct definition of the term.  In the context of functions "side
effect" means an effect other than returning a value.

With the caveat that 'side-effects' means what you mean it to mean not
what other programmers mean it to mean.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370CB55D.368D8772@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com> <usJUJnJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <7ehbl0$99g$1@server.cntfl.com> <370c6ecf.45013124@netnews>`

```
> A correct definition of the term.  In the context of functions "side
> effect" means an effect other than returning a value.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> what other programmers mean it to mean.

I disagree.  A side-effect is an effect other than the intended primary
effect.  Obviously, your definition doesn't apply to a print subroutine
but is more narrowly defined to work with functions.  Apparently the
ANSI committee believes functions should be allowed to do more than
return values.  We could even write a function where the value returned
is simply a return-code indicating success of the function.

Are other languages used this way?  For what real-life examples would
this be a recommended procedure?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eih7m$a0a@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com> <usJUJnJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <7ehbl0$99g$1@server.cntfl.com> <370c6ecf.45013124@netnews> <370CB55D.368D8772@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
As the person who started this thread,

A) "side-effect" is the term being used in J4 work to mean "something other
than the returned value is changed".  (It may mean other things to other
people in other environments - but this is what I mean - and what I believe
that all of J4 and WG4 mean.) It makes no difference whether this was
"intended" or not.

B) There is *slight* disagreement about whether the (traditional in COBOL)
concept of "last-used state" of a function is or is not a "side-effect".  In
other words, can a function that does NOT have "side-effect" be re-invoked
and remember something from its last invocation?  (When I use the term
"side-effect", I do *not* consider "last used state" to be a side-effect.)

C) I started this particular thread in relationship to a previous post on OO
concepts and "encapsulation".  There seems to be no doubt in programming
language that *some* procedural constructs should be "encapsulated" so only
specific types of things can happen.  It is my believe/hope that COBOL
user-defined functions be "encapsulated" so that they ONLY change the
returned-value.  This is not, however, the J4/WG4 desire - or the current
draft solution.

D) (Apparently - I am not a great Computer Science expert) Some programming
languages *do* restrict functions (unlike called procedures) to ONLY
impacting the returned value - while other programming languages do not.  It
is probably (certain?) that no matter what rules your write, programmers
will be able to "trick" the compiler into allowing side-effects from
functions.  However, it is also possible to write rules to reduce the
likelihood of this - especially accidentally.

E) The draft of the next Standard has modified CALLed subprograms so they
can be used as "user-defined functions" by any invoking program EXCEPT for
programs written in COBOL!  (In other words, a C program can reference a
COBOL subprogram as a function - with a returned value - but a COBOL program
can't do that.)  It seems to me that IF (and I don't agree with this in
theory - but see it as the draft's current approach) a user-defined function
can do everything that a subprogram can do and vice versa - then I don't see
any reason to create a new type of structure.  I would prefer that the
language be changed to simply allow "inline references" to subprograms that
have a RETURNING phrase - rather than creating a new FUNCTION-ID type
structure.  (There are some "odd" differences in the current draft between
functions and subprograms - for example the RETURNING phrase for one is on
the PROCEDURE DIVISION header and for the other it is on the EXIT PROGRAM
statement)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370E63F3.836CBA05@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com> <usJUJnJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <7ehbl0$99g$1@server.cntfl.com> <370c6ecf.45013124@netnews> <370CB55D.368D8772@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eih7m$a0a@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote (heavily snipped):

> A) "side-effect" is the term being used in J4 work to mean "something other
> than the returned value is changed".  (It may mean other things to other
> people in other environments - but this is what I mean - and what I believe
> that all of J4 and WG4 mean.) It makes no difference whether this was
> "intended" or not.

1) With "side-effect" as programming-term is meant "the change of the contents
of one or more of the arguments of a function that returns a value".

So:

C: x = f(a,b);
COBOL: move function f(a,b) to x [OR compute x = function f(a,b)]

f has side effect(s) if after the given statement the contents of a and/or b has
changed.

This  is meant to mean: the calling/activation of f not only fills/changes x,
but as a side-effect also changes a or b.

The term does not mean that side effects are wrong, allthough one could argue to
try to avoid this construction.
Other activities of f, f.e. changing it's own internal variables (perhaps to
remember something for a subsequent call) or f.e. changing a file, are in this
meaning of "side-effect" not a side effect.

2) Meaning with "side effect" something like "unforeseen / unwanted effect that
has not been intended nor designed" is also valid usage of the english language,
but in most scientific talk about programming, people mean with "side effect"
the narrow meaning described under 1) above.

In this very deep and long winding thread these two different meanings are mixed
up used by message senders and readers/repliers and some of you out there are
misunderstanding each other that way.

!!!
The Frog (COBOL)     <--- Not in the committee, so no side-effects
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eigs1$qs9$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <370A6575.45FB@compuserve.com> <7ed8ri$gkj$1@server.cntfl.com> <usJUJnJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <7ehbl0$99g$1@server.cntfl.com> <370c6ecf.45013124@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <370c6ecf.45013124@netnews>...
>'Twas Wed, 7 Apr 1999 23:18:08 -0400, when "Rick Smith"
><ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
[...]
>>If the purpose of the function is to update a master file, and return
>>status of success or failure; I would not consider the update of the file
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>defined in computerdom.

However, the update of the master file is the desired, primary effect.
Calling the update "secondary to returning a value" seems rather odd.
Hence, it appears to me that, within computerdom, the term "side
effect" lacks the power to clarify.

>>Hence, I stand by my statement that "Well-tested
>>programs do not have 'side-effects'."
>
>With the caveat that 'side-effects' means what you mean it to mean not
>what other programmers mean it to mean.

Precisely! ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370CB42F.59F7E109@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
A peripherial or secondary effect of a program could include using
computer time.  After all, the purpose of the computer isn't to use the
computer, it's to provide a result.

But nobody normally thinks of computer time as "a side effect".  On the
other hand, if a small change in code results in a great increase in
computer time, we DO tend to think of that as a side effect, and work to
trace it down and eliminate it.

Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Robert M. Pritchett wrote in message ...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Side effects (was Re: Subscript Out Of Range

```
You mean "unplanned side-effects" and I agree completely. Function 
routines should be able to open files and allocate memory for their own 
use - so long as this is transparent to the invoker. I was referring to 
"designer side effects" such as allocating memory for the invoking 
program and returning a pointer to it, which I regard with horror.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ed0rn$dn0$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net> <37078537@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e7top$3n0_001@news.netdirect.net> <3708BFF2.F9946187@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eaov4$gm9@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3708EBA1.BD573BA8@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> I'll use this note to put in a "plug" for one of the things that I still
>> think is the WORST parts of the draft new Standard.  (The committee has
>> heard my complaint - and said that I am wrong - but I still hope that
enough
>> people will object to get them to change their minds.)
>>
>> In the new Standard, programmers can create "user defined functions"
(these
>> are NOT a part of OO - but you will see why I think it is related from my
>> objection).  It has always been my understanding that functions (unlike
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I know that it is impossible to preclude ALL side-effects (after all you
can
>> pass a (new) pointer data item - for example - as a parameter) but I
really,
>> REALLY wanted the Standard to prohibit user-defined functions from doing
as
>> much as possible OTHER THAN SENDING BACK a returned-value.
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>as a function?

Because Bill's "understanding that functions (unlike called subprograms)
are supposed to be (relatively) small pieces of procedural code ..." is
wrong!

Dartmouth BASIC (IIRC) and other interpreted BASICs do have such
limitations (DEF FN). Algol, PL/I, FORTRAN, Pascal, C, C++, and
Visual Basic do not!

In most languages, the only difference between a FUNCTION and a
SUBPROGRAM is that a FUNCTION returns a value to the point of
invocation. In all other respects, there are is no difference in capability.

The committee did a good job in defining (user-defined) FUNCTIONs
for COBOL.

The "side-effects", to which Bill objects, where also discussed several
months ago (see DejaNews, "Re: "structured programming" & code
reusability", around 12/9/98).
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Maybe there should be a compiler option to prohibit or allow side effects?
Do any other languages do it this way? I love the idea of user-written
functions, it's long overdue, but I agree with the concern about side
effects. It would be nice to be able to write an application with mostly
function calls.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3708BC38.DED079C1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net> <370582d3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7e6l0c$22g_001@news.netdirect.net>`

```
> Wrong, wrong, wrong. This approach makes the grossly unwarranted assumption
> that errors will cause a crash, when in fact many errors go undetected for a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> happened.

Anticipate errors and figure out what should be done about them ahead of
time.  The obvious example of this is the divide by zero error.  Decide
ahead of time what result is desired when the divisor is a zero and test
for zero before dividing.

If your table is too small, send a message and then go to your standard
abort routine.
```

```
See below. ===>


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
```
