Doug Miller wrote in message <7e3tpk$2j0_001@news.netdirect.net>...

>What you suggest is far worse.
>+
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>crash occurs or before anyone notices the loop.

===> Not if you have good subscript/range/etc. checking. And even if it
does, the corrupted data will show up in the dump.

And this is a good idea?
>
>Ideally, no program should ever crash for any reason. Best practice is to
code
>programs to detect errors before they cause such trouble, and terminate
>gracefully after emitting messages containing details sufficient to enable
the
>programmer to determine the cause.

===> That's true in the general sense, for catching errors that result from
bad data or other input; the program should always defend itself there to
reduce/prevent GIGO. But if an impossible condition happens that could
clearly only result from bad design or coding, especially from bad
assumptions, you want it to fail as quickly and obviously as possible,
prevent further execution that could depend on erroneous results, and
provide as much valuable information as possible. You can always browse the
FDump in your sysout queue using SDSF and Find the variables you want to
check, and print parts of it if you like, rather than print the whole
thing. The idea is to do as much of this as possible in the development and
testing phases so that it doesn't happen in production. But if it does, if
it's this kind of error, it's still the best way to handle it. Note that
most production errors would be caused by bad data and those should
certainly be caught by the program logic itself and recovered from nicely.
For those kinds of errors, you're right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3705F577.41D6047E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Agree completely. In fact, one should write the program such as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Let it crash (or loop, or ...) instead of trying to cover up the errors.

You will notice in my (rather large post) that I included a 'WHEN OTHER'
on the digits saying that it just cannot happen and terminating.  I
coded it OK but what about the junior that has a hack (literally) next
week.  This is defensive but will 'never' happen in production code, a
really good optimiser will remove my 'boots and braces', but so what if
it does not it costs almost nothing.

Production code should handled these errors and (if not 'inhouse')
continue doing something useful if somewhat limitted.  For example:

   PERFORM XYZ
      VARYING XXX from ! by 1
         UNTIL XXX >= XXX-MAX

D  IF XXX > XXX-MAX
D     DISPLAY 'YOU GOOFED'
D     STOP RUN
D  END-IF

Another example may be:

  IF XXX > XXX-MAX
     DISPLAY '1234W:  Table overflow please submit to support@foo.com'
     DISPLAY '        Continue processing without some data results may'
             ' be inaccurate'
  END-IF

For inhouse code it is simpler for simple fixes to crash the program and
get the programmer to change it overnight.  Ever tried to debug that
memory overrun. Dumps get corrupted, reports are just plain strange, it
crashes in production and runs perfectly in development.

The one thing I hate is sequential files with status codes.  The
compiler expects you to check everything and guess what happens when you
forget something.  I have removed status codes and found elusive bugs in
minutes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370642b1@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <3705F577.41D6047E@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:3705F577.41D6047E@zip.com.au...
> Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The ideal program should be so 'brittle' that ANY change except
when done absolutely correctly should crash the program immediately.
When a program is 'brittle' it is hard to hack it without causing a crash.

As always, it is assumed that whatever input needs to be checked
is checked. I'm not talking about omitting checking and hoping that
invalid input will crash the program. That is unacceptable.

Think of the alternative:  no matter how badly the program is hacked
it just keeps on trucking, week after week until finally someday it
is discovered that its output is slightly incorrect...

I realize that most people will disagree with me and that production
programs should be kept running at any cost. So be it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ File Status codes

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#zYIGqJg#GA.122@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <OBYRc8Lf#GA.358@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <3704b93e@news3.us.ibm.net> <3705F577.41D6047E@zip.com.au>`

```
Yes, amazing how often counter-productive it is especially as a client site
standard, when if you simply omit the FS code in the program and something
strange happens, you get the FS code and a meaningful error message in the
dump/sysout anyway, and it's almost always an unusual error in either
operations, JCL, or programming that needs to be fixed and then never needs
to be checked again. The at end, invalid key, dup, etc. clauses are usually
all the program should care about and you're already going to put code in
for those anyway. Anything else is really beyond the scope of what the
program should be designed to handle.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File Status codes

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

On Tue, 6 Apr 1999 21:00:32 -0500, "Robert M. Pritchett"
<NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> wrote:

>Yes, amazing how often counter-productive it is especially as a client site
>standard, when if you simply omit the FS code in the program and something
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>program should be designed to handle.

From a mainframe or professional shop persective - yes.  (And I happen
to share this perspective).  However, I also develop END USER
applications to average Joe User.  Spewing a "Error 35 on Master File"
message is like telling them "General Protection Fault".  No good
info, and no useful recourse.  So I fully and completely check and
recover internally for any "strange" file status values.  And when the
"This should not occur" message is displayed - I give enough
informaiton to debug MY problem in the program and get the user
running again.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I don't think that the DECLARATIVE will work for this (yet - in the next
Standard it will).  Do you know about using NOBOUND as a compiler directive
to NOT check for subscript violations?  Then your program logic can check
for "out of range" situations.  There is currently no "general purpose"
recovery routine in Micro Focus (like an MVS ESTAE - for example) to get
control and return.  This is, however, exactly the paradigm that the next
Standard uses for "common exception handling".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
When I will own the program, I check the subscript against a maximum
each time I increment.

Another trick is to put a constant after your table.  Make sure the
constant is still constant.  This is more useful when you expect lots of
maintenance in the table.

01   my-table-max-size        pic 9999  value 1000.
01   my-table-index           pic 9999  value zero.
01   my-table   ...          occurs 1000    ...


01   table-end                pic x(09) value 'table-end'.


fill-table.
    add 1             to my-table-index
    if my-table-index > my-table-max-size
        perform error-routine
    end-if
    move read-record   to my-record (my-table-index)
    if table-end = 'table-end'
        continue
    else
        perform error-routine
    end-if
    read read-file
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e0fjc$c71$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I believe this approach may work on some machines, but I don't think it can
be supported as predictably working according to the standard.  My
understanding is that there is no defined relationship between any two
01-level items in WORKING-STORAGE; they are wholly independent structures.
If contiguity is required, then they must themselves be defined as part of a
structure.

The approach works, of course, if my-table and my-table-end are subordinate
items in a record, but that means they're not 01-level items any more.

Note also that OCCURS on a 01-level item is expressly forbidden by ANSI
X3.23-1974 (Page III-3), ANSI X3.23-1985 (page VI-26), and (at least the
July 1997 version of) the draft standard  (page 286).

    -Chuck Stevens




requirement that any two 01-level items be contiguous in memoryThere is no
implied relationship between any two 01-level items in working-storage


Howard Brazee wrote in message <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>...

<snip>

>Another trick is to put a constant after your table.  Make sure the
>constant is still constant.  This is more useful when you expect lots of
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>    read read-file
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703DE14.42367F7E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e0fjc$c71$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
You're correct that this is not part of the standard.  But lots of shops
have operated for years by having huge fillers at the end of tables to
handle overflow when they needed larger tables than their compilers
allowed.   Scary, but true.  An optimizing compiler could break their
code!!!

Of course that doesn't mean they can't do this check.  Only instead of
making it an 01 level check, make it an 05 level check.

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> I believe this approach may work on some machines, but I don't think it can
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> >    read read-file
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e11f1$6q9$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com> <7e0fjc$c71$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3703DE14.42367F7E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Well, I guess my point is that this is an approach I would hardly recommend
as a general solution.  It certainly wouldn't make for portable code!

In the general case, ISTM that the best and safest way to check for a
possible out-of-range subscript is to check for a possible out-of-range
subscript yourself.

    -Chuck Stevens


Howard Brazee wrote in message <3703DE14.42367F7E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>You're correct that this is not part of the standard.  But lots of shops
>have operated for years by having huge fillers at the end of tables to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I believe this approach may work on some machines, but I don't think it
can
>> be supported as predictably working according to the standard.  My
>> understanding is that there is no defined relationship between any two
>> 01-level items in WORKING-STORAGE; they are wholly independent
structures.
>> If contiguity is required, then they must themselves be defined as part
of a
>> structure.
>>
>> The approach works, of course, if my-table and my-table-end are
subordinate
>> items in a record, but that means they're not 01-level items any more.
>>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >    read read-file
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eik7s$f7l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> When I will own the program, I check the subscript against a maximum
> each time I increment.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Besides the already expressed concerns over the lack of guarantee that 01
levels will be contiguous, why bother with 'table-end' when you've already
determined "if my-table-index > my-table-max-size"?

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370CE405.30AA23D9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net> <7e09r9$kth@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eik7s$f7l$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> 
> In article <3703B628.1A82E3B3@NOSPAMhome.com>,
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ed Stevens

The first concern is solved by making it an 05 level.  The second
technique's primary purpose is to catch poorly maintained programs.  In
actual life, I have found putting in such a filler is a bigger aid in
doing a dump analysis than anything else.  I especially like a
"working-storage ends here" filler, since I haven't used compilers which
moved 01 levels around.  If I have a few spots where I have expected
values, I can catch some weird things.

Of course, everybody can gain advantage by using MY-TABLE-MAX-SIZE
instead of a constant.  That makes maintenance much easier.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subscript Out Of Range

- **From:** wobconsult@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e17d6$ag0$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvslo$2l3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In article <7e08tm$1df$1@news.igs.net>,
  "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> You can normally trap errors in the declarative section.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Something that I've been doing for several years now is to dynamically
calculate a table hi-bound at the start of the program. EG:

01  WS-TABLE-REC.
    03  WS-TABLE-ENTRY OCCURS 100 TIMES
                       PIC X(20).
01  WS-VARIABLES.
    03  WS-TABLE-MAX   PIC S9(08) BINARY.

COMPUTE WS-TABLE-MAX = (LENGTH OF WS-TABLE / LENGTH OF WS-TABLE-ENTRY (1)).

Always use WS-TABLE-MAX as your maximum subscript range. Therefore, no matter
what the occurs is (the table could be in a CopyBook) you'll always be safe.

I'm not sure if this can be used with the LENGTH FUNCTION in ANSI '85, but you
could probably get around any difficulties by loading up some fullwords and
then performing the calculation.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

<a id="t-365bb61681"></a>
## Thread: C calling MFCOBOL — 10 msgs

### C calling MFCOBOL

- **From:** Ben Brandis <ben@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<922957784.705753@proxy>`

```
I have a runtime system compiled on Windows 95 that is written in C and
MFCOBOL which calls a number of *.int compiled cobol programs. It works
fine...

I need to port it to Windows NT. NT doesn't seem to like the cobol part
of the runtime system (although it is 32 bit). I managed to remove the
cobol section of the runtime, converting it to C. Now my problem is the
"cobcall" function, which works fine on the new Windows 95 version,
crashes the Windows NT version.

Unfortunately, I can't compile the thing on Windows NT. It needs to be
compiled in Windows 95 and be capable of running on the NT machine.

I can't find any documentation on the "cobcall" function, except for the
UNIX version (which explicitly says it's only UNIX).

Any info at all would be greatly appreciated.

Ben.
```

#### ↳ Re: C calling MFCOBOL

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37035FDE.9BD9451C@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<922957784.705753@proxy>`

```
Hi Ben.

Ben Brandis wrote:
> 
> I have a runtime system compiled on Windows 95 that is written in C and
> MFCOBOL which calls a number of *.int compiled cobol programs. It works
> fine...

Please say what version of Micro Focus COBOL you are using.
 
> I need to port it to Windows NT. NT doesn't seem to like the cobol part
> of the runtime system (although it is 32 bit).

Please explain what you mean by "doesn't like". Do you get an error
message ? What is it, and what are you doing when you get it ?

> I managed to remove the
> cobol section of the runtime, converting it to C. Now my problem is the
> "cobcall" function, which works fine on the new Windows 95 version,
> crashes the Windows NT version.

By "the new Windows 95 version", do you mean your rewritten C version ?
Please say how it "crashes" - error messages etc.

> Unfortunately, I can't compile the thing on Windows NT. It needs to be
> compiled in Windows 95 and be capable of running on the NT machine.
> I can't find any documentation on the "cobcall" function, except for the
> UNIX version (which explicitly says it's only UNIX).

cobcall() should work the same on all Micro Focus 32-bit products.

Can you break the cobcall() and associated code into a standalone
demo for this problem ? Please post it (include a demo COBOL routine
for it to call, and any compiler directives/options which may be
significant). This is your best bet for getting a solution.

Cheers, Kev.
```

#### ↳ Re: C calling MFCOBOL

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dvnug$jo2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<922957784.705753@proxy>`

```
Ben,


Ben Brandis wrote in message <922957784.705753@proxy>...
>I have a runtime system compiled on Windows 95 that is written in C and
>MFCOBOL which calls a number of *.int compiled cobol programs. It works
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>crashes the Windows NT version.

The COBOL run-time system code is the same for Windows 95 and NT. Therefore
the crash must either be as a result of a difference in the operating
systems themselves, NT being a lot stricter about invalid memory accesses
for example, or a bug elsewhere which fortunately isn't causing the app to
crash on Win95. If you can provide more details on what is going wrong then
perhaps one of the contributors to the newsgroup (myself included) will be
able to help eliminate the problem.

>
>Unfortunately, I can't compile the thing on Windows NT. It needs to be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>UNIX version (which explicitly says it's only UNIX).

The call is not documented as it is only provided for compatibility with
applications being migrated from UNIX. However, it should still function in
exactly the same way and therefore your UNIX documentation will be valid.

Regardless of that, you said in your post that you have removed the COBOL
code from your application so I would not have thought you would need to
retain the reference to 'cobcall'. Can you explian what it is being used for
and the parameters being passed to it ?

>
>Any info at all would be greatly appreciated.
>
>Ben.
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: C calling MFCOBOL

- **From:** Ben Brandis <ben@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37096339.1FFD9307@byronics.com.au>`
- **References:** `<922957784.705753@proxy> <7dvnug$jo2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
 Apologies for not being particularly clear on what the exact problem is - day
before a long weekend and all...

Gael Wilson wrote:

> Ben,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to help eliminate the problem.

> >
> >Unfortunately, I can't compile the thing on Windows NT. It needs to be
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

We have an accounting system that has been around for 15 years. It was
originally designed to work on a UNIX system with dumb terminals. This was all
written in cobol and C. The result of this is that we have over 500 subprograms,
all compiled on a SCO UNIX OS (so recompiling this just for Windows NT is not
particularly feasible).

A couple of years ago, we added a Windows 95 client (written in VB) that would
run the PC as a workstation on the UNIX server. This works fine.

We (being the company) then decided to port the entire thing over to Windows so
the UNIX server was optional. We still strongly recommend to our clients that
they use the UNIX system rather than Windows since it's more reliable, but
occasionally we installed a Win95 system.

Now a client has a Windows NT workstation on a Windows NT server and the ported
runtime system will not execute (at all). The reason I didn't quote any error
messages is that I'm not getting any.

When I said I removed all the cobol from the program, I meant only from the
runtime system itself. Because all the modules are written in cobol, the runtime
still needs to call the actual cobol programs.

I've now stripped the runtime system back to this:

#include <stdio.h>

void main ()
{
    char ProgName[32];

    strcpy (ProgName, "mycbl");
    cobcall (ProgName, 0, NULL);
}

and compile it into rts32.exe. It doesn't get much simpler than that...

When I run it (by double clicking on rts32.exe) it does absolutely nothing (on
the NT machine). If I bring up a command line and punch in "rts32", it returns a
blank line and then back to the command prompt.

Whether the file "mycbl.int" exists makes absolutely no difference whatsoever.
It doesn't seem to even look for the file. At least in the Windows 95 system I
get an error message telling me "mycbl" doesn't exist.

It's very frustrating (I've been working on this for a week). I'm sure it will
turn out to be something completely stupid. Since none of this is originally my
code, maybe I'll be able to blame it on the guy who wrote in a bug in the first
place :-) but that means finding what's going wrong first.

Ben.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C calling MFCOBOL

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ecgkl$frq@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<922957784.705753@proxy> <7dvnug$jo2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <37096339.1FFD9307@byronics.com.au>`

```
Ben,

I suspect that the problem is that the RTS32.EXE that you have created has
been linked with the static run-time library. As that library exists
primarily in order to allow standalone modules it does not contain the
support for loading INT/GNT code, hence the error you saw. If you relink the
EXE using the shared run-time (which is the default) that should, hopefully,
solve the problem.


Ben Brandis wrote in message <37096339.1FFD9307@byronics.com.au>...
> Apologies for not being particularly clear on what the exact problem is -
day
>before a long weekend and all...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: C calling MFCOBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ecosd$gdh@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<922957784.705753@proxy> <7dvnug$jo2@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <37096339.1FFD9307@byronics.com.au> <7ecgkl$frq@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <3709CFCE.27617031@byronics.com.au>`

```
Ben,

Sorry for any confusion caused by my previous note, the static run-time
library is mfrts32s.lib, the shared run-time library is mfrts32.lib. So, you
are using the correct one.

The LINK statement you specified includes cbllds.lnk, which is the output
from running CBLNAMES.EXE. The command for that is not in your reply but it
is worth mentioning that you only need to run it and include its output in
the link step if COBOL objects are included in the EXE. However, that in
itself should not cause a problem unless the -M option is specified on the
command line to CBLNAMES. If you do that CBLNAMES creates its own main()
which could explain why nothing happened when you ran the EXE. So, what I
would suggest doing (which is what I tried, successfully) is :

link -out:rts32.exe -subsystem:windows rts32.obj mfrts32.lib...

or

cblnames -s rts32.obj

then link exactly as before.

If that still does not work I suspect you may have an installation problem
on your NT machine given that you saw an error message on Windows 95. The
error is displayed by mftxtwin.dll (or mfscreen.dll if mftxtwin cannot be
found). Check that they are both in the same directory as mfrts32.dll and
that the directory containing them is included in the PATH environment
variable.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

has
>> been linked with the static run-time library. As that library exists
>> primarily in order to allow standalone modules it does not contain the
>> support for loading INT/GNT code, hence the error you saw. If you relink
the
>> EXE using the shared run-time (which is the default) that should,
hopefully,
>> solve the problem.

Gael Wilson wrote:

> Ben,

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Nope, still didn't work.

I've used the following commands:

link -out:rts32.exe -subsystem:windows rts32.obj mfrts32.lib msvcrt.lib
kernel32.lib wsock32.lib user32.lib winspool.lib

and tried:

cblnames -s rts32.obj
link -out:rts32.exe -subsystem:windows @cbllds.lnk mfrts32.lib msvcrt.lib
kernel32.lib wsock32.lib user32.lib winspool.lib

Both of which have the same result. No error messages, just returning
me straight back to the OS.

> If that still does not work I suspect you may have an installation problem
> on your NT machine given that you saw an error message on Windows 95. The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> variable.

This happens exactly the same on three separate NT machines, so I don't think
the NT setup is the problem. I've also checked and made sure both the .dll files
are on the system and it makes no difference.

I finally found the MFCOBOL manuals (buried in the back of a bookcase). In a
section on linking programs, it says:

"Dynamic linking is not supported with the static linked run-time system for
32-bit COBOL systems."

Is this refererring to the difference between mfrts32s.lib and mfrts32.lib or is
it something else? Maybe there's extra libraries that have to be linked to run
on NT?

Is it worth me going through the installation of the C and COBOL compilers on an
NT machine, or should the program work fine if it's compiled in Win95?

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>

```
Ben,

To briefly answer your questions, you should not need any different
libraries for NT vs Win95. The product should run identically on each
platform, as should applications created using it. Therefore, provided the
environment is set up correctly there should be no difference.

However, as the problem does not appear to be anything obvious I think that
the best way forward would be to contact Merant Technical Support and
provide them with a copy of your demo program to look at. They will also
need details of the C compiler version you are using and details of yoiur
environment settings etc.

Sorry I couldn't be more help on this.

### US-PA-Harrisburg Programmer/Analysts, Unisys 2000, COBOL, DMS, DPS

- **From:** warnold@arnold-hanafin.com (W. J. Arnold)
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** misc.jobs.contract,us.jobs.contract,comp.lang.cobol,pa.jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<3703d154.26153421@news.emi.net>`

```
POSITION: Programmer/Analyst

JOB SUMMARY: Rewrite and new development of COBOL/DMS and
COBOL/DMS/DPS programs for subsystems within "Client Information
System".

ENVIRONMENT: Multiple Unisys 2200 mainframes.  PC workstations using
Infoconnect/Accessory Manager for UTS emulation, Windows 95 and MS
Office 97 suite.

MANDATORY MAINFRAME SKILLS: COBOL, DMS, DPS, ECL, IPF/ED/CTS

MANDATORY PC SKILLS:  Windows 95, MS Word

DESIRED PC SKILLS:  Infoconnect/Accessory Manager.

EDUCATIONAL REQUIREMENTS: BS/MS Computer Science, Information Systems,
Business, or Equivalent

REQUIRED EXPERIENCE: Must have at least 3 years experience doing
analysis and programming on a Unisys 2200.

EMPLOYMENT ARRANGEMENT: CONTRACT

START DATE: IMMEDIATE

LOCATION: Harrisburg, PA

DURATION: 12 months

COMPENSATION: NEGOTIABLE 

OTHER REQUIREMENTS: US CITIZEN or PERMANENT RESIDENT


Arnold-Hanafin Corporation
4800 NW Boca Raton Boulevard, Suite 2
Boca Raton, Florida 33431-4804

Voice:	561-982-9570 / 800-891-1086(toll free)
Fax:	561-982-9896 / 877-323-4485(toll free)
Email:	resume@arnold-hanafin.com
Web page: http://www.arnold-hanafin.com
```

## Thread: NetExpress 3.0 and custom API calls — 3 msgs

### NetExpress 3.0 and custom API calls

- **From:** pjgries@powernetonline.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e00pt$6bn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have a problem using some functions in a custom API. I have custom functions
that return "Numeric" values and those I have working fine. The problem I am
having is when I need to return a return code and a character string from the
API function.
Example:
        CALL "apiCharReturn" USING BY REFERENCE BIGCHAR,
                                   BY REFERENCE SMALLINT,
                                   BY VALUE 0 SIZE 4
                             RETURNING RETURNCODE (I want BIGCHAR too!)
        END-CALL
Now apiCharReturn does send RETURNCODE but I want RETURNCODE AND the returned
value of BIGCHAR. Is this possible? If not both, I would want the BIGCHAR
instead of the RETURNCODE (more valuable to me).

I am a VB programmer thrust in the world of MF Cobol so be gentle.
Thanks in advance for any help you can provide a novice Cobol programmer.

Please respond via email.

Peter J Gries   pjgries@powernetonline.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.0 and custom API calls

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gw@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e04n3$ob7@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7e00pt$6bn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Peter,

As the BIGCHAR and SMALLINT parameters in your example are passed BY
REFERENCE, the addresses of those parameters are passed to the
apiCharReturn function. So, if the function modifies the contents of those
addresses what is actually happening is that the parameters in the calling
program are being modified, which is what you want.


pjgries@powernetonline.com wrote in message
<7e00pt$6bn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>I have a problem using some functions in a custom API. I have custom
functions
>that return "Numeric" values and those I have working fine. The problem I
am
>having is when I need to return a return code and a character string from
the
>API function.
>Example:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now apiCharReturn does send RETURNCODE but I want RETURNCODE AND the
returned
>value of BIGCHAR. Is this possible? If not both, I would want the BIGCHAR
>instead of the RETURNCODE (more valuable to me).
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress 3.0 and custom API calls

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <todd.lucas@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3706863e@news1.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7e00pt$6bn$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Peter,

I have been doing alot of mixed-language programming lately using Micro
Focus Workbench 4.0 (and Netexpress 3.0) with Visual Basic 5.0.  Here is
some sample code that I got to work - I think it will do what you require.
You are correct in using the "by reference" clause.  I have found with MF
Cobol that you only need a "returning" clause when you have defined the VB
declare as "Function" instead of "Sub".  I usually just define everything as
"Sub" and use the "by reference" - different strokes for different folks...

Anyway, hope it helps!

Thanks - Todd
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@ibm.net


VB 5.0 Code - saved in .BAS module:

'***************************************************************************
*
' PRNTMAN Subprocedure Definitions:
'***************************************************************************
*
Public Declare Sub PRNTMAN Lib "PRNTMAN.DLL" (ByRef DocPath As Any, ByRef
DocTitle As Any, ByRef ReturnCode As Long, ByRef ReturnMsg1 As Any)

'***************************************************************************
*
' PRNTMAN Subprocedure Parameter Allocations:
'***************************************************************************
*
Private DocPath                     As Cobol_String_80_Struct
Private DocTitle                    As Cobol_String_80_Struct
Private ReturnCode                  As Long
Private ReturnMsg1                  As Cobol_String_80_Struct

Private Type Cobol_String_80_Struct
    Cobol_String_80                 As String * 80
End Type

'***************************************************************************
*
' PRNTMAN_Print_File Subprocedure:
'***************************************************************************
*
Public Sub PRNTMAN_Print_File(ParmDocPath As String, ParmDocTitle As String)

' Ensure that received parms are in fixed-length string field:
    LSet DocPath.Cobol_String_80 = ParmDocPath
    LSet DocTitle.Cobol_String_80 = ParmDocTitle

' Call the Cobol routine to retrieve and print the specified Document:
    Call PRNTMAN(DocPath, DocTitle, ReturnCode, ReturnMsg1)

' If call to program failed, then display the results to the user:
    If Not ReturnCode = 0 Then
        msg = "Could not Print the specified Document..." + Chr(13) +
Chr(13) + ReturnMsg1.Cobol_String_80
        Style = vbOKOnly + vbExclamation + vbDefaultButton1
        Response = MsgBox(msg, Style, "Airgroup - Print File")
    End If
End Sub




VB 5.0 Code - You could use this to call the function in the .BAS module:
    Call PRNTMAN_Print_File("c:\autoexec.bat", "This is a report title")




Micro Focus Cobol Code:

     1$set mf charset(ascii)

19******************************************************************PGMNAR
    20*
*PGMNAR
    21* Notes on Compiling and Linking:
*PGMNAR
    22*
*PGMNAR
    23*    To compile and link using Micro Focus Workbench v4.0:
*PGMNAR
    24*       > cbllink prntman.cbl -S -D -Oprntman.dll
*PGMNAR
    25*
*PGMNAR
    26*    To compile and link using Micro Focus Netexpress:
*PGMNAR
    27*       > cbllink prntman.cbl -S -D -Oprntman.dll
*PGMNAR
    28*
*PGMNAR

29******************************************************************PGMNAR
    30 identification division.
    31 program-id.                     prntman.
    32
    33 environment division.
    34 special-names.
    35     call-convention 74 is win32api.
    36
    37 data division.
    38 working-storage section.
    39 01  print-routine-parameters.
    40     03  printfile-sub           pic  x(13) value 'PC_PRINT_FILE'.
    41     03  print-status            pic  x(2) comp-5.
    42     03  print-errmsg            pic  x(80).
    43     03  print-name-info.
    44         05  print-name-len      pic  x(2) comp-5.
    45         05  print-name          pic  x(62).
    46     03  print-path-info.
    47         05  print-path-len      pic  x(2) comp-5.
    48         05  print-path          pic  x(253).
    49
    50 01  ws-subroutines.
    51     03  delfile-sub            pic x(15) value 'CBL_DELETE_FILE'.
    52
    53 linkage section.
    54
   114 01  prntman-document-path       pic x(80).
   115 01  prntman-document-name       pic x(80).
   116 01  prntman-return-msg          pic x(80).
   117 01  prntman-return-code         pic s9(9)   comp-5.
   118
   119
   120
   121 procedure division win32api using prntman-document-name
   122                                   prntman-document-path
   123                                   prntman-return-code
   124                                   prntman-return-msg.
   125
   126 0000-main  section.
   127* Fields used for testing:
   128
   129*    move 'c:\airgroup\ag\r.txt'     to prntman-document-path.
   130*    move 'Airbill #00000201'        to prntman-document-name.
   131*    move spaces                     to prntman-return-msg.
   132*    move +0                         to prntman-return-code.
   133
   134* Ensure document name is null terminated:
   135     move spaces                     to print-name-info.
   136     move 64                         to print-name-len.
   137
   138     string
   139         prntman-document-name       delimited by '   '
   140         x'00'                       delimited by size
   141         into print-name
   142     end-string.
   143
   144
   145* Ensure document path is null terminated:
   146     move spaces                     to print-path-info.
   147     move 255                        to print-path-len.
   148
   149     string
   150         prntman-document-path       delimited by '   '
   151         x'00'                       delimited by size
   152         into print-path
   153     end-string.
   154
   155* Print the file - allow user to select a printer and font, and
   156* also display the progress of printing:
   157
   158*    Note:  This is a Micro Focus call-by-name procedure, and is
   159*    Note:  fully documented in the Micro Focus manuals.
   160
   161*    Notes on the third print parameter bit settings - set the
   162*    selected bit to "ON" to allow the following functions:
   163*
   164*    0 (01) - display a printer dialog to allow printer selection.
   165*    1 (02) - display a font dialog to allow font selection.
   166*    2 (04) - print in portrait orientation (invalid if bit0 set).
   167*    3 (08) - print in landscape orientation (invalid of bit0 set)
   168*    4 (16) - display progress indicator.
   169
   170     move +0                         to print-status.
   171     call printfile-sub           using print-path-info
   172                                        print-name-info
   173                                        by value 19 size 4
   174                                        by value  0 size 4
   175                              returning print-status
   176     end-call.
   177
   178* If there was an error, then handle it:
   179     perform 9000-process-print-status.
   180
   181* Move return code and message information for caller access:
   182     move print-errmsg               to prntman-return-msg.
   183     move print-status               to prntman-return-code.
   184     move print-status               to return-code.
   185
   186* Return to caller:
   187     goback.
   188 0000-exit.
   189     exit.
   190
   191
   192
   193 9000-process-print-status  section.
   194* If there was an error, then handle it:
   195     evaluate print-status
   196         when +0
   197             move spaces                         to print-errmsg
   198             call delfile-sub using prntman-document-path
   199         when +1
   200             move 'Could not Open Printer device.'
   201                                                 to print-errmsg
   202         when +2
   203             move 'Invalid printer control code specified.'
   204                                                 to print-errmsg
   205         when +3
   206             move 'No printer device associated with specified han
   207-                 'dle.'                         to print-errmsg
   208         when +4
   209             move 'Out of Memory while printing.'
   210                                                 to print-errmsg
   211         when +5
   212             move 'Failed to open file.'
   213                                                 to print-errmsg
   214         when +6
   215             move 'Disk full while spooling file.'
   216                                                 to print-errmsg
   217         when +7
   218             move 'Print job aborted.  No file spooled to Print Ma
   219-                 'nager.'                       to print-errmsg
   220         when +8
   221             move 'Printer information structure invalid.'
   222                                                 to print-errmsg
   223         when +9
   224             move 'No default printer found.'
   225                                                 to print-errmsg
   226         when +10
   227             move 'Error attempting to display printer dialog.'
   228                                                 to print-errmsg
   229         when +11
   230             move 'Write failure to printer and/or spooler.'
   231                                                 to print-errmsg
   232         when +12
   233             move 'No fonts found usable with this Printer.'
   234                                                 to print-errmsg
   235         when +13
   236             move 'The font requested does not exist.'
   237                                                 to print-errmsg
   238         when +14
   239         when +24
   240             move 'User cancelled the print job.'
   241                                                 to print-errmsg
   242         when +18
   243             move 'Could not load the specified bitmap image.'
   244                                                 to print-errmsg
   245         when +19
   246             move 'Invalid bitmap image ID.'
   247                                                 to print-errmsg
   248         when +20
   249             move 'Could not free the bitmap image.'
   250                                                 to print-errmsg
   251         when +21
   252             move 'Could not print the bitmap image.'
   253                                                 to print-errmsg
   254         when +22
   255             move 'Invalid parameter passed to print routine.'
   256                                                 to print-errmsg
   257         when +23
   258             move 'Internal error - print request cancelled.'
   259                                                 to print-errmsg
   260     end-evaluate.
   261 9000-exit.
   262     exit.

## Thread: Is Microfocus the only PC based version of COBOL available ? — 4 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-c01841977d)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is Microfocus the only PC based version of COBOL available ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dvrro$551@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7dtv6f$pvi$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <7dubrr$pq3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
For all the people who have suggested Fujitsu, I don't think you saw the
original post.  The person want to have an MVS-like work environment (JCL,
etc).  Micro Focus does offer this; Fujitsu does not.  I think that
CA-Realia may support it also.  Interestingly enough, VisualAge COBOL (from
IBM) does not - but does have the MOST identical COBOL to the mainframe
(assuming your mainframe ONLY uses the newest COBOL compilers).

I think if you REALLY want to fully emulate the mainframe environment, your
best bet is to go with MERANT (Micro Focus') Mainframe Express product - but
the other products may well be cheaper - so it will depend on how much it is
worth to you to fully emulate the mainframe environment as opposed to just
the mainframe compiler.
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

They definitely have the emulation bit down.  I found it to be extremely
tedious and time consuming to setup the environment.  I never found an
equivalent to the compile-link-go setup that we use on the mainframe.

We never managed to get much response from support when we were evaluating
their product since everything we do is in batch. Their batch environment had a
bug it in that prevents the demo projects from working at all.  We have not
heard back from them on a resolution in 18 months.  If you are needing this
tool for CICS development, they might be more responsive.

Good luck.
```

want to take a look at our "Solution" product.

It allows you to access data files and call Subroutines on MVS from the PC
via Micro Focus Animator and the new Mainframe Express - all with no coding
changes!  No more downloading test files from MVS to the PC;  No more "skip
to cursor" to bypass those subroutines that can't be called from the PC.

It also allows other functionality as well - check out our web-site at:
www.c-cubed.net

## Thread: Calling a script from MF Conol — 2 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-c4dde2be4e)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling a script from MF Conol

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37035DB0.D50C670@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<922660391snz@hibou.demon.co.uk> <27zL2.9316$PY.99358@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <922915161snz@hibou.demon.co.uk>`

```
Tony Geraghty wrote:
> 
> In article <27zL2.9316$PY.99358@news.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> is looking into them.

Tony,

The simplest way is to execute the script using the SYSTEM call. As the
SYSTEM call will use your $SHELL to execute whatever command is given
to it, you can just use whatever shell notation applies. ie:

CALL "SYSTEM" USING "./my_script &" & x"00"
Shell notation ------------------^
(SYSTEM requires a nul-terminated string )

Note that the COBOL screen handler may get confused if the script you
are running asynchronously writes to the terminal - this is not the
case if the SYSTEM call was synchronous (although obviously, it can't
read what was written to the screen by the shell script).

Cheers, Kev.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling a script from MF Conol

Kev,

Thank you.  It seems that the idea is to kick off a print program 
which will not need to commumicate with the calling program.

The program is part of a rewrite/Y2K conversion of an existing 
client/server system; the current system runs a print program as an 
asynchronous process via a call to Tuxedo which allowed the users to 
contimue woriking while the proprt was printed. In the rewrite this 
was replaced by a subprogram call, with the obvious results.
 
I've suggested that he should have a look at the communications 
manual but he doesn't know if the rewrite is also a C/S system and 
seems reluctant to call the people who did the conversion work.

tony

<a id="t-30a283a487"></a>
## Thread: Any object Cobol projects in Dallas area? — 2 msgs

### Any object Cobol projects in Dallas area?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ulb3YDMf#GA.293@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
I'm very comfortable with most of the modern extensions in Cobol/MVS,
MicroFocus, etc. and I've taken classes in C++ but I've never really done
any object-oriented programming (although I like the concept), so I've
never used the new object-oriented extensions in Cobol. Does anyone know of
any client companies (end users) doing projects using these? I'd be happy
to do a short contract at a more modest than usual rate if I could get on a
team doing this kind of project, if it's a well-managed project in a
well-run company. This would need to be in the Dallas area.

Does anyone have any opinions on whether there's a future in
object-oriented Cobol programming? Could this become a good niche
expertise? Might some Cobol-using clients be more comfortable moving in
this direction rather than to different
languages/packages/systems/environments?


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Re: Any object Cobol projects in Dallas area?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37044b40.364560642@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<ulb3YDMf#GA.293@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
On Thu, 1 Apr 1999 22:24:02 -0600, "Robert M. Pritchett"
<NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> wrote:

>I'm very comfortable with most of the modern extensions in Cobol/MVS,
>MicroFocus, etc. and I've taken classes in C++ but I've never really done
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I wish you luck.  I think general acceptance of OO COBOL will wait
till after the standard that defines it is approved and implemented.  

There is too much risk of change in this area at present, and no
common implementation.  

Based on PROGRAMMER acceptance of the '85 COBOL changes, (Just NOW
getting into most shops) I'd say it'll be 2010 before you see any
breadth of OO COBOL development.

>Does anyone have any opinions on whether there's a future in
>object-oriented Cobol programming? Could this become a good niche
>expertise? Might some Cobol-using clients be more comfortable moving in
>this direction rather than to different
>languages/packages/systems/environments?

*Most* (Not all) COBOL new development is extending existing systems.
Fitting OO COBOL into existing code is a HARD HARD fit.  For BRAND new
development, you have two hurtles.

1.  Finding someone willing to use COBOL for a brand new project.
There is still considerable Myopia and just plain ignorance about just
WHAT COBOL can do TODAY.  There's NO reason to use any other language
for business development.  None.

2.  Finding staff willing to embrace the OO Philosophy.  It borders on
religious, and mixed with the politics of business you have a mess.
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol and use of underscores

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
Check your specific compiler for information on this.  It is *not* Standard
and is NOT portable - but some compilers do allow it already (and as
mentioned in another note, it is also allowed in the draft of the NEXT
Standard)

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703F408.46C0@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36FE28C1.7E977541@IMN.nl> <37006d72.179103284@netnews> <37014124.F4B52E47@IMN.nl>`

```
> Randall Bart wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > the resulting field elementary.

The '85 standard is not vague.  It says "The unique data item is 
considered an elementary data item without the JUSTIFIED clause."  Is 
this vague?  One has to read all the rules.

The draft standard does not endorse anything.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37041409.87419014@netnews>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36FE28C1.7E977541@IMN.nl> <37006d72.179103284@netnews> <37014124.F4B52E47@IMN.nl> <3703F408.46C0@compaq.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 01 Apr 1999 14:32:40 -0800, when Don Nelson
<don.nelson@compaq.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>> Randall Bart wrote:
>> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>this vague?  One has to read all the rules.

But is reference modification on a group item legal?  AFAIK the 1985
standard is vague on that.

>The draft standard does not endorse anything.

I'm not sure what you don't like about it, but for "endorses" read
"explicitly allows".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another contest

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37050CE4.7E6@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<36F95E2C.649915D7@IMN.nl> <36FE28C1.7E977541@IMN.nl> <37006d72.179103284@netnews> <37014124.F4B52E47@IMN.nl> <3703F408.46C0@compaq.com> <37041409.87419014@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 
> But is reference modification on a group item legal?  AFAIK the 1985
> standard is vague on that.

Not that I can see.  It is allowed on any item whose usage is display.  
All alphanumeric items automatically have usage is display (not 
explicity, but implicitly because they can't be any other usage).  
I'll admit that it is not that straightforward, but every implementor 
that I know of did it right.

> >The draft standard does not endorse anything.
> 
> I'm not sure what you don't like about it, but for "endorses" read
> "explicitly allows".
>
 
To me "endorse" means to state that you recomend the use of this or 
that.  If you say "explicitly allows" I do understand what you mean.  
Basically C95 implictly allows it.  The proposed standard explicitly 
allows it by accident because a rule was put it saying you could 
specify reference modification on a group that is not strongly typed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol - Linking

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370374cc.309652584@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<iwZL2.1156$8X3.1763@news.flash.net> <7dqbms$dme@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <KFJM2.264$qL5.106@news.flash.net>`

```
On Thu, 01 Apr 1999 12:30:34 GMT, "Tom" <bivenst@flash.net> wrote:

>Thanks Bill.
>Apologies for my ignorance, but how do you indicate the program is MAIN?

Starting in column 8 on the first line place:

@OPTIONS MAIN

Make sure it is all upper case, that you spell OPTIONS with an S and
that the @ is in column 8.
```

#### ↳ Re: entry level cobol jobs?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ejS1S8Vf#GA.362@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<37038E6A.1A4AE033@hiwaay.net>`

```
I wish I had an easier answer. I grabbed every part-time, summer, temp.,
and co-op job (that had anything to do with programming) that I could get
my hands on while in college. I co-op'd at $700+/month and even did
contracting at $4/hr., of course back then the minimum wage was $1.60/hr.
It made a HUGE difference, I found work fairly quickly, I don't remember
how long it took. In fact I never graduated but that was because I got
screwed transferring and 5 years of college (and 140 hours) was more than
enough. Anyway, it worked.

One tip I heard from fellow consultants - although this may apply better to
experienced programmers wanting to get experience in more current
technology - is to do volunteer work for free somewhere e.g. nonprofits for
a short time, enough to prove yourself, and then see if you can arrange to
do some work for them (or for some place they could give you a lead to) for
very cheap, then you have actual paid experience on your rï¿½sumï¿½. Call it an
internship or something to explain away the low rate, so you don't get
stuck with a ridiculously low rate or salary on your first serious full
time job, which this should hopefully help you get. Good luck.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


ron quillen wrote in message <37038E6A.1A4AE033@hiwaay.net>...

>where does a person go to get an entry level cobol position?
```

## Thread: Byte level files — 4 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-855225b242)._

#### ↳ Re: Byte level files

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3702e6c6.273288273@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7dt8h1$5q4$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Wed, 31 Mar 1999 08:41:12 -0500, "Donald Tees"
<donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>Several months back, there was a thread on reading a byte sequential file in
>Cobol. I believe that the FD was set up for a single byte ... but I cannot
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>before.

I tried to post this once but it didn't make it:

With Fujitsu and MicroFocus

Select Byte-File Assign To "filename.dat".

FD  Byte-File.
01  Byte-Rec Pic X.

Open Input Byte-File
Perform Until All-Done
   Read Byte-File
            At End Set All-Done To True
            Not At End
                  Perform Process-Byte
    End-Read
End-Perform
Close Byte-File


With Realia, change the select to:

Select Byte-File Assign To "filename.dat[B]".
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990331231731.10121.00000405@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<370299B5.1904C0E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <370299B5.1904C0E@NOSPAMhome.com>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes:

>
>What version of COBOL are you using?  I thought COBOL for MVS required
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This works fine for mainframe COBOL for MVS / ADCycle\370.
It is restricted to INPUT files only that can be defined in this manner.
This allows for any record length up to the largest 01 level. and lets the
system handle the BLOCK buffering.
Output files can use BLOCK CONTAINS 0 to get the optimum blocksize 
on NEW files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining Non-Empty Files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dvq7j$m8l@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-3003991951030001@i30.dialup.seanet.com> <3702301C.2E0CD32B@NOSPAMhome.com> <7dtm6j$dkm$1@callisto.clark.net> <7du3it$rlu@netnews1.apci.com> <0VAM2.55622$Mb.25424564@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`

```
The reserved word RETURN-CODE is for a memory location which COBOL will use
to populate register 15 before it terminates at a STOP RUN or GOBACK. It is
also the area where COBOL puts the contents of register 15 when a CALLed
subroutine returns control back to the calling program.  Other  COBOL verbs
never put anything in this memory location.

Lanny Levine wrote in message
<0VAM2.55622$Mb.25424564@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>...
>Alan,
>
>    Did you never get into trouble even though you're not setting the
return
>code (register 15) just before the GOBACK? Is it not possible, for
instance,
>that the CLOSE could set register 15 to zero after it had been set to 16
>because the file was empty?
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>> same number of DD statements in the JCL.  Unfortunately, whenever a
new
>>>>> file is added to the step in the job, the program needs to be
modified,
>>>>> both in the Data and Procedure Divisions.  Although simple, this is a
>>>>> maintenance nightmare.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>> having the DD name in the step which the program is executing in, is
it
>>>>> possible for the COBOL program to "find the file", open it, see if it
>is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>> using COBOL II.  Also, each file named in the table has the same
record
>>>>> length.
>>>>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

hi,
interesting topic, recently I had to determine if  1 file only was empty  in a
job stream. Spoke to some colleagues, since we had easytrieve on our system, I
was able to use easytrieve to determine whether I had an empty file or not  and
set the condition code to 
whatever I wanted it to be.   I don't have the code with me at home, but if
anyone is interested let me know i will post it. In fact using easytrieve I
could set a return code if I had no records or any number of records I wanted. 
ps. does anyone know how I can check for an empty file using fileaid?

eric
```

<a id="t-090fee41a9"></a>
## Thread: OS/390 Debug Tool commands — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### OS/390 Debug Tool commands

- **From:** Steve Higgins <higgins_sj@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7dvu1l$3pc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
OK, I've given up! How do you get Debug Tool (part of COBOL for MVS & VM) to
stop when a variable contains a specific value. I've tried all sorts of
stuff. The closest I've come is the following:

   IF VAR = 'VALUE' THEN ; END-IF;

But this obviously did nothing.

The manual is next to useless.

Anyone have any ideas?

Steve Higgins

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/390 Debug Tool commands

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e09kn$kmo@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7dvu1l$3pc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Steve Higgins wrote in message <7dvu1l$3pc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>OK, I've given up! How do you get Debug Tool (part of COBOL for MVS & VM)
to
>stop when a variable contains a specific value. I've tried all sorts of
>stuff. The closest I've come is the following:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

From my "usually reliable source",

  It really depends on what he wants to do.  The general answer
is the QUIT command, but what he writes depends on how often he
wants to test 'VAR'.

General:
    IF VAR = 'VALUE' THEN QUIT;

To test at every statement:
    AT * IF VAR = 'VALUE' THEN QUIT ELSE GO;

And to test at every statement without stopping at every statement:
  Here's the DT command I entered:
      AT *
        PERFORM
          IF WK-SEARCH-TYPE = 'D'
            QUIT ;
          ELSE
            GO ;
          END-IF ;
          GO ;
        END-PERFORM ;

This allowed the app to run without user intervention
at each breakpoint.  When the variable reached "D",
the quit was issued and user was given the end
of session prompt.
```

## Thread: Print codes — 1 this month / 6 total

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-97574d9f03)._

#### ↳ Re: Print codes

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3703DB7B.A0283340@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7do2c2$1gf$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"Attached" utility program worked for us!

No time to explain, rather straightforward though: Create the ASCII file
and
then call this utility

The COBOL Frog

Donald Tees wrote:

> I have a bunch of heritage code running in win95 (Fujitsu Cobol).  Several
> of the reports go 132 columns, and were handled in the past by a dot matrix
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> printer, then copy a basic ASCII file to it.
---------- *** START PRINTER.CBL *** ----------
       Identification division.
       Program-id. PRINT-A-FILE.

       Working-storage Section.

       01 i                            pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 Printer-retcode              pic 9(4) comp-5.
       01 Printer-handle               pic x(4) comp-5 .
       01 Window-handle                pic x(4) comp-5.

       01 Printer-file-read-information.
           03 fi-file-name             pic x(80).
           03 fi-access-mode           pic x comp-x.
           03 fi-deny-mode             pic x comp-x.
           03 fi-device                pic x comp-x.
           03 fi-file-handle           pic x(4).
           03 fi-file-offset           pic x(8) comp-x.
           03 fi-byte-count            pic x(4) comp-x.
           03 fi-flags                 pic x comp-x.
           03 fi-buffer-size           pic 9(4) comp-5 value 128.
           03 fi-buffer                pic x(130).

       01 Document-title.
           03 Document-title-length    pic x(2) comp-5 value 8.
           03 Document-title-value     pic x(8) value "Document".


       *> set of bits defining printer options
       *>    1  bit 0  display a printer control dialog
       *>    2  bit 1  display a font dialog box
       *>    4  bit 2  print portrait (not with bit 0 or bit 3)
       *>    8  bit 3  print landscape (not with bit 0 or bit 2)
       *>   16  bit 4  display progress indicator dialog box
       *> rest of bits must be 0
       01 Flags                        pic x(4) comp-5.

       01 Font-family-name.
           03 Font-name-length         pic x(2) comp-5.
           03 Font-name-value          pic x(11).

       *> Font size in points
       01 Font-size-value               pic x(4) comp-5.

       *> set of bits defining font style
       *>    1  bit 3  bold
       *>    2  bit 2  strikeout
       *>    4  bit 1  underline
       *>    8  bit 0  italic
       *> Rest of bits must be 0
       01 Font-style                   pic x(4) comp-5.


       Linkage section.

       01 Printer-param.
          Copy PrintPar.

       Procedure Division using Printer-param.

       Main.
           Move zero to Printer-retcode

           Move Font-size to Font-size-value

           Move Font-name to Font-name-value
           Move 11 to Font-name-length

           *> General print options:
           Move 0 to Flags

           If Control-dialog-on
               Add 1 to Flags
           End-if

           If Font-dialog-on
               Add 2 to Flags
           End-if

           If (Print-orientation-set = "P")
               Add 4 to Flags
           Else
               Add 8 to Flags
           End-if

           If Progress-bar-on
               Add 16 to Flags
           End-if

           *> Font style options:

           Move 0 to Font-style

           If Font-style-bold-on
               Add 1 to Font-style
           End-if

           If Font-style-strike-on
               Add 2 to Font-style
           End-if

           If Font-style-underline-on
               Add 4 to Font-style
           End-if

           If Font-style-italic-on
               Add 8 to Font-style
           End-if

           Move File-to-print to fi-file-name

           Perform Print-file

           Perform Error-routine
           Goback
           .


       Print-file.
           Move 1 to fi-access-mode
           Move 3 to fi-deny-mode
           Move 0 to fi-device

           Call "CBL_OPEN_FILE"
             using fi-file-name,
                   fi-access-mode,
                   fi-deny-mode,
                   fi-device,
                   fi-file-handle
           End-call

           If (return-code not = zero)
               Move 90 to Printer-retcode
               Perform Error-routine
               Goback
           End-if

           Call "PC_PRINTER_OPEN"
             using Printer-handle,
                   Document-title,
             by value Flags,
             by value Window-handle,
             returning Printer-retcode
           End-call

           Call "PC_PRINTER_SET_FONT"
             using Printer-handle,
                   Font-family-name,
             by value Font-size-value,
             by value Font-style,
             returning Printer-retcode
           End-call

           If (Printer-retcode = zero)
               Move zero            to fi-file-offset
               Move zero            to fi-flags
               Move fi-buffer-size  to fi-byte-count

               Call "CBL_READ_FILE"
                 using fi-file-handle,
                       fi-file-offset,
                       fi-byte-count,
                       fi-flags,
                       fi-buffer
               End-call

               Perform Read-file
                 until (return-code not= zero)

               If (fi-buffer not= spaces)
                   Perform varying i from fi-buffer-size
                     by -1 until (fi-buffer (i:1) not = space)
                       Continue
                   End-perform
                   Move x"0D0A" to fi-buffer (i + 1: 2)

                   Call "PC_PRINTER_WRITE"
                     using Printer-handle,
                           fi-buffer
                     by value fi-buffer-size
                     returning Printer-retcode
                   End-call
               Else
                   Move x"0D0A" to fi-buffer (1:2)
                   Move 2 to  fi-buffer-size
                   Call "PC_PRINTER_WRITE"
                     using Printer-handle,
                           fi-buffer
                     by value fi-buffer-size
                     returning Printer-retcode
                   End-call
               End-if

               Call "PC_PRINTER_CLOSE"
                 using Printer-handle
                 returning Printer-retcode
               End-call
           End-if

           Call "CBL_CLOSE_FILE"
             using fi-file-handle
           End-call
           .


       Read-file.
           Call "PC_PRINTER_WRITE"
             using  Printer-handle,
                    fi-buffer
             by value fi-buffer-size
             returning Printer-retcode
           End-call

           Add fi-buffer-size to fi-file-offset
           Move space to fi-buffer

           Call "CBL_READ_FILE"
             using fi-file-handle,
                   fi-file-offset,
                   fi-byte-count,
                   fi-flags,
                   fi-buffer
           End-call
           .


       Error-routine.
           Move Printer-retcode to Error-code

           Evaluate Error-code
           When  0
               Move "Printed successfully"      to Error-mssg
           When  1
               Move "Failed to open printer"    to Error-mssg
           When  2
               Move "Bad printer control code"  to Error-mssg
           When  3
               Move "Printer not open"          to Error-mssg
           When  4
               Move "Out of memory"             to Error-mssg
           When  5
               Move "File not found"            to Error-mssg
           When  6
               Move "Disk full"                 to Error-mssg
           When  7
               Move "Printer aborted"           to Error-mssg
           When  8
               Move "Bad info structure"        to Error-mssg
           When  9
               Move "No default printer found"  to Error-mssg
           When 10
               Move "No COMMDLG.DLL found"      to Error-mssg
           When 11
               Move "Write Failure"             to Error-mssg
           When 90
               Move "File not found"            to Error-mssg
           When other
               Move "** Error unknown **"       to Error-mssg
           End-evaluate
           .
       End program PRINT-A-FILE.
---------- *** E N D PRINTER.CBL *** ----------
---------- *** START PRINTPAR.CPY *** ----------
       13  File-to-print                 pic X(80).
       13  Error-code                    pic 9(4) comp-5.
       13  Error-mssg                    pic X(30).
       13  Control-dialog-set            pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Control-dialog-off            value "F".
           88  Control-dialog-on             value "T".
       13  Font-dialog-set               pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Font-dialog-off               value "F".
           88  Font-dialog-on                value "T".
       13  Progress-bar-set              pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Progress-bar-off              value "F".
           88  Progress-bar-on               value "T".
       13  Print-orientation-set         pic X(1) value "P".
           88  Print-portrait                value "P".
           88  Print-landscape               value "L".
       13  Font-name                     pic X(30) value "Courier New".
       13  Font-size                     pic X(4) comp-5 value 8.
       13  Font-style-bold-set           pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Font-style-bold-off           value "F".
           88  Font-style-bold-on            value "T".
       13  Font-style-strike-set         pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Font-style-strike-off         value "F".
           88  Font-style-strike-on          value "T".
       13  Font-style-underline-set      pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Font-style-underline-off      value "F".
           88  Font-style-underline-on       value "T".
       13  Font-style-italic-set         pic X(1) value "F".
           88  Font-style-italic-off         value "F".
           88  Font-style-italic-on          value "T".
---------- *** E N D PRINTPAR.CPY *** ----------
---------- *** START PRINTTST.CBL *** ----------
       Identification division.
       Program-id. TST-MAIN.

       Data division.

       Working-storage section.

       01 Prt-Param.
          Copy PrintPar.

       Procedure division.

       Main.
           Move "c:\AUTOEXEC.BAT" to File-to-print

           Call "PRINTER"  using Prt-Param
           Display Error-code
           Display Error-mssg

           Stop run
           .

       End program TST-MAIN.
---------- *** E N D PRINTTST.CBL *** ----------
```

- **Date:** 1999-04-01T00:00:00

```
I strongly second that advice, and I'd like to add that a good deal of
thought should be given to setting compile/link/run default options. LE
especially has lots of options, and I've seen many client sites that appear
to have given little or no thought to these issues, requiring much time and
effort to be spent on overriding default options that could've been used on
"actual work". In some places, there was so much that needed overriding
that it couldn't all fit on a Parm, and at one client it was doubtful that
their default options would even allow programs to run above the line, thus
defeating much of the purpose of the new Cobol and LE.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA

that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!

- **From:** tom@taltech.com (Thomas Lutz)

The company that I work for sells a serial communications program
called the Software Wedge that you may find to be a good tool for
adding serial communications capabilities to your application. The
Software Wedge is an executable program that can pass serial data back
and forth to other programs using either DDE (Dynamic Data Exchange)
or by converting incoming serial data to keystrokes (i.e. it stuffs
the keyboard buffer with the incoming serial data).
The program is extremely easy to use and is designed to have you up
and running sending and receiving serial data directly from within
your application in just a few minutes.
Please visit http://www.taltech.com for more information.

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### cost of Fujitsu v4 ???

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370579FE.282D@netbox.com>`

Can someone enlighten me on why Fujitsu sells their v4 system for, I
think, about $200, when you can buy the book "Mastering COBOL" by Carol
Baroudi from Amazon or Borders for 39.99 and it includes the v4 CDROM
including PowerCobol ?  Is the book version crippled or incomplete in
any way ?

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: cost of Fujitsu v4 ???

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990402235229.21217.00001098@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<370579FE.282D@netbox.com>`

```
In article <370579FE.282D@netbox.com>, Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com> writes:

>
>Can someone enlighten me on why Fujitsu sells their v4 system for, I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Are you sure the book has v4 or is it v3?

Fujitsu is selling the support for $200-500 depending on the package selected.
The software itself is essentially free.  At least in the way that it was
presented 
to my employers.  Either way whatever is included on the CD is not crippled.
I have their 'Demo Cd' from V2 and V3.  Both work just fine for creating
complete  working applications.  V3 was the first to support 32-bit although
the development  had references to doing work in the 16-bit mode for debugging
and recompile under 32-bit for release.  V4 everything is 32-bit and the IDE is
significantly better than V3.
```
