#### ↳ Re: cost of Fujitsu v4 ???

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3706e2d6.534463839@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<370579FE.282D@netbox.com>`

```
On Fri, 02 Apr 1999 21:16:30 -0500, Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
wrote:

>Can someone enlighten me on why Fujitsu sells their v4 system for, I
>think, about $200, when you can buy the book "Mastering COBOL" by Carol
>Baroudi from Amazon or Borders for 39.99 and it includes the v4 CDROM
>including PowerCobol ?  Is the book version crippled or incomplete in
>any way ?

V 4.2 is out now, so the one in the book is a down level version.
It's not supposed to be crippled at all.  I cautiion you, however, if
installing over v3, first UNINSTALL v3 then REMOVE the directory.
Then install.
```

#### ↳ Re: cost of Fujitsu v4 ???

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r5hO2.243$Bs3.30658@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<370579FE.282D@netbox.com>`

```
Hi group,

I'll answer the questions here and set things straight.  The book is
actually $49 and comes with the "watered-down" version of Professional
COBOL. It may be sold for less depending on who is selling it. It is not
crippled.  If the person chooses the Enterprise version, SOME versions that
come with Enterprise will be timed, but the Professional portion or the
regular version part will not time out.  The Mastering COBOL CD offering
the V4 compiler with COBOL97 and PowerCOBOL does NOT contain everything,
especially for PowerCOBOL.   For those wanting to know the differences
between Standard, Professional, and Enterprise, it is explained in
http://www.adtools.com in the link "What COBOL".

Now to show what has been removed from the regular version comparing the
Mastering COBOL CD to the purchased one from Fujitsu, read below:

Fujitsu COBOL97 Compiler V4L10 Level with the following restrictions:

* Optimization
* ODBC (SQL)
* Runtime Installer
* CBL_ routines (Import Library is removed)
* Micro Focus to Fujitsu conversion utilities (Source and file converter
are
removed)
* CGI routine (COPY Book and DLL are removed)
* Call interface for Fujitsu File system (LIB and DLL are removed)
* 2G bytes file system (DLL is removed from CD-ROM)
* Simplified inter-application communication (Server EXE is removed)
* Accessing Btrieve file function (Btrieve files are removed)

PowerCOBOL GUI Builder at V4L10 level with the following controls (dlls)
removed:

* drivelist and folderlist and filelist control
* Timer and drivelist and folderlist and filelist control
* shape control
* image, animation and mci control
* excelconnection and db access control
* DDE and excelconnection and db access control

Hope this helps clear up the question regarding pricing. Purchasing the
compiler directly from Fujitsu will give you all the tools you want from
the compiler.  Also because the features in 4.2 are strongly improved,
programmers will find want to upgrade to 4.2 after finding satisfaction to
begin with basically from using the watered down version.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support

Bob Berman wrote in message <370579FE.282D@netbox.com>...
>Can someone enlighten me on why Fujitsu sells their v4 system for, I
>think, about $200, when you can buy the book "Mastering COBOL" by Carol
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370BE540.79A0@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<370579FE.282D@netbox.com> <r5hO2.243$Bs3.30658@news20.ispnews.com>`

```
Gary Roush wrote:
> I'll answer the questions here and set things straight.  The book is
> actually $49 and comes with the "watered-down" version of Professional
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> begin with basically from using the watered down version.

Gary - do I understand your reply to mean that the book version (watered
down professional) still encompasses more than the Standard version ? 

Bob

#### ↳ Re: New user on Fujitsu COBOL V3 help me

- **From:** "Ian Stoddart" <Ian@stoddart60.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be7cdd$9722a7c0$da1e883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`
- **References:** `<36F8A59F.4AEFF02@medecine.unige.ch>`

```
Alain Chappuis <Alain.Chappuis@medecine.unige.ch> wrote in article
<36F8A59F.4AEFF02@medecine.unige.ch>...
> Hello
> I'm a new user on Fujitsu COBOL V3 and I need some help.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Alain,
I think your problem is in the directory name "program files".  See Support
FAQ COBOL V3 on the Fujitsu web site.

Point 3 says "Fujitsu COBOL does not work correctly if a directory contains
a blank".
Point 4 says "Fujitsu COBOL does not compile correctly if a directory in
the path or the file name contains a blank".

So Fujitsu, why make the default install directory C:\Program
Files\...........?
Regards,
Ian.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New user on Fujitsu COBOL V3 help me

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3704e746.404508249@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<36F8A59F.4AEFF02@medecine.unige.ch> <01be7cdd$9722a7c0$da1e883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`

```
On 2 Apr 1999 07:54:33 GMT, "Ian Stoddart"
<Ian@stoddart60.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>
>So Fujitsu, why make the default install directory C:\Program
>Files\...........?
>Regards,
>Ian.

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New user on Fujitsu COBOL V3 help me

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XShO2.248$Bs3.33588@news20.ispnews.com>`
- **References:** `<36F8A59F.4AEFF02@medecine.unige.ch> <01be7cdd$9722a7c0$da1e883e@stodfamily.u-net.com>`

```
Ian,

The default directory is C:\FSC    and has been for a long time.
Unfortunately the user can change it and if they don't follow instructions
according to the book regarding spaces in the folder, there will be
problems. To anyone that has done this, I would recommend uninstalling the
Fujitsu COBOL program completely and install it again and be sure to accept
the defaults as <drive>:\FSC and go from there. Then what the programmer
wants to do will start working.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support

Hajo,

There is no V3 for Sun Solaris. The compiler for V4 only will run on the
Sun Solaris platform and only if you purchase it specifically for that
platform. It is explained on the website at: http://www.adtools.com  Look
up products and then click on Sun Solaris and go from there.  Hope this
helps.

Regards,
Gary Roush
Fujitsu COBOL Support

take.

I have a bit of COBOL education I took in college. I droped out after one
and a half terms.(personal problems) I'm trying to teach myself, but it's
going slow.

I can't afford to go back to college right now, but I still want to learn.

I'm thinking about looking for a low level entry position that I could learn
from, but I'm afraid that my lack of education and experience will just slam
doors in my face. I don't have any problem with the logic (at my level) I do
have a problem thinking up of real business like problems to solve that i
would use as examples to at least get my foot in the door.

Any suggestions will help.

Thanks, Marc
```

#### ↳ Re: Good corespondence courses?

- **From:** coboljit@aol.com (COBOLJIT)
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990405012501.11049.00000859@ngol06.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<sIiN2.207$Ks6.561899@news.bctel.net>`

```
In article <sIiN2.207$Ks6.561899@news.bctel.net>, "Ana"
<24miyu@mail.geocities.com> writes:

>
>Hi I was wondering if any of you know of any good corespondence courses to
>take.
>

We have a self study course used by corporations, government agencies and
training establishments to train COBOL programmers. Based on the best selling
COBOL Text book, "Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days" the course materials include
the textbook, a study guide, compiler, study aids to help a student find errors
in their code, an excellent book on the basics of computers and a great
computer glossary.  There are other study materials included, and to provide
more advanced data there is a book of tips and tricks for COBOL programmers.

Our materials are recommended by both Futitsu and MicroFocus.

Once the student has grasped the basics of COBOL, we have an excellent
technical handbook on COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000, and a catalogue with a
collection of computer books with a section on other "COBOL related" books.



Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Good corespondence courses?

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3708F474.C1C@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<sIiN2.207$Ks6.561899@news.bctel.net> <19990405012501.11049.00000859@ngol06.aol.com>`

```
COBOLJIT wrote:
> 
> In article <sIiN2.207$Ks6.561899@news.bctel.net>, "Ana"
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 818-951-5240


Before you buy this "self study course" outlined by COBOLGYP, you should
read the review at Amazon.com.  In part: "This book teaches you ANSI 74
unstructured COBOL.  ANSI 85 COBOL 
(which is now 13 years old) is mentioned but not used in coding
examples. The ANSI 89 addition to the standard (instrinsic functions) 
is not covered at all.  Modularity and encapsulation can be done quite 
well even in '74 COBOL but the reader is completely uneducated
after finishing this book. And the reader would never guess that most 
modern COBOL compilers (including new IBM mainframes) support
complete object orientation and that it is fully included in the 
forthcoming ANSI standard. If you want to maintain old code on 
IBM mainframes, this book will get you by. Otherwise, even the most
diligent reader would be unfit to code at every COBOL installation 
I have ever seen."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good corespondence courses?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370973ba.702655256@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<sIiN2.207$Ks6.561899@news.bctel.net> <19990405012501.11049.00000859@ngol06.aol.com> <3708F474.C1C@bellsouth.net>`

```
On Mon, 05 Apr 1999 17:35:29 GMT, Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>
wrote:

>COBOLJIT wrote:
>> 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>read the review at Amazon.com.  In part: 

...snippity...

While I agree to a great degree that the book is outdated - there has
been a second edition of the book.  Amazon.com has included reviews of
the FIRST edition with the reviews for the SECOND edition - a bad, but
wide spread practice at their site. (check out the revies for TY COBOL
in 21 days - the first edition and compare them to the second).  As an
author, I can tell you that these reviews DO have an impact, and it's
unfair the way they are attributed to the second edition.   With my
book, we made corrections to the first printing.  If someone got
really angry the could complain about the extra period in CHAPT04C
that makes the program not compile.  So far all that have written to
me or who have contacted the publisher have been friendly and
understanding.  But I still wait for that one individual to ROUTE the
book because of a single typo - one that, BTW was not included in ANY
of my manuscripts that went back to the publisher - but where someone
got a little crazy with cut and paste, or search and replace.  Anyway
these mistakes happen and books and authors get crucified for them.  I
know that TY COBOL in 21 Days has been updated in the second edition
with many "bonus" chapters and additional information.  I am
prejudiced and I like my book better (I never even looked at TY in 21
before I finished mine), but I understand the "review" issues at
Amazon.  You have to take them each with a grain of salt.

BTW:  My book is doing better in the foriegn market than it is in the
US.  Mainly, I think, because COBOL is still being taught in most
Universities abroad.  I am thrilled.   At the same time, I'm concerned
about the poor use of English in the most recent review (on the US
site).  It's interesting also that they cross post the US reviews on
their foriegn sites, but not on the US site - so my great review on
the Amazon.co.uk site doesn't get any play in the US!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Good corespondence courses?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1@bellsouth.net>

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> unfair the way they are attributed to the second edition.

I have the second edition, not the first.  I'm not familiar with the
first edition, but it looks like stuff was added for Y2K solutions (like
intrinsic functions and the date), but other than that, the review could
be written for the second edition.

<a id="t-e0ee027907"></a>
## Thread: Coul someone send me the ODBC cobol driver for Visual Basic. — 1 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-e0ee027907)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Coul someone send me the ODBC cobol driver for Visual Basic.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John Bradley" <j.bradley@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<467N2.5743$fb4.7707@news2.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<36FA0FF2.1600104F@sge.it> <3700800f.5002022@news.skynet.be> <37012A9F.1FF71572@siber.com> <3701cc74.519646@news.skynet.be>`

```
Alain,

The reason that ODBC drivers for COBOL files seem relatively expensive is
that they are, generally, as complicated and difficult to build correctly as
a relational database management system and the market for them is somewhat
limited.

A COBOL VSAM ODBC "driver" is unlike the Intersolv (Merant) drivers in that
it must implement (in addition to the ODBC API) a SQL parser, execution plan
generator, query optimizer, and execution plan interpreter that is capable
of presenting a relational (i.e., normalized tables and row/column values)
view of unnormalized indexed files with, typically, highly overlaid record
structures.  Doing this correctly is very difficult.  Much more difficult
than interfacing to an existing relational database or "flat" text file.

The methods used to accomplish this by our products are, in fact, patented
in the U.S. and Britain.  In our case, (Relativity Data Server), we give
away the client ODBC driver, but the server engine which runs on the machine
with the COBOL data files costs something.

Regards,
John Bradley (Liant)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37050F68.57CC@us.ibm.com>`

```
Am on a Y2k contract and client is asking about recapturing COBOL source
from object.  Code runs on an IBM MVS system, but unsure of original
compiler -- assume OS/VS COBOL or older.
Does anybody have experience with or know of a de-compiler that is
reasonably workable?
Thanks.
Bill Morgan
```

#### ↳ Re: any workable de-compilers out there

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e357k$e1e@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37050F68.57CC@us.ibm.com>`

```
There is a company (I always forget their exact name - but it is something
like "Source Recovery")  They have a web site and they are mentioned in a
bunch of the IBM COBOL migration documentation.

NOTE: This is ONLY for IBM mainframe COBOL.  "Decompilers" for other
platform/compilers do exist - but I haven't heard very good opinions
expressed about them.
```

## Thread: BIG: Cobol parser project — 31 msgs

### BIG: Cobol parser project

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>`

```
Here is my cobol parser project version 0.01.  It compiles (Fujitsu V3),
it appears to reliably pull out the working storage on my single test
program.

Zero testing, caveat emptor, work very much in progress.

This is a first on the PC for me, normally on the big iron.  How do I
actually assign files from outside the program?

Note that the program must compile in both MVS and PC so make the
answers close.  I am not happy with the file handling because it wont
compile on MVS.

Has anyone got a text list of reserved words?

Eventual purpose:

1.  Output obsolete words from the program.

2.  Parser to ensure adherence to standards.  No point doing what a
machine can do (Jump in here Donald :->)

2.  Automatically add end-if, etc.

3.  Code formatter base.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol parser project

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e54ko$fvd$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>`

```
Files are assigned in the select clause for Fujitsu:

SELECT FILE ASSIGN TO DATA-NAME.
move "C:\area\name.ext" to data-name.
open ...

Ken Foskey wrote in message <3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>...
>Here is my cobol parser project version 0.01.  It compiles (Fujitsu V3),
>it appears to reliably pull out the working storage on my single test
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>3.  Code formatter base.
```

#### ↳ Re: BIG: Cobol parser project

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990403091412.08111.00000732@ngol08.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
writes:

>Here is my cobol parser project version 0.01.  It compiles (Fujitsu V3),
>it appears to reliably pull out the working storage on my single test
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This looks like a neat project to see the final result.
I'd like to follow along and possibly attempt to use the end product 
for standardized code review of the programmers that I support.

As for MVS/PC compatibility, you've already broken that rule with
permitting 255 char lines for the program source.
By placing the file name in the assign statement also breaks the 
MVS compatibility rule.
Code it as if you were on the MVS box using the implementor names,
the only PC specific that would have to be added is the 'line sequential'
organization. By using the 'MVS Implementor names', you will be forced
into using the 'Runtime Environment Variables' screen.  The 'Runtime
Environment' is where you would setup the equivalent of your JCL DD names.

Hope this gets you started towards bringing this to a 'near' single source
dual platform product.  
** As a note for code maintenance:  I'd keep all the code in a single file
   commenting out what is specific to the opposing environment and possibly
   add some kind of identifier tag in 73-80 [old style 80-column coder here].

Best of luck to you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BIG: Cobol parser project

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370624d0@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au> <19990403091412.08111.00000732@ngol08.aol.com>`

```
Ken,
To circumvent all the problems with files do this:
Write another Cobol program to do the file I/O.
The call that with a control block containing an operation
code (open, get, close). That way, the main (and
difficult program can be completely portable and the
(trivial) I/O program can be made as non-portable as you need.

Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:19990403091412.08111.00000732@ngol08.aol.com...
> In article <3705EE1E.EF7AB4FA@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
> writes:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>    commenting out what is specific to the opposing environment and
possibly
>    add some kind of identifier tag in 73-80 [old style 80-column coder
here].
>
> Best of luck to you.
>
```

about the actual file.

to set an environment variable to tell it where copy members are
and what their extension is, but you can ALWAYS use just
COPY TOKEN.

I am able to use copy syntax as 
   COPY 'LIBFIL'.
and this works on both FujiCOBOL and COBOL for MVS AD\Cycle370.
On the PC I have the copybooks in a \CPY directory with NO FILE EXT.
This has worked beautifully for the last year under v3 and v4.  It does pose
a little problem with editting the libraries, but these are 'supposed' to be 
copies of Mainframe libraries and I don't want to get them out of sync.
Any library maintenance is done on the mainframe and then downloaded back to
the PC development area.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BIG: Cobol parser project

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)

writes:

>> the PC development area.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

I think that the point was that on the PC Kevin was coding 
COPY 'LIBFIL.CPY'
and on MVS
COPY LIBFIL.

My point was that enclosing the libfil name in quote/apost works in 
both environments and you should not have to all the extension to the
libfil name.  In my case, I choose to operate without extensions as it 
simplifies my download procedures.  I do place the copylib files in a 
specific directory that is referenced in the compiler options.

You indicated that there is compiler option to indicate what extension 
is used, where might I find this?  Would you be familiar enough with the 
editor to be able to tell how to adjust the options so I do not have to use
the CBL/COB extensions yet still follow the COBOL syntax highlighting rules???

Thanks in advance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BIG: Cobol parser project

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com>`

```
Well, I really don't use the Fujitsu editor so I don't have the problem.
The compiler option is something you might find on some compilers
(my stuff compiles on about 20 different ones and some you have to
tell where/what copybooks are).
My point was simply that sticking with the fully portable COPY XXXX.
without extension or apost/quotes is always the 'best'.
Then you simply adjust to what your particular compiler wants.

If you have special wishes (like in your case, no extension) you
sometimes lose out.

Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com...

start Day II

PARSER   - Simple start up
PARSPARM - Read in the token parameters (remove the 'special'

PARSID   - Identification division

PARSPROC - Procedure division

the wife gets back it is back to the back burner.

Learning experience:

I use the attached copybook in linkage through multiple subprograms.
Notice that active-token-idx is defined COMP.  I originally defined it
as an index as in:

 05  TOKEN-buffer                      occurs 10000
                                       indexed by TOKEN-IDX
                                                  active-token-idx
                                                  last-token-idx.

And the actve-token-idx was not passed back.  Each subproggram defined
it's own local copy.  Debugging is wonderful.  (For example token-idx
happy clicks up in the tokeniser but all other programs think it is 1).

Is this correct?  Is it actually defined what will happen?

One for the standards gurus to chew on.

Question 2:  How do you define a copybook as a requirement on the
compiler.  I 'fixed' the problem but it did not go away in the build
because V3 did not sense the copybook change and automatically
recompile.

looks like an editor.  Is this the default editor that I somehow cleared
in my tinkering?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote in message <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au>...
   <snip>
>
>Learning experience:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
  <snip>

Yes, there are some "funny" rules about INDEX items - but the bottom-line is
that they are ALWAYS "local".  (there is an distinction as you see below
between index data-items and indices themselves.) The "solution" is to have

 05  TOKEN-buffer                      occurs 10000
                                       indexed by TOKEN-IDX
                                                  active-token-idx
                                                  last-token-idx.
 05  Save-Index-Table  Occurs 3 times.
      10  Each-Index   Usage Index.

Then you
   A) save the current index values into the table
   B) "pass" the table of indices as well as your buffer to the subprogram
   C) in the subprogram set your indices to the passed-saved values
   D) do your logic
   E) save the new indices values back into the table
   F) return to the calling program
   G) reset the calling program's indices value to whatever the subprogram
put in the table

*OR* alternatively, don't use indices - but use "subscripting" with another
defined data item (such as your COMP solution).

(Someone else may have a better way of using the same "index values" in
calling and called programs - but this is the approach that I think is
clearest.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: BIG: Cobol parser project

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3707f7f9@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote
 > Ken Foskey wrote in message <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au>...
> >(snip) the actve-token-idx was not passed back.  Each subproggram defined
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, there are some "funny" rules about INDEX items - but the bottom-line
is
> that they are ALWAYS "local
>(snip)
> *OR* alternatively, don't use indices - but use "subscripting" with
another
> defined data item (such as your COMP solution).

Use subscripting instead of indexing. Compilers can optimize subscripts
to behave like indices. That is add 10 instead of 1 if the item indexed is
10 characters long. And in any case, the subscripting time if very small
anyway compared to the operation, e.g.
  02   TABLE-ITEM    PIC X(20) OCCURS 2000 TIMES.
  02   THE-SUB          PIC S9(5) COMP.

IF TABLE-ITEM (THE-SUB) = "QWERTYUIOPASD"
      do something
END-IF
```

crazy standard that you have to use indexes all the time, even when they're
not appropriate, because someone a long time ago said they're more
efficient, even though that really depends on what you're doing.

<jtrifon@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>do not necessarily agree with does not make it crazy. And yes, even today,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>TO SUB. Have you tried a SEARCH of a table with a subscript recently?

What I think is meant might be something like ... oh, where a number
that is input by the user refers DIRECTLY to a table element.  For
example, a table of Months.  The user enters 11 and you lookup
November.  Making the table of months reference ONLY by an index and
not via a subscript is silly.  You have to "jump through hoops" as it
were to get the entered value into the index.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OvjMrkSg#GA.250@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Exactly. Indexes are fine for sequential access, or via Search or Search
All, but for random access, subscripts are not only easier to read, they're
actually more efficient - even the compiler manual usually says so. Forcing
programmers to use indexes in these inappropriate cases not only obscures
and complicates the code, it's less efficient, or at least no better.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370BD7F1.5296@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net> <OvjMrkSg#GA.250@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Robert M. Pritchett wrote:
> 
> Exactly. Indexes are fine for sequential access, or via Search or Search
> All, but for random access, subscripts are not only easier to read, they're
> actually more efficient - even the compiler manual usually says so.

This depends entirely on the implementation and on what you are doing.  
In many, subscripts are never more efficient.  And, if the subscript 
is not some binary type it is never more efficient.  However, 
efficiency should not be that much of a concern.  One reason I have 
heard for using indexes is that they are associated with only one 
table and it prevents a class of problems where the user gets mixed up 
using the wrong subscript with some table.  However, to each his/her 
own.

> Forcing
> programmers to use indexes in these inappropriate cases not only obscures
> and complicates the code, it's less efficient, or at least no better.
> 
See above
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pX1P2.1801$HB5.460843@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net> <OvjMrkSg#GA.250@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <370BD7F1.5296@compaq.com>`

```
Don Nelson wrote:
> ...
>One reason I have heard for using indexes is that they are associated
>with only one table and it prevents a class of problems where the user
>gets mixed up using the wrong subscript with some table.
> ...

I agree with this reason, Don.  In fact, the reasoning is so strong to
my mind that I never use index data items.  They are accidents looking
for a place to happen.  I don't necessarily avoid subscripts, there are
cases when using them makes the code simpler or clearer than indexes,
and efficiency is not an issue.  In fact, the overall logic of using
indexes can be less efficient than subscripts, if you must frequently
convert between indexes and numeric values, or frequently compare
indexes with numeric data items or indexes of tables with different
element sizes.  An individual indexed reference should never be less
efficient than subscripting, of course. :-)
```

I assume you mean "01 whatever USAGE INDEX".  As you say, they are

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

That is true in most cases.  If you are comparing against constants 
most compilers use the computed value in place of the constant so 
there is no loss.  The important thing is to make the code obvious and 
easy to maintain.  Unless something is in a tight look that is 
executed a zillion times small bits of efficiency should not be too 
important.

>An individual indexed reference should never be less
> efficient than subscripting, of course. :-)
 True

- **Message-ID:** `<370C1680.380CFD9@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net>`

The "jump through hoops"  is an extreme  exaggeration. In the trivial month lookup
example, it still is only 1 line of code difference.
Ignoring any validation of the data, which really isn't a consideration here:

ACCEPT mm.
SET mm-ndx TO mm
DISPLAY month-name ( mm-ndx)
*-------------- vs ---------------------*
ACCEPT mm.
DISPLAY month-name ( mm)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N62P2.1820$HB5.466358@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net> <370C1680.380CFD9@ix.netcom.com>`

```
John Trifon wrote:
>The "jump through hoops"  is an extreme  exaggeration. In the trivial month
>lookup example, it still is only 1 line of code difference.
>Ignoring any validation of the data, which really isn't a consideration here:

There are situations when, for every use of a subscript/index to actually
reference a table, you must reference the subscript/index many times for
its occurrence number value.  In these cases, use of an index certainly
can complicate and obfuscate the code to some degree over the use of a
subscript, and can be less efficient, relatively speaking.  The specific
ratio depends on the machine architecture, the compiler, and the particular
requirements of the logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<923579702.23618.0.nnrp-03.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<370657fd@news3.us.ibm.net> <19990403160215.08023.00001025@ngol04.aol.com> <370683c6@news3.us.ibm.net> <3706BD4E.D92E137B@zip.com.au> <7e6v1h$9lr$2@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <37075024.E53F4038@zip.com.au> <7e8rve$214@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <#9CaVbJg#GA.333@nih2naaf.prod2.compuserve.com> <370AC317.5656C981@ix.netcom.com> <370aeb58.798827435@news1.ibm.net> <370C1680.380CFD9@ix.netcom.com> <N62P2.1820$HB5.466358@news3.mia>`

```
I guess everyone's saying its horses for courses.

For some things indexes are much faster.  I vaguely remember that on the
WANG VS there was a study that showed a vast improvement (I can't remember
the figure) on indices over subscripts.

However it does depend on the compiler and hardware.  When we converted our
COBOL to run on AS400, I investigated and subscripts were actually faster
than indices.  This was partly because of the compiler and partly because of
the way AS400 described tables at the MI level.

E.g on a table of 6 byte long elements, the VS compiler would generate SET
index UP BY 5 as ADD 30 TO index whereas the AS400 would generate it as
MULTIPLY 5 BY 6 and ADD the result to index.  So the VS worked out literal
increments at compile time but the AS did it at run time.

Conversely the AS retained structure and array information at the MI level
and could use a subscript directly to pick up an element.

I checked out the AS back in 1990 and things have probably changed since
then.  Certainly everything's much faster and I've certainly stopped
worrying about things like this.

Regards
Rick

Robert M. Pritchett wrote:
> 
> There's a place for using I/O modules, I don't know for sure if this is it,
> but I have seen places where these are sorely overused, like it's been
> adopted as some kind of standard and is required even though in many cases
> it's inappropriate and even harmful to software quality.

I think it is.  I can write an MVS version of the IO and keep the IO
problems separate from the real working (portable?) code.

Status check.

Who was it that said that this is difficult.

I re-engineered the general routines a couple of time already.  The
infrastructure takes a long time to create in these sorts of projects. 
I think it is getting mechanical now just defining the actual rules of
cobol.

I used Arnold's table to create a comprehensive list of tokens via
program and a complementary token copybook.  If I want to add extra word
I can automatically regen the copybook and the keywords table using a
bildtokn command.  Primitive, this is a project on it's own.

I will release a new work in progress once the code for the working
storage is complete.

Does anyone want to tackle the environment division for me?  I will send
latest code base on request by a volunteer.

Ken

PS:  Has Fujitsu corrected the VERY obscure messages when it has a
problem with the 'end-' stuff.  I missed an end-if and it said an

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

> 
> 2.  Automatically add end-if, etc.
> 
> 3.  Code formatter base.

The only reason I have this kind of data is that I wrote a program to
analyze code for compliance to my shop's standards.  Not to reformat
(which I consider to be a much more difficult project), but as an aid
for structured code walkthru's.  After several years of maintenance,
this program is around 8000 lines, but it also checks for dead data,
non-referenced paragraphs, uninitialized variables, comparisons between
numeric and non-numeric data, recursive calls, et cetera.

I would estimate the lexer portion of this monster to be roughly 2000
lines, including data definitions.

Here it is.  It's big:

Dennis,

Please send me e-mail at support@adtools.com and give me your information
on what you're using, what you're not able to do.  Need more details and
then I can get back to you.  PowerGEM Plus is mostly for companies that
have developers working on the same project and the project being worked on
is tracked accordingly. Works very well for those that understand how it
works.  If working individually, it is not necessarily needed.  It is only
included with the Enterprise version.

<a id="t-1b3b9e669c"></a>
## Thread: Y2K ERROR QUESTIONS — 1 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

_Thread started [1999-03](1999-03.md#t-1b3b9e669c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K ERROR QUESTIONS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3705a32d.3178839@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<7d3aj5$sfc$1@samsara0.mindspring.com> <7d4cme$k5j$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <36f9f7a2.0@duster.adelaide.on.net> <7dg2jt$mas$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 26 Mar 1999 13:38:06 GMT, Ed Stevens wrote:

[various snippages]

>  goble@kin.on.net wrote:

>> Of cause this will probably require a rewrite of any files used, which
>> should be fairly easy, if the dates are below 2000.

Easy?  Keep reading.


>Of course, resizing the variables in the files is the "best" and most solid
>way of fixing the problem, even though it doesn't address all the issues.  It
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>avoid changing a data structure.

Compound these issues with the fact that any "all at once" conversion
such as this cannot be undone should something go wrong, unless all
transaction data, changes, etc., since the conversion are also
available.  This does not even address A/B testing and such.

The other thing that cannot be overlooked is history/archival data.
Should all such data be converted?  Should it be retained in both
forms?  Should programs be maintained in versions that process old
data and also in versions that process new data?  (I hope not!)  If
so, how do we deal with data that crosses the Y2K barrier (the
original problem all over again)?  How is data subjected to such a
conversion affected by record retention policies, legal requirements,
and the like?

None of this even begins to deal with files that are shared between
system, sites, platforms, customers, etc.  It should be easy to see
how such an apparently simple concept can rapidly spiral toward
complexity.

As a side note, relative to the "data structures live forever" idea,
many people would be surprised how many 80 column card-type records
still exist in production systems (even in some newer programs).
While many of these have apparently been replaced by user-friendly
data entry methods, it is often the case that, behind the scenes, the
data is written to the old 80 column card image and then fed to the
batch input/editing routine.
```

## Thread: PR: Metagenix and Relational Solutions Inc Sign ResellerAgreement — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### PR: Metagenix and Relational Solutions Inc Sign ResellerAgreement

- **From:** Greg Leman <gleman@metagenix.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.databases,comp.databases.olap,microsoft.public.sqlserver.datawarehouse,comp.databases.pick
- **Message-ID:** `<37064CE6.FE97D778@metagenix.com>`

```
DURHAM, North Carolina, April 3, 1999  Metagenix, Inc.
(http://www.metagenix.com) announced today that it has signed a reseller
agreement with Relational Solutions, Inc., a Cleveland, Ohio-based data
warehousing services company.  Under the agreement, Relational Solutions
will resell MetaTrans(TM), Metagenixï¿½s tool for extracting and
processing metadata, as well as the entire Metagenix product line.

MetaTrans(TM) facilitates the rapid processing of COBOL metadata in
DataStage(TM), an industry-leading data extraction, transformation, and
load tool.  The ability to process COBOL metadata quickly and accurately
was important for Relational Solutions.

ï¿½This agreement is significant for us, ï¿½ said Rob York, President of
Relational Solutions, Inc.  ï¿½It allows us to deliver more timely
solutions for our clients.  It augments our current tool set for
extracting data for warehouse deployment.  Many of our clients have
mainframe-based systems.  MetaTrans is key for getting this type of data
into a warehouse environment.ï¿½

ï¿½Naturally, we are proud to have Relational Solutions as a partner.
Having built their entire business in data warehousing, their expertise
is extensive,ï¿½ said Lance Carroll, Vice-President of Sales and Marketing
for Metagenix, Inc.


About Metagenix, Inc.
Metagenix is a data access software and services company specializing in
data warehouses and data migrations.  The company has significant
expertise in metadata, data extraction, and automated code generation
technology.  Founded in 1998, Metagenix assists its customers directly
or with third party resellers through its successful Business Partner
Program.  Metagenix serves many key markets including legacy to
relational database migrations, data extraction and transformation, data
conversion, Year 2000 conversions, data warehousing, and geographic
information systems.  Metagenix products are complemented with a diverse
array of consulting, support, implementation, and education services,
which focus on providing customers with turnkey solutions to critical
business issues.

About Relational Solutions, Inc.
Relational Solutions, Inc. is a strategic group of highly skilled data
warehouse professionals. The company can work with your current staff
and help to identify the roles and responsibilities required to ensure
the successful implementation of your data warehouse project as well as
the on-going success and management of the warehouse.  RSI was founded
in January of 1996 with the idea of teaming your existing staff with
focused expertise to provide the total data warehouse solution.  With
the RSI methodology, your in-house expertise is maximized, with the
return on your investment.  Relational Solutions has partnerships with
all the major relational database vendors as well as multi dimensional
database vendors.  Partnerships also exist with extraction and
transformation vendors as well as front end access tools.
```

<a id="t-6b7720a3a3"></a>
## Thread: Help! How to get first job — 70 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Jakamo" <linus@total.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
Hi and thanks for reading this.

    My name is Steve. I have a BA, and a certificate as a programmer/analyst
from a real school (a certified college that gives out real diplomas, and
certificates), not a diploma mill. Because of the work term that is required
to graduate, where we were evaluated for grades and had to submit a formal
report, I have six months experience in C, VB5 and Crystal Reports. My
question is where did you people get your first job, and how? I can't find
one. I have been looking for 9 months. I started before I graduated and have
been looking ever since. Entry level used to be your first job. Now it seems
in most cases companies want 1 to 3 years experience for entry level, and
one told me that even though they said 1 to 3, they really wanted 2 - 5. Its
the catch 22. I can't get the experience without a job, and can't get the
job without the experience! I am not attaching my CV because this is not one
of those can you give me a job letters, rather I just want to get an idea of
what you people did and if possible, a few pointers. You can reply to this
newsgroup or to my e-mail address on the bottom.

Thanks in advance for your responses.

Steve (ve2ska@arrl.net)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Joe Moer" <jmoer@cnx.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<3708fbf1$0$29949@fountain.mindlink.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
I was 18 and dating the chairmans daughter when he offered me a job as a
programmer.
Of course, I still had to go to school to get the job.
His company paid for that.  They paid me whilst I was in school.
And when I was done I worked for them for two years.
Bought a ticket to Canada and hopped on the plane.
(The girlfriend had dumped me a few months prior)

When I arrived in Vancouver on Saturday I went to the library and looked
up the names of a few computer companies in the high-tech listings.
I had my first interview Monday afternoon and had made a decision on
a job offer by Tuesday.

After two years there, I gave my resume to 24 different employment agencies
in Vancouver and was soon offered positions by six different companies.
I took the position with the best career opportunities over the one with the
highest salary.

Pretty lucky eh?

RBA

news:K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net...

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** scholl@callisto.acsu.buffalo.edu (Edward P Scholl)
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7eb0od$4fp$1@callisto.acsu.buffalo.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
Hello Steve,
A couple things:

1)  does your school have a career center?  many schools have them, and they
    often have lists of companies that the school maintains a relationship
    with.  they may also put on job fairs where you can hand out dozens of
    resumes, and possibly get an on the spot interview.
2)  bug your friends.  if you recently graduated from school, i'm assuming
    you know other people in your major.  find out where they got jobs.  see
    if they can pass your resume along to HR for their company.
3)  if you know some of your professors well, see if they have any contacts
    they can provide for you.
4)  try a head-hunter.
5)  maybe try some temp work with a firm such as manpower who can put you
    into a company that may end up hiring you full-time.
6)  if your school has an alumni association, check with them to see if they
    can help you.
7)  keep sending out those resumes!  i know when i was job hunting, i always
    had at least a dozen with me, no matter where i went, because you never
    know who you're going to bump into that may be able to help you out.
    

    are you getting interviews?  if you are, but aren't getting jobs, you 
    should polish up your interviewing skills.  and if you're not even 
    getting interviews, you probably should polish up your resume, cause 
    CS is in high demand right now.  remember, your resume is the only 
    impression a company has of you.  one last thing- how flexible are
    you in relocating?  that may be a big factor in a entry-level.


good luck.

-ed


Jakamo (linus@total.net) wrote:
: Hi and thanks for reading this.

:     My name is Steve. I have a BA, and a certificate as a programmer/analyst
: from a real school (a certified college that gives out real diplomas, and
: certificates), not a diploma mill. Because of the work term that is required
: to graduate, where we were evaluated for grades and had to submit a formal
: report, I have six months experience in C, VB5 and Crystal Reports. My
: question is where did you people get your first job, and how? I can't find

: one told me that even though they said 1 to 3, they really wanted 2 - 5. Its

: job without the experience! I am not attaching my CV because this is not one

: what you people did and if possible, a few pointers. You can reply to this

: Thanks in advance for your responses.

: Steve (ve2ska@arrl.net)

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Steven K. Hoskin" <Steve.Hoskin@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7ebc2k$m3q$1@nntpd2.cxo.dec.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```

Jakamo wrote in message ...
 >      [...]
 > Its the catch 22. I can't get the experience without a job, and can't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 > bottom.


I doubt the approach I initially took will help you much, but I have learned
a few things over the years.

I was a gung ho, patriotic kid at 16.  Though I was college and scholarship
bound, I decided at 16 I'd enter the military service.  By 18, the fervor of
this interest had waxed, not waned, and so I joined.  I was three days from
signing up in the Marine Corps when I discovered an educational program the
Air Force had that was unique to that service and appealed to me in its
structure and process.  I joined the Air Force.

I was in the service for 11 years and never bothered to go to school; this
was decidedly NOT the wisest element of my service time  :-).  Nonetheless,
I got eleven years' experience on a multitude of platforms, in a number of
languages, and held four distinctly different positions in programming,
database design & administration, help desk/tech support/training and system
management, with a touch of operations thrown in for good measure.

Needless to say, experience wasn't an issue when I went hunting for work in
the "real world".  Unfortunately, the lack of a degree was.  Also, since I'd
joined the service directly from high school, I had no experience in looking
for work -- it had always come to me, except for the military, and they
basically take anybody who can pass the test and for whom there is a job
opening -- especially true in those days.  So knowing WHERE to look was the
thing I had to learn.

I landed my first post-military job in the computer industry by way of a
contract position; my operations experience, of all things, got my foot in
the door.  When the company I was contracted to as an operator turned up
needing a system manager for the platform I'd last managed, they already
knew me and my work ethic, and my experience on the resume' was relatively
impressive, and after an interview process that, as far as I could tell, was
a mere formality, I was in.

This won't help you much, because you don't have the experience yet.

What I learned during that job hunt, however, is that you have to go
seriously proactive.  Here's a few pointers:

Almost every public library has a set of books which contain the STC codes
(I think STC is correct, it's been awhile since I've had to look them up)
where all the companies in the country are organized by the type of business
they are.  Find the code for computer programming or whatever and start
jotting down company names and phone numbers, and, where supplied, the name
of a contact within the company.

While doing this, don't forget your local businesses.  Keep an eye on the
newspaper for companies that are expanding and might use computers in their
business management.  Even working as an office assistant on Word under
Windows 95 will get you experience that you could claim in seeking a help
desk job, for example.  Be creative; don't limit yourself to computer
companies.  Almost everybody uses at least PCs for SOME function.

And, though it is the scourge of the business, don't forget to hit the
Yellow Pages<tm> and hit on all the contractor/temp/headhunter type
companies.  If nothing else turns up, they might land you doing something
really boring with computers which, while paying the rent, also gives
you...EXPERIENCE.  And, to be honest, the next time I went hunting for a
job, I did the full proactive search, and the first thing to turn up was, in
fact, a contractor position as a system manager, and after the initial
contract period, I was taken on board in the same job as a permanent
employee.  So don't knock the professional prostitution rings too soundly --
they can still pay your rent, get you experience, and potentially land you a
job.  They get you in touch with people in the business in any regard, and
from everything I've heard and seen, NETWORKING yourself is still the key to
an easy and successful job search.  It may be WHAT you know and HOW you
present it that lands you the job, but it's WHO you know that gets you the
interview.

Never burn bridges.  If you wind up in a hellhole of a job, make sure you
leave with as many clean links as you can.  You never know when the boss
that pissed you off there may drop your name in his friend's hat when you're
looking for work the next time around.  Most people aren't jerks on purpose;
something generally drives the behavior pattern, such as stress from being
in a bad job, for example.

That's about it.  Best of luck in the hunt!

_________________________
Steven K. Hoskin
Compaq Computer Corporation
SSDEVO System Manager
Steve.Hoskin@compaq.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Ken Washington" <washngtn@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7ec1nc$9pr$1@news-2.csn.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```

    I have five suggestions for you.  Take them for what they're worth

1.    Check your local newspaper for Computer centric Career Fairs.
        These fairs are put on / attended by companies seeking to
        grow their workforce by some arbitrary % -- usually 10-20%.


2.    Go back to school -- Not because a Master's will open any new
        doors for you, but because as a student, you are eligible to take
        advantage of the Job Placement program(s) therein.  I began
        working as a Co-op student in such a program in my Sophomore
        year & worked in that capacity through my Senior Year -- pretty
        much full time until I graduated.  I didn't feel I was offered what
I
        was worth, but was happy there & accepted a full time position
        when I graduated.


3.     Take an inward look.  Ask yourself if there is something about
         you -- your appearance, attitude, dress, skills & abilities, sex,
         accent, etc., that is turning prospective employers off.  Some
         of these things you can do little about, but discrimination is
unlawful
         in this country, and you can always improve your skill set.  I have
         been gainfully employed in this industry for about 15 years, and
         currently spend 10-20 hours/week outside my full time job working
         to improve my skills.


4.     Get a broker.  You can generally earn more money, and have the
         job searching done for you by working for a contracting/consulting
         firm.  Here are a few companies I could recommend.  Compuware,
         Hall Kinion, Scitor, Access Data, Staff Options.


5.     Use the Internet.  There are several useful job sites where you can
         post your resume for view by thousands of headhunters.  Use the
         search engines to find 'Entry' level jobs, or related work like
'Tester'.
         A friend of mine was just promoted up from Tester to Developer
         because the company needed more developers and decided to
         look within.  Here are a few of my favorite sites.
http://www.dice.com
         http://jughead.trinet.com/jobs.html, http://www.tjobs.com.
         http://www.cmagroup.com, http://www.vjf.com




Ken Washington
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Phlip" <phlip@politizen.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7ec3fu$ajg@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ec1nc$9pr$1@news-2.csn.net>`

```
Ken Washington wrote:

>5.     Use the Internet...

Ken forgot MonsterBoard (unsure of the URL but it's probably
obvious).

6.      Do Web work. HTML coding has gone from Computer Scientist
level to Amanuensis level in the last few years; you can get in if
you also know something about the topic of the Web site involved, or
are willing to work at home for sub-minimum wages and startup hours.
(Psyched?)

7.      Put your resume on the Web. Technicals have no reason to
ever put a resume on paper again, and any company or headhunter who
requires this, or any applicant who does this, is not worth looking
at.

8.      Know the Software Engineering Management side of things. In
general, the industry has an incredible brain-drain at the top (due
to tragedies like early retirement), but has incredible risk at the
bottom. Nobody wants a neophyte because (IIRC) 99.99% of all
projects go over budget, and executives are familiar with hearing
management (where the blame actually lies) complaining "all these
new guys you authorized slowed us down!" Be prepared to cite chapter
and verse from books like /Rapid Development/ or /Death March/ if
you suspect they are leading towards questions like "how fast can
you type?"

>   ...related work like 'Tester'...

"Tester" should be the _only_ entry level job out there with
promotion to coding, but too many managers are busy treading water
and falling behind to take time out to read the copious industry
literature screaming this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Paul Parkhurst" <paulmp@nospam_usa.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<SBVN2.30128$FZ5.11294@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
    Well, just so you'll know (and I'm hoping I'm not beating a dead horse):
the computer industry is almost unique in the fact that, while it requires
very specialized, knowledge-based skills, education is secondary or tertiary
to finding a good job.  In most other professions such as this, education is
used as a yardstick to measure your competence (who would hire a lawyer
without a law degree, no matter how many years of experience he'd had?).
Not so for engineering.  What matters is your experience and you're
ablities.  I've never (I mean *never*) been asked about my education when
interviewing for a job.  All they've wanted to know is what kind and how
many years of experience I've had.

    I know you're probably saying "Tell me something I don't know!", but
this is just the nature of the computer industry.  With that said, here's
some questions for you:

    First, do you honestly only have 6 months experience with VB and C++?
Six months ago, were you completely clueless about these languages?  If not,
then you've probably had more experience than you realize.  When was the
first time you took a C class?  When was the first time you created a
program in VB?  Don't stretch it too much, of course (I'm sure you know you
should never, never, NEVER lie to get a job), but give yourself a little
credit, too.

    Next, what sort of jobs have you had before?  Were any of them
computer-related?  Did you do any programming for them?  Mention anything
having anything to do with computers that you did in any of your previous
jobs.

    Also, who do you know?  Does a friend or a friend of a friend or college
professor or family member or someone (anyone) know anyone who needs an
entry-level programmer/analyst?  Nepotism is a great way to get a job :-)
And if you don't like getting jobs this way, stop worrying about it:  in the
computer field, if you can't do the work, they'll fire you no matter who you
know or how you got in.  It's all a matter of getting your foot in the door.
Once you're in, though, you're on your own.

    Finally... are you sure you're looking in the right places?  Are you
looking through ads or going to headhunters or what?  Headhunters probably
aren't a good idea when you're starting out because these firms usually
don't want to deal with finding people $40K a year jobs.  They make a % of
what you get paid, so they'll probably only deal with junior- or mid-level
or higher.  Also, don't bother with contracting firms since they normally
don't deal with entry-level.  Have you gone through any programs at school?
There's a lot of companies out there who will hire you directly out of
school and pay you decently (and even pay for your Masters program).  Make
sure you check with your school counsellor for these sorts of programs.

    You may have to take some less-than-glamorous jobs in the beginning just
to get experience (building computers, updating web pages, writing reports,
being the office lackey, etc.).  However, it's definitely worth it :-)  I
had a string of crap jobs, including my own business, before becoming a
"real" engineer.  Each of them (all in computers, of course) gave me more
and more experience until it impressed someone enough and I got hired.  By
that time, my big problem was underestimating my earning potential :-)  I'm
still amazed by how much $$ you can milk out of this industry.  It just
takes getting that first REAL job :-)

    Anyway, hope this helps.  Hang in there; you'll get hired and you'll be
on your way :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`

```
I give preference to people that bring code to an interview. Anybody that
does not ... well, would you hire an artist without looking at their work,
or a writer without reading their books?

If a grad brought me a really super resume on floppy as well as on paper
(HTML, or something that took some effort), I would be more likely to hire.
I used to hire by dropping in to the computer room at four in the morning
(at the local university), but now that everybody good has their own
computer, that does not work anymore.  Perhaps these students should send
out their resume on floppy or CDROM.

I consider academic experience as "experience".  If a student had "4 months
Cobol" on their resume, and said in the interview that this was a course
where they were coding everyday, I do consider that as valid experience.
Work term stuff is also important.

Actually, I prefer to hire new grads.  That way I can warp them into coding
the way I like before they get hardening of the arteries (like me).
;<)

<370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7eddj3$jld$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net> <370A256A.7A32BF60@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message

>I agree, since this heading is "How to get first job".  I certainly
>don't want programmers to bring listings from their previous or current
>job with them.  But even with this age discrimination talk, I haven't
>seen experienced programmers need to show code to prove their skills.

I have, unfortunately.  There is a difference between twenty years of
experience and one year of experience twenty times.

Mind you, I would agree that there is much less need with experienced
programmers, but then I can talk to them about style with some expectation
that they are aware of it.  Neophytes seldom even realize that there is such
a thing.

I think the biggest problem of beginners is that they have never done
anything large or sophisticated. There is a major jump in programming
knowledge when a person does their first 10,000 line program. If a beginner
has done something that large, even as a volunteer project, then I want to
see it, regardless of language.  If it is decently done, then I do not
consider them a beginner.
```

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> experience and one year of experience twenty times.

But usually we can tell those two apart in the interview.  

Beginners don't know the language (IS speak, not COBOL) well enough -
sometimes they need to show us instead of tell us.

> Mind you, I would agree that there is much less need with experienced
> programmers, but then I can talk to them about style with some expectation
> that they are aware of it.  Neophytes seldom even realize that there is such
> a thing.

We are in agreement here.
 
> I think the biggest problem of beginners is that they have never done
> anything large or sophisticated. There is a major jump in programming
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> consider them a beginner.

Good point.  Maybe they should bring two programs, one concise to
illustrate their style, and one large to show scope.   But keep them
both in the brief case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** zszabo@asid-1.umd.edu (Zsolt Szabo)
- **Date:** 1999-04-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f03cc$5cp$1@asid-1.umd.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <370A256A.7A32BF60@NOSPAMhome.com> <7eddj3$jld$1@news.igs.net> <7esj1h$o25$1@alexander.INS.CWRU.Edu>`

```
In article <7esj1h$o25$1@alexander.INS.CWRU.Edu>,
Richard Smol <bl227@cleveland.Freenet.Edu> wrote:

>Would that mean that experience in FSF projects (you know, GNU and thelike)
>is also valid? 
>

Personally, I think it's much more valid than a real programming job. Reason 
being that if someone has enough enthusiasm to program in their spare time, 
they probably make a fairly decent programmer. There are of course exceptions 
to the rule but if you want to hire someone new and you know that this person 
programs in their spare time, you are probably more likely to hire them than 
someone who does not.

I almost got a job once merely because I posted a message to a newsgroup 
indicating that I would like to start a freeware project. I'm not even a 
programmer. Of course then again I almost got hired once as a security 
analyst simply for posting some stuff to alt.2600 ;-)

P.S. (shameless plug) if anyone has any ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING jobs available 
(I am, in fact, a EE grad student) - I wouldn't mind doing an internship over
the summer ;-)
```

On Tue, 06 Apr 1999 09:16:58 -0600, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:


>I agree, since this heading is "How to get first job".  I certainly
>don't want programmers to bring listings from their previous or current
>job with them.  But even with this age discrimination talk, I haven't
>seen experienced programmers need to show code to prove their skills.

I was hired at my present full time position based partly on my code
samples.  I sent a whole application I wrote.  And my boss quizzed me
on it too.  Why would someone with 15 years need to do this?  I wasn't
being hired as a "code cranker" alone.  I was hired to handle COBOL
systems on multiple platforms, and to be able to "hit the ground
running" - and not need any time to come up to speed.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Karl Thomas" <kathomas@nox.nyx.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7eiafn$prp$1@samsara0.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net>`

```

Donald Tees wrote in message <7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net>...
>I give preference to people that bring code to an interview. Anybody that
>does not ... well, would you hire an artist without looking at their work,
>or a writer without reading their books?


How do you do that though? I do have what most people would consider a
decent amount of work experience writing a fairly large system in C.  I once
went to an interview about a year ago and while I thought the interview went
well I wasn't sure that I had clenched it.  Then right when the interview
was about to be over I asked him whether he wanted me to show him the
programs that I had written  (I do all my programming on my own laptop) and
it impressed him to the point that he made me an offer -- I ended up staying
at my current company though because of a couter-offer.

After the interview I then wondered what type of impression showing programs
that were made for another company give a future prospective employer at a
larger company.  Even though I didn't divulge any proprietary information
about my current employer.  I would think that a company might frown on a
potential candidate who would so easily show off another company's programs.

Is it appropriate to show work that you did for another company even if you
never actually give them the source code?  The program was nothing
revolutionary that any other company could do anything with.
```

Well, I was speaking of a student, and also thinking of my own experience.
I own all the code I write, so proprietary considerations do not arise.
They would not for a student, either.

Certainly, if you write code for someone else, you may not be able to demo
it, and you may not be able to show anybody else the code, based on ethical
considerations.  I would be a bit hesitant, though, of a programmer that
came in and said "I cannot show you code because I have never written
anything for myself".

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7fab0c$q6f$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net> <7eiafn$prp$1@samsara0.mindspring.com> <7eihch$5ef$1@news.igs.net> <370D702C.EF18A78C@acm.org> <3717bbfc.21384937@news.clorox.com>`

```
That is a huge assumption.  First of all, speaking as the owner of a company
that sells software, I would have absolutely no objection to any of my
programmers showing code that they had written at an interview.  I would
expect them to ask permission, and I might put a few restrictions on what
they did, but I certainly would not object.

I *would* advise them to note that it is Copyrighted, ask that the Copyright
message be included, and recommend that they state permission was granted.

There is s huge difference between giving someone the source code for a
project, and including a few pages of sample code in a resume.  Anybody good
enough to gain the knowledge contained in a 75,000 line project from half a
dozen sample pages is certainly good enough to write the system themselves.

Piero wrote in message <3717bbfc.21384937@news.clorox.com>...
>On 08 Apr 1999 20:13:35 PDT, Patricia Shanahan <pats@acm.org> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Piero Giorgi
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dskirk@usa.net (david )
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717a43b.3075100@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl> <370a09c7.3377590@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <7ed7u9$gb5$1@news.igs.net> <7eiafn$prp$1@samsara0.mindspring.com> <7eihch$5ef$1@news.igs.net>`

```
On Thu, 8 Apr 1999 11:19:30 -0400, "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
wrote:

>Well, I was speaking of a student, and also thinking of my own experience.
>I own all the code I write, so proprietary considerations do not arise.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>...<snip>...

hmmm.... interesting topic -- seeing source code at the interview...
i've interviewed new programmers for 30+ years and have rarely seen
this -- to me, it was always appreciated as it demonstrates that the
interviewee takes programming as a serious profession. 

having said that, it can also be dangerous... some concerns:

 - showing HOW you designed the program and WHY you designed it that
way are more important to me than the code itself -- although the code
is important. if you have code, also have a structure chart or other
graphic to display architecture. be prepared to defend it -- shows you
have confidence.

 - some institutions and employers may have coding standards that are
archaic... (I've seen such...) and the code you display may be
offensive to a potential employer who follows more current practices -
but this can backfire if it's the other way around and your code is
clean, crisp, top-down design, decomposed, structured -- and the
potential employer still believes that GOTO and EXIT are the only way
to code.  few employers want to hire "experts" who are going to tell
them how to do their jobs... so you can easily get into quagmire here,
either from being ahead or behind....  

 - a major issue -- few employers who interview are close to the
programming process. many jokingly comment that they haven't coded in
years -- and are obviously proud of it. in this case, if you offer to
review your fancy piece of code, the interviewer may send you off to
show to a programmer and, bingo, the interview is over. AND -- the
programmer who looks at your code may see you as a threat... kiss off
the job offer.  

 - on the issue of whether you can take code you wrote for a current
employer.... i believe you can 'scrub' the code from anything
proprietary and turn into a generic piece... bring only one component
so the architure itself is not displayed. by doing this - and
explaining to interviewer why you did it -- you exhibit a sense of
integrity and professionalism that can't be determined by any
questioning... points in your favor...  sensitive issue? yes, so play
it that way.   

good luck,
david

david              dskirk@usa.net
_________________________________
```
