##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** bl227@cleveland.Freenet.Edu (Richard Smol)
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7esidl$m7b$1@alexander.INS.CWRU.Edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <7ea2jf$4ss$1@news2.xs4all.nl>`

```

In a previous article, royv@rojaal.com (Roy G. Vervoort) says:

>I can confirm what paul is telling you, I have 15 programmers working for
>me. At a job interview we ask people to demonstrate what they can do. 

Huh? That is totally moronic. If at a job interview I would be asked to
'write a program' out of the blue, I would immediately leave. It would mean
that a company doesn't value good design and insight, but prefers a bunch
of hackers spitting out code.

A good programmer is not someone who memorized all language features. 
It's not so bad if a programmer has to grab a book once in a while.... I'd
even prefer a programmer to do that instead of just blindly cramming out
programs that might be far more efficient with better design and thought.
I see too many programmers even *refusing* to pick up books because it
would mean 'they are not good enough yet'. That's bad practice.

I am of opinion that a proficient programmer can master any language within
reasonable amounts of time. A good education can help a lot with that.
Insight and having a good perspective is way more important that an
encyclopedial knowledge of language features.

Greetz,

RS
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Neil@zipolli.freeserve.co.uk (Neil Britton)
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<s51O2.6149$LX.2564894@WReNphoon3>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
This might seem a bit immoral, but I lied about my previous experience when
getting my first programming job.

Get to know as much as you can about the type of application that you want
to lie about. Putting this on a C.V. should get you that all important
interview. Then if you know your technical stuff, you get a reasonably easy
interview and they don't check your references - your in! You would be
surprised how lax most employers are when it comes to checking these things.

For the record I used this technique to get my first programming job. I was
working as an electronics engineer and wanted to become a programmer. I
learned how to program in C, then claimed that I had written an application
which was similar to one that I'd been using to analyse circuits.

I've since used this approach when I've wanted to gain experience with
different technologies.

MY TOP 10 HINTS ARE

1. Know your programming languages. (do you know how to declare function
that returns a pointer to a function C)

2. Don't be over ambitious. (Say for instance that you only coded the
software and that someone else was responsible for the most of the design)

3. Any claimed experience should be with a stand alone system. (Networked
systems or systems with databases are more complex and harder to lie about)

4. Ask lots of questions as this will limit their time for
asking you awkward questions.

5. Be prepared for questions on the process of developing software. Such as
how did you gather your requirements. How did you manage your configutaion
control. Did you use any design tools. You should have covered this in your
course.

6. Always remain cool, even if you suspect that you've been rumbled.

7. Avoid jobs in the same field as you claimed experience. (Strangely most
employers think programming ability is more
important than expertise in the field of the application)

8. If possible get a job using computers as a user. As describing a system
that you've used is easier than inventing one that you haven't.

9. Remember that anyone who interviews you DOES want to give
you the job. If they didn't want to employ you then they wouldn't be
interviewing you.

10. Play the lottery, with a lot of luck you won't need to get a job.




**** Posted from RemarQ - http://www.remarq.com - Discussions Start Here (tm) ****
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Robert Blum" <r.blum@gmx.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7eakgu$nrd$1@eve.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3708d27c.36968402@news.trxinc.com>`

```

cyndyh@hotmail.com wrote in message <3708d27c.36968402@news.trxinc.com>...
>I am currently a student in the programming field.  One of my
>instructors gave us a really good idea.  Print copies of your programs
>and include them either with your resume or take them to interviews.

PLEASE do not include them with your resume. Anything that _any_ interviewer
might consider significant is way to long to attach it to a resume.

Remember: more than three pages in your application, and your chances are
degrading.

If you want to show your skills, _ask_ them if it is OK to send some
samples.

However, bringing them with you to the interview is always a good idea. You
should also keep some copies of your resume around; Sometimes you're
scheduled for an interview, but the paperwork is still in human resources...

Bye,
  Robert
```

On that note always keep copies of your resume with you. When
going on that vacation, you never know who will be sitting next
to you on the plane or at the airport. It is a good way to get
some contacts anyway.

Just a note when I interview people I always ask them to write
some code to solve a problem. The problem changes form interview
to interview, but always will be relevent to a proposed skill set
they have. This is a good way to weed out those who say they can
do things and those who can.

|

a good software company.  In addition to being the test lead on projects, I
developed C and shell script testing tools for the group, and also worked on side
projects own my own time to gain experience.  I later moved into a development
position with the same group.   Maybe something similar could work for you.  Good
luck...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Thomas Werthmann-Auzinger <thomas@businessevolution.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<370D08D2.79D03678@businessevolution.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <37085C17.9954629C@freenet.co.uk>`

```
Forget about the code for a second. Anybody can learn to code in about three months.
Hiring someone full time is quite a risk and and investment for a company.
Internships are usually much easier to get. Not only can this be the foot door at
that particular company (i.e. if they like you they might keep you full time), but
it will also give you that real life experience on your resume that can help you
land a real job somewhere else. I prefer candidates who at least *tried* to do
something computer related during their summers instead of working at the local
video store.

Candidates who have worked in teams (academia or industry) get my preference, as
well as people who had to work with real life vague and changing requirements,
instead of textbook examples. Having worked on a product that had to satisfy a
customer is better than a contrived project that's complex for complexity's sake.
Being able to tackle a task in a domain of which you don't have any clue at the
beginning is also a plus. And, even though English is not my first language (or
maybe because of that): showing disrespect for the language is defintely a negative.
There is no excuse for a "buggy" resume, you can always (and should) let someone
else proofread it.

Just my $0.02,

Thomas

CyberDot wrote:

> Yeah, well Mine is a 'Give me Job' letter, I've been looking for well over 3yrs

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I got my first job when I asked the lady down the street if I could do some
yard work like mowing and stuff like that. She was very nice, she was this
botanist and was always telling me interesting things about plants and
gardening. She paid me 7/ hour for 2 hours a week. Working for her and a
number of other people I saved all my money for the summer and bought myself
a new bmx bike. It was one of those nice colorful ones with yellow spoked
rims and blue tires. I thought the blue tires were neat but as they got worn
they got more black and faded blue. When I bought new tires I bought black
ones and they looked neat. I wonder what I ever did with that old bike
......

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Scot P. Floess" <sfloess@widget.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<370BC071.BF7B3924@widget.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
Its all about selling yourself and experience.  Don't go overboard on your
resume to the extent that says, "hey, I got all my experience while in college
classes!"  To an employer, it looks like you have no experience.  Flower up
your resume.  Don't specifically lie, however, tell only what you want them to
hear.  Don't make your education section the biggest and most dramatic - it
will stick out like a sore thumb.  If you've done some work, make that shine.
You must give the impression you've done something - other than a school
project.

My point is, you don't have to lie, just give as enought information to conveey
you have done something and have experience.  There is nothing wrong with being
honest, but by all means, don't make it sound like all your only experience is
school - and that's it...

So, place your education at the bottom of your resume....

Also, if you've done any kind of work - side projects, etc, bring along your
code, or include as an attachment to your resume....


Jakamo wrote:

> Hi and thanks for reading this.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve (ve2ska@arrl.net)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** mcd@kanjisoft.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ek33c$opc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
In article <K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>,
  "Jakamo" <linus@total.net> wrote:

> rather I just want to get an idea of
> what you people did and if possible, a few pointers. You can reply to this
> newsgroup or to my e-mail address on the bottom.
>
Steve,

I'm a big advocate doing computer related projects in your spare time. For
example, I wrote a shareware kanji learning program and an insurance
homeowners rating program in Microsoft Access and those two projects landed
me a contract job doing Access programming.

Now I'm trying to break into the Java world. I've got Sun Java Programmer
certification and will soon have the Sun Developer certification.  After
that, I plan to some put some kind of project on the web where a prospective
employer can check out my programs. Needless to say, all of this free work
will pay off in a substantial increase in my career opportunies.

On attaching code samples, I saw an article in the Java Developer's journal
where an employer was very happy to see code samples. I would probably just
refer them to a web site where the code was posted.

Also, what type of work are you doing now? If you are in an office, there is
guaranteed to be some kind of need for a person with computer skills. Learn
how to improve current procedures by using spreadsheets/databases instead of
paper, etc.  There is always a crying need for people who can/want to
understand information tools.

Good luck - rememember, persistence pays off.

Mark

                     Learn Kanji with KanjiCan 2.0

                          www.kanjisoft.com

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Rob Cupples" <cyclone@ghg.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<370d8fab.0@news.ghgcorp.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
I was just plain lucky to interview with a company that was too cheap to pay
people with real experience.  It was a government contractor more interested
in putting warm bodies behind desks to fill a head count than finding the
right people for the job.

I worked hard and because my competition was so poor I stuck out like a sore
thumb as somebody known to get the job done.  The whole time I was there we
were understaffed.  They made plenty of offers but they were so low that
only people straight from college ever accepted with the exception of a guy
who claimed to have 15 years programming experience who turned out couldn't
write 3 lines of C in a month.  Managers didn't seriously check out
candidates.  After they hired somebody who couldn't do the job they were too
scared to fire them because of company politics.  They didn't want any black
marks on their record that would ban them from later promotions.

Turnover was high.  Most people either built up their experience and
eventually left for 20-50% raises or just stayed there because they were
completely unemployable elsewhere.  The downside in my case was that I
caught the attention of a manager and started getting assigned
grooming-for-management tasks.  I had no interest in management at this
company.  I just wanted to get my 2-3 years experience needed by better jobs
and move on like several others before me had.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "The North View" <mark.serlinSPA~M@S~PAMbtinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7eqf23$qev$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
I'm a degree student in London. I got a great summer job coding stuff for
interactive TV (html / javascript / C) by downloading the list from
www.jobserve.com and sending out loads of emails.

I had to attend 2 interviews, they didn't ask to see any work but at the 1st
interview I had to talk knowledgeably about html code etc. and at the 2nd
there was some technical chat but mostly they wanted to know if I was
personable and a teamworker. Then I got the job...

My other summer job (the year before) I got through word of mouth ( sorry
:-(  ). If an employer really wants to test you they might ask you to write
a prog for them there and then (has been known esp. in London).  A friend of
mine did this and got the job having achieved about 50% of what the prog was
supposed to do - his employer told him that this was way better than the
raft of graduates he had just interviewed!

Good luck
Mark

PS I graduate this year & like Java................;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** bill <wgblackmon@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
Lie, take a chance to get in, get in and work your ass off. Make yourself
indispensable. All you can do is fail - and learn a lot on the way. Otherwise
you'll never get a job and never get started. Employers will lie to you,
recruiters will lie to you, brokers will lie to you -  the industry is based on
wishful thinking - everyone wants everything now for as little as possible and
those who can't promise that get left in the cold, so everyone lies. All you can
do is get in - one way or another - and do your best. However, don't
misrepresent your abilities too much because you will be tested immediately on
the job. Work up a demo at home and show it if its' necessary. Put applets on
the internet. Keep trying and eventually you'll be in the right place at the
right time. If I hadn't stretched the truth in the beginning 6 years ago, I'd
still be trying to get some experience so I'd have some experience so I could
get a job and get some experience because, after all, there is so much demand
for programmers, but none for those with less than 2 years experience in 7
different areas...... Good luck!

Jakamo wrote:

> Hi and thanks for reading this.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve (ve2ska@arrl.net)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Patricia Shanahan <pats@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<37119447.AEAF7F07@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>`

```
Please, please, be honest. Say what you know and have done, but don't
claim more than that. Lying is not a good basis for any working
relationship.

On the original question, I got my first job based on a degree in
mathematics and some programming course work, by filling in a lot of
applications, without telling any lies. On the other hand, it was
easier then to get a programming job without a CS degree than it is
now.

Patricia

>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Kitty Carr <kcarr1_not@beltsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<uKdQ2.5867$0m3.1351014@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>`

```
bill wrote:
> 
> Lie, take a chance to get in, get in and work your ass off. Make yourself
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> those who can't promise that get left in the cold, so everyone lies. 

Steve, the original poster, received a lot of invaluable advice from
professionals who know their stuff, but those who advised him to lie are
wrong, wrong, wrong!  In the first place, an assumption is made that the
interviewer is a buffoon ("After all, how many times do they actually
check references?").  The fact that this is posed as a question
indicates that the person advising you to lie doesn't KNOW if references
are checked or how thoroughly they are checked.  If you *must* make an
assumption, assume that your references WILL be checked. 

In the second place, the assumption is made that "employers will lie to
you, recruiters will lie to you," ALL God's chillun will lie to you, so
Quick Hurry Get Your Lies in First and Beat Em' to it!!  This not only
shows contempt for the company/employer that YOU, after all, want to
work for, but it reveals a serious attitude problem - if you ARE hired
with this "US vs. THEM" attitude, you won't be there long.  Within a
month you'll be sowing suspicion and discord among the other employees
by spreading your philosophy about how THEY lie and take advantage of
the employees.

The importance of the resume itself cannot be overstated.  It should be
PERFECT.  I've seen resumes with so many misspellings and grammatical
errors, that, were I in a position to hire/not hire, most of them would
be rejected out of hand.  When you write your resume, you should
surround yourself with dictionaries and grammar books.  Then you should
have at least two people who are proficient with the English language
proofread it.  The resume, in most cases, is how a future employer forms
his first impression of you.

I guess I said all that to say this:  Don't Lie.  It's never right, it's
always wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** scott@eviews.com (Scott Ellsworth)
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7et891$mi@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <uKdQ2.5867$0m3.1351014@news1.mia>`

```
In article <uKdQ2.5867$0m3.1351014@news1.mia>, Kitty <"kcarr1 at bellsouth dot net"> wrote:
>Steve, the original poster, received a lot of invaluable advice from
>professionals who know their stuff, but those who advised him to lie are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>assumption, assume that your references WILL be checked. 

Fully agreed - never, ever lie.  My references were checked for the
job I just took.  They were asked about the companies where I
worked with them, and the projects on my resume at those companies.
Since the resume was accurate, and the references did work with me,
they could answer the questions.  Imagine the fun if I had lied.

Perhaps more important, while I have not done exactly what the new 
outfit will want done, I have done things using the languages and
technologies they use.  By representing what I know, how I work, and
who I am as honestly and accurately as possible, I have maximized
the chance that the job I have just gotten will be what I want and
within my capabilities.  I had to make that call, but by giving them
honest information, they could give me some idea about what I would
be facing and whether I was suited to it.

Scott

Scott Ellsworth          scott@eviews.com
"When a great many people are unable to find work, unemployment 
results" - Calvin Coolidge, (Stanley Walker, City Editor, p. 131 (1934))
"The barbarian is thwarted at the moat." - Scott Adams
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Matt Curtin <cmcurtin@interhack.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>`

```
>>>>> In article <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>,
    bill <wgblackmon@mindspring.com> writes:

bill> Lie, take a chance to get in, get in and work your ass off.
[...]
bill> Otherwise you'll never get a job and never get
bill> started. Employers will lie to you, recruiters will lie to you,
bill> brokers will lie to you - the industry is based on wishful
bill> thinking - everyone wants everything now for as little as
bill> possible and those who can't promise that get left in the cold,
bill> so everyone lies.
[...]

He's lying.

Honestly state your strengths and your interests.  If someone lied to
me in order to get a job, they'd be out the door as soon as it became
clear.  I have had people lie to me in interviews.  Fortunately, I
understand the subject matter well enough to be able to ask followup
questions that will find these people and remove them from
consideration immediately.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** fukspammers@fukspammers.com (fukspammers)
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<37161170.375532647@news>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu>`

```

We had one guy that had C++ written on his resume.  We ask him a
fairly tough C++ question to start and he could not answer.  So, we
kept asking him easier questions.  We told him to write the any C++
class no matter how simple.  He could not even do that.  I don't
understand why people put things on their resumes that they obviously
can't do.  Do they really think that people are going to hire a liar?

On 12 Apr 1999 10:58:43 -0400, Matt Curtin <cmcurtin@interhack.net>
wrote:

>>>>>> In article <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com>,
>    bill <wgblackmon@mindspring.com> writes:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Matt Curtin cmcurtin@interhack.net http://www.interhack.net/people/cmcurtin/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f59q3$3s4$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news>`

```
fukspammers wrote in message <37161170.375532647@news>...
>
>We had one guy that had C++ written on his resume.  We ask him a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>can't do.  Do they really think that people are going to hire a liar?

I cannot tell you how much I agree with you. The worst thing one may ever do
is to lie in a resume. There will eventually come a day when a lie will be
uncovered and then one is stuck.

Unfortunately, history is full of people who has been able to lie their way,
however I believe that for such a specific task as being programmer, this is
a very dangerous path as it is most certain that one will have to prove ones
claimed capability. It is better to be honest and possibly surprise with
additional knowledge.

In general response to the subject:

I have been interviewing a few for programming positions, and I have always
been asking technical questions. If they may not know what to answer or have
the wrong answer that's ok, given that they have not claimed knowledge to
the subject. If they had, the fall was significant in my evaluation.

The first thing I looked for in a resume, was layout and spelling. If the
resume was crowded and/or had misspellings, the score lowered. Pay attention
to use of fonts and possibly colors. Let friends evaluate your resume. This
is your first step, if you give a bad impression here, it is very difficult
to improve later. Do not mix fonts, hardly use different shapes. Keep it
professional. Pictures and colors has nothing to do on a resume. You may add
a picture of yourself though. You should however be careful doing so,
because people have very different opinions of being well dressed.

Second, do not list irrelevant items, if I am going to hire a programmer I
do not care whether he/she had a career as scout leader in his/hers youth.

Third, do not tell about your projects in detail. If you were in a Y2K
projcet tell so and nothing else. Details are to be discussed at an
interview.

Fourth, show knowledge to the position, target the subjects that were
requested, even if you do not know anything about it. If, for instance, the
position requires knowledge to bar codes and you don't know anything. See
what you may find of information about barcodes, get a brief knowledge, say
that you don't know much, but are very interested in the subject.

Fifth, call the company request general information about them, remember to
be polite and make sure they reckognize your name.

Finally, keep it short. Long resume's are annoying, at most 3 pages.

At the interview, be active, though not annoying. Ask questions about the
position technically, ask questions about the company and in particular its
products. Do NOT mention salary and equal before the interviewer brings up
the subject. Be confident, though not humble. Most important, relax,
although this is of course important, it is not the end of the world if
there eventually is a no.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** james007@imap1.asu.edu
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f8afu$ndg$1@news.asu.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f59q3$3s4$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I agree with most of what you said but a few points....

: The first thing I looked for in a resume, was layout and spelling. If the
: resume was crowded and/or had misspellings, the score lowered. Pay attention
: to use of fonts and possibly colors. Let friends evaluate your resume. This
: is your first step, if you give a bad impression here, it is very difficult
: to improve later. Do not mix fonts, hardly use different shapes. Keep it
: professional. Pictures and colors has nothing to do on a resume. You may add
: a picture of yourself though. You should however be careful doing so,
: because people have very different opinions of being well dressed.

I've been told to never put a picture om a resume because there may be 
some (even unintentional) opinions formed about you from your looks before 
even getting an interview..... never underestimate the evil of quotas....

: Second, do not list irrelevant items, if I am going to hire a programmer I
: do not care whether he/she had a career as scout leader in his/hers youth.

True, though some things may catch peoples eyes.  I was told it helped my 
getting my old internship that I had 'ROTC experience' on my resume 
because the hiring guy was ex military.  :)  And any military experience 
shows at least better-than-average leadership and discipline experience.

: Third, do not tell about your projects in detail. If you were in a Y2K
: projcet tell so and nothing else. Details are to be discussed at an
: interview.

Here I disagree.  Some people scan through thousands of resumes and if 
you have a project (don't go in boring detail) listed then it will catch 
their eye, otherwise you might get that "Thanks for trying" postcard in a 
few weeks.  Unless the project was really nothing to talk about, I WOULD 
mention what you've worked on cause that says way more than just what 
languages you know.

: Fourth, show knowledge to the position, target the subjects that were
: requested, even if you do not know anything about it. If, for instance, the
: position requires knowledge to bar codes and you don't know anything. See
: what you may find of information about barcodes, get a brief knowledge, say
: that you don't know much, but are very interested in the subject.

: Fifth, call the company request general information about them, remember to
: be polite and make sure they reckognize your name.

: Finally, keep it short. Long resume's are annoying, at most 3 pages.

No disagreement here, interviews are all about impressing people..... and 
boring them with lack of knowledge or long resumes is a bad idea.  I try 
not to go over a page and a half......

: At the interview, be active, though not annoying. Ask questions about the
: position technically, ask questions about the company and in particular its
: products. Do NOT mention salary and equal before the interviewer brings up
: the subject. Be confident, though not humble. Most important, relax,
: although this is of course important, it is not the end of the world if
: there eventually is a no.

These are all good too, though it's always easy to SAY "be relaxed".  :)  
Do seem as interested as possible in the company.... for sure.

: Cheesle

James
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f8el5$g0b$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f59q3$3s4$1@news.cerf.net> <7f8afu$ndg$1@news.asu.edu>`

```
james007@imap1.asu.edu wrote in message <7f8afu$ndg$1@news.asu.edu>...
>I've been told to never put a picture om a resume because there may be
>some (even unintentional) opinions formed about you from your looks before
>even getting an interview..... never underestimate the evil of quotas....

I do agree, I have however noticed it is becoming common, hence, I intended
to say that, ok, but I wouldn't recommend it.

>True, though some things may catch peoples eyes.  I was told it helped my
>getting my old internship that I had 'ROTC experience' on my resume
>because the hiring guy was ex military.  :)

This could be a double-edged sword, what if the hiring guy were a pacifist?

>Any military experience shows at least better-than-average leadership and
discipline experience.

Discipline alright, but leadership? No, definitely not.

>Here I disagree.  Some people scan through thousands of resumes and if
>you have a project (don't go in boring detail) listed then it will catch
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>languages you know.

By saying for example: "Y2K project for company xyz, perl programming" you
say enough. If this is interesting, the hiring guy will investigate further
otherwise it is not interesting. How would you be able to say stop once you
start describing a project? It is way more dangerous that one may write too
much than too little.

Remember there is going to be an interview, if all subjects were answered
through a resume what would you then talk about? Besides, the attention to
such descriptions hardly get the attention they deserve. I know of my own
experience that when you go through resume 52 in row, you don't want another
"book". Sorry, but that is how it is. I dont believe I am the only one
thinking like that.

>No disagreement here, interviews are all about impressing people..... and
>boring them with lack of knowledge or long resumes is a bad idea.  I try
>not to go over a page and a half......

I agree, after some thoughts I was not happy with my suggestion of 3 pages.

>These are all good too, though it's always easy to SAY "be relaxed".  :)

You're right. Unfortunately, the ability to relax is also something coming
with the experience :-) "The hen and the egg" once over :-)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** james007@imap1.asu.edu
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news>`

```
Well I took 2 months longer to get a job BECAUSE I was too honest on my 
resume.  According to one of my hiring agencies, lots of people put down 
that they know something if they've 'seen' it at a workplace before.  He 
said I was way too humble and honest on my resume.  I've 'dabbled' in 
about 10 different languages, but I would only consider myself 
'proficient' at C++ and Java so that's all I really put on my resume.... 
even at my current job I've seen 2 other people try to get hired who had 
'Java programmer' on their resumes, but then my boss sat them down and 
asked them to write a simple program that reads a text file and outputs 
to the screen each line of the file.  The first guy could do anything, 
was putting stuff like: char* String s = 'out'; and other non-sensical 
shit.  The other guy put like 3 class definitions in one file and was 
almost as bad, but at least he looked like he had seen some java code 
before.  
  So the moral is, you can lie and get interviews left and right but be 
prepared to be embarassed if they sit you down and ask you to code 
something.  Or you can wait it out and be honest like me........ I guess 
all these liars slip through the cracks and get jobs at places that don't 
check up on them because there are so many technical jobs..... sad.  The 
thing that pisses me off is knowing that the majority of programmers out 
there that make more than me, probably have half the programming ability 
I do........ but no one ever said life was fair.......

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7fa4fq$m0m$4@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>`

```
That is not honest either.  If you have dabbled in ten languages, then that
is what your resume should say. I have written huge systems in Fortran, and
would put that on my resume.  I would also note that I have not used the
language in twenty years, and it would take me at least two weeks to get
back up to speed in it.

There is a difference between selling yourself short and telling the truth.
A basic familiarity with a language is worth something.  As long as it is
not misleading, then you should include everything pertinent.
james007@imap1.asu.edu wrote in message <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>...
>Well I took 2 months longer to get a job BECAUSE I was too honest on my
>resume.  According to one of my hiring agencies, lots of people put down
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>: >Matt Curtin cmcurtin@interhack.net
http://www.interhack.net/people/cmcurtin/
>
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Phlip" <see@web.page>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Matt Curtin <cmcurtin@interhack.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<xlxvhewcgnh.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>`

```
>>>>> On 16 Apr 1999 21:22:29 GMT, james007@imap1.asu.edu said:

james007> Well I took 2 months longer to get a job BECAUSE I was too
james007> honest on my resume.  According to one of my hiring
james007> agencies, lots of people put down that they know something
james007> if they've 'seen' it at a workplace before.  He said I was
james007> way too humble and honest on my resume.  I've 'dabbled' in
james007> about 10 different languages, but I would only consider
james007> myself 'proficient' at C++ and Java so that's all I really
james007> put on my resume....  

You weren't "too honest".  You understated your experience.

In this case, it isn't honest to put only Java and C++ on your
resum�.  It would be honest to say "proficient in Java and C++; some
experience with..."

Here's what I have on my resum�, under a heading called, oddly enough, 
"languages":

  Over a dozen, including: Perl, Java, C/C++, Lisp, Pascal,
  various languages for Unix systems, data processing, text
  markup, and more.

The point with this statement isn't to list everything that I know.
Neither is it to list only things in which I consider myself expert.
The idea is to list some languages that I know which people will
recognize and to show breadth, that I can get my hands around a
language, whether it's imperative, functional, procedural, or object
oriented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scott@eviews.com (Scott Ellsworth)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00

- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <xlxbtgtkhr0.fsf@gold.cis.ohio-state.edu> <37161170.375532647@news> <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>`

```
In article <7f89ml$nc9$1@news.asu.edu>, james007@imap1.asu.edu wrote:
>Well I took 2 months longer to get a job BECAUSE I was too honest on my 
>resume.  According to one of my hiring agencies, lots of people put down 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>'proficient' at C++ and Java so that's all I really put on my resume....

You should have put something on your resume like I did at ane point -
familiar with C++, Java, Postscript, used Python, HTML, etc.  If you
know it enough to program well all day, every day, then move it to the
familiar line.  PS moved up and down, as I used it for a few months,
then let it lie for a few years.

Also, life is too short to waste.  If you put basic on a resume,
expect to be writing VB code.

Scott

Scott Ellsworth          scott@eviews.com
"When a great many people are unable to find work, unemployment 
results" - Calvin Coolidge, (Stanley Walker, City Editor, p. 131 (1934))
"The barbarian is thwarted at the moat." - Scott Adams
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7esuul$mr1$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>`

```
State and federal gov't are good places to get a start.  Under the federal
civil service system, if you meet the qualifications and score high enough on
the register, the hireing agency pretty much *has* to hire you.  Some states
pattern their own civil service in the same manner.

I got my start with a state agency with zero exprience AND zero training (my
degree was a Bachelor of Music Education and my work experience was all in
music and teaching).  The state register listed a job called "Computer
Programmer-Analyst Trainee."  Requirements were "a 4-year college degree" (no
required major).  I took a placement exam, which was a battery of
standardized aptitude and general knowledge tests.  I scored well enough to
start within the top 3 on the register. The way the system worked, agencies
looking to fill positions could only interview the top 3 candidates on the
register and had to hire from those three.  The third agency I interviewd
took me.  That was 18 years ago.

In article <K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net>,
  "Jakamo" <linus@total.net> wrote:
> Hi and thanks for reading this.
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ed Stevens
(Opinions are not necessarily those of my employer)

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

## Thread: 3rd RFD: comp.soft-sys.acucorp — 2 this month / 4 total

_Thread started [1999-01](1999-01.md#t-5ddd1d9bbc)._

##### ↳ ↳ 2nd CFV: comp.soft-sys.acucorp

- **From:** Neil Crellin <neilc@wallaby.cc>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** news.announce.newgroups,news.groups,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<923260369.12666@isc.org>`
- **References:** `<907917797.22206@isc.org> <915475698.556@isc.org> <917822140.20230@isc.org> <922401846.23510@isc.org>`

```
                      LAST CALL FOR VOTES (of 2)
               unmoderated group comp.soft-sys.acucorp

This CFV is to be distributed only by the votetaker.  It is not to be
posted to newsgroups, or mailed to mailing lists or individuals, except by
the votetaker.  Ballots or CFVs provided by anyone else will be invalid.

Newsgroups line:
comp.soft-sys.acucorp	All aspects of the use of Acucobol.

Votes must be received by 23:59:59 UTC, 15 Apr 1999.

This vote is being conducted by a neutral third party.  Questions
about the proposed group should be directed to the proponent.

Proponent: Scott Woodward <scottw@southpacfinance.com.au>
Votetaker: Neil Crellin <neilc@wallaby.cc>

RATIONALE: comp.soft-sys.acucorp

Acucobol is a company based in San Diego CA. Currently all
users and developers use email to communicate ideas and
problems to Acucobol (now known as Acucorp). These ideas
and problems however are not released to the general populous
of Acucobol users. This presents a problem for both Acucobol
and the Users.

The purpose of creating this comp.soft-sys.acucorp newsgroup
is to have a central point of contact for all users. Discussions
involving Acucobol-specific information would be greatly enhanced
by this group due to the world wide use of Acucobol.

Due to the specific nature of some of the Acucobol products, there
is a need for a separate group to discuss these topics as opposed to
just the Cobol language.

Here are some reasons in favor of a comp.soft-sys.acucorp:

Currently, most developers cannot easily come in contact with
other developers other than by attending Acucorp's yearly developer's
conference.

Acucobol contains significant extensions to the COBOL standard.
Discussion of these extensions would be of little interest to
comp.lang.cobol readers who are not Acucobol developers.

There are a number of Acucorp products which are used in conjunction
with Acucobol that do not directly involve COBOL. Because these
products tend to be used in larger, more complex systems they also tend
to cause more discussion than the base language. Such discussion would
be only marginally on topic in comp.lang.cobol.

CHARTER: comp.soft-sys.acucorp

This newsgroup is for discussion of all aspects of the use of
Acucobol.  Some examples of relevant topics are:

  Coding techniques
  Learning and teaching materials
  Discussions on coding
  User Support
  Technical Support
  User Discussions
  General Information

The primary purpose of this group is to pass information between the
users of Acucobol. Explicitly discouraged are the posting of commercial
advertisements, any kind of flaming and personal arguments, as well as
the posting of binaries of any kind.

END CHARTER.

IMPORTANT VOTING PROCEDURE NOTES: READ THIS BEFORE VOTING

The purpose of a Usenet vote is to determine the genuine interest in
reading the proposed newsgroup, and soliciting votes from uninterested
parties defeats this purpose.  Do *not* distribute this CFV;  instead,
direct people to the official CFV as posted to news.announce.newgroups.
Distributing specific voting instructions or pre-marked copies of
this CFV is considered vote fraud.

This is a public vote:  All email addresses, names and votes will be
listed in the final RESULT post.  The name used may be either a real
name or an established Usenet handle.

At most one vote is allowed per person or per account.  Duplicate
votes will be resolved in favor of the most recent valid vote.

Voters must mail their ballots directly to the votetaker.  Anonymous,
forwarded, or proxy votes are not valid, nor are votes mailed from
WWW/HTML/CGI forms (which should not exist).  Votes from nonexistent
accounts are also invalid, and the votetaker will reject any "munged"
address he cannot decipher immediately.

Please direct any questions to the votetaker at <neilc@wallaby.cc>

HOW TO VOTE:

Extract the ballot from the CFV by deleting everything before and after
the "BEGINNING OF BALLOT" and "END OF BALLOT" lines.  Don't worry about
the spacing of the columns or any quote characters (">") that your
reply inserts.  Please, DO NOT send the entire CFV back to me!

Fill in the ballot as shown below.  Please provide your REAL NAME and
indicate your desired vote in the appropriate locations inside the ballot.

Examples of how to properly indicate your vote:

  [ YES     ]  example.yes.vote
  [ NO      ]  example.no.vote
  [ ABSTAIN ]  example.abstention
  [ CANCEL  ]  example.cancellation

DO NOT modify, alter or delete any information in this ballot!
If you do, the voting software will probably reject your ballot.

When finished, MAIL the ballot to: < voting@uvv.wallaby.cc >
Just "replying" to this message should work, but check the "To:" line.

If you do not receive an acknowledgment of your vote within three
days contact the votetaker about the problem.  You are responsible
for reading your ack and making sure your vote is registered correctly.

If these instructions are unclear, please consult the Introduction to
Usenet Voting or the Usenet Voting FAQ at http://www.stanford.edu/~neilc/uvv.

======== BEGINNING OF BALLOT: Delete everything before this line =======
.-----------------------------------------------------------------------
| 2ND CALL FOR VOTES: comp.soft-sys.acucorp
| Official Usenet Voting Ballot <CSA-0002> (Do not remove this line!)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| information; ONLY your name) after the colon on the following line:

Voter name:

| Insert YES, NO, ABSTAIN, or CANCEL inside the brackets for each
| newsgroup listed below (do not delete the newsgroup name):

 Your Vote   Newsgroup
 ---------   -----------------------------------------------------------
[         ]  comp.soft-sys.acucorp

======== END OF BALLOT: Delete everything after this line ==============


comp.soft-sys.acucorp Bounce List - Please contact me about your vote
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
altnews@uniserve.com                                           Robert Broughton
Barticus@att.spam.net                                              Randall Bart
paular@ozemail.com.au                                          PAULA REBESCHINI
warrenp@ASPMnetdoor.com                                           Warren Porter

This CFV was created with uvpq 1.0 (Feb  6 1999).
PQ datestamp: 980322
```

other developers other than by attending Acucorp's yearly developer's
conference.

Acucobol contains significant extensions to the COBOL standard.
Discussion of these extensions would be of little interest to
comp.lang.cobol readers who are not Acucobol developers.

There are a number of Acucorp products which are used in conjunction
with Acucobol that do not directly involve COBOL. Because these
products tend to be used in larger, more complex systems they also tend
to cause more discussion than the base language. Such discussion would
be only marginally on topic in comp.lang.cobol.

CHARTER: comp.soft-sys.acucorp

This newsgroup is for discussion of all aspects of the use of
Acucobol.  Some examples of relevant topics are:

  Coding techniques
  Learning and teaching materials
  Discussions on coding
  User Support
  Technical Support
  User Discussions
  General Information

The primary purpose of this group is to pass information between the
users of Acucobol. Explicitly discouraged are the posting of commercial
advertisements, any kind of flaming and personal arguments, as well as
the posting of binaries of any kind.

END CHARTER.



comp.soft-sys.acucorp Final Voter list

NOTE:  This is not [to be used as] a mailing list.  The email addresses
are posted only to help verify the interest poll.  Thank you.

<a id="t-bd8bddd149"></a>
## Thread: Your Comments... — 20 msgs

### Your Comments...

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PjHN2.37$EW1.867@vic.nntp.telstra.net>`

```
OK,

I am strong advocate and promoter of the COBOL language. I have 15+ years
experience in the industry and can program in C, VB, Delphi, COBOL and many
others. I am fed up with people stating ' COBOL is dead...'. Statements like
these are only spoken by ignorant, insecure people that want to project that
they know a lot in order to impress. My belief is that each language holds
its own.

One thing I'd noticed though, in the last few years, is that I can do more
and more in COBOL than before, without having to resort in any of the other
languages. Take the case of Fujitsu's PowerCOBOL for example. I no longer
have a need to use VB as much any longer.

However. It looks like some of our "own" COBOL people want to prevent this
progression of the language (I feel). The other day, I had a discussion
(maybe an argument?) with another fellow COBOL programmer, who insisted that
certain COBOL extentions are not necessary. His view was also sahred by
others...!

Based on that, I've assembled a list of questions that anyone can contribute
to. These were the "sticky points" of our conversations.

1) OO COBOL EXTENTIONS TO THE COBOL STANDARD ARE NOT NECESSARY.
THEY WILL COMPLICATE THE LANGUAGE AND RESULT IN ITS DOOM.

2) IS THE 'EVALUATE' STATEMENT MORE EFFICIENT (FASTER) THAN THE 'IF'
STATEMENT, IN THE WAY IT GETS EXECUTED (NOT WHETHER IT IS EASIER TO CODE OR
NOT)? SOME PEOPLE THINK, IT COMPLICATES  CODING, AND IT PROVIDES NO
BENEFITS. IF THE ANSWER DEPENDS ON THE INDIVIDUAL COMPILER, HOW ABOUT IBM'S
MAINFRAME COBOL2 COMPILER.

3) WHERE I CURRENTLY CONSULT, IT SEEMS I AM THE ONLY ONE USING 'INLINE
PERFORMS'. AS FAR AS I AM CONCERNED, THEY RESULT IN BOTH MORE EFFICIENT
PROGRAMMING AND FASTER EXECUTION CODE. AM I WRONG?

4) THERE SEEMS TO BE A TENDENCY OF NOT USING SOME FANTASTIC COBOL 85
COMMANDS AND OPTIONS. FOR EXAMPLE THE 'NOT INVALID' OPTION OF THE FILE I-O
STATEMENTS. IS THERE ANYTHING WRONG WITH USING THESE? WHAT ABOUT A SIMPLE
REPORT PROGRAM(WHY USE EAZYTRIEVE?) IN A 'READ, END-READ' LOOP?

5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
INDIVIDUALLY. SHOULD ONE CODE IN SECTIONS OR PARAGRAPHS EXCLUSIVELY AND WHY?

6) 'MOVE CORRESPONDING' DOESN'T RESULT IN FASTER EXECUTABLE CODE, THAN A
GROUP OF INDIVIDUAL 'MOVE' STATEMENTS, BUT RATHER IT FACILITATES AN EASIER
WAY OF CODING (AGAIN BASED ON IBM'S COBOL2 COMPILER)

7) ARE THERE ANY BENEFITS IN HAVING A WHOLE WORKING-STORAGE SECTION CODED AS
ONE 'SINGLE' "01" LEVEL (BELIEVE ME. I'VE ACTUALLY SEEN THIS IN ONE OF THE
SITES I WORKED AT. SMALL PROGRAM BUT...).

8) LEVEL 66 AND 77 OBSOLETE.


Regards

Theo

COBOL lives...
```

#### ↳ Re: Your Comments...

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3707553F.7BC36329@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<PjHN2.37$EW1.867@vic.nntp.telstra.net>`

```
Westley Lodge Pty Ltd wrote:
> 
> OK,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> THEY WILL COMPLICATE THE LANGUAGE AND RESULT IN ITS DOOM.

I'm no fan of OOP.  But I might try out O-O extensions if they're
available in the compiler, and I can figure out how it would help me.

> 
> 2) IS THE 'EVALUATE' STATEMENT MORE EFFICIENT (FASTER) THAN THE 'IF'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> MAINFRAME COBOL2 COMPILER.

Construct two equivalent examples, one with nested IF's and one with an
EVALUATE.  Generate the disassembly (use the LIST option in IBM COBOL II
and higher).  Inspect the generated code.  I doubt there's any
particular efficiency advantage one way or the other in the generated
code.  I use EVALUATE and like it, and that's in IBM COBOL II.

> 
> 3) WHERE I CURRENTLY CONSULT, IT SEEMS I AM THE ONLY ONE USING 'INLINE
> PERFORMS'. AS FAR AS I AM CONCERNED, THEY RESULT IN BOTH MORE EFFICIENT
> PROGRAMMING AND FASTER EXECUTION CODE. AM I WRONG?

Again, if efficiency in the generated code is your criterion, run your
own tests and inspect the generated code.  In my experience, COBOL II
and higher use a very sophisticated optimizer, so code you've put in a
paragraph may end up being copied in-line where it's called.

I user in-line PERFORM loops.  New features are frequently shunned by
those people who say, "we've always done it this way in OS/VS COBOL,
anything else is probably bad".

My only recommendation is to use short in-line PERFORM's.  If the
indented code is quite complex it probably ought to be moved into a
paragraph of its own.  That may be more of a personal preference than an
absolute judgement on best practices.

> 
> 4) THERE SEEMS TO BE A TENDENCY OF NOT USING SOME FANTASTIC COBOL 85
> COMMANDS AND OPTIONS. FOR EXAMPLE THE 'NOT INVALID' OPTION OF THE FILE I-O
> STATEMENTS. IS THERE ANYTHING WRONG WITH USING THESE? WHAT ABOUT A SIMPLE
> REPORT PROGRAM(WHY USE EAZYTRIEVE?) IN A 'READ, END-READ' LOOP?

I've used this feature, NOT AT END, END-READ, and like it.  The shop
standards people may blow a fuse.  Where I work, there's a general rule
that any I-O statement must be isolated in a paragraph by itself. 
Doesn't mean it can't be a loop, or can't set an EOF switch, or count
the number of records read/written, or perform some edit in a lower
level paragraph.  

> 
> 5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
> THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
> INDIVIDUALLY. SHOULD ONE CODE IN SECTIONS OR PARAGRAPHS EXCLUSIVELY AND WHY?

This subject frequently gets a lot of heated debate here.  I'm not
convinced that calling a section is faster than calling a paragraph. 
Again, generate some assembly code and see what the compiler really
does.  The current shop standards where I work are:  paragraphs only,
and no GO TO's or perform thru exit's.  Of course, there are a lot of
older programs that have GO TO and PERFORM ... THRU EXIT.

I haven't used SECTIONs in the procedure division in years, not even
with internal sorts.  
 
> 
> 6) 'MOVE CORRESPONDING' DOESN'T RESULT IN FASTER EXECUTABLE CODE, THAN A
> GROUP OF INDIVIDUAL 'MOVE' STATEMENTS, BUT RATHER IT FACILITATES AN EASIER
> WAY OF CODING (AGAIN BASED ON IBM'S COBOL2 COMPILER)

MOVE CORRESPONDING is no help to me.  I work with shop standards that
require each data division item to have a consistent prefix throughout
the 01 record.  So the datanames cannot legally by qualified and comply
to the shop standards I work with.

Saves some typing, though.

> 
> 7) ARE THERE ANY BENEFITS IN HAVING A WHOLE WORKING-STORAGE SECTION CODED AS
> ONE 'SINGLE' "01" LEVEL (BELIEVE ME. I'VE ACTUALLY SEEN THIS IN ONE OF THE
> SITES I WORKED AT. SMALL PROGRAM BUT...).

Here's an interesting one.  I have seen compilers that re-ordered the
data division so that all 01 records with any initial data were grouped
together, and all 01 records with NO initial data were placed in a
seperate group.  If you rely on the order of 01 records when analyzing a
storage dump, this can be very frustrating.

Putting the entire working-storage section in one 01 record would solve
this problem the hard way.  I prefer to add VALUE clauses to every data
item in the working-storage section.

> 
> 8) LEVEL 66 AND 77 OBSOLETE.

I've been coding COBOL for 20 years now, and I've never used level 66,
nor have I ever worked on a production program that had a level 66.  I
don't miss level 77, and I don't believe it had any effect in the IBM
mainframe COBOL compilers I have worked with, except for requiring
elementary data items.

Based on a comment a few weeks ago, I believe some early COBOL compilers
actually used level 77 to manage storage in an unusual way.

> 
> Regards
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL lives...
```

```
Westley Lodge Pty Ltd wrote:
> 
> OK,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> its own.
I have 15 years exp. in Pascal,C,Delphi,Cobol,Clipper,Macro,SQL etc. and
I totally
agree with you. There is no perfect language at all. Actually when I
program things end-user would like to have, I see Cobol more powerfull
than
any other languages. Why ?
It runs on almost every platforms including MsDos, which is most cheaper
then
any other. I've been in position to watch our concurence stuffing PC's
with
some Visual stuff in the place of current VAX/Vms Machine. 
Each PC cost avg. $2000-$2500 to work properly with Win NT's. They have
200 PC's,
so 200x$2000=400000$, server is another $20000. 
Each change at their program written in Delphi tooks at least 2-3 days.
I don't
agree with people saying well it is almost year 2000, now is Visual
stuff trend.
They usually don't know even how a program would looks like at the end,
not to
mention how to come to some results. Thru my prg.years I made several
libraries
for several platforms including currently VAX/VMS Cobol. 
I can make a program who on example collects some data, then shows it
into a table,
sorting printing etc. in a 4 hrs. I hadnt been able to do that even in
Clipper,
I am a fast writer tho. Not to mention even data dameges, broken indexes
etc..

> 1) OO COBOL EXTENTIONS TO THE COBOL STANDARD ARE NOT NECESSARY.
> THEY WILL COMPLICATE THE LANGUAGE AND RESULT IN ITS DOOM.
Don't quite understand this.

> 2) IS THE 'EVALUATE' STATEMENT MORE EFFICIENT (FASTER) THAN THE 'IF'
> STATEMENT, IN THE WAY IT GETS EXECUTED (NOT WHETHER IT IS EASIER TO CODE OR
> NOT)? SOME PEOPLE THINK, IT COMPLICATES  CODING, AND IT PROVIDES NO
> BENEFITS. IF THE ANSWER DEPENDS ON THE INDIVIDUAL COMPILER, HOW ABOUT IBM'S
> MAINFRAME COBOL2 COMPILER.

Cobol's IF is much faster then EVALUATE on VAX, Alpha and PC platform.
It depends
on how cobol compiler is writen tho. But EVALUATE is more readble then
IF, so
at the points where if have to decide which one will be in the code, 
I use EVALUATE's at the places they execute only few times, and IF's in
several PERFORM's
where I need efficient code.

> 3) WHERE I CURRENTLY CONSULT, IT SEEMS I AM THE ONLY ONE USING 'INLINE
> PERFORMS'. AS FAR AS I AM CONCERNED, THEY RESULT IN BOTH MORE EFFICIENT
> PROGRAMMING AND FASTER EXECUTION CODE. AM I WRONG?

What is INLINE PERFORM ?

> 4) THERE SEEMS TO BE A TENDENCY OF NOT USING SOME FANTASTIC COBOL 85
> COMMANDS AND OPTIONS. FOR EXAMPLE THE 'NOT INVALID' OPTION OF THE FILE I-O
> STATEMENTS. IS THERE ANYTHING WRONG WITH USING THESE? WHAT ABOUT A SIMPLE
> REPORT PROGRAM(WHY USE EAZYTRIEVE?) IN A 'READ, END-READ' LOOP?

I never actually use AT END or INVALID form. I rather check every error
at the
run-time and then decide will program be continuing or not 
So I use declarative section
DECLARATIVES.
Start-Dec.
   USE STANDARD ERROR..... <All file handles>
End-Dec. 
END DECLARATIVES.

and then issusing READ <file> REGARDLESS
                  IF FS-Ok THEN
                  ...

> 5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
> THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
> INDIVIDUALLY. SHOULD ONE CODE IN SECTIONS OR PARAGRAPHS EXCLUSIVELY AND WHY?

Actually I didn't test that, but I think difference is more logicaly
then physical.
Compiled code would produce CALL to that routine and then RETurn. But as
I've said
didn't test it.

> 
> 6) 'MOVE CORRESPONDING' DOESN'T RESULT IN FASTER EXECUTABLE CODE, THAN A
> GROUP OF INDIVIDUAL 'MOVE' STATEMENTS, BUT RATHER IT FACILITATES AN EASIER
> WAY OF CODING (AGAIN BASED ON IBM'S COBOL2 COMPILER)

I use MOVE CORR only in stupid program's (don't get me wrong, I call
them stupid,
when they aren't part of main code, but they are more some kind of
util's).
MOVE CORR has to be very danger in cases, you are changing program after
5 years
and puting some new variables in that group. So I prefer taking control
over each
field in a group.

> 7) ARE THERE ANY BENEFITS IN HAVING A WHOLE WORKING-STORAGE SECTION CODED AS
> ONE 'SINGLE' "01" LEVEL (BELIEVE ME. I'VE ACTUALLY SEEN THIS IN ONE OF THE
> SITES I WORKED AT. SMALL PROGRAM BUT...).

At working-storage I usually do grouping, like an example

01 c-CONSTANTS.
   03 c-Current-Date PIC 9(8).
   03 c-Current-User PIC 9(4) COMP.
01 a-ACCEPTS.
   03 a-Date.
      05 a-Year	 PIC 9(4).
      05 a-Month PIC 9(2).
      05 a-Day   PIC 9(2).
   03 a-User   PIC 9(4) COMP.

> 8) LEVEL 66 AND 77 OBSOLETE.

I never had to use 66's and 77's. Just 88's
> 
> Regards
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL lives...
YEAH
```

#### ↳ Re: Your Comments...

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37078649.576315143@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<PjHN2.37$EW1.867@vic.nntp.telstra.net>`

```
On Sun, 4 Apr 1999 20:40:12 +1000, "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd"
<wlodge@hotkey.net.au> wrote:


>1) OO COBOL EXTENTIONS TO THE COBOL STANDARD ARE NOT NECESSARY.
>THEY WILL COMPLICATE THE LANGUAGE AND RESULT IN ITS DOOM.
>

Based on observations of OO systems that HAVE met their doom, I can
tell you that it's the PLANNING and the implementation of OO that is
the problem, not the platform.  

Melding an OO system into a non OO system is a big mistake - IMHO -
and virtually impossible.  So, for brand NEW development OO might be
the way to go - given a large enough project that can take advantage
of the benefits of OO.   

There will be initial prejudice against it, just like the changes in
the 1985 standard that were/are slow to be accepted.  Given the
difficulty many companies claim in finding experienced COBOL
programmers, it will be even harder to find skilled OO COBOL
programmers.  This too will lead to the lack of use of OO COBOL.

Presently, I am VERY reluctant to make use of OO COBOL -- the
different vendors all implement the proposed standard (under flux)
differently - if at all - and it's something I only want to learn
ONCE, not learn and re-learn and re-learn.  

The root question, is it NEEDED?  It will make interface to divergent
systems (COBRA - which in theory I really do like) easier.  It will
give COBOL programmers the advantages of the OO programming style.  

Disclaimer:  I'm NOT a big OO fan.  For small projects, the design
overhead is significant - if you want it to work.  I'm on the fence as
to it's economy.  Cost of development is what will make or break it.
If it's cheaper it'll thrive, if not it will die.  Even if the OO
portion of the COBOL standard is not accepted, COBOL will continue.

One last comment on the issue - COBOL has a great opportunity to get
OO right.  I've seen many OO projects DIE - and I'm not sure if it was
because they were OO, because they were in C++ (A language not best
suited for business development).  However, I do know, first hand,
that even those programming with OO, on many occasion, struggled with
the implenentation of the Theory, and often had trouble explaining
what they though they understood about OO.  These were very
intelligent people, not designing or coding their first OO system.
This scares me. 


>2) IS THE 'EVALUATE' STATEMENT MORE EFFICIENT (FASTER) THAN THE 'IF'
>STATEMENT, IN THE WAY IT GETS EXECUTED (NOT WHETHER IT IS EASIER TO CODE OR
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is just patently false.  I can give you example after example of
Code that is hard to read and understand - even when the rules are
clearly stated, that is CLEAR and EASY to understand once the Evaluate
is introduced to the code.  Check out the example in my book, Chapter
9, pp147-153 - Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.  This should
help make it clear.  

>3) WHERE I CURRENTLY CONSULT, IT SEEMS I AM THE ONLY ONE USING 'INLINE
>PERFORMS'. AS FAR AS I AM CONCERNED, THEY RESULT IN BOTH MORE EFFICIENT
>PROGRAMMING AND FASTER EXECUTION CODE. AM I WRONG?
>

No, you are right - generally.  I was in exactly your position at my
last shop.  Imagine my pleasure upon changing positions and finding
code I am to maintain that already uses these, and is much easier to
maintain.  

I find the style faster, but when I first started using it I went
overboard.  I had code with pages and pages of statements between
Perform and End-Perform, with lots of nested performs and nested IF's.
This is better coded by breaking out the large blocks of code into
paragraphs and performing them inside the in-line logic.  

When you make use of this style, the different optimizers can do a
better job and lead to faster executing programs.  

This one is almost political.  Violating even the informal standards
of a shop can lead to hate and discontent.  You need to educate, and
not force this on them.

>4) THERE SEEMS TO BE A TENDENCY OF NOT USING SOME FANTASTIC COBOL 85
>COMMANDS AND OPTIONS. FOR EXAMPLE THE 'NOT INVALID' OPTION OF THE FILE I-O
>STATEMENTS. IS THERE ANYTHING WRONG WITH USING THESE? WHAT ABOUT A SIMPLE
>REPORT PROGRAM(WHY USE EAZYTRIEVE?) IN A 'READ, END-READ' LOOP?
>

I think this is two questions in one.  

4A - it's mostly ignorance.  The don't know it's there.  Get a copy of
Don Nelsons "Cobol 85 for Programmers" and keep it on your desk.
Refer to it often, and let others borrow it.  It'll make a difference.
There is nothing wrong with using NOT INVALID and INVALID - but I have
seen some differences in what the different COBOL vendors allow you to
use within these.  Some will let you get away with more reads with
invalid/not invalid - nesting these imperitives, while others will
not.  From the way I read the standard, nesting them is not allowed. 

I tend to use them and PERFORM other things within them.

4B - I think you are asking about report writer.  On a mainframe (when
it's avaialble)  it's a fine thing.  On most PC and UNIX
implementations, it's OK.  Under Windows, if you want to use native
Windows printing, or any third party printing tools, it's next to
useless. 

>5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
>THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
>INDIVIDUALLY. SHOULD ONE CODE IN SECTIONS OR PARAGRAPHS EXCLUSIVELY AND WHY?
>

I think you mean Performing not calling.  Sections were designed to
allow memory segmentation - something not necessary today.  If writing
a new program I do not use sections.  If modifying a new one, if it
has ANY sections in the procedure division, I use a section for any
new paragraphs.  This makes sure that if a section is perfromed,  the
program will not fall through my new paragraph by accident. I also add
them at the END of the program, for this same reason - I don't want to
break a fall through that presently exists.  

Today, with a modern compiler, there is no reason to use a Section in
the procedure division.

>6) 'MOVE CORRESPONDING' DOESN'T RESULT IN FASTER EXECUTABLE CODE, THAN A
>GROUP OF INDIVIDUAL 'MOVE' STATEMENTS, BUT RATHER IT FACILITATES AN EASIER
>WAY OF CODING (AGAIN BASED ON IBM'S COBOL2 COMPILER)
>

This too is a political issue.  I used to HATE it when I saw items in
a program that had to be qualified.  I've since come around.  However,
if you are a slow typist, and you want to modify only a single item,
having to qualify it can make you angry.  I tend to use this only for
control break programs, making the code much cleaner and easier to
maintain.  I never introduce it into an existing program that does not
use it.

>7) ARE THERE ANY BENEFITS IN HAVING A WHOLE WORKING-STORAGE SECTION CODED AS
>ONE 'SINGLE' "01" LEVEL (BELIEVE ME. I'VE ACTUALLY SEEN THIS IN ONE OF THE
>SITES I WORKED AT. SMALL PROGRAM BUT...).
>

Depends on the machine.  One thing it will prevent is erroneous
redefines.  Secondly, I have seen CICS programs that used this to save
off all of WS into Temp Storage or the communications area. There can
be advantages in storage use also.  I tend to group like used items so
that I can use Initialize against the group.

>8) LEVEL 66 AND 77 OBSOLETE.

77 -- On SOME platforms there's no advantage.

66 - I have found it quite useful in Y2K remediation - using more of
it in the last year than I have in the last 16.  I would not call it
obsolete.  But I would not use it frivolously.

>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL lives...

With Gusto!
```

#### ↳ Re: Your Comments...

- **From:** Jeffrey Friedman <jfriedm@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37079A0D.D4265100@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<PjHN2.37$EW1.867@vic.nntp.telstra.net>`

```
See my responses below:

Westley Lodge Pty Ltd wrote:

> OK,
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> THEY WILL COMPLICATE THE LANGUAGE AND RESULT IN ITS DOOM.

No - if you don't like them, don't use them.  But lots of folks will eventually
jump on the OO bandwagon - and the COBOL committee has gone out of its way to
make sure it is obvious whenever OO language elements are being used.

> 2) IS THE 'EVALUATE' STATEMENT MORE EFFICIENT (FASTER) THAN THE 'IF'
> STATEMENT, IN THE WAY IT GETS EXECUTED (NOT WHETHER IT IS EASIER TO CODE OR
> NOT)? SOME PEOPLE THINK, IT COMPLICATES  CODING, AND IT PROVIDES NO
> BENEFITS. IF THE ANSWER DEPENDS ON THE INDIVIDUAL COMPILER, HOW ABOUT IBM'S
> MAINFRAME COBOL2 COMPILER.

It is probably never faster than IF's - but in a simple form EVALUATE is a much
nicer form of case statement than pages of nested IF statements.It is designed
around design table processing so allows for the more complex formats. And that
is probably why in general it does not general as efficient code as IF as it is
a much more complex statement to optimize (in general).  But for those folks who
use decision tables, the facility is there.  For the rest of us, in its simplest
form, COBOL finally got a case statement!

>
> 3) WHERE I CURRENTLY CONSULT, IT SEEMS I AM THE ONLY ONE USING 'INLINE
> PERFORMS'. AS FAR AS I AM CONCERNED, THEY RESULT IN BOTH MORE EFFICIENT
> PROGRAMMING AND FASTER EXECUTION CODE. AM I WRONG?

All of the ANSI85 structured facilities are great - but I do have to admit that
inline performs are probably the most useful.  I use them all the time.  I would
guess that in general you are probably correct:  since the code is generated
locally to the test/branch there is probably no page faults involved with its
execution - whereas old "remote" performs typically page-fault to get to the
performed routine, then page-fault when it comes back to test the condition,
etc..  If the code being executed is anything more than trivial though, I tend
to prefer the remote perform for maintainability of the program - once code
becomes nested more than 2 or 3 levels deep I find it harder to maintain.  Also,
if a given piece of code's function  can be given a name (i.e. if it
"compress-account-number") then I prefer making a paragraph so named and using
it - often it can be used from more than one place.

>
> 4) THERE SEEMS TO BE A TENDENCY OF NOT USING SOME FANTASTIC COBOL 85
> COMMANDS AND OPTIONS. FOR EXAMPLE THE 'NOT INVALID' OPTION OF THE FILE I-O
> STATEMENTS. IS THERE ANYTHING WRONG WITH USING THESE? WHAT ABOUT A SIMPLE
> REPORT PROGRAM(WHY USE EAZYTRIEVE?) IN A 'READ, END-READ' LOOP?

Nope - nothing wrong with these at all.  I use them all the time.

> 5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
> THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
> INDIVIDUALLY. SHOULD ONE CODE IN SECTIONS OR PARAGRAPHS EXCLUSIVELY AND WHY?

Everyone has a different opinion here.  My opinion is that either is ok, only
that you be consistent.  If you PERFORM section-name then always PERFORM
section-name; if you PERFORM A THRU A-EXIT then always use that structure.  When
a program has a mix of different PERFORM types, it is harder to determine where
a given routine ends.

>
> 6) 'MOVE CORRESPONDING' DOESN'T RESULT IN FASTER EXECUTABLE CODE, THAN A
> GROUP OF INDIVIDUAL 'MOVE' STATEMENTS, BUT RATHER IT FACILITATES AN EASIER
> WAY OF CODING (AGAIN BASED ON IBM'S COBOL2 COMPILER)

Yes, but.  Almost nobody understand the CORR rules well enough to maintain code
which uses ADD, SUBTRACT, or MOVE CORRESPONDING.  It often does not do what
people expect.  It is usually more important for maintainability to avoid it.
But again, I have spoken with folks who a big into data-driven design and
CORRESPONDING is one of their favorite features.  I usually avoid it myself
though - even for trivial YY/MM/DD moves, it is easy enough to replicate the 3
MOVE statements using most editors today.

>
> 7) ARE THERE ANY BENEFITS IN HAVING A WHOLE WORKING-STORAGE SECTION CODED AS
> ONE 'SINGLE' "01" LEVEL (BELIEVE ME. I'VE ACTUALLY SEEN THIS IN ONE OF THE
> SITES I WORKED AT. SMALL PROGRAM BUT...).

Implementation-specific question.  Probably not a good thing though as most
implementations have limits on 01-sizes whereas the entire Working-Storage
Section can be much larger.  Also, on machines with segmented addressing
structures (such as the 80x86 not in 32bit addressing), dealing with
multi-segmented data structures often leads to much less efficient generated
code.  You usually cannot go crazy with 01's either though - as many
implementation have a limit on the number of 01-level data items.  Grouping data
as appropriate may result in more efficient code - so putting data which is used
together in the same 01 is often a good idea.

> 8) LEVEL 66 AND 77 OBSOLETE.

I agree there is little need for 77's anymore.  And anything you can do with a
66 you can do with REDEFINES.  Not worth the incompatibilities to obsolete them
in the Standard, but they could probably be declared archaic. Espcially with the
new SAME AS proposed feature to make fields which are the same as some other
field.

>
> Regards
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL lives...

Just some of my opinions.

Jeff Friedman
```

> > 5) THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'SECTIONS' AND THERE IS A PLACE FOR 'PARAGRAPHS' IN
> > THE LANGUAGE. CALLING A SECTION IS MUCH FASTER THAN CALLING PARAGRAPHS
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> a given routine ends.

As soon as COBOL has the ability to exit paragraphs, the contingent in
favor of either of the above will go away.  It can't come soon enough.

My opinion is that NEITHER are ok.  I'd like to see a switch which
disallowed all dropping through to paragraphs.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-06T00:00:00
