#### ↳ Re: Wang COBOL on MicroFocus Workbench

- **From:** Thomas Junker <ztjunker@zwt.znet>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370F535E.DFC1C4BE@wt.net>`
- **References:** `<S17P2.13864$LX.5386629@WReNphoon3>`

```
Paul Fournier wrote:

> I am attempting to do a relatively small Y2K fix to Wang COBOL using
> MicroFocus Revolve together with either Workbench or NetExpress.  The code
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

I don't have any experience with Workbench.  I do have a Wang VS sitting
here on my desk, though, a 256-user system with Wang VS COBOL 74 on it. 
Wang also has a COBOL 85 which I have used at other sites.  As far as I
know, the largest obstacle to compiling Wang COBOL on any other platform
would likely be the USAGE IS DISPLAY-WS if the program is interactive. 
If it is not interactive and you get a funky compile error and want to
run it past me, I'll look at it to see if I can see where a Wang
extension to COBOL might be involved.

The "old-timers" at MicroFocus must be kids, or live in a cave.  Roughly
65,000 Wang VS systems were manufactured and shipped over the last 21 or
22 years.  There is still an installed base of VS systems, still
hardware and software support, and still new models being designed and
manufactured.  If you live in Colorado, your state lottery is run on
Wang VS.  If you live in Arkansas, your Dept. of Health matters are all
handled on a very nice network of very large and some small VS systems. 
If you deal with The Hartford or Equitable insurance companies, Wang VS
computers process your insurance and/or manage massive image bases that
take the place of paper document files.  If you live in Oklahoma, the
generation of your electricity depends on Wang VS computers.  Major
funds transfer applications run on Wang VS systems at banks in New York,
New Zealand, Korea, and other places.  If you have a Dell or Compaq PC,
chances are that some of the internal cables were manufactured by a
supplier whose entire manufacturing control and accounting was run on
Wang VS systems.  If you've bought passive network components anytime in
the last 10 or 15 years from the largest present distributor of such
items, your order was entered on a VS and everything from the EDI
ordering of stock to your invoice was done on a VS.  Etc.

The most recent VS was officially released by Wang Global on March 22,
1999, the first VS that tops 1,000 users and devices.  It is the
VS18000, some 60 or 70 times faster than the original 32-user VS80 of
1977.  Also announced was VS Web Server, of which I am the author.

The VS supports nine or ten languages in an integrated development
environment.  The VS supports 17-way indexed files and
rollforward/rollback recovery at the OS level.  Record compression is an
option for most files, and is handled transparently by the file system
using the VS' hardware compression instructions.  Compression is the
default for indexed files and print files.  Shared Subroutine Libraries
(SSLs) have been commonly used in the VS since the mid-1980s, the
equivalent of DLLs.  Native VS workstations run a multitasking OS of
their own that manages up to four independent workstation windows and
accepts personality downloads as well as running code you can write in a
PL/I-type language.  PC client workstations can run over LAN/WAN using
TCP/IP.

The Wang PACE database and 4GL supports Host Language Interface database
statements in COBOL and RPG.  PACE can generate default applications
containing customizable navigable screens for list, display, add,
delete, modify, list add, list modify and query involving no code
generation whatsoever.  HLI table update & retrieval/default and
viewtable retrieval exits can be generated in skeleton form at the touch
of a button, ready to compile, complete except for the PROCEDURE
DIVISION code you may wish to add to perform custom functions.

The interactive workstation extensions in Wang COBOL are quite good,
unmatched as far as I know in other COBOLs.  A single DISPLAY-WS
statement creates an entire screen definition complete with literal
text, display-only fields, modifiable fields and field attributes. 
Variable display data fields have COBOL variable items as source and/or
object, and FACs are accessible for modification by the program. 
Procedural statements read and write to the workstation with full
control over individual field attributes and cursor position.  It's a
major headache to translate that kind of comprehensive workstation
interoperability from Wang COBOL to IBM mainframe COBOL or the little
machine COBOLs.  A lot of function usually has to be discarded along the
way.

The VS and VS COBOL are very much alive.  What's unusual is to hear of
VS COBOL programs being modified on a PC.  There's rarely any need to do
that.

Regards,

Thomas Junker
ztjunker@zphoenix.znet

(remove the z's)

http://www.phoenix.net/~tjunker/wang.html
The Unofficial Wang VS Information Center
```

SET FHREDIR =C :\ MY DIRECTORY \ MY ARQUIVO CFG

/cm cciipx32

### Cobol calling C

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ej1am$uak$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have a program written in Borland C. I wish to call this from a COBOL
program written in Microfocus Netexpress. Where do I start. Do I create a dll
with the c program and link this to the cobol program in a new project or
what?

Can some one please tell me how I go about this starting from the start with
just the two files cobolprogram.cbl and cprogram.cpp.

Please help as this is a crisis!!
Cheers,
Dec.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol calling C

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <ked@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370DDA77.2583EC7B@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7ej1am$uak$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
9416617@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> I have a program written in Borland C. I wish to call this from a COBOL
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> just the two files cobolprogram.cbl and cprogram.cpp.

It depends on what you want the architecture of your deliverables to
be, but here are three basic ways:

1) Link the C and COBOL objects together.
   Create a .obj from the .cpp file and include the .obj in your
   COBOL project. Your project must obviously create a .EXE or .DLL
   for this to work. 

2) Dynamically load the C DLL.
   Create a DLL from the C code and load it in COBOL using:
   01 pptr USAGE PROCEDURE-POINTER.
   PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        SET pptr TO ENTRY "DLLNAME.DLL"

   The entry points in the DLL should now be visible to your COBOL
   code. This will work from .INT and .GNT code also.

3) Import the C DLL.
   Create a DLL from the C code and link the COBOL project with the
   import library for that DLL (the same way as option 1).
   You may need to compile your COBOL code with the LITLINK option
   (or use the equivalent call convention on those CALLs).
   
This will work for Microsoft VC++ - so unless there are any Borland
specific issues, you should be OK. Note that the default NetExpress
COBOL calling convention is equivalent to Microsoft's __cdecl (the
default for C, also).

Cheers, Kev.
```

Dear Mr. Brazee,
Dear COBOL people,

The way usenet is set up, it is very difficult to prevent this kind of
thing.

The person using the address yeti_smacka2@my-dejanews.com has been
harrassing a friend of mine, who is a very fine COBOL programmer, and
uses the COBOL newsgroups in the course of her work.

What needs to be done to restrain this yeti_smack2 is already in
process. 

I'd like to extend my personal apologies, in my character as the
founder of the alt.magick newsgroup, for any inconvenience or
annoyance that this might have caused you.

Best Wishes,

Josh Geller, UD

## Thread: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** "rh" <husonr@netwave.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```
I am not sure if this problem is a Fujitsu COBOL problem, a Visual Basic
problem, or a Windows NT problem,  it is however a PROBLEM.

I have installed on my machine Visual Basic Enterprise 5.0 as well as
Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 enterprise version.  I purchased Fujitsu COBOL because it
advertised the ability to work well with Visual Basic.

I am writing an application that uses Visual Basic for all main parts of the
application, which calls some DLL's (with linkage sections) that I have
created using Fujitsu COBOL Project Manager.  This application is to be used
on computers running Windows NT.  The application works perfectly on my
Windows NT machine.  I am trying to distribute this application to others.
I have used the Visual Basic Application Setup Wizard to create distribution
diskettes.  I have made sure that the setup includes all of the DLL files
and I am loading them into whatever directory the application is being
loaded into.  I can load up from the setup diskettes on my production
computer and everything works fine, however when I load the setup diskettes
onto a clean computer (without Visual Basic or Fujitsu COBOL) on it the
application does not run properly.  All aspects of the Visual Basic part of
the application run perfectly, but when the application tries to run any of
the COBOL created DLL's I get a Visual Basic runtime error saying that file
not found for whichever Fujitsu COBOL DLL is being called.

My question is do these DLL's have to be entered into the registry for the
application to know where to find them?  If this is the case is there a way
in the Fujitsu COBOL compiling, linking process that I can make the DLL self
registering?  If not any good ideas how to get the DLL's registered via
distributed setup diskettes? Any and all information/help would be
appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370e8fa1.1037528025@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```
On Fri, 9 Apr 1999 13:07:34 -0500, "rh" <husonr@netwave.net> wrote:


>My question is do these DLL's have to be entered into the registry for the
>application to know where to find them?  If this is the case is there a way
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>appreciated.

Fujitsu compiled systems require the DLL files you created AND the
DLL's from the Fujitsu runtime.  

If you are getting or have received the 4.2 upgrade there is something
very exciting in it - the COBOL Wrapper.  You can use this to properly
install the necessary (and nothing else) runtime and your custom
application.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elq8t$g9$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```

rh wrote in message <923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>...

>My question is do these DLL's have to be entered into the registry for the
>application to know where to find them?  If this is the case is there a way
>in the Fujitsu COBOL compiling, linking process that I can make the DLL
self
>registering?  If not any good ideas how to get the DLL's registered via
>distributed setup diskettes? Any and all information/help would be
>appreciated.

I do not know Fujitsu Cobol in particular, but I presume that they do use
generic Windows API to load the DLL's. In that case you should just follow
standard Windows rules. Which means that storing them into the current
directory should do the trick. In general terms I see no reason why you
should deal with the registry when it comes to DLL's. Though it may be that
Fujitsu does something weird.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<923785373.403259@woody.hotkey.net.au>`
- **References:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```
VB5 includes its own runtime support. Fujitsu needs its own. Look in the
manual for the runtime support DLL's and put them in the applications dir
(or provide a PATH to them).

I used it in many such situations and I never had a problem.

Regards

Theo Charalambous
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fm1ml$mb2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```

rh wrote in message <923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>...

>My question is do these DLL's have to be entered into the registry for the
>application to know where to find them?  If this is the case is there a way
>in the Fujitsu COBOL compiling, linking process that I can make the DLL
self
>registering?  If not any good ideas how to get the DLL's registered via
>distributed setup diskettes? Any and all information/help would be
>appreciated.

You can do it in the COBOL.CBR setup file, like so:


[WEIGH IN]
@CBR_SCR_KEYDEFFILE=\ADSN.PROGRAMS\COBOL.KBD
@NoMessage=YES
@EnvSetWindow=UNUSE
@ScrnFont=(Terminal,20)
@ScrnSize=(54,7)
@WinCloseMsg=OFF

[WEIGH IN.ENTRY]
SioBaud=WSC32.DLL
SioDTR=WSC32.DLL
SioGetc=WSC32.DLL
SioParms=WSC32.DLL
SioReset=WSC32.DLL
SioRTS=WSC32.DLL
SioDone=WSC32.DLL

The program name "WEIGH IN" is contained in quotes in the program-id.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Basic 5.0 calling Fujitsu COBOL 4.0 created DLL's

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fm1pl$mk7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>`

```

rh wrote in message <923681151.543.81@news.remarQ.com>...

P.S. to that last ... you can also set the path or put them in the same
directory and it will also work.
```

## Thread: HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR — 18 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR

- **From:** jmatiles@yahoo.com (jojo atlies)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c5pP2.16176$LX.6103782@WReNphoon3>`

```
I am a college student in need of help with a program using the cobol
compiler. Below is the output for the program.


                       ***MENU***

                   A. Addition
                   B. Subtraction
                   C. Multiplication
                   D. Division

                  CHOOSE ONE OPERATION:

                  ENTER FIRST NO.:
                  ENTER SECOND NO.:

                  THE ANSWER IS:

After choosing a letter the program must be able to compute after entering
the two nos.
Will appreciate any info. on how to make this program run. Thanx
You could also E-mail me at jmatiles@yahoo.com or at jmatiles@excite.com




   -**** Posted from RemarQ, http://www.remarq.com/?a ****-
 Search and Read Usenet Discussions in your Browser - FREE -
```

#### ↳ Re: HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370e8cb5.1036780520@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<c5pP2.16176$LX.6103782@WReNphoon3>`

```
On Fri, 09 Apr 1999 06:31:00 -0800, jmatiles@yahoo.com (jojo atlies)
wrote:

>I am a college student in need of help with a program using the cobol
>compiler. Below is the output for the program.

Want to really impress the instructor?  Get my book, Sams Teach
Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours and show him how to parse the formula
entered into a program and perform the associated operation.

Be sure to give credit to the book when you show him, and don't make
it the solution to your homework assignment!
```

#### ↳ Re: HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990409125733.04133.00000886@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c5pP2.16176$LX.6103782@WReNphoon3>`

```
perform initialize-para
perform display-menu-para
accept menu-opt
accept first_no
accept second_no
perform validate-para
if true-numbers
    case menu-opt
         when "A"
             compute third_no = first_no +                                     
                                                            second_no
        when "B"
              compute third_no = first_no -                                    
                                                                          
                                                  second_no
        when "C"
             etc
       end-case
        display third_no
else
   display error-msg
end-if
goback


When working with division make sure second_no <> zeroes

TW
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>

in the first place, and wrong by people that readily supply the programs. We
should encourage people to code, but not to copy.

If anybody is so interested or willing he can do my lawn once a month. (How
willing are you...?).

Regards

Theo Charalambous
```

Agree, but not to the extend to do someones homework. What kind of evolution

nothing but copying existing work, there will be no progress, no capability
of individual work.

Copiers are the simplest of kind, those who are bringing the world forward
they inherit knowledge, evaluates it and bring up new thoughts.

accomplish the task, it would have been wiser to give him a code fragment

solution.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HOW TO PROGRAM A CALCULATOR

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371750d6.9849180@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<923785876.798481@woody.hotkey.net.au> <19990411003516.02137.00001328@ng-fr1.aol.com> <3710c291@news3.us.ibm.net> <7et4cq$opf$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no> wrote:

>Agree, but not to the extend to do someones homework. What kind of evolution
>would that bring. If those who are to bring further what we have done knows
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Cheesle

I lurk all the time in comp.lang.cobol.  The rule of thumb in
assisting a "homework" sounding question is for the poster explain
what he/she knows already and post a small code sample showing that
explaination and the problem.  At least the poster is showing he/she
is trying to learn the material but just got "stuck."  

We all had "brain drains" every now and again and need a new angle to
the material.  If the "student" can't even begin on the problem, give
a brief description of how we professionals would approach it then ask
the "student" to provide a sample code showing how far he/she took the
advice.  Then focus on the solving the immediate situation to help
"unstuck" the student.  A kind word now and again goes a long way.

Just my two cents worth adjusted for inflation,

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.

 .--------------------------------------------------------------------.
 | "People should have two virtues:  purpose- the courage to envisage |
 | and pursue valued goals uninhibited by the defeat of infantile     |
 | fantasies, by guilt and the failing fear punishment;  and wisdom- a|
 | detached concern with life itself, in the face of death itself."   |
 |                                                     Norman F. Dixon|
 '--------------------------------------------------------------------'
```

You will find people willing to help you.  But you haven't yet asked for
help.  Do you have code which you can't compile?  If so, tell us what
compiler you're using.  If you haven't any code, and expect us to do
your homework for you, you won't have any sympathy from us.  If you have
errors you can't understand, post the relevant code and error messages
and you should find us helpful.

Actually, few students go to the effort of typing in the full assignment
before asking us to do their homework for them.  You are to be commended,
but probably not helped until you at least make an effort.  Then, if you
write the program and cannot get it work, type *that* in.  You will get lots
of help.

<a id="t-e1884a6c8e"></a>
## Thread: RM/COBOL Reference Manual — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### RM/COBOL Reference Manual

- **From:** "Angelo Scarnata" <egosc@tin.it>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elfvi$obj$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Dear friends,
I have found this newsgroup and I'd like to know if
someone knows where I can find the RM/COBOL 85
Reference Manual in electronic format or if someone
has already it. I'm an Italian student of computer
engineering and it would be very useful for my job.
Thanks a million to whoever knows the answer...

Regards,
Angelo Scarnata
scarnata@tin.it
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL Reference Manual

- **From:** gary_tatsch@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f2baa$bll$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfvi$obj$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
Angelo,

You are going to have to contact Liant Software for this, their website is:
www.liant.com.

The RM/COBOL-85 Reference Manual states: "No part of this publication may be
reporduced, stored in a retrieval system, or transmitted, in any form or by
any means, electronic, mechanical, photocopied, recorded, or otherwise
without the prior written permission of Liant Software Corporation."

So to obtain a a Reference Manual by any other means would be illegal.

-Gary

<a id="t-c3e5a88d63"></a>
## Thread: Sections vs Paragraphs — 43 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I have watched the different views of your all regarding use of sections in
cobol.

It has been very interesting reading, and I am somewhat surprised that it
appears most do not want them, although apparently depending of which
country one belong to.

However, noone seems to vote yes to the use of sections due to improve the
readability of the program.

There was one gentleman briefly mentioning it, although he preferred to call
sub programs rather than breaking down a single program in sections.

I myself, being programming cobol and pascal for several years and later
delhpi and C prefer the breakdown into sections. I do this because I may
then easily look back to old code and, in most cases, I know what it does,
where it starts and where it ends in no time. If using paragraphs, this
could be a tedious task.

This view of mine, may of course rely on the fact that I learned pascal
before cobol, and as such were well into "sectioning" my programs. Although
paragraphs has its benefits, I feel that this option is of more bad than
good

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elu7k$4n8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote in message

>I myself, being programming cobol and pascal for several years and later
>delhpi and C prefer the breakdown into sections. I do this because I may
>then easily look back to old code and, in most cases, I know what it does,
>where it starts and where it ends in no time. If using paragraphs, this
>could be a tedious task.


Yes, but then the way I code, every routine starts with a paragraph name,
and ends on the next paragraph name.  Always.  No exceptions. In the
extremely rare case that I would use a GO TO, then there is only one place
it *can* go.  That is to the head of the paragraph.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7et6j6$pi2$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <7elu7k$4n8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7elu7k$4n8$1@news.igs.net>...
>Yes, but then the way I code, every routine starts with a paragraph name,
>and ends on the next paragraph name.  Always.  No exceptions. In the
>extremely rare case that I would use a GO TO, then there is only one place
>it *can* go.  That is to the head of the paragraph.

Well, as I have said in other posts here. This strategy is excellent as long
as one know and control the code, if you were to look into other code, or
others that did not know your set of rules where to look into yours, there
could not be such an assumption. At least not in the beginning.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 
> I have watched the different views of your all regarding use of sections in
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks,

Probably because it's just as easy to read a paragraph as it is to read
a section.  In a structured, program without dropping through, there is
no difference in readability between paragraphs and sections.  The only
people advocating dropping through from one paragraph to another are
those who have the second paragraph being only an EXIT paragraph.

The only advantage of SECTIONS I have seen (in modern programming), is
if your environment mandates one SECTION.  This occurs in copies, IDMS
copies, and in older compilers SORTs.  This is a valid advantage.

If you don't drop between paragraphs, and you don't drop between
sections, how is one more readable than the other?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote in message
>The only advantage of SECTIONS I have seen (in modern programming), is
>if your environment mandates one SECTION.  This occurs in copies, IDMS
>copies, and in older compilers SORTs.  This is a valid advantage.

Well. I tend to break down in sub routines to keep each section as small as
possible, thus readable and preferably reusable. Like this:

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
MAIN SECTION.
    PERFORM INIT-PROGRAM.
    PERFORM CORE-FUNCTIONS.
    PERFORM CLEAN-UP.
MAIN-EXIT.
    EXIT.

INIT-PROGRAM SECTION.
INIT-PROGRAM-001.
    PERFORM INIT-VARIABLES.
    PERFORM SETUP-SCREEN.
    PERFORM OPEN-FILES.
INIT-PROGRAM-900.
INIT-PROGRAM-EXIT.
    EXIT.

This nesting, although implying a lot more writing, thus inefficient. Makes
the program extremely well readable. It is my opinion that this is a pre
when your program is becoming more than a 100 lines. I have made programs of
2000+ lines using this structure, and it is always easy to look back into
it.

>If you don't drop between paragraphs, and you don't drop between
>sections, how is one more readable than the other?

I agree with you here, I do however find it too easy to be tempted to do
things like:
    PERFORM paragraph THRU paragraph
and
    paragraph-1
        do some stuff
        IF something
            GO TO paragraph-2

In both cases you will not have a strictly limited scope which is easily
read.
```

I guess that section gives a new 64K area, paragraph stays in the same
64K area.
In DOS environment section is used to deal with procedure codes > 64K.
MF COBOL V3 requires sorting section not sorting paragraph.

I don't know whether there is any difference in 98/NT environment...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elpck$5r$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I do the exact same thing...  Without sections and exits.  I don't see
>how adding the word SECTION makes it more readable.

You are right in that there is no significant differnce so far, however, the
final EXIT statement inevitably terminate it and, at the same time
documenting it. On the other side, one is free to implement a GO TO jump
into another section's paragraph's, so...

I do however prefer it, possibly because of the demand of a body structure
one have in pascal (BEGIN END) and C ({ }). I have never really understood
why it is not demanded in COBOL.

When first comparing to pascal and C, another two features I have been
missing in cobol is local variables (local to sections) and that sections
had the oportunity to be passed parameters. I know one may accomplish this
using separate programs, I do however feel that this would not be the same.
Sort of taste I suppose.

>I've done too much maintenance to be tempted to do that.

You're right, however, rookies may not think that way, with disastrous
results without guidance :-)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370E7842.F9905B75@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elpck$5r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:

> I do however prefer it, possibly because of the demand of a body structure
> one have in pascal (BEGIN END) and C ({ }). I have never really understood
> why it is not demanded in COBOL.

That's why I would like some way to enforce the standard that every
paragraph structure was terminated by the end of a paragraph.  No drop
throughs.  Then you know where the terminator is.
 
> When first comparing to pascal and C, another two features I have been
> missing in cobol is local variables (local to sections) and that sections
> had the oportunity to be passed parameters. I know one may accomplish this
> using separate programs, I do however feel that this would not be the same.
> Sort of taste I suppose.

Ahhh, that makes sense, you're thinking in C.  At one time mainframe
COBOL programmers had to worry about memory segmentation.  Now very few
COBOL programmers need to think that way.  And we don't think of
paragraphs or sections as being computer functions the way C programmers
do.  It's a subtle distinction.  Now someone brought up on OO would
probably think sub programs more than you or I would.
```

- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elpck$5r$1@news.cerf.net> <370E7842.F9905B75@NOSPAMhome.com> <7et50j$p08$1@news.cerf.net>`

> Probably. I am greatful to have had the oportunity t learn the background
> for SECTIONs in this newsgroup. I do however still wonder why there is such
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle

I can't see why anybody would reject more readable code either.  But
adding the word SECTION to the end of a paragraph name doesn't make that
paragraph/section more readable.

up, when they add a new paragraph to the end of the program, not
noticing that the previously performed paragraph is really a SECTION.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O1vP2.1580$qx1.43881@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elpck$5r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
>documenting it. On the other side, one is free to implement a GO TO jump
>into another section's paragraph's, so...


You gave yourself away here. GO TO statements are not used nor needed in
structured programming. You obviously are allowing for an unstructured
structure to exist in your programs.

You can get the same readability with paragraphs:
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN.
    PERFORM INIT-PROGRAM.
    PERFORM CORE-FUNCTIONS.
    PERFORM CLEAN-UP.
    GOBACK
    STOP RUN
0000-MAIN-EXIT.
    EXIT.

INIT-PROGRAM.
    PERFORM INIT-VARIABLES.
    PERFORM SETUP-SCREEN.
    PERFORM OPEN-FILES.
INIT-PROGRAM-EXIT.
    EXIT.


Notice the STOP RUN and GOBACK they are specified in most COBOL programs as
the actual program terminator. Only one of these is necessary. GOBACK is
usually safer than STOP RUN although less descriptive of its function.

The EXIT paragraphs are not needed unless you want them for readability.

What I was told in school was that SECTIONS were necessary for exception
processing. I have only used it for this in school, never in the field.

Don't allow for GO TO's and they won't be used and you'll save yourself some
headaches in the long run.
```

I would not say that I gave myself away, as a matter of fact I feel that the

>usually safer than STOP RUN although less descriptive of its function.

My apology if I had used both, I do know these basics, and if they both

>

>

Exactly, for readability.

I have never used them due to such limitations. Of what others have stated,

some
>headaches in the long run.

That is correct, however, what you are saying and what others have stated

maintaining code you know. What if you were set to examine my code? I bet a
sectioned code would be easier to understand than paragraphed code.

Cheesle

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37122F9D.B31D4BE7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elpck$5r$1@news.cerf.net> <O1vP2.1580$qx1.43881@storm.twcol.com> <7et5d9$p5j$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> That is correct, however, what you are saying and what others have stated
> heavily relies upon the fact that you consider a world where you are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cheesle

Why?  

I see two options:

1.  Your code has logic dropping from paragraph to paragraph.

2.  Your code performs sections without dropping from paragraph to
paragraph.

In case 1, you're bypassing the advantages of structured programming.
In case 2, you can save typing by removing the word SECTION, and
othewise readability has not changed.

In either case, adding a new paragraph can kill the program.  Be kind to
maintenance programmers - your name is on the program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dskirk@usa.net (david )
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3718a96e.4406540@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Fri, 09 Apr 1999 13:11:05 -0600, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>Cheesle wrote:
>> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've done too much maintenance to be tempted to do that.

if i'm reading the inserts correctly here, i agree with brazee.
sections offer no improvements over paragraph names and any use of
PERFORM THRU is automatically error-prone (uh-oh... do i hear
incoming?...<g>)

to me, the major issue is one of design. i still see many programmers
designing while writing the code.  in my opinion, this is where most
of our problems begin. when programmers speak of a need to make a
module 'readable' i instinctively know the design is poor. Otherwise,
the code would be intrinsicly readable.  with good design, i believe
each paragraph should automatically exit back to a higher level. where
a paragraph falls through to another paragraph, it is indication that
paragraphs do not represent a design. use of EXIT for any reason seems
inappropriate, because 1) it is never needed and 2) it can create a
false sense that it is being executed... and often it isn't.  

just my thoughts... (I know we'll never reach consensus here...<g>)
david

david              dskirk@usa.net
_________________________________
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3710BE60.46E59E8A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <3718a96e.4406540@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
david wrote:
> 
> to me, the major issue is one of design. i still see many programmers
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> each paragraph should automatically exit back to a higher level.

This was something that was worrying me about the parser project. 
I did not start with a pretty diagram and then proceed to work
solidly though the code.  Instead I created a basic test rig type
program and then broke it up.  This same thing has happened to the
working storage parser.  I sat down with code and made it work.

There is a requirement to actually 'just code the bloody thing'
sometimes.  The details of the idea are stored in the mind of a
programmer who will break down the problem into smaller parts. 
These small parts sometimes become significantly large in which
case it is broken down again (or restructured).  This is a very
special case of code seeking the correct answer.  The design is
firmly in my mind's eye even if it is not on paper.

The parser project is not typical of my work because there was no
business requirements analysis, followed by a functional
specification, followed by actual code.  In most cases I can sit
down with these and churn exactly what is documented without any
hesitation.  I suspect that we have all had those gotcha's that
require us to take an entirely different tack when coding the
actual result though.

I suppose what I am saying is that generalizations about design
from the coding reorganizations is probably of limited
application.

Regarding the undercurrent of your statement, the code will
naturally be more readable because the programmer thinks about
it.  The answer has to be yes.  Long names for the perform make
the comments redundant, etc.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7et5lm$p6h$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <3718a96e.4406540@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
david wrote in message <3718a96e.4406540@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>On Fri, 09 Apr 1999 13:11:05 -0600, Howard Brazee
><brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>incoming?...<g>)

Seen from the perspective of the calling statement, you are absolutely
right. However, if you are at the called part of code. Paragraphs loose in
terms of readability to sections, as a section with an terminating EXIT tell
you loud and clear that this is the end, while you would have to go back to
the calling source to see if an paragraph ends or not (PERFORM mmm THRU nnn
or PERFORM mmm)

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37122CD7.956A0595@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370E3B28.836B3183@NOSPAMhome.com> <7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <370E50C9.FF86AE45@NOSPAMhome.com> <3718a96e.4406540@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7et5lm$p6h$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> Seen from the perspective of the calling statement, you are absolutely
> right. However, if you are at the called part of code. Paragraphs loose in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> or PERFORM mmm)

A section with a terminating EXIT tells you that the programmer intended
it to be the end.  

Of course a section without a terminating EXIT says the exact same
thing.

And a paragraph in a shop with standards which require no drop throughs
tells you the same thing.  I prefer this technique myself.

I have seen people cut and paste the wrong GO TO 0100-EXIT.  I have seen
programmers stick in new paragraphs within performed sections and
perform-thru's, not expecting drop throughs.

If your section has multiple paragraphs, it allows drop throughs. 
That's what I object to.  Or if it doesn't allow drop throughs, it is
the same thing as a paragraph except that a maintenance programmer can
add a paragraph at the end of the program, not expecting it to be
reached any way except by performing that paragraph - but it gets
reached by performing the section above it, dropping through to the new
paragraph.

The maintenance programmer can see the terminating exit paragraph above
it.   They are common in shops which perform PARAGRAPH-1 THRU
PARAGRAPH-1-EXIT.   Or he may forget to type in the word SECTION. 
Either way, there's an unintentional drop through.

No, SECTIONS may be necessary for memory management, but they obfuscate
maintenance.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
UGH...get rid of the GOTO's.  The truly sad thing about using sections is that
you are forced to use GOTO's to break out of a Section.  Currently I use
Sections only for sorts.  Say it slowly..."Paragraphs are our friends..."

I learned Pascal also and the only difference I see between sections in Pascal
and paragraphs in COBOL is that in PASCAL you are required to order the
sections. THat is you must put the called sections before the section that
calls it.  Why is that?

TW
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371008C3.7566@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>`

```
Twymer wrote:
> 
> UGH...get rid of the GOTO's.  The truly sad thing about using sections is that
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> calls it.  Why is that?

It's so that when passing variables in procedure/function calls the compiler knows
what kind of variable(s) you are passing.  Unlike COBOL, Pascal (and C) can pass
an actual value rather than a reference (address) to the variable.  When you say
something like "do_this(field_1, field_2)" it may be that field_1 is passed by
reference (and can be changed) while field_2 is passed by value and cannot be
changed.  The actual procedure would be declared like 
"procedure do_this(var a_var_1:integer, a_var_2:integer);"
The specification of "var" in front of the first variable says that it is to be
passed by reference (in other words, the address rather than the value of the
variable is pushed on to the stack).

If you want to call a procedure or function that appears later on in the program
you can use a "forward declaration", such as:

procedure do_this(var a_var_1:integer, a_var_2:integer) forward;
(I'm not sure if that's exactly correct, but close enough.)

Once this is done you can call it from anywhere below that declaration, but the
procedure itself can be lower in the program.

Now, take into account it's been over ten years since I've done any Pascal
programming, so I hope I didn't screw anything up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MWVP2.1951$qx1.52683@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com> <371008C3.7566@sprynet.com>`

```
>procedure do_this(var a_var_1:integer, a_var_2:integer) forward;
>(I'm not sure if that's exactly correct, but close enough.)


The use of variables and their reference type has absolutely 100% nothing to
do with their definition in the source code.

On of the previous posts states the single pass orientation of some of the
earlier PASCAL compilers. This is both true and incomplete. In reference to
what I was taught in PASCAL, it was also to force top-down structured
programming. Comparing any language which has PROCEDURES, not SECTIONS, and
local variables to COBOL is irrelevant. Languages containing procedures
which have local variables have no reference point to COBOL. The closest you
could come would be to code multiple COBOL programs within the same source
code file. Using the appropriate END-PROGRAM to designate the barrier
between one program and the next. I have done this, to prove it can be done,
but I have never actually seen it used. In such instances you could use a BY
REFERENCE or BY VALUE in the call statement to simulate the VAR modifier in
PASCAL.

PASCAL is PASCAL and COBOL is COBOL. Substituting coding styles between the
two for such different languages is just the work of a MADMAN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7erkrs$pja$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com> <371008C3.7566@sprynet.com> <MWVP2.1951$qx1.52683@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
[...]
>... Comparing any language which has PROCEDURES, not SECTIONS, and
>local variables to COBOL is irrelevant. Languages containing procedures
>which have local variables have no reference point to COBOL.

In COBOL, nested sub programs are procedures with local variables.

> The closest you
>could come would be to code multiple COBOL programs within the same source
>code file. Using the appropriate END-PROGRAM to designate the barrier
>between one program and the next.

This is close to nested programs. The position of the END PROGRAM
statement (relative to other programs) determines whether multiple
programs or a nested program is being defined; although any source
file may contain both.

> I have done this, to prove it can be done,
>but I have never actually seen it used.

Based on prior discussions, in this news group, it appears that
nested programs are not popular. I have done both nested programs
and multiple programs within the same source file. Each may be useful
under some conditions.

See the article "Remodularation of COBOL programs through Scope
and Data Flow Analysis" by Tony Cahill, University of Limerick.
< http://www.csis.ul.ie/RsrchPubs/Remodcob.htm >
Section 6 covers nested programs as a step in converting un-modular
programs.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ep2p9$l74$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>`

```

Twymer wrote in message <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>...
>UGH...get rid of the GOTO's.  The truly sad thing about using sections is
that
>you are forced to use GOTO's to break out of a Section.  Currently I use
>Sections only for sorts.  Say it slowly..."Paragraphs are our friends..."

When using sections, GO TO is not required in many implementations.
Use EXIT SECTION as the last statement in the first paragraph of each
section. Use the first paragraph to drive the rest of the section. EXIT
SECTION will be in the next standard.

>I learned Pascal also and the only difference I see between sections in
Pascal
>and paragraphs in COBOL is that in PASCAL you are required to order the
>sections. THat is you must put the called sections before the section that
>calls it.  Why is that?

It provided a means for validating the interface for procedures
and functions; but did not allow for separate compilation. The
sucessor languages, Modula and Oberon, allow for separate
compilation by providing a means for declaring the external
interfaces.

An interface validation mechanism for COBOL is in the next
standard -- the REPOSITORY paragraph.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>

news:19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com...
> I learned Pascal also and the only difference I see between sections in
Pascal
> and paragraphs in COBOL is that in PASCAL you are required to order the
> sections. THat is you must put the called sections before the section that
> calls it.  Why is that?

check forwards references.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7et61q$par$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7elfe1$qav$1@news.cerf.net> <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>`

```
Twymer wrote in message <19990410141757.06795.00001085@ng04.aol.com>...
>UGH...get rid of the GOTO's.  The truly sad thing about using sections is
that
>you are forced to use GOTO's to break out of a Section.  Currently I use
>Sections only for sorts.  Say it slowly..."Paragraphs are our friends..."

You do not need to use GOTO's to break a section. On the other side, (I may
ignite a fire now :-) ) If properly used, GOTO's are ok. After all, when it
all is broken down to machine language, there is plenty of GOTO's.

>I learned Pascal also and the only difference I see between sections in
Pascal
>and paragraphs in COBOL is that in PASCAL you are required to order the
>sections. THat is you must put the called sections before the section that
>calls it.  Why is that?

Not quite true, one may do what is called a forward declaration of a
procedure/function. On the other hand, this is just a question of design. If
a program is properly planned and designed, this should not be a matter of
concern.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elg87$k7u@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
If you are looking at using SECTIONS to "encapsulate" portions of your code
(for readability and/or maintainability) you *might* want to look at nested
programs - which were introduced in the '85 Standard. Personally, I don't
use them much - but I think they would really interest those who are used to
using SECTIONS for putting "logic" where it belongs.

Note: With some compilers nested programs have severe performance problems -
with others they do not.  You would want to check this out before changing
your own programming paradigm
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elpjl$ah$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <7elg87$k7u@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7elg87$k7u@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>If you are looking at using SECTIONS to "encapsulate" portions of your code
>(for readability and/or maintainability) you *might* want to look at nested
>programs - which were introduced in the '85 Standard. Personally, I don't
>use them much - but I think they would really interest those who are used
to
>using SECTIONS for putting "logic" where it belongs.

As I mentioned in my other message here, this is absolutely an option, in
particular to get the features of parameter passing and local variables. The
downside of this however being that one may get a huge number of files.
Thus, rapidly causing an administration issue of it.

>Note: With some compilers nested programs have severe performance
problems -
>with others they do not.  You would want to check this out before changing
>your own programming paradigm

Performance is one issue, another is that file/record locked conditions may
raise unless one know what one is doing.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7emeuo$hub@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <7elg87$k7u@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7elpjl$ah$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote in message <7elpjl$ah$1@news.cerf.net>...
>
>William M. Klein wrote in message <7elg87$k7u@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>>If you are looking at using SECTIONS to "encapsulate" portions of your
code
>>(for readability and/or maintainability) you *might* want to look at
nested
>>programs - which were introduced in the '85 Standard. Personally, I don't
>>use them much - but I think they would really interest those who are used
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>particular to get the features of parameter passing and local variables.
The
>downside of this however being that one may get a huge number of files.
>Thus, rapidly causing an administration issue of it.

Can you explain what you mean by this?  I truly don't understand.  Using
COBOL nested programs does NOT cause any increase in "number of files" in
any way that I am aware of.  If you are talking about source or object code
files, then these are all together (unless you CHOOSE to use a COPY
statement for nested programs).  If you are talking about user input/output
files - then the use of GLOBAL (and/or EXTERNAL) files should make this a
non-issue as well.

Now, just to be clear - the use of "separately compiled programs"
(dynamic/static calls - not nested programs) certainly DOES increase the
number of program files - but that isn't what I am talking about at all.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** kownby@humboldt1.com (Kindrick Ownby)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370f52f1.16449598@news2.humboldt1.com>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
On Fri, 9 Apr 1999 09:11:43 -0700, "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
wrote:

>I have watched the different views of your all regarding use of sections in
>cobol.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>readability of the program.
[...]

I vote for the use of sections for the above reason.

In addition, since I am still writing for the IBM System/36 platform,
absolutely need sections so I can construct overlays when I get
near to the partition size limit (PAIN to the max!).

I do sometimes call major functions via a dynamic call mechanism
which puts the subroutine in another partition.

Within a program my rules are

 - each individual major thru minor function is contained in a 
   separate section

 - the main section is named A00-name, major functions are named
   Bnn-name, intermediate Cnn-name, lower still Dnn-name, etc

 - paragraphs are never performed

 - PERFORM THRU is never used

I also think this organization enhances the maintainability of the
program.

An exception to the TRHU restriction is when a sort has multiple
sources of input

           SORT SORTI-FILE ASCENDING KEY SORTI-KEY
               INPUT  PROCEDURE IS 
                       B19I-OPEN-BALANCES THRU B22I-INVOICE
               OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS B23I-REPORT.

but the input routines are themselves sections.

Kindrick
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sections vs Paragraphs

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7elq16$bq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <370f52f1.16449598@news2.humboldt1.com>`

```
Kindrick Ownby wrote in message <370f52f1.16449598@news2.humboldt1.com>...
>>However, noone seems to vote yes to the use of sections due to improve the
>>readability of the program.
>[...]
>
>I vote for the use of sections for the above reason.

Horray! Then we are two :-)

>In addition, since I am still writing for the IBM System/36 platform,
>absolutely need sections so I can construct overlays when I get
>near to the partition size limit (PAIN to the max!).

You have my sympathy, I did once have the problem myself using MS Cobol 2.

> - each individual major thru minor function is contained in a
>   separate section
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>program.

This is way the same strategy I use, a typical layout for me would be;

MYSECT SECTION.
MYSECT-001.
MYSECT-nnn.
MYSECT-900.
MYSECT-EXIT.
    EXIT.

Where 001 and EXIT is used to be unique identifiers for start and end of the
section.
Any need for extra paragraphs is matched with nnn. (Preferably avoided).
An GO TO exit would always be the MYSECT-900, such that I may easily jump to
the end of the section with a search for MYSECT-EXIT.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: COBOL and Touch Screens — 3 msgs

### COBOL and Touch Screens

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990409195526.05092.00000632@ng106.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

If anybody has used COBOL and Touch Screens could they please send me an
e-mail.  I am interested in how difficult a job it is.

Thanks 
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Touch Screens

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oEMP2.7061$w6.1885005@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990409195526.05092.00000632@ng106.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote:
>
>If anybody has used COBOL and Touch Screens could they please send me an
>e-mail.  I am interested in how difficult a job it is.

The touch screens I know about look to the program like a mouse.  When
the user touches the screen, it is like a mouse click at that location.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Touch Screens

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37121583.13114462@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<19990409195526.05092.00000632@ng106.aol.com>`

```
bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Afternoon,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Hennessey

Bob...

Most touch screen drivers we have encountered over the years with our
products simply allow the touch screen to operate in exactly the same
manner as a mouse.  In other words, if your current application uses a
character mode or GUI mouse, then the touch screen should operate
without automatically.

In the event that your COBOL application does not have mouse support,
then you would probably need to make sure that the COBOL application
can work with the mouse before a touch screen could be supported.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-8a209c3c8f"></a>
## Thread: Indexes are overused — 5 msgs

### Re: Indexes are overused

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantF9xosG.74I@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3923579702.23618.0.nnrp-03.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
"Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk> writes:

>I guess everyone's saying its horses for courses.

>For some things indexes are much faster.  I vaguely remember that on the
>WANG VS there was a study that showed a vast improvement (I can't remember
>the figure) on indices over subscripts.


I just love to dispel such folklore issues of how this is
better or that is slower.  A few months ago I wrote a series
of articles under the title "Application Tuning" for the
Wang e-zine, HotVS.  If you want to read them go to www.hotvs.com,
hit the [archives] button, go back to the start of the series in
November.  The main jist of the series is how much savings can be
achieve using different techniques.  One comparison is index vs.
subscripts.  The overall theme was code lookups from a file and
by pre-loading a table and doing indexed searches it was possible
to lose 99.6% of the fat.

It excites me that you examined the difference in index operation
on a VS and the AS/400.  The method of implementation on the
Wang VS is the norm (using offsets) but then the AS/400 is a
strange bird.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370E4FC7.FA8CCC60@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3923579702.23618.0.nnrp-03.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <dbryantF9xosG.74I@netcom.com>`

```
David K. Bryant wrote:

> I just love to dispel such folklore issues of how this is
> better or that is slower.  A few months ago I wrote a series
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> to lose 99.6% of the fat.

That's what I would expect.  At least until the table grows beyond the
size accounted for in the program and we don't get our output.  Then you
measure efficiency differently.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantF9xzqM.MK5@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<dbryantF9xosG.74I@netcom.com> <370E4FC7.FA8CCC60@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes:

>David K. Bryant wrote:

>> The overall theme was code lookups from a file and
>> by pre-loading a table and doing indexed searches it was possible
>> to lose 99.6% of the fat.

>That's what I would expect.  At least until the table grows beyond the
>size accounted for in the program and we don't get our output.  Then you
>measure efficiency differently.


That was just the example used to get people thinking
of ways to make their apps run faster.  The first was
to avoid I/O by doing lookups instead.  Then to look
at how to make the lookups more efficient with the
objective being to get them to use SEARCH ALL where
appropriate.

The examples read the entire file into Working Storage
and worked from there.  In a later series I'll address
caching lookups.  Search WS first, if not there read
from disk and stow it away for future use.  I've been
able to get some pretty good results this way.

Unfortunately the responses I get from some programmers
is, "Oh, but then I have to think too much."

Seriously, that's a quote.

Anytime I can chop 95+ percent from the runtime by just
shifting from record lookups to tabling and SEARCH ALL 
I'll do it.  I doesn't take much thinking and not much
typing either.


But the thread here was "are indexes faster than subscripts".
Yes they are.  Are they worth the tiny bit of effort?  Yes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uCW1CtQh#GA.295@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<dbryantF9xosG.74I@netcom.com> <370E4FC7.FA8CCC60@NOSPAMhome.com> <dbryantF9xzqM.MK5@netcom.com>`

```
In a situation where data must be looked up thousands of times more
frequently than being changed, sure, use a sort procedure to input the data
and build it into a sorted table to support a Search All. Or if it's a lot
more data, sort it and load it into an indexed file via JCL and just use
indexed read access in your program. Neither has anything to do with
indexes vs. subscripts except that the Search All uses indexes
automatically. For truly random access in a program, subscripting is
probably more efficient than indexing and is a lot easier to read.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexes are overused

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eln3m$lk$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3923579702.23618.0.nnrp-03.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <dbryantF9xosG.74I@netcom.com>`

```
David K. Bryant wrote in message ...
<snippage>
>Wang e-zine, HotVS.  If you want to read them go to www.hotvs.com,
>hit the [archives] button, go back to the start of the series in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Yes, but that is something altogether different.  A table VS a file is going
to get rid of 95% of the fat regardless of whether you use an index or a
subscript to address the table.  You're figure compares memory speed to disk
speed, not one addressing calculation compared to another.
```

- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

I have got many files in Microfocus Cobol which I must import in other
environments, suche as ACCESS, EXCEL, OS/400.
The operating system is UNIX.
With an FTP I can download it on a PC.
Then I can see what's inside the files.
The only problems come with numeric fields which - they told me - are
in "COMP-3" format.
I'd like to have a tool which (giving it an input a file with the
positions, the length and the types of these non-character fields)
TRANSLATES THIS RECORD FORMAT IN ALL ASCII PLAIN TEXT.

Consider I don't know anything about Cobol, Microfocus and COMP-3
format, but I have quite good programming experiences in other
programming environments, so that I can send and share many other
tools (written by me) for importing and exporting files in commercial
environments.

However many thanks to anybody who will give me a help.

PAOLO
paoreffo@tin.it

#### ↳ Re: *** HELP, please from Italy !!! *** I want to translate a Microfocus COBOL file in ASCII plain text.

- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7em5td$jjm$2@news-2.news.gte.net>`

```
If you are interested in the bit patterns for various COBOL data types,
download my free text and graphics tutorial COBDATA.ZIP at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html

If you do not have access to a COBOL compiler, this will at least tell you
what COMP-3 data looks like and may enable a conversion.

I guess I better start thinking again about writing that 'generic' converter
as a DLL for Windows:

DECLARE FUNCTION CobolToAscii LIB "mylib" (CobolFD AS lpzString, OutputFD AS
lpzString, Options AS ConversionOptions, InputDataRecord AS lpzString,
OutputDataRecord AS lpzString)

Yeah, yeah, I see it now: if I want to handle binary data, I'll have to play
with the parameters, since I don't want to prematurely terminate the input
or output when I find x'00'.  Maybe I'll just add some kind of function
where you pass me the FDs and I return you the REQUIRED sizes of the input
and output buffers.

Heck, I already have the DOS code to calculate data sizes parsing out a
COBOL FD (see COBFD.ZIP file at above URL); so now that I have a DLL
compiler, maybe I'll actually DO this.

Interest? Suggestions? Marketers? Care for a mint?

MCM

#### ↳ Re: *** HELP, please from Italy !!! *** I want to translate a Microfocus COBOL file in ASCII plain text.

- **From:** Warren Porter <warrenp21@ASPMnetdoor.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<370E6E95.25AD79CC@ASPMnetdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<370e3e80.1526355@news.tin.it>`

```
If you have access to a COBOL compiler, that would be the simplest way to
convert the files.
If not, it seems you need the definition of COMP-3, sometimes called
"packed decimal".
In this usage, every byte except the last one contains two digits.  If you
define each byte as
unsigned char, divide by 16 to get the higher digit and mod by 16 to get
the lower digit
The final byte contains the least significant digit and the sign: x'c' or
x'f' means positive while
x'd' is negative.

x'12345c' = 12345 (positive)
x'00348d' =    348 (negative)

Paolo REFFO wrote:

> I have got many files in Microfocus Cobol which I must import in other
> environments, suche as ACCESS, EXCEL, OS/400.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> format--

Warren Porter
---
Remove numbers and uppercase to reply.
```

```
Hello,

#### ↳ Re: IBM VS COBOL 2 Ver. 5.1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eme87$hcs@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7em2k9$u7r$1@news07.btx.dtag.de>`

```
1) your reference to "VS COBOL 2 Ver 2.1" has been confused and frightened.
If you really mean "VS COBOL II - Release 2" - that product has been out of
support since about 1987 - and never had a R2.1.  If, on the other hand, you
really mean IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM R2.1 - then you are in luck and this
is pretty easy.

2) All "modern" IBM COBOLs (VS COBOL II - and the newer IBM COBOL for
this-and-that) have a relatively easy facility for "restricting" the use of
certain verbs and constructs.

Check out,
 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYIC201/1%2e5%2e3

For the web-less, this is:
 1.5.3 Creating or Modifying Additional Reserved Word Tables
in
 Title: COBOL OS/390 & VM V2R1 OS/390 Installation
 Document Number: GC26-9045-01

NOTE WELL:  One "problem" with this facility is that IBM provides little or
no assistance in "upgrading" a customized reserved word table from release
to release of their products.

NOTE2:  This facility lets you EITHER create an "error" message when one of
these verbs is used - or a "warning" message.  It also lets you create a
table that can be OPTIONALLY used by programmers at compile time (via the
WORD compiler option) - or you can set this as a non-overridable table so
programmers MUST use your choice of words.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7enhrl$9ih$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7em2k9$u7r$1@news07.btx.dtag.de>`

wrote:

#### ↳ Re: Checking Bits

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7env4k$c46$1@juliana.sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3711d6c4.24339164@news.cris.com>`

```
Joe,

        This is my tried and true method I use on the IBM Mainframe ===>

        03    WS-BYTE                 PIC    X(01).
        03    WS-BIT-RSLT          PIC    X(08).
        03    WS-HWORD-X.
                05    WS-HWORD    PIC   S9(04)    BINARY.
        03    WS-SUB                   PIC   S9(04)    BINARY.

        MOVE 'A'                          TO WS-BYTE.
        MOVE ZERO                    TO WS-BIT-RSLT.
        MOVE +1                          TO WS-SUB.
        MOVE LOW-VALUE       TO WS-HWORD-X (1:1).
        MOVE WS-BYTE             TO WS-HWORD-X (2:1).

        PERFORM UNTIL WS-SUB IS GREATER THAN +8
                IF     WS-HWORD-X (2:1) IS GREATER THAN X'7F'
                        MOVE '1'           TO WS-BIT-RSLT (WS-SUB:1)
                        END-IF
                ADD WS-HWORD   TO WS-HWORD
                ADD +1                      TO WS-SUB
                END-PERFORM.

        When the PERFORM falls through, WS-BIT-RSLT will equal '11000001',
which is the bit-pattern of an upper-case EBCDIC 'A' (B'11000001'). You can
then use Reference Modification to test if a bit is present ('1')or not
('0').

        HTH....

Cheers,

WOB,
Atlanta

Joe Hans wrote in message <3711d6c4.24339164@news.cris.com>...
>Does anyone know how to test the value of the bits of an alphanumeric
>field?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Checking Bits

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WBMP2.7056$w6.1883561@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<3711d6c4.24339164@news.cris.com>`

```
Joe Hans wrote:
>Does anyone know how to test the value of the bits of an alphanumeric
>field?
>
>I have a PIC X, and need to find out which bits are on (i.e. set to
>1).  So I want to return 8 answers.  Any ideas?


       01  BIT-WORK.
           03  BW-BYTE         PIC X(01)      VALUE SPACE.
           03  BW-ORD          PIC 9(04) COMP VALUE 0.
           03  BW-BITS.
               05  BW-BIT128   PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT64    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT32    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT16    PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT8     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT4     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT2     PIC  9(01).
               05  BW-BIT1     PIC  9(01).
           03  BW-BIT          REDEFINES BW-BITS
                               OCCURS 8 TIMES
                               INDEXED BY BW-X
                                   PIC  9(01).

       SPLIT-BYTE-INTO-BITS.
           COMPUTE BW-ORD = FUNCTION ORD(BW-BYTE) - 1.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 8 BY -1 UNTIL BW-X < 1
               DIVIDE 2 INTO BW-ORD
                   GIVING BW-ORD REMAINDER BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.

       MERGE-BITS-INTO-BYTE.
           MOVE 0 TO BW-ORD.
           PERFORM VARYING BW-X FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL BW-X > 8
               COMPUTE BW-ORD = BW-ORD + BW-ORD + BW-BIT(BW-X)
           END-PERFORM.
           MOVE FUNCTION CHAR(BW-ORD + 1) TO BW-BYTE.
```

<a id="t-84c6322292"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe to Unix Cobol conversion? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe to Unix Cobol conversion?

- **From:** strangiato@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7emnir$svd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi all

I don't have much info. on this, but I know someone who wants to convert
systems in IBM mainframe Cobol to Unix based Cobol.  They want to avoid code
changes as much as humanly possible.

I imagine that the difference in collating sequences (EBCDIC -> ASCII) might
cause some problems in sorting ??

Apart from this does anyone have any thoughts/comments on this project and
how it could be achieved?

Thanks

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe to Unix Cobol conversion?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7enohe$lcg$2@news-1.news.gte.net>`
- **References:** `<7emnir$svd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Data WILL sort differently - in EBCDIC, letters a-z sort before numbers 0-9;
in ASCII it's the other way around - unless the programs are compiled with a
different APLHABET, PROGRAM COLLATING SEQUENCE and/or sort-merge level
COLLATING SEQUENCE IS clause.

These vary tremendously by compiler and before you implement any of these
schemes you should get it written down as a firm policy or you will end up
with a terrible mess as you bring on new maintenance programmers.

Best advice: train the users to get used to seeing numbers precede letters.

MCM

Most of the compiler vendors who target this audience have documentation,
software, consulting, etc to deal with such conversions.  They are possible
but do have lots of "gotchas" if you don't plan them in advance.

Both IBM and Micro Focus strongly target this market.  Other vendors provide
solutions that can work.
```

Hi,
what exactly does he want ?
If he wants to convert his mainframe software to UNIX we have the right
tools for him.
Our tool is called ICS-CASE. In general, it`s a development tool for
portable COBOL
software, which means, that your programs will run on NEARLY EVERY (!)
target platform.
The part of our tool which your friend might need is the migration tool.
This will migrate
your software (and data !) from every platform into the PC environment. From
there, it can
be converted to the target platform you like, e.g. UNIX.
For further information mail to ics-khatib@fen.baynet.de .

Hope to hear from you,

Calle

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe to Unix Cobol conversion?

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<923782477.868180@woody.hotkey.net.au>`
- **References:** `<7emnir$svd$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

I've ported a mainframe (CICS) application to a PC under UNIX, OS/2 and NT
always using MicroFocus. In order to avoid your problem, you can set the
default standard as being EBCDIC rather than ASCII. After that everything
else is transparent to you.

If you  need to have files generated by your application, used by other
'ASCII' systems, then use an EBCDIC to ASCII converter.

Good luck

Regards

Theo Charalambous
```

## Thread: Procedure-Pointers and Fujitsu 4.20 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Procedure-Pointers and Fujitsu 4.20

- **From:** keithwalk@aol.com (KeithWalk)
- **Date:** 1999-04-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990410161411.06183.00001166@ng128.aol.com>`

```
All...

Just for the heck of it I've been trying to convert the classic
"WINHELLO.CBL" MF Cobol source into Fujitsu 4.20 standard Cobol.
To the best of my knowledge, Fujitsu 4.20 has no Procedure-Pointer
capability.  Does anyone have an idea on a work-around that will
allow me to complete this exercise without resorting to PowerCobol.
Thank you!

Keith F. Walker   :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure-Pointers and Fujitsu 4.20

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37101f35.1357448@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990410161411.06183.00001166@ng128.aol.com>`

```
On 10 Apr 1999 20:14:11 GMT, keithwalk@aol.com (KeithWalk) wrote:

>All...
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank you!

I've been fiddling with the Windows API and such of late with Fujitsu
COBOL 4.2 no less.  (I like the new directive ALPHAL(WORD) btw).

Anyway, my problem is one of vocabulary.  By a procedure pointer, do
you meat using the MF method of "SET xxxx to ENTRY xxxx"?  If so,
these just are not necessary with Fujitsu COBOL.  You DO need the
Windows API however, which I just downloaded today.  Go to:

http://msdn.microsoft.com/developer/sdk/platform.asp

and download the ibldenv.exe and add the appropriate libraries from
the \lib dir after install, to your project.
```
