##### ↳ ↳ Re: Revised Y2K Date...9/9/99?

- **From:** "Gary L. Smith" <gls@infinet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bPzR2.274$Nt.688@news2>`
- **References:** `<19990409074107.16739.00000533@ng31.aol.com> <sXGQ2.153$Nt.298@news2> <7f023p$lvk$1@callisto.clark.net> <BiVQ2.197$Nt.477@news2> <7f25lt$pga$1@clarknet.clark.net> <k7wR2.9005$CU.2583166@news4.mia>`

```
In comp.software.year-2000 James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:
: I can't remember LOW-VALUES / HIGH-VALUES EVER being anything
: OTHER than the lowest and highest for that collating sequence.
: Character '0' will /was never the lowest, nor was '9' ever the
: HIGHEST, as letters followed and preceded in EBCDIC, and in ASCII.

The only thing this proves is that your memory doesn't encompass
everything that's ever happened.  There have been many character encoding
schemes aside from EBCDIC and ASCII, and many variant collating sequences. 
I once worked on a system where HIGH-VALUES was ALL '9', and while I 
could be mis-remembering the ZEROS as LOW-VALUES, I would be surprised if 
it hadn't happened on at least one system somewhere. 
```

```
In article <bPzR2.274$Nt.688@news2>, "Gary L. Smith" <gls@infinet.com> wrote:
+In comp.software.year-2000 James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote:
+: I can't remember LOW-VALUES / HIGH-VALUES EVER being anything
+: OTHER than the lowest and highest for that collating sequence.
+: Character '0' will /was never the lowest, nor was '9' ever the
+: HIGHEST, as letters followed and preceded in EBCDIC, and in ASCII.
+
+The only thing this proves is that your memory doesn't encompass
+everything that's ever happened.  There have been many character encoding
+schemes aside from EBCDIC and ASCII, and many variant collating sequences. 
+I once worked on a system where HIGH-VALUES was ALL '9', and while I 
+could be mis-remembering the ZEROS as LOW-VALUES, I would be surprised if 
+it hadn't happened on at least one system somewhere. 

HIGH-VALUES and LOW-VALUES are required by the Cobol standard to be the 
highest and lowest values, respectively, in the collating sequence.
+
```

<a id="t-05861f0d93"></a>
## Thread: Table Help — 4 msgs

### Table Help

- **From:** Jeff Quinn <jayq@ma.ultranet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371512DB.7627F557@ma.ultranet.com>`

```
I need a little help with a table. I have a program that prints out
company letters A thru G which different agents can write business in.
However not all agents can write in all the companies and the program is
set up to either move a letter or spaces which leaves space(s) between
the letters and I need the letters to be next to each other and the
spaces at the end. I know how to do it using a ton of ifï¿½s and else if
ï¿½s but I donï¿½t have enough experience using tables and I seem to have a
mind block. Can anyone give me a hand. Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Table Help

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QVkR2.8184$Ws1.2134608@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<371512DB.7627F557@ma.ultranet.com>`

```
Jeff Quinn wrote:
>I need a little help with a table. I have a program that prints out
>company letters A thru G which different agents can write business in.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>mind block. Can anyone give me a hand. Thanks.

From your description I take it that you are looking for a way to remove
embedded spaces from text fields (e.g. "A C E G" into "ACEG   ").

Using 2 work areas:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  IN-TEXT                     PIC  X(07) VALUE "A C E G".
       77  OUT-TEXT                    PIC  X(07).
       77  IN-PTR                      PIC  9(02) COMP.
       77  OUT-PTR                     PIC  9(02) COMP.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       DO-IT-TO-IT.
           DISPLAY ":" IN-TEXT ":".
           PERFORM CRUNCH-TEXT.
           DISPLAY ":" OUT-TEXT ":".
           STOP RUN.

       CRUNCH-TEXT.
           MOVE SPACES TO OUT-TEXT.
           MOVE 1 TO OUT-PTR.
           PERFORM VARYING IN-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL IN-PTR > FUNCTION LENGTH(IN-TEXT)
               IF IN-TEXT(IN-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
                   MOVE IN-TEXT(IN-PTR:1) TO OUT-TEXT(OUT-PTR:1)
                   ADD 1 TO OUT-PTR
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.

Using 1 work area:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77  WORK-TEXT                   PIC  X(07) VALUE "A C E G".
       77  IN-PTR                      PIC  9(02) COMP.
       77  OUT-PTR                     PIC  9(02) COMP.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       DO-IT-TO-IT.
           DISPLAY ":" WORK-TEXT ":".
           PERFORM CRUNCH-TEXT.
           DISPLAY ":" WORK-TEXT ":".
           STOP RUN.

       CRUNCH-TEXT.
           MOVE 1 TO OUT-PTR.
           PERFORM VARYING IN-PTR FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL IN-PTR > FUNCTION LENGTH(WORK-TEXT)
               IF WORK-TEXT(IN-PTR:1) NOT = SPACE
                   MOVE WORK-TEXT(IN-PTR:1) TO WORK-TEXT(OUT-PTR:1)
                   ADD 1 TO OUT-PTR
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
           PERFORM UNTIL OUT-PTR > FUNCTION LENGTH(WORK-TEXT)
               MOVE SPACE TO WORK-TEXT(OUT-PTR:1)
               ADD 1 TO OUT-PTR
           END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: Table Help

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3ojm$960$1@news.igs.net>`

>company letters A thru G which different agents can write business in.

#### ↳ Re: Table Help

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37161C46.5542@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<371512DB.7627F557@ma.ultranet.com>`

```
Jeff Quinn wrote:
> 
> I need a little help with a table. I have a program that prints out
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> mind block. Can anyone give me a hand. Thanks.

If you have the latest report writer syntax in your COBOL, you can use 
relative columns and PRESENT WHEN clauses, e.g.
  03  LINE + 1.
    05  COL 1     VALUE "fixed bit".
    05  COL + 2   VALUE "company A bit"  PRESENT WHEN co-A-flag = 1.
    05  COL + 2   VALUE "company B bit"  PRESENT WHEN co-B-flag = 1.
    05  ...
```

<a id="t-f8678d1b7f"></a>
## Thread: Printable character routine — 3 msgs

### Printable character routine

- **From:** "Frederick G. Pierz" <fpierz@cds1976.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3394$1hk$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`

```
I am attempting to write a routine that will examine a record and remove and
character that isn't printable. Using IF's and INSPECT's seems very slow.

Does anybody have any ideas how to speed the routine up.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Printable character routine

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37155879@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f3394$1hk$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`

```
Frederick G. Pierz <fpierz@cds1976.com> wrote in message
news:7f3394$1hk$1@Nntp1.mcs.net...
> I am attempting to write a routine that will examine a record and remove
and
> character that isn't printable. Using IF's and INSPECT's seems very slow.
>
> Does anybody have any ideas how to speed the routine up.

depending on your platform, you might be able to do the following:

01  char-handling.
     02  the-value   pic s9(4) comp-4 value zeroes.            (or comp-5)
     02  filler            redefines the-value.
            03  filler        pic x.
            03  the-char pic x.          (may have to switch these two
around)

 01  printable-values.
       02  filler  pic x(15) value    "000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "1111111111111111".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "1111111111111111".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "1111111111111111".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000001111100000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "1111111111000111".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000011111111100".
       02  filler  pic x(16) value  "0000000000000000".
 01  filler redefines printable-values.
       02  print-mark pic x occurs 255 times.

01  the-text.
      02  the-text-char   pic x occurs 80 times.

01  the-subscripts.
      02  char-nbr    pic s9(3)  comp.
.....
perform transform-to-printable
    varying char-nbr from 1 by 1
      until char-nbr > 80
....
transform-to-printable.
     move the-text-char (char-nbr) to the-char
     if the-value = zero
     or print-mark (the-value) = "0"
           move "." to the-text-char (char-nbr)          [or whatever]
     .


the table has a 0 for non-printable chars and a 1 for
printable chars. You will have to set these correctly depending
the character set. The above settings are for illustration only.
I have arbitrarily chosen "." as to print for non-printables.
A different approach would be to store in the table what you
want printed for each character.
```

#### ↳ Re: Printable character routine

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3oeu$92u$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7f3394$1hk$1@Nntp1.mcs.net>`

```
I can do that with my editor (using a macro command) at the rate of about a
megabyte per two seconds. I also have a macro that will do it using
wildcards for the filename.

and
>character that isn't printable. Using IF's and INSPECT's seems very slow.
>

## Thread: Unix MF Cobol calling C.. how? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Unix MF Cobol calling C.. how?

- **From:** <thatoneguy@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3l0h$622$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
We're on MF COBOL 4.? and we have to call some utilities in C and pass
parameters.  But they don't pass.
Does anyone know the technical specs on how MF COBOL passes it's parms?  Do
you know of any way around the issue?

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Unix MF Cobol calling C.. how?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3uii$nlv@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7f3l0h$622$1@camel29.mindspring.com>`

```
Look in your reference material (COBOL LRM - or System Reference manual) and
look at

   call-convention

This will tell you BOTH what the default is and how to "change it".
```

<a id="t-191c0f02cb"></a>
## Thread: Free certifications offered on-line to COBOL programmers — 1 msgs

### Free certifications offered on-line to COBOL programmers

- **From:** ayemell@aol.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990413221615.876$PE@newsreader.com>`

```
Dear COBOL Programmer,

 Through April, TekMetrics has extended its free service of COBOL
certifications conducted through it's internet-based CAT engine.  Simply go
to http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/certtop.html to order your free
certification. You can either elect to take the test right away, or come
back later, using the PIN you are provided when you order.

To qualify as a Certified COBOL Programmer, you must pass the examination
with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master COBOL
Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.  At the end of the
test, your score will be provided (with a scoring range of 1-5).  Once
certified, you can add the logo associated with the test (standard or
master logo, depending on how well you do) to your resume or website.
Furthermore, those that pass prior to 4/19 will be mailed a certificate,
once again, at no cost (after which time their may be a nominal fee for the
shipping of the certificates). There's no catch.  We're simply trying to
prove a concept during a beta trial period. Thus far, the feedback has been
extremely positive!

Why bother getting certified by TekMetrics?
1)  More and more corporations recognize TekMetrics Certifications as an
    indication not only of the technical workers knowledge level, but also
    as an indicator that they were willing to demonstrate their skills to
them. 2)  It's free and it's fun.  They won't be free forever, but they are
    now. So take advantage of them.  Furthermore, those who have completed
    the tests generally indicate that they enjoyed the experience and
    considered both the delivery of the test and the items themselves to be
    of high quality.
3)  Over 4000 technical workers from around the world are ordering our
    certifications each week, and that number is steadily rising.
4)  Strut your stuff.  See if you are good enough to make the top 10 list.
5)  Find out both your strengths and weaknesses in COBOL.

At the end of the test, you can request specifics on how you did, by
subtopic. And yes, that too is free.  All we ask for in return is your
feedback at the end of the exam.  We will be continually focusing on the
improvement of the delivery of the test and quality of the questions.  Any
feedback you can provide, positive or negative, will help us greatly.

Another FAQ is, "How do you know the person who supposedly took the test
was actually the same person?  What if it was his hotshot 'COBOL
Programmer' brother, sister or friend?  In other words, what is to prevent
him from cheating?"  Our solution:  we allow employers to spotcheck the
technical workers with a short test at no cost at their corporate
locations, if they so choose.  Corporations have unanimously applauded this
solution.

Lastly, data you provide in the certification process is not used without
your permission other than to store your certification record and verify to
valid employers at their request.  We do, however, publish names on our
site in a Top Ten Scores list for each category, but no addresses, phone
numbers, email, etc.. are shown.  For more information, see our privacy
statement at http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/privacy.html  Well, that's
pretty much it! We're trying to establish a new cost-effective and
convenient alternative to today's certification environment.  During this
beta trial period, you will be the benefactor of this fun and challenging
experience.  If you have any questions/concerns regarding the COBOL
certification, please contact Deborah Manley at
<mailto:deborah.manley@tekmetrics.com>.

Good luck and Thank you!
Ann Marie
Ann Marie Ludlow
TekMetrics, Inc.
www.tekmetrics.com
```

<a id="t-a584a32b99"></a>
## Thread: Cobol vs. PL/1 — 1 msgs

### Re: Cobol vs. PL/1

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<924078105.251.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7evg1a$sr7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
You're right.  It does vary between COBOL implementors.

On WANG VS and IBM AS400, COMP is packed decimal.  In MF COBOL on unix, COMP
is binary.  I believe that MF COBOL equates COMP with COMP-4 but it may be
COMP-5.

Any way you have to know how it is implemented in your COBOL.

If the implementation is binary then the form PIC S9(n) COMP would give:
  BIN(15) if n=1 - 4
  BIN(31) if n=5 - 9.
If I remember my PL/1 correctly.

Regards
Rick


jgis@my-dejanews.com wrote in message <7evg1a$sr7$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...
>according to Accredited Standards Committee last revision of COBOL :
>
>"The USAGE COMPUTATIONAL clause specifies that a radix and format specified
by
>the implementor is used to
>represent a numeric item in the storage of the computer. Each implementor
>specifies the precise effect of the
>USAGE COMPUTATIONAL clause upon the alignment and representation of the
data
>item in the storage of the
>computer, including the representation of any algebraic sign, and upon the
>range of values that the data item may hold." This implies that this may
vary
>between COBOL implementors.
>
>My question is what is the the equivalent of PIC S9(4)  COMP  IN PL/1 ?
That
>is how do you declare this in Pl/1?
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

## Thread: Help! How to get first job — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: Help! How to get first job

- **From:** "Kai Andresen" <kai.andresen@oslo.online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<KR%Q2.139$y75.1120@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <uKdQ2.5867$0m3.1351014@news1.mia> <7f06g3$5hc$1@asid-1.umd.edu>`

```
Zsolt Szabo wrote in message <7f06g3$5hc$1@asid-1.umd.edu>...
>Sorry, but I disagree. Employers definitely seem to have an "us vs.
>them" attitude. When was the last time that an employer offered to pay
>an employeewhat they were actually worth right from the start, rather
>than hoping that this employee would undervalue their worth and go for
>a lower salary?

How could an employer to be know your market value? Do a good job, prove
you're worth more money and ask for a talk on the subject of your wages
and other benefits you would like.

>There is a definite bias here; employers will always pay less but never
>more-- unless they absolutely have to (in which case they are really
>not paying "more", but rather what they personally perceive as "too
much").

You would agree that a programmers job is to make good code, whats an
employers job? Lets assume he's supposed to make more money out of his
investors money.

>I don't claim that lying is the best policy, but I know from experience
>and based on what others tell me that recruiters and employees will
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>more.

How can you say that is to screw him?
When he proves to be good at what he does, he can ask for more -
possibly by refering to an offer from another company (if that's the
case!).

Honesty is the allways the best. But you got to be truthful about what
you actualy CAN do, as to what you still CAN NOT do. And asked for what
you expect to be payed, do not answer an amount you want be satisfied
with - who's fooling who?! If you do it to make sure you get the job -
be aware of that it was your call - go on from there.

>After the interview was finished, he told me that the recruiters
>"ganged up" on him and made him feel worthless and completely
>undervalued his competence, skill and intelligence.

If it is very important to get just this job, one can live with that. If
it is just another prospect one can afford to ask if this is the tone
used in daily busines in that company... Doing that in a friendly and
open minded manner will most probably stun them, positively.

>Ever since then, every interview I have walked into, I immdiately put
>on my Dolph-Lundgren-"I must break you" face. It's the only policy that
>seems to work with these types.

Does this give you the jobs you get happy with?
```

<a id="t-27f60a4143"></a>
## Thread: COBOL critique needed. — 42 this month / 43 total

### COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** aarond23@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I am a long time lurker in this group and am now a novice COBOL programmer.
I would like to know if this simple program is anything near "proffessional".
Thank you.
(Don't laugh).

********************************************************************************

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. CH8EX1.
       AUTHOR. AARON DAVIS.
       INSTALLATION. IVY TECH.
       DATE-WRITTEN. MARCH 29, 1999.
       DATE-COMPILED.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
              SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
              OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

              FILE-CONTROL.
                          SELECT STOCK-FILE
                          ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
                          ASSIGN TO "A:\CH08EX01.DAT".
                          SELECT REPORT-FILE ASSIGN TO "A:\OUTPUT.TXT".

       DATA DIVISION.

              FILE SECTION.

              FD STOCK-FILE
                 RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
                 DATA RECORD IS STOCK-RECORD.

              01 STOCK-RECORD.
                 02  IN-STOCK-NUM            PIC 9(9).
                 02  IN-DESCRIPTION          PIC X(16).
                 02  IN-QUANTITY             PIC 9(5).
                 02  IN-UNIT-COST             PIC 999V99.

       FD      REPORT-FILE
               DATA RECORD IS REPORT-RECORD.
               01 REPORT-RECORD PIC X(80).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
              01 FLAGS.
                 02 EOF-FLAG              PIC X(3) VALUE "NO".


              01 HOLD-FIELDS.
                 02 HOLD-STOCK-NUM            PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.

              01 COUNTERS.
                 02 ITEM-COUNTER                PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.

              01 ACCUMULATORS.
                 02 TOTAL-QUANTITY        PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 TOTAL-UNIT-COST       PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 TOTAL-EX-COST         PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 TOTAL-TOTAL-QUANTITY  PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 TOTAL-TOTAL-UNIT-COST PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 TOTAL-TOTAL-EX-COST   PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.
                 02 AVERAGE               PIC 9(9) VALUE ZERO.

              01 PAGE-HEADING.
                 02 PIC X                 VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(10)              VALUE "03/29/1999".
                 02 PIC X(14)              VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(12)              VALUE "STOCK REPORT".
                 02 PIC X(13)              VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(6)               VALUE "PAGE 1".

              01 HEAD-LINE.
                 02 PIC X                    VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(6)                 VALUE "NUMBER".
                 02 PIC X(5)                 VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(11)                VALUE "DESCRIPTION".
                 02 PIC X(6)                 VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(8)                 VALUE "QUANTITY".
                 02 PIC XX                   VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(9)                 VALUE "UNIT COST".
                 02 PIC XX                   VALUE SPACES.
                 02 PIC X(13)                VALUE "EXTENDED COST".


              01 DETAIL-LINE.
                 02                      PIC X  VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-STOCK-NUM          PIC 9(9).
                 02                      PIC X VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-DESCRIPTION        PIC X(16).
                 02                      PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-QUANTITY           PIC ZZ,ZZZ.
                 02                      PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-UNIT-COST          PIC $ZZZ.99.
                 02                      PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-EX-COST            PIC $ZZ,ZZZ.99.

              01 AVERAGE-LINE.
                 02                     PIC X(7) VALUE "AVERAGE".
                 02 O-ITEM-COUNTER             PIC 999.
                 02                     PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-AVERAGE           PIC $ZZZ,ZZZ.99.

              01 TOTAL-LINE-1.
                 02                      PIC X(6) VALUE "TOTALS".
                 02                      PIC X(5) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-TOTAL-QUANTITY     PIC ZZZ,ZZZ.
                 02                      PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-TOTAL-UNIT-COST    PIC $ZZ,ZZZ.99.
                 02                      PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
                 02 O-TOTAL-EX-COST      PIC $ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZZ.99.


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       1000-MAIN-CONTROL.

       PERFORM INIT-PARA
       PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "YES"
                 IF IN-STOCK-NUM NOT = HOLD-STOCK-NUM
                         PERFORM DESCRIPTION-BREAK-PARA
                 END-IF
                 PERFORM PROCESS-PARA
                 PERFORM READ-PARA
       end-perform
       perform description-break-para
       perform END-PARA
                 STOP RUN.

       INIT-PARA.
                  OPEN INPUT STOCK-FILE
                  OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE
                  PERFORM READ-PARA
                  WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADING
                  AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
                  WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM HEAD-LINE
                  AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
                  MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO HOLD-STOCK-NUM.

       PROCESS-PARA.
                 MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO O-STOCK-NUM
                 MOVE IN-DESCRIPTION TO O-DESCRIPTION
                 MOVE IN-QUANTITY TO O-QUANTITY
                 MOVE IN-UNIT-COST TO O-UNIT-COST
                 COMPUTE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST = IN-QUANTITY
                 *IN-UNIT-COST
                 MOVE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST TO O-EX-COST
                 ADD IN-QUANTITY TO TOTAL-QUANTITY
                 ADD IN-UNIT-COST TO TOTAL-UNIT-COST
                 ADD QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST TO TOTAL-EX-COST
                 ADD 1 TO ITEM-COUNTER
                 WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE
                 AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.

       DESCRIPTION-BREAK-PARA.
                 MOVE ITEM-COUNTER TO O-ITEM-COUNTER
                 MOVE TOTAL-QUANTITY TO O-TOTAL-QUANTITY
                 MOVE TOTAL-UNIT-COST TO O-TOTAL-UNIT-COST
                 MOVE TOTAL-EX-COST   TO O-TOTAL-EX-COST
                 COMPUTE AVERAGE=TOTAL-EX-COST / ITEM-COUNTER
                 MOVE AVERAGE TO O-AVERAGE
                 WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM TOTAL-LINE-1 AFTER
                 ADVANCING 2 LINES.
                 WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM AVERAGE-LINE AFTER
                 ADVANCING 2 LINES.

                 MOVE 0 TO AVERAGE
                 MOVE 0 TO ITEM-COUNTER
                 MOVE 0 TO TOTAL-QUANTITY
                 MOVE 0 TO TOTAL-UNIT-COST
                 MOVE 0 TO TOTAL-EX-COST
                 MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO HOLD-STOCK-NUM.


       READ-PARA.
                READ STOCK-FILE
                      AT END MOVE "YES" TO EOF-FLAG
                END-READ.

       END-PARA.

                CLOSE STOCK-FILE
                CLOSE REPORT-FILE





-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3716905c@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
<aarond23@my-dejanews.com> wrote in message
news:7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com...
> I am a long time lurker in this group and am now a novice COBOL
programmer.
> I would like to know if this simple program is anything near
"professional".

looks OK to me. A few quibbles follow below:

>        PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FLAG = "YES"
>                  IF IN-STOCK-NUM NOT = HOLD-STOCK-NUM
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
don't mix upper and lower case



>        INIT-PARA.
>                   OPEN INPUT STOCK-FILE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

instead of ending the last statement with a period, consider placing the
period on the
next line, like this:

>                   MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO HOLD-STOCK-NUM
>                 .

that way, if you add statements to the paragraph, you don't need to move the
period,


>        PROCESS-PARA.
>                  MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO O-STOCK-NUM
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>                  AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.

use blank lines to show logical groupings of statements,
e.g.:

>        PROCESS-PARA.
>                  MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO O-STOCK-NUM
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>                  MOVE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST TO O-EX-COST

>                  ADD IN-QUANTITY TO TOTAL-QUANTITY
>                  ADD IN-UNIT-COST TO TOTAL-UNIT-COST
>                  ADD QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST TO TOTAL-EX-COST

>                  ADD 1 TO ITEM-COUNTER
>                  WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM DETAIL-LINE
>                      AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
>                .
>

The names could be a bit better:
END-PARA and friends are not the best choice; hoe about CLOSE-FILES?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37172F01.3C10828B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I will be picky on the standards stuff not the actual logic,
different strokes:

There is not a single comment in the whole program.  Please add
the frilly bits, it will help you in six months when you try to
work it out. This is a tiny program so it might not be that big a
deal.


The following are all obsolete.  Do not use them in programs.  If
you want to include them anyway put a comment in front of them
>        AUTHOR. AARON DAVIS.
>        INSTALLATION. IVY TECH.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>               OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.

This is PC ONLY stuff.   Just a comment...
>                           ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
>                           ASSIGN TO "A:\CH08EX01.DAT".

The record does not add up..  This would be an error in MVS.  Do
not code the record contains, unless it is particularly important
for some reason.
>                  RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                  02  IN-UNIT-COST             PIC 999V99.


** really picky **  use a single byte for a flag.
>                  02 EOF-FLAG              PIC X(3) VALUE "NO".

**  May be picky **  The single blank at the front is a standard
for MVS when this column has a special character added to it.
>               01 PAGE-HEADING.
>                  02 PIC X                 VALUE SPACES.

I prefer the following format, it shows the statement as a unit of
work:
           COMPUTE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST = IN-QUANTITY *
                                              IN-UNIT-COST

>                  COMPUTE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST = IN-QUANTITY
>                  *IN-UNIT-COST

I prefer the following (but many will argue) :
             CLOSE STOCK-FILE
                   REPORT-FILE

>                 CLOSE STOCK-FILE
>                 CLOSE REPORT-FILE

But I am **extremely** picky about my code.
Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990418110717.19252.00002147@ng24.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37172F01.3C10828B@zip.com.au>`

```
>The record does not add up..  This would be an error in MVS.  Do
>not code the record contains, unless it is particularly important
>for some reason.
>>                  RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS

That is exactly why you code it. If your file is
*supposed* to contain 80 characters, and you
code for 79 or 81 or some such, would you
rather find out at compile time, or at execution time, when the program chokes
because it's presented with an 80 character file when it's expecting something
else? By not coding it,
you've eliminated a useful cross-checking feature and gained nothing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ekNS2.5186$qx1.115717@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<37172F01.3C10828B@zip.com.au> <19990418110717.19252.00002147@ng24.aol.com>`

```
>That is exactly why you code it. If your file is
>*supposed* to contain 80 characters, and you
>code for 79 or 81 or some such, would you
>rather find out at compile time, or at execution time, when the program
chokes
>because it's presented with an 80 character file when it's expecting
something
>else? By not coding it,
>you've eliminated a useful cross-checking feature and gained nothing.


If you actually compile it and run it in test the RECORD CONTAINS errors
will show up. Furthermore if you specify it in JCL where it belongs than the
problem is avoided, with the exception of coding a LRECL which is too small.
But even then a simple test run will reveal the problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B2D32.875A3EBA@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <37172F01.3C10828B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:

> The record does not add up..  This would be an error in MVS.  Do
> not code the record contains, unless it is particularly important
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                  02  IN-UNIT-COST             PIC 999V99.

It is always important that your record descriptions agree with what you
think they should be.  By adding the RECORD CONTAINS clause, you get the
compiler to double check your figures.   Avoiding a free check like this
seems to me to be a bad practice.


> I prefer the following (but many will argue) :
>              CLOSE STOCK-FILE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >                 CLOSE REPORT-FILE

OK, I'll argue.  Separate OPEN and close statements make it easier to
determine which one blew up.  So I separate them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7A8F.83D038FB@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <37172F01.3C10828B@zip.com.au> <371B2D32.875A3EBA@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I use a copybook for everything.  I don't specify it in the JCL. 
All programs that reference the file reference the copybook.  If
the copybook changes all programs are recompiled (or they crash in
testing with a LRECL error.

No conflict, no problem,  no error prone adding up, no JCL
changes. Magic

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717389D.43FF950F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
aarond23@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> I am a long time lurker in this group and am now a novice COBOL programmer.
> I would like to know if this simple program is anything near "proffessional".
> Thank you.
> (Don't laugh).

Just glancing through it quickly, (and figuring formatting looks a bit
odd on the web), I say it looks more professional than half of what I
see in the industry.  (I don't know about "proffessional").  Your style
is simple, structured, and likely to be correct.  I did wonder though
why you had one paragraph name with a number prefix, but not the rest. 
The main reason we use number prefixes is to make it easy to find
paragraphs quickly.  (I like being able to look for a numeric character
in column 8 besides being able to guess where 9000-READ-MYFILE is).

My own stylistic choices also don't care for paragraph names such as
READ-PARA.  We know it's a paragraph, instead I prefer
READ-ACCOUNT-FILE.    When you have multiple input files, that's more
useful.

I have never seen the closes in a paragraph by itself.

I like having a comment in the last line saying ***** CH8EX1 procedure
division ends here ******.   This is a practice which was common in the
days of punched cards, but is still useful when you are editing the
compile listing and want to get to the last line of the source code.

Shop standards also usually have something such as this where REMARKS
used to be:
000500*****************************************************************
000600**                                                             **
000700**  THIS PROGRAM CREATES BILL CYCLE TRANSACTIONS FOR THE FAM   **
000800**  TO BRS DISBURSEMENT FEED JOB.  THE INPUT FILE IS CREATED   **
000900**  BY PROGRAM SBA590.                                         **
001000**                                                              *
001100*****************************************************************
001200**                                                             **
001300**             R E V I S I O N     H I S T O R Y               **
001400**                                                             **
001500*****************************************************************
      *
      * SR8202   HJB JANUARY, 1999
      *         ADDED M-BILL-CYCLE-TYPE CHECK FOR "WO" (SIPR615)
001600*
      * SR8000   JB  MAY, 1997
      *         PROGRAM WRITTEN TODAY

Notice, the latest revision is at the top.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990418105619.19252.00002145@ng24.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3717389D.43FF950F@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>I have never seen the closes in a paragraph by itself.
>

I do this so that file closings are easily performed for both normal and (user
conrolled) abnormal terminations. The paragraph that opens the files sets a
switch to
true. At normal termination, the close paragraph is performed. The abnormal
termination paragraph checks the switch and performs the close paragraph when
necessary before calling for an ABEND. I've heard this referred to as a
"graceful exit." It also performs paragraphs that display I/O counts & current
date/time. This is useful
info for tracking down the problem and differentiating between messages issued
by multiple occurrences of the same ABEND in the same production cycle. In
cases
of large file/long running programs, I also
set up the output files as CATLG,CATLG. Sometimes the contents of output files
can help determine the cause of the problem.
When you're figuring out an ABEND that's holding up the rest of the daily
production cycle at 2:00 in the morning, there's no such thing as too much
information!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kCIR2.850$vl5.103444@dfiatx1-snr1>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
1. Does it work?
2. Is it relatively efficient?
3. Is it maintainable?

If you can answer, "yes" to all three, it's professional quality.

If you got paid for it, it's professional.

MCM

"proffessional".

good case that the comments are just as important as the code....

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717d41d@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3717B4F7.3B9D6A35@boeing.com>`

```
as a program ages (and many Cobol programs are 30+ years old)
the comments become less and less useful as they tend not to be
maintained (they should, but reality shows they  aren't). Wrong
comments are worse than no comments. I've known people to first
delete all comments when assigned to do maintenance on an old
program.

Stephen D. Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com> wrote in message
news:3717B4F7.3B9D6A35@boeing.com...
> No program should be considered good without comments.  I can make a
> good case that the comments are just as important as the code....
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I am a long time lurker in this group and am now a novice COBOL
programmer.
> > I would like to know if this simple program is anything near
"proffessional".
> > Thank you.
> > (Don't laugh).
> >
> >
****************************************************************************
****
> >
> >        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[182 more quoted lines elided]…
> (206) 662-5937                Act ridiculously......
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371887F0.CDBE5A3F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <3717B4F7.3B9D6A35@boeing.com> <3717d41d@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> as a program ages (and many Cobol programs are 30+ years old)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Good QA stops the comments going bad.

Is it better to do put some help in and have it possibly go bad or
to assume it is bad and just not make the attempt.  In the first
'if' the comments are maintained it save a lot of time, in the
second it always takes time to find out what the program does.

Yes you can get misled by comments but we fix 'em so they do not
lead others astray.

Ken

PS:  My current boss says "I don't believe in comments", but I
will chip away at this.
```

I agree.  I tend to use a comment block at the top that gives an overview of
the code, and attempts to give a general map of how the code is organized,
but within the code I much prefer to just make the code as readable as
possible.  The only exception would be a block comment before a performed
paragraph where the code is a bit tricky, where I would do the same thing in
a micro-sense.

Aaron,

basic construct and logic is OK, I also agree with Leif's comments re
putting the full stop on a line by itself and breaking the code up by using
blank lines.

Also if you compiler supports it make your accumulators COMP fields. As COMP
fields are pure binary fields, there is not data conversion betweeen display
and comp fields required when performing arithmetic operations on them.


aarond23@my-dejanews.com wrote in message
<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>...

>I would like to know if this simple program is anything near
"proffessional".
>Thank you.
>(Don't laugh).
>
>***************************************************************************
*****
>
>       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ndAR2.4045$qx1.89650@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
>       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                          SELECT REPORT-FILE ASSIGN TO "A:\OUTPUT.TXT".

You should assign every file a status variable which can be checked after
each IO. Select statements should have at least a blank line in between.


>       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>
>       1000-MAIN-CONTROL.

Most times if paragraph numbering is used, start with 0000-MAIN. I also
prefer to only have a 1000-INITIALIZE, 2000-PROCESS AND 9000-TERMINATE in
the 0000-MAIN paragraph. Seems useless, but it is just habit to me and many
I have seen.

-PARA seems redundant.

>
>       PERFORM INIT-PARA
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>                  OPEN INPUT STOCK-FILE

If file status is used, check status after every IO and OPEN statement.
Possibly even CLOSE statements, but that can get ugly. All READs and WRITEs
should be in separate paragraphs, then checking file status logic will be in
one place.

>                  OPEN OUTPUT REPORT-FILE
>                  PERFORM READ-PARA
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 MOVE IN-STOCK-NUM TO O-STOCK-NUM

Another style could be to separate move statements into 2 lines, one for
MOVE and one for the TO (indented 2 spaces). Then if you have many OF
qualifiers it will fit nicely on a line and allow for standard indentions
for multiple TO fields. It at least makes the indentions look pretty. Such
as:

                 MOVE IN-DESCRIPTION
                   TO O-DESCRIPTION
                 MOVE IN-QUANTITY
                   TO O-QUANTITY
                 MOVE IN-UNIT-COST
                   TO O-UNIT-COST
                 COMPUTE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST
                       = IN-QUANTITY
                       * IN-UNIT-COST
                 MOVE QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST
                   TO O-EX-COST
                 ADD IN-QUANTITY
                  TO TOTAL-QUANTITY
                 ADD IN-UNIT-COST
                  TO TOTAL-UNIT-COST
                 ADD QUANTITY-TIMES-UNIT-COST
                  TO TOTAL-EX-COST
                 ADD 1 TO ITEM-COUNTER
                 WRITE REPORT-RECORD
                       FROM DETAIL-LINE
                       AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
                 .


.................................


>
>       READ-PARA.
>                READ STOCK-FILE
>                      AT END MOVE "YES" TO EOF-FLAG
>                END-READ.

An 88 level conditional would look better and checking and handling errors
depending on which are generated by your system such as:

        4000-READ-INPUT-FILE.
             READ STOCK-FILE
             PERFORM 8000-CHECK-INPUT-FILE-STATUS
             .
         ..............
        8000-CHECK-INPUT-FILE-STATUS.
             EVALUATE TRUE
                WHEN GOOD-RECORD-STATUS
                     CONTINUE
                WHEN BAD-RECORD-STATUS
                     DISPLAY 'BAD FILE STATUS FROM READ OF INPUT-FILE:'
                             INPUT-FILE-STATUS
                     GOBACK
                     <<or>>
                     STOP RUN
             END-EVALUATE
             .




Breaking up paragraph numbering can be taken to extremes. Mostly I only
differentiate between the following:
0000-   main processing
1000-   initialize of fields and files, open/close
2000-   standard processing
4000-   IO routines read/write/rewrite file and DB
8000-   error checking and handling
9000-   termination paragraphs close, report footer/totals

It may seem tedious and lengthy to break programs up like this, but the
organization will repeat itself regardless of how you write them. By
breaking it up you will make the program easier to maintain and test
possibly even shortening development time.

If you know you are going to do a bunch of similar programs, 1 input, 1
output, possibly an exception file, you can generalize the file ID's. This
will make it easier to just copy one program to another and change the
report logic. Your IO routines will remain generally the same, status check
paragraphs as well as most of the initialize paragraphs.

Don't forget, any time you are dealing with files, you should allow for
errors rather than ABENDS/Crashes. If you catch the errors, chances are you
will display a much more meaningful message than any generated by COBOL.
Files all have at least 3 conditions:
EMPTY    file status '10' after OPEN statement or first READ
NORMAL   file status '00'
MISSING  file status '35' after OPEN statement

The status variables may vary, 00 and 10 are definitely standard. These will
also be helpful when using indexed files to determine "record not found" and
such.


I know I mad a lot of red marks, but your program was very easy to
understand. Due to the relatively simple logic required, using my
programming techniques would probably have made it a little less efficient.
When the logic gets more complicated you will definitely want to break it up
in a similar manner to my suggestions. The idea of learning a good
programming style helps only when you have the freedom to develop a program
from scratch. Most times you will be trying to figure out someone else's
code and repair it. I have worked on some that were written as far back as
1972. I don't believe there was a big "structured programming" movement that
far back. Of course I was only 4 in 1972, so I could be wrong.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37173B23.E5174938@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ndAR2.4045$qx1.89650@storm.twcol.com>`

```
> Most times if paragraph numbering is used, start with 0000-MAIN. I also
> prefer to only have a 1000-INITIALIZE, 2000-PROCESS AND 9000-TERMINATE in
> the 0000-MAIN paragraph. Seems useless, but it is just habit to me and many
> I have seen.

SECTION lovers may choose to have several 2000- prefixes.  I have seen
more often 2100-, 2200-, etc.


> Another style could be to separate move statements into 2 lines, one for
> MOVE and one for the TO (indented 2 spaces). Then if you have many OF
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    TO O-QUANTITY

What an ugly, style!   I have done that when my descriptions were too
long, or when I wanted to align the two variables so that it was obvious
what the differences were.  In this case, I would move the O- under the
N-, so DESCRIPTION aligned up.


> >       READ-PARA.
> >                READ STOCK-FILE
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> depending on which are generated by your system such as:

I have mixed feelings about this.  Hard coding "YES" saves the
maintenance programmer from checking back to see the value.  With the
SET EOF-ACCOUNT-FILE TO TRUE command, this goes away.  It will be even
better when we can set it to false.

I prefer a non generic EOF-FLAG.   Name the file.

> code and repair it. I have worked on some that were written as far back as
> 1972. I don't believe there was a big "structured programming" movement that
> far back. Of course I was only 4 in 1972, so I could be wrong.

I have written COBOL code older than that.  I hadn't heard of structured
programming for another decade almost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XaNS2.5183$qx1.115639@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ndAR2.4045$qx1.89650@storm.twcol.com> <37173B23.E5174938@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>What an ugly, style!   I have done that when my descriptions were too
>long, or when I wanted to align the two variables so that it was obvious
>what the differences were.  In this case, I would move the O- under the
>N-, so DESCRIPTION aligned up.


I agree it is ugly. I started using it when working on a very complicated
history database which had many OF qualifiers, sometimes 2 or 3. I kept
doing it and found easier to modify. It may only save a few keystrokes. I
geuss its just habit.

>I have mixed feelings about this.  Hard coding "YES" saves the
>maintenance programmer from checking back to see the value.  With the
>SET EOF-ACCOUNT-FILE TO TRUE command, this goes away.  It will be even
>better when we can set it to false.


If you don't know the status codes, their values and meaning, then obviously
you should use a redundant file check, such as SET INPUT-FILE-EOF TO TRUE.
But in such a case you shouldn't be writing COBOL IO routines if you can't
look these codes up. As far as portability, this would add effort to moving
from one system to another. Personally I would rather put forth the effort
to look up and maintain the status codes to force people to actually know
what a few of them mean.

>
>I prefer a non generic EOF-FLAG.   Name the file.

I mentioned generic filenames specifically for when you know you are going
to have 3 or fewr files and a bunch of similar files being processed in the
same manner, such as report generating. In any other case with multiple
input and/or output files, naming them definitely makes more sense.

>I have written COBOL code older than that.  I hadn't heard of structured
>programming for another decade almost.

Does anyone know when exactly structured programming originated, or became
wide spread?
```

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 19 Apr 1999 17:28:06 -0400, "Jeff" <a@a.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> WHEN it becomes wide spread, let me know - OK?


The viewpoint on this is very different from shop to shop.

I had formal structured programming training in 1982, under my current
employer.  It was presented by Ken Orr and Associates using Warnier/Orr
diagrams.  My shop imediately locked in a bunch of standards, and we
have absolutely REQUIRED their use.  Now 95% of our production COBOL
source follows our structure rules and the big payback is that
maintenence is a breeze!  There were several sucess factors involved:
 * 100% management support
 * Got the shops head-techies on board first
 * Our head-techies wrote a COBOL code generator
   that automated the creation of structured pgms.
 * Everyone learned they could write stuff a
   lot faster if they stuck to the standards.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL critique needed.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fibs5$qb7$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ndAR2.4045$qx1.89650@storm.twcol.com> <37173B23.E5174938@NOSPAMhome.com> <XaNS2.5183$qx1.115639@storm.twcol.com>`

```

Jeff wrote in message ...
[...]
>Does anyone know when exactly structured programming originated, or became
>wide spread?
>
It is generally acknowledged that the paper by B�hm and Jacopini
provded the seed for what is called "Structured Programming."

    C. B�hm and G. Jacopini, "Flow Diagrams, Turing Machines,
    and Languages with Only Two Formation Rules,"
    Communications of the ACM, May 1966, pages 366-371.

E. W. Dijkstra used the term "Structured Programming" in 1969.

    E. W. Dijkstra, "Notes on Structured Programming," EWD 249,
    Technical University, Eindhoven, Netherlands, 1969.

As a result of this paper, some have called Dijkstra "the father
of structured progamming."

Books for using structured programming with different languages
began appearing in 1972.

Edward Yourdon, in 1975, wrote "... some COBOL groups have
begun finding that their version of COBOL ... lacks some of the
features that makes structured programming convenient. Some
have discovered, for example, that their version of COBOL
compiles nested IF statements incorrectly ...; others have found
that the compiler limits the number of PERFORM statements
that may appear in programs; ... "

    Edward Yourdon, "Techniques of Program Structure and
    Design," Prentice-Hall, 1975, page 180.

The period 1975 through 1979 was a time of advances in the
development of tools and methodologies for designing systems
for which structured programming was the method for writing code.

Certainly, by the early 1980's, structured programming had become
wide spread.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ File Status Codes (was Re: COBOL critique needed.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f72m3$r14@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7f5qou$dbp$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ndAR2.4045$qx1.89650@storm.twcol.com>`

```
Jeff wrote in message ...
>>       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>each IO. Select statements should have at least a blank line in between.


This is almost as "religious" a topic as what editor to use or whether to
use Paragraphs or Sections.  You will get highly animated replies related to
this suggestions both PRO and CON).

On IBM mainframes (for example), it is almost (but not quite) universal to
include AND CHECK file status codes for all indexed (VSAM) files - but is
highly unusual (and often frowned upon) to include a file status field for
sequential (QSAM) files.  There are (valid?) reasons for doing this - having
to do with how the (proprietary) COBOL system works with the (proprietary)
operating system.

The bottom-line is that *IF* you reference a file-status field in your
Select/Assign clause - then you *MUST* check it after every I/O operation -
and be aware of the following:

1) Not all "successful" I/O results in an '00' file status; other 0x values
are successful - but meaningful - if you care.

2) All file status codes in the 9x range are implementor defined and you
need to understand how to decipher and handle them. If you are attempting to
create "portable" code, these may well be a problem.

3) *NEW* file status codes *can* be added when maintain occurs to your
compiler or run-time (because of ANSI/ISO Standard changes or new 9x values
by the implementor).  If you use file status code checking, try to put it in
"common  code" (called routine or COPY statement) that can EASILY be
enhanced to handle new values.
```

```
>>>       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
>>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>


Specifically I usually only use the STATUS check for EOF. But, if you don't
know what the codes mean you shouldn't be using them. I make it a point to
know them everywhere and on every system I use. I use them habitually, and
this forces me to learn them. It also may be slightly more efficient, and
would only be important for extremely large quantities of IO.
```

Aaron,

very picky about the way things are done not the business concept

Ken

>that would not even be mentioned in 90% of the shops around.


Very Very good point.

As previously mentioned, "if you get paid for it, its professional."

Sloppy code does help one learn why structured programming emerged in the
first place.

Remember to look at the comments. I only saw one that looked like it could
be an error, the one about the accumulator decimal points. Most commented it
was an easy to follow program, and that is the most important thing to
writing a program. Most peoples styles are a result of college professors,
and shop standards. Both of which nit-pick at the justifications for the
standards. New programmers will have to learn these and adapt to them.

A very good start.
```

```
Hi Aarond,
    your programm looks not so bad.
I donï¿½t have read all the replies, but here are my changings which I would use:
1)  use of file status
2)  read first record and then process "perform until"-loop one advantage is that
you donï¿½t have to initialize the stop-condition

aarond23@my-dejanews.com schrieb:

> I am a long time lurker in this group and am now a novice COBOL programmer.
> I would like to know if this simple program is anything near "proffessional".
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>                           ASSIGN TO "A:\CH08EX01.DAT".

new:
                            file status is file-status

>
>
>        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

new.
                    01  file-status.
                          02 fstat1 pic x.
                               88 file-ok value "0".
                          02 fstat2 pic x.

>        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>

>        1000-MAIN-CONTROL.
>
>                PERFORM INIT-PAR

                   perform read-para
                   perform until not file-ok
                       IF IN-STOCK-NUM NOT = HOLD-STOCK-NUM
                            PERFORM DESCRIPTION-BREAK-PARA
                       END-IF
                       PERFORM PROCESS-PARA
                       perform read-para
                   end-perform
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RMCOBOL/85 & ORACLE

- **From:** Albert Ratzlaff <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717147D.F9E9C790@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<7f4qsk$jj3$1@unbe.sarenet.es> <37160c28.12200817@news.enter.net> <37167696.341748267@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> On Thu, 15 Apr 1999 15:57:48 GMT, rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> install, so they should be able to find it!

It does not work with RM/COBOL, because RM/COBOL programs are compiled to
intermediate code and then interpreted by a runtime "runcobol". But Liant
has a product that used to be called DB/Plus (I think they changed the
name), that allows a transparent access to Oracle (and Informix and some
other) databases. You don't even recompile. You activate a kind of file
server, and if the runtime finds the file in the database, it "connects"
you there. If not, it looks in the normal places - the file system.
You use the database tables like normal COBOL files. Every write, update,
delete is commited, so you don't really have transactions (in the
database). Also, no SQL.
If you need SQL, you would have to write your own routines with Oracle's
ProC, and link them to the RM/COBOL runtime. Then you could call, for
example, CALL "SQL-XXX" USING "whatever".

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

#### ↳ Re: RMCOBOL/85 & ORACLE

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f58ks$3g1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7f4qsk$jj3$1@unbe.sarenet.es>`

```
itziar lopez wrote in message <7f4qsk$jj3$1@unbe.sarenet.es>...
>What do I need to use an Oracle Db from a  cobol program ?

Acucobol offers native access to Oracle db, see www.acucorp.com for details.

Cheesle
```

## Thread: cobol & web — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### cobol & web

- **From:** "Vyacheslav Danovich" <vdanovich@empireone.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W3pR2.1222$fl5.206241826@dca1-nnrp1.news.digex.net>`

```

there any way to put in on the web?
```

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f58mh$3g5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<W3pR2.1222$fl5.206241826@dca1-nnrp1.news.digex.net>`

```
Vyacheslav Danovich wrote in message ...
>I've got an existing multiple file program written in AcuCobol 4.0.  Is
>there any way to put in on the web?

Acucobol has plugin capability for browsers. See www.acucorp.com for
details.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol & web

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371744f1.5819911@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<W3pR2.1222$fl5.206241826@dca1-nnrp1.news.digex.net>`

```
"Vyacheslav Danovich" <vdanovich@empireone.net> wrote:

>I've got an existing multiple file program written in AcuCobol 4.0.  Is
>there any way to put in on the web?
>
>

Vyacheslav:

Do you want to put it "on the web" or do you want to put it "on the
Internet?"  I know this question sounds a bit strange, but there
really is a difference.

In our efforts to place a COBOL user interface "on the web" we ran
into so many problems...not with the COBOL, but with using a world
wide browser as a user interface mechanism.  To put it bluntly...world
wide web browsers as commercial application user interface
environments really stink.

Sometimes it IS necessary to use a web based "front end" for your
application.  My personal "rule of thumb" is that when you want to
reach millions of people with your application, then going with a web
browser interface is probably the way to go...BUT if you have a
specific group of people you want to have access to your application,
then you really should look at other methods of "front ending" your
COBOL application.

It is possible to run your COBOL application as a "thin client"
application across TCP/IP which means that you are running it NOT
through a web server/web browser environment, but bypassing the entire
web and running it in a much MORE native and much less interpretive
manner.

You avoid the instabilities and massive overhead of web browsers as
well as the lack of control of end users installing the beta version
of the latest and greatest browser.

Although we provide thin client enabling tools for COBOL, we are by no
means the only ones who do this.  I think that Acucobol can provide
you with thin client tools and you can also write your own thin client
"front end" to your COBOL application using products such as Visual
Basic or Delphi.

I just wanteed to hop up on the old soapbox and get people to start
thinking about the fact that just because you want to run an
application across the Internet, does NOT necessarily mean you have to
use the world wide web to do so.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

---

- **From:** jflieder@hornet.csc.calpoly.edu (Jeremy Benjamin Flieder)
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.visual,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<7f516f$jji$1@cscnews.csc.calpoly.edu>`
- **References:** `<K0UN2.1485$Pv5.43249@news.total.net> <3711322B.FD84C31C@mindspring.com> <37123217.3489744@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37129C91.F576F2CE@bounce.net>`

```
In reading these posts, I'd just like to throw my $.02 into the mix...

First of all, lying isn't necessary to get a job... if you got the skills
that the employer needs, then you have a legitimate chance of getting the
job. However, I see no real harm in embellishing a little or tailoring a
resume; maybe putting stuff that you were capable of but never actually did.

My sister basically did this on her job search... she has a master's degree
in botany, but found that there are practically no jobs out there, so she
learn a little programming and HTML and agressively looked for a job.
Specifically, she'd spend 40 hrs/wk sending out tailored resumes & cover
letters, contacting to companies to check if they'd received the resume thus
showing interest, and studying up on the company before the interview. She
eventually landed a job doing some HTML and she's now learning DB admin
stuff.

I just got a job myself after a pretty half-assed search for about 4 months.
Actually, I'm typing this from the training course... they're teaching
elementary UNIX right now. Anyway, I just graduated w/ a BS in Comp. Sci.;
other than that, I worked on a co-op for 6 months one year before and I was
working at a R&D facility on campus programming in Java. I fully utilized my 
campus career services; I brought in my resume probably a dozen times and it
was reviewed by like 10 different people. I attended the interview skills
class, I went to each job fair, and signed up for on campus interview.
Granted I didn't get my current job this way (I got this one by passing out
my resume at a job fair not run by the school), I know at least 10 people
that did. I think what prevented me from getting second interviews or not
getting offers was that I didn't show any knowledge of whatever company I
was interviewing with. All I did on the interview for my job was ask some
lame question about one of their products... I got an offer 2 days later. I
guess the best advice is just be persitent and have faith that you are good
enough and there is a job out there for you.

Hope this helps and good luck....

						Jeremy Flieder
						NEON, Inc.
```

<a id="t-4bc15c8e47"></a>
## Thread: **Urgent - Latest draft of work related to next Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### **Urgent - Latest draft of work related to next Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f5i8m$lll@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
For those interested in the revision schedule for the next Standard, the
latest *DRAFT* is available at:

  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0187.doc

I am *not* a member of any delegation to the WG4 (ISO) COBOL working group -
but I think that the various country delegations are currently "reviewing"
this schedule.  If you have an opinion about this schedule (especially if
you understand ISO "requirements") - I suggest that you look at this
document

    AND LET YOUR COUNTRY DELEGATION KNOW

what your opinion is (good, bad, or otherwise).

For those in the US, you can email your comments to

     doncobol <at> mediaone.net

For those outside of the US,  I suggest that you check the WG4 home page and
find out how to contact your national delegation.  See
  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0187.doc

It is my personal opinion that this schedule (like every schedule before it)
is overly optimistic - but neither do I see any "better" schedule that would
both meet ISO (SC22) rules *and* produce a quality/useful document.   I am
posting this note because I (unlike some others) feel that there is too
little "outside" input to the standardization process.
```

<a id="t-2cbc63ce05"></a>
## Thread: How are COMP and COMP-3 used — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: How are COMP and COMP-3 used

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iZsR2.917$fP2.45443@paloalto-snr1>`
- **References:** `<3712C050.104E@freeseworks.com>`

```
For information on the bit patterns used in most COBOL data types, download
the free text and graphics tutorial COBDATA.ZIP from
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html.

As far as "why" a designer will choose COMP v COMP-3, COMP-5, etc, it
usually is a function of the specific target hardware on which the product
will run.  However, numeric fields (i.e., those on which you wish to do
arithmetic) should always be COMP or COMP-whatever for best perfromance.

MCM
(author of the tutorial mentioned above)

As one of the "winning" NO votes, I want to restate that I really DO welcome
all AcuCOBOL *and* Acucorp discussions in comp.lang.cobol.

My suggestions would be:

1) Put AcuCOBOL or Acucorp (depending on whether you are talking about COBOL
or add-on products) in the Subject line (possibly first)

2) Those in comp.lang.cobol who do NOT want to view Acucorp issues, should
add this word to their NG "filter file".

Should (in 6 months or so), there be "too many" Acucorp posts - then, I
would support a revised definition of a new newsgroup.
```

<a id="t-9a902a9f7b"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu - Select/Assign for SD's - ANSI Conforming in V4.2 — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu - Select/Assign for SD's - ANSI Conforming in V4.2

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f3v4o$o0b@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Already covered in comp.lang.cobol - but I am putting this out with a new
"subject" for the purpose of DejaNews)

For those who have (ever) had problems with specifying a literal in the
Select/Assign statement associated with an SD - in Fujitsu COBOL,  there has
been a change made in V4.2.  The message against this is no longer an
"error" message - but is an informational message only.  Therefore, you can
get a "clean compile" with either an implementor-name or a literal in the
Assign clause.

Therefore, if you have a problem with such source code, you have two choices
available to you:

1) Upgrade to V4.2 (or higher?)

2) Change your
      Select SD-Name Assign to "C:wherever/there".
          to something like
      Select SD-Name Assign to Any-ignored-name-you-want.
```

- **From:** sshealy8558@my-dejanews.com

We are trying to run a cobol program on AIX 4.2.1 on an SP.  We are using IBM
Cobol Set(v 1.0)  to compile.  The program compiles and links  fine... but
when we run it we get the following error.

IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'INFILE'. File status is: 35.
File  system indicated: File Not Found.  Message routine called from offset
0x490 of routine _iwzCallUseProc.  _iwzCallUseProc called from offset 0xea8
of routine _iwzSeqOpen.  _iwzSeqOpen called from offset 0x354 of routine
_iwzGenericOpen.  _iwzGenericOpen called from offset 0x328 of routine
MSASPIP1. IWZ901S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.

runip[12]: 15646 IOT/Abort trap

A snippet of the Code is included below. The error message seems to indicate
either the file is not there or that we don't have permission to read the
file.  But niether of these are true.

We use a script to set the enviroment variables for the files and is shown
below.

set FILEIN=/tapefs/ipclm.ebc
set COM=/tapefs/comip
set EDI=/tapefs/ediip
set ORI=/tapefs/oriip
set DGN=/tapefs/dgnip
set PRC=/tapefs/prcip
set CON=/tapefs/conip
set OCR=/tapefs/ocrip
set SPA=/tapefs/spaip
set VAL=/tapefs/valip
set REV=/tapefs/revip
ip.run

We are very new to runing COBOL on the AIX platform.  Any help or experience
would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks,
Scott Shealy
sshealy@asgnet.psc.sc.edu

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-370.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT  INFILE ASSIGN FILEIN.
           SELECT  OUT-COM               ASSIGN  COM.
           SELECT  OUT-EDIT              ASSIGN  EDI.
           SELECT  OUT-ORIG              ASSIGN  ORI.
           SELECT  OUT-DGNS              ASSIGN  DGN.
           SELECT  OUT-PRC               ASSIGN  PRC.
           SELECT  OUT-COND              ASSIGN  CON.
           SELECT  OUT-OCRNC             ASSIGN  OCR.
           SELECT  OUT-SPAN              ASSIGN  SPA.
           SELECT  OUT-VAL               ASSIGN  VAL.
           SELECT  OUT-REV               ASSIGN  REV.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD  INFILE
           RECORDING MODE IS V
      LABEL RECORD IS STANDARD
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           RECORD VARYING 455 TO 7502
           DATA RECORD IS RECIN.

       ..... Record layouts

PROCEDURE DIVISION.  00001350  00001360  0000-MAINLINE.  DISPLAY 'Opening
FIles 'COM.  PERFORM 0100-OPEN-FILES.  PERFORM 0150-READ-INPUT-FILE.  PERFORM
0500-PROCESS-RECORDS UNTIL END-OF-FILE.  PERFORM 0200-CLOSE-FILES.  STOP RUN.

  0100-OPEN-FILES.  00001370  OPEN  INPUT  INFILE  00001380  OUTPUT OUT-COM 
00001390  OUTPUT OUT-EDIT  OUTPUT OUT-ORIG  OUTPUT OUT-DGNS  OUTPUT OUT-PRC 
OUTPUT OUT-COND  OUTPUT OUT-OCRNC  OUTPUT OUT-SPAN  OUTPUT OUT-VAL  OUTPUT
OUT-REV.  00001400  0150-READ-INPUT-FILE.  00001410  READ INFILE  00001420 
AT END MOVE 'Y' TO WS-END-OF-FILE.  00001430  IF WS-END-OF-FILE = 'N'  ADD 1
TO TOTAL-INPUT-RECS-READ.  00001520

       0200-CLOSE-FILES.

... Rest of program

Thanks for any help,
Scott Shealy
sshealy@asgnet.psc.sc.edu

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own

#### ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ERROR

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fj0o2$sll@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7f7np5$1vl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
The reply from my "usually reliable sources" is as follows,

"It looks like Scott is using the "C Shell".  In his script to set the
environment variables and run his program are statements such as "set
FILEIN=/tapefs/ipclm.ebc".  His COBOL program then uses the environment
variable FILEIN to get the input file name.  But the "set" command of the
"C Shell" does not set environment variables, it sets local shell
variables.  To set environment variables he should use the "setenv"
command:
   setenv FILEIN /tapefs/ipclm.ebc
Then the COBOL runtime will find FILEIN and its value."

Let me (and/or the NG) know if this "solves" the problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ERROR

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<429W2.1169$Sc5.4310@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<7f7np5$1vl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I don't use that compiler but here are two things I could think of:
- Does the environment variable need to hve a special name, e.g. start with
"dd_" ?
- Do you have to export the environment variable to have it available in
COBOL's context
   e.g.  instead of "set DGN=/tapefs/dgnip" you would write "export
DGN=/tapefs/dgnip"

All the best,

- Darius

## Thread: API calls from MF COBOL — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### API calls from MF COBOL

- **From:** "Guus" <guus.van.der.kleij@twinsoft.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f6uck$rka$1@zonnetje.NL.net>`

```
Hi there,

I'm currently conducting some test to do the following:

I'm trying to build a service from COBOL that performs some database-queries
and than waits a given time, before repeating the operation, etc.

I know how to create a time-loop within the COBOL program in order to
generate the wait time, but this will always take up system resources.

Another posibility (and my choice) is to invoke the Sleep-API from Windows
NT (where the program wil have to run on).

I can find how to call the API in my Micro Focus manuals.
I can create an executable and run it, but whenever I run it, I get an error
on the API-call.

I've linked the KERNEL32.LIB and 2 Micro Focus libraries. Do I need the
USER32.LIB?

Does anyone have an example program and directions on the way it is linked?

Any help is welcome.

Thanks.
```

Guus <guus.van.der.kleij@twinsoft.nl> wrote in message
news:7f6uck$rka$1@zonnetje.NL.net...
> Hi there,
>
> I'm currently conducting some test to do the following:
>

database-queries
> and than waits a given time, before repeating the operation, etc.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Another posibility (and my choice) is to invoke the Sleep-API from
Windows
> NT (where the program wil have to run on).
>

> I can create an executable and run it, but whenever I run it, I get an
error
> on the API-call.
>

the
> USER32.LIB?
>
> Does anyone have an example program and directions on the way it is
linked?
>
> Any help is welcome.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
SPECIAL-NAMES.
     CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS WIN32API.

* Sleep for 1 second
CALL WIN32API "Sleep" USING
    BY VALUE 1000 size 4
END-CALL.

Relevant compiler directives are "CASE" and "LITLINK".

Link using  -- cbllink  -L -R4.0 -S -omyProg.exe myProg.obj
kernel32.lib

If you're planning on waiting for a long time you may be better off
using WaitForMultipleObjects() rather than Sleep, so you have a
mechanism for waking up the sleeping task.
```

#### ↳ Re: API calls from MF COBOL

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f7jng$60l$1@news.cerf.net>`
