```
Guus wrote in message <7f6uck$rka$1@zonnetje.NL.net>...
>I've linked the KERNEL32.LIB and 2 Micro Focus libraries. Do I need the
>USER32.LIB?
>

Why would you link? These are shipped as DLL's. I don't know how you use a
DLL in MF, but I am most certain it is possible, and a better approach.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: API calls from MF COBOL

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SBKR2.889$KO5.82004@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7f6uck$rka$1@zonnetje.NL.net> <7f7jng$60l$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle <cheesle@online.no> wrote in message
news:7f7jng$60l$1@news.cerf.net...
> Guus wrote in message <7f6uck$rka$1@zonnetje.NL.net>...
> >I've linked the KERNEL32.LIB and 2 Micro Focus libraries. Do I need
the
> >USER32.LIB?
> >
>
> Why would you link? These are shipped as DLL's. I don't know how you
use a
> DLL in MF, but I am most certain it is possible, and a better
approach.
>
> Cheesle
>
>
KERNEL32.LIB is an export library for the DLL. It does not contain the
routines in the DLL. It contains redirections to the routines in the
DLL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: API calls from MF COBOL

## Thread: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs) — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

- **From:** bfetml@aol.com (BFETML)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990415215548.11150.00000903@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
hi,
Any one out there knows how syncsort determines how many work fields to use and
how many cylinders, tracks, blocks to use in the sort, in primary and secondary
allocations?

thanks

eric
```

#### ↳ Re: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XQJR2.170$HP5.4116@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<19990415215548.11150.00000903@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
The Syncsort manual I have is old (and so is the version I'm using,
3.4, back from 1989), but since you've mentioned cylinders, I take it
you're on a mainframe?

If you are, it basically comes down to providing enough SORTWKnn
space, at least a little larger than the sorting file, and preferably
about double.  Your documentation should contain a recommendation
formula.  If it doesn't, post back (with your version and OS), and
I'll dig through mine and check.

Example, if I have a file with SPACE=(221,(200,100),RLSE),AVGREC=K
wherever the SORTIN file is being created;
then I usually try for SORTWK01 DD
UNIT=WORK,SPACE=(221,(400,200),RLSE),AVGRECK=K.

If you're experiencing a shortage of usable sort space at your
location, you might want to consider cutting down the amount.



BTW: While Syncsort (at least in 3.4) will allow you to use SORTWKn as
long as you're only doing SORTIN and SORTOUT, if you have multiple
output files using SORTOFnn, you must have SORTWKnn.  That is to say,
you have to have two digits after SORTWK, or Syncsort will be unable
to recognize the ddnames.  I don't know if this has been corrected in
later versions of Syncsort.



BFETML <bfetml@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19990415215548.11150.00000903@ng-ca1.aol.com...
> hi,
> Any one out there knows how syncsort determines how many work fields
to use and
> how many cylinders, tracks, blocks to use in the sort, in primary
and secondary
> allocations?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990416230241.19001.00000569@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<XQJR2.170$HP5.4116@news.uswest.net>`

```
In article <XQJR2.170$HP5.4116@news.uswest.net>, "ChrisOsborne"
<chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> writes:

>
>If you are, it basically comes down to providing enough SORTWKnn
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Where I am, we let Syncsort figure out the SORTWK's on its own.  We only
code the SORTIN and SORTOUT.  On the EXEC line we add the following
PARM='DYNALLOC=(SORT,32),BMSG'.
This has worked beautifully when working with 45M records of 1K-bytes.
```

>I'll have to read up on dynamic allocation . . .
>
>. . . okay, what the manual says seems pretty easy.  I'll try it out.
>

Your shop *may* have dynamic allocation
set up as the default. You can find out quickly
by attempting to sort a large file with no SORTWKs and no DYNAMIC parm. If it
doesn't choke, dynamic is the default.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** bfetml@aol.com (BFETML)
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990421015414.16653.00003034@ng148.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418101521.19252.00002118@ng24.aol.com>`

```
hi,
thanks for your replies to this sortwknn question.  It took me a while to find
anything definitive, but Dr. Watson finally returned from his safari, and
unfortunately what I have is still not conclusive, but I better write this
stuff down before I forget it.

From the posts I have concluded that:
1.sortwknn dd are not always necessary.
2.Use more storage than the input file up to double its file size.

From a more recent syncsort manual:
1. Use cylinders if you can it is more efficient.
2. Secondary allocation is not necessary (I think,need to double check this
point).


From my team members and associates:
1.Take this old synsort manual it might have something.
2.Let us know what you find out.
3.Just use what every one uses.

From a really old Syncsort manual:
1.
 (A * 1.15) /B will give you the total number of tracks you need.
A=(# records  * avg record length) -basically A is tota1 bytes.
B=Track capacity of the device you are using.

So if I have recfm =fb, lrecl= 1000, #recs=5000
I need (1000*5000)/47476

47476 because it looks like at our site 3380 disks are used for sorts.  (I got
47476 from a jcl text)

hence I need 5000000/47476=105.3 tracks. It is probably wise to round upwards
in this case, so I need 106 tracks to do this sort.

Since for the 3380 for max  blksize of 47476 1cylinder = 15tracks
then 106/15 =7.06 cylinders.

If I am using as I plan to do use sortwk01 thru 03 usually,  7/3 =2.3. Rouding
up again  If I use 3 cylinder for each of the 3 sortwknn I should be fine. 
Since I do not want any abends ever. I might consider using a secondary
allocation of half the primary allocation for all 3 sortwknn.

Everything above seems to be relatively accuarate.  It is the last paragraph I
am uncertain about.  comments? any systems folks out there?

thanks everyone for your responses once again.

eric
```

#### ↳ Re: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pnAR2.4047$qx1.89097@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990415215548.11150.00000903@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
>Any one out there knows how syncsort determines how many work fields to use
and
>how many cylinders, tracks, blocks to use in the sort, in primary and
secondary
>allocations?


That would depend on the length and type of the chosen sort, include, and
omit fields which are used. It would further depend on the current file
size, output file(s) size(s) and the available space on the particular
devices which are available to your DASD class,job class, or whatever is
used to determine devices.

No simple answers. Just take it for granted that SyncSort knows what its
doing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: syncsort sortwknn field? (mvs)

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990416081935.11450.00002097@ng-ch1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<pnAR2.4047$qx1.89097@storm.twcol.com>`

```
we ususally just allocate our own work space(s) by using SORTWKn where "n" is a
number 1-6 or more, and RLSE.
We usually give each a SPACE parameter of 100 tracks primary and 100 tracks
secondary.

TW
```

## Thread: question on subprograms — 6 msgs

### question on subprograms

- **From:** twozoogs@mailexcite.com (zoogs)
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net>`

```
A few questions on calling subprograms:

1) Do you call the subprogram once or for every file read?

2) Can I move the new data from the subprogram into working-storage in
the calling program?

I'm using an ibm-risc-6000 computer at school.

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: question on subprograms

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f8v83$9g5$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net>`

```

zoogs wrote in message <3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net>...
>A few questions on calling subprograms:
>
>1) Do you call the subprogram once or for every file read?

## You call a subprogram every time you need it to do something.
It does its thing and returns control to the calling program. If its
thing is to read the entire file and return the number of records,
you call it once. If it is to read one record and return that record
to the calling program for further processing, you call the subprogram
for every record in the file.
The number of times a subprogram can get called varies from zero to
infinity.

>
>2) Can I move the new data from the subprogram into working-storage in
>the calling program?


## Yes. The calling program (generally) passes WORKING-STORAGE
data which, in turn, is defined in the LINKAGE SECTION of the called
program. From the LINKAGE SECTION in the called program, you may
move data to the local WORKING STORAGE section of the subprogram.


>I'm using an ibm-risc-6000 computer at school.


## For the questions you raised, the type of computer is (usually)
irrelevant.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: question on subprograms

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371af80a.529726@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net> <7f8v83$9g5$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
On Fri, 16 Apr 1999 22:32:52 -0500, "Jerry Peacock"
<bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:

>## Yes. The calling program (generally) passes WORKING-STORAGE
>data which, in turn, is defined in the LINKAGE SECTION of the called
                                        ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^  -->> NOT
necessarily
>program. From the LINKAGE SECTION in the called program, you may
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^  -->> NOT necessarily
>move data to the local WORKING STORAGE section of the subprogram.


FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question on subprograms

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fd02s$35n@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net> <7f8v83$9g5$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <371af80a.529726@news.indigo.ie>`

```
I can't tell from this note, exactly who is saying what to whom, BUT,  it is
*always* legal to move data from the Linkage Section to Working-Storage
section in a sub-program - in an ANSI conforming program.

There *may* be issues issues for some programs that are not ANSI conforming
(i.e. that use extensions).  I can think so some RARE cases where
"addressability" to a linkage section item isn't available - such where a
SET ADDRESS statement hasn't been performed yet, or for CICS items that have
yet to be "set".  However, once addressability to such items has been
established, even in these "extension" applications, it is then legal
(valid) to move data from linkage section to working-storage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: question on subprograms

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#4GSKXpi#GA.332@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3717cda4.3325503@news.prodigy.net> <7f8v83$9g5$1@birch.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <371af80a.529726@news.indigo.ie> <7fd02s$35n@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Another example would be optional parameters for subroutines designed to
handle different number of parameters depending on certain conditions, such
as the value of one or more parameters which are not optional.

>"addressability" to a linkage section item isn't available - such where a
>SET ADDRESS statement hasn't been performed yet, or for CICS items that
have
>yet to be "set".  However, once addressability to such items has been
>established, even in these "extension" applications, it is then legal
>(valid) to move data from linkage section to working-storage.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7fa7sg$ohc$1@news.igs.net>`

## Thread: Conversion — 4 msgs

### Conversion

- **From:** "Kris Goegebeur" <eximco@unicall.be>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f6tpp$ra9$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
Hello,

Can someone provide a tool/utility/procedure/... to convert COBOL data files
to plain ASCII-text.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37173171.389590600@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f6tpp$ra9$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
On Fri, 16 Apr 1999 10:55:41 +0200, "Kris Goegebeur"
<eximco@unicall.be> wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

Let me explain.  

This is like asking - "Can someone provide a
tool/utility/procedure/... to convert a RDBS data file to plain
ASCII-text".  Which one?  

Try to convert a Oracle database to ASCII, without using Oracle!
Try to convert a Microsoft Access database (.mdb) file, without using
Access!
There are numerous brands of COBOL on numerous systems - SOME use
proprietary, vendor invented, file strutures.  Others use the native
file structure of the platform involved.  

We need more information.  Companies such as LIANT provide ODBC
drivers to access many different type of COBOL data files.  There is a
company called "Data Junction" that makes a very good tool for
converting various data formats, take a look at that.  Tell us which
COBOL version, brand and platform and we'll try to help further.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** emilne@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f7o76$25m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7f6tpp$ra9$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
The Fujitsu v3 COBOL includes a data converter that should do the job.	You
can download the package from www.adtools.com.

> Can someone provide a tool/utility/procedure/... to convert COBOL data files
> to plain ASCII-text.


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Conversion

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3717d509.2387675@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f6tpp$ra9$1@trex.antw.online.be> <7f7o76$25m$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 16 Apr 1999 16:24:18 GMT, emilne@my-dejanews.com wrote:

>The Fujitsu v3 COBOL includes a data converter that should do the job.	You
>can download the package from www.adtools.com.
>

The 4.2 product comes with a MicroFocus File format to Fujitsu
converter, but v3 did not (and I'm not sure about v4).  The data
converter will convert plain ASCII to indexed, and Fujitsu indexed to
Ascii.  It does not handle Realia, AcuCOBOL, MicroFocus, MBP etc, data
file formats.
```

### Problems with a signed pic clause *******

- **From:** vinesDRKDDD@ukonline.co.uk
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>`

```

I am a student.......beating my head against the putter
for the last 3 nights......finally got the program to run with good
output.  Went to pretty it up, and have tried everything that I can
find in my books (stern & stern / welburn & price) 

the pic I have is :  PIC SZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9V99 SIGN IS TRAILING SEPARATE
CHARACTER.

I have tried every variation of this that is imaginable.......in
addition to what is in the books.  I am using microfocus on my pc at
home.  This is the only error that I have, and it is killing me.

also - the requirements are calling for a right most fixed space and
CR symbol for a negative value.

if you can think of anything, please give me a shot back......

more direct e-mail is slplss@earthlink.com

once again thank you,

Jason

#### ↳ Re: Problems with a signed pic clause *******

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37174B9B.ACCE6F5E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>`

```
vinesDRKDDD@ukonline.co.uk wrote:
> 
> I am a student.......beating my head against the putter
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jason

What's your error?
If you are supposed to use CR for the negative value, why isn't that
part of your picture?  CR, DB, +, and _ are PICTURE symbols which are
used to display signs.  B is a picture symbol which creates a space.   

Your commas will print out, but they make it a display field, not a
number anymore.  So you probably want a decimal point instead of a V
(implied decimal).

Generally, you use the V to show decimal point when you're going to do
calculations, then move it to something like PIC CRZZZ,999,999.99B to
display/print it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems with a signed pic clause *******

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K2IR2.16$_U3.29703@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>`

```

vinesDRKDDD@ukonline.co.uk wrote in message
<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>...
>
>I am a student.......beating my head against the putter
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have tried every variation of this that is imaginable
[snip]
>
>also - the requirements are calling for a right most fixed space and
>CR symbol for a negative value.
>
>if you can think of anything, please give me a shot back......

Hmmm.  I pick up my copy of Welburn & Price, Third Edition, look in the
_index_ for CR and am whisked away to Chapter 5.  In my book Figure 5-18
(fixed insertion editing - credit (CR)) has almost exactly what you need.

You might also check Numeric Editing - Special Insertion, PICTURE Symbol .
(Decimal Point).

The PICTURE symbols S and V  are  _not_ editing symbols.  Also, rid yourself
of the SIGN clause.

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems with a signed pic clause *******

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PBIR2.38$jb4.12364@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>`

```
You use the 'S' and 'V' PIC characters for a signed numeric data
item in internal format.  You use '.' and either '-', '+', 'CR' or
'DB' for a numeric edited field for human readable output.  Here
is how the sign editing characters work:

  RECV  VALUE IS  VALUE IS
  PIC   NEGATIVE  POSITIVE
  ----  --------  --------
  '-'     '-'       ' '
  '+'     '-'       '+'
  'CR'    'CR'      '  '
  'DB'    'DB'      '  '

The "SIGN IS TRAILING SEPARATE" has no effect on this PIC.  That
phrase controls the way 'S' is stored for numeric data items, not
numeric edited items, where the PIC supplies the exact format.

Unless specifically instructed to do so, you should not place a fixed
sign to the left of a zero suppressed field as you have below.  Look
at these pictures and the results when used on two consecutive fields
on a printed report, separated by one space:

     PICTURES          MOVE 12.34 and -56.78
  --------------   -------------------------------
  SZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.99   '         12.34 -        56.78'
  ---,---,--9.99   '         12.34         -56.78'
  ZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.99-   '        12.34          56.78-'

As you can see, in the first example above, the negative sign for the
second value can easily be erroneously associated with the positive
12.34, or missed altogether when looking at the 56.78.

One other tip.  Numeric data is naturally right justified, and alpha-
numeric data is naturally left justified.  When formatting a report
with two adjacent, associated fields, one numeric and the other a long
alphanumeric field such as name, try to place the numeric field to the
left of the alphanumeric field, so the eye can easily associate the
two.  Take a look at how some bonehead at my bank formatted part of
their checking account statements:

  DATE   DESCRIPTION                                            AMOUNT
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99
99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX                                       999.99

On a long listing, you need a straight edge of some sort to associate
the often short descriptions on the left with the amounts on the right.

Here is how it should have been formatted:

      AMOUNT   DATE   DESCRIPTION
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
      999.99 99/99/99 XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

Because of their proximity, the eye has no difficulty in associating
the amount with the correct date and description, even when they are
very short.

Remember: reports are meant for human eyes; always format your reports
for easy and accurate visual assimilation.  The same applies equally,
or even more so, to formatting your source code, which is also meant
for human eyes.  The compiler doesn't care, your eyes and brain do.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problems with a signed pic clause *******

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990416134210.11458.00002444@ng-ch1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<PBIR2.38$jb4.12364@news4.mia>`

```
it has been a while since I have messed around with MF Cobol, but it used to be
that this version would only accept SIGN LEADING SEPERATE.  I remember this
because our UNIX version had SIGN TRAILING and in oredr to test in on the PC
side using MF Cobol we had to change every statement to read SIGN LEADING.

Do not know if this helps, but if anyone knows if MicroFocus has changed this,
I would appreciate knowing.

TW
```

#### ↳ What separate signs are good for

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#tFpPCIi#GA.141@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7f7eau$m2d$1@apple.news.easynet.net>`

```
Where separate signs come in handy is for conversions, especially to
different platforms and character codes, where you don't want to trust that
the internal formats will be correctly converted. Sign overpunch codes (and
sign nybbles for packed fields) could be mistranslated, especially if
numeric display fields are converted from EBCDIC to ASCII (because they're
character fields after all) but different overpunch codes are used in that
particular cobol.

The safest (and probably most time-consuming) way to convert e.g. IBM
mainframe data to Unix or PC is to first reformat each file (copy from old
to new) converting ALL numeric fields (whether binary, packed decimal, or
numeric/zoned display) to e.g. Pic S9(m)V9(n) Sign Separate. Your choice of
leading or trailing but be consistent. Floating point fields of course need
to be converted to an appropriate format, I'm not sure what, may need more
custom tailoring in each case. The idea is to produce a format that's still
numeric rather than numeric edited, but still has a separate sign so
there's no overpunch characters. If your target Cobol compiler supports
de-editing or the new functions that return the numeric value of a numeric
edited data item, that's even better, you can convert to an appropriate
numeric edited format so it's easier for people to read too. You could
insert a space or suitable delimiter e.g. | between each field if you like
on output.

Then you'll have a simple all-character file that you can just run through
a dumb EBCDIC to ASCII character translator, and on the target machine,
just write the reverse program from the original (or a different one if
needed) to reformat the data to what you need. Since you're doing a
downsizing conversion in the first place, you might want to do some
conversion anyway while you're at it (e.g. date fields for Y2K) so take the
opportunity. You might be feeding the character data file (especially if
you used commas for delimiters) to a database loader anyway. You can also
do error checking and special conversions on fields that are flags or codes
or values from a certain list or involve a table lookup function - any
needed data cleanup.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

- **From:** wtsquid@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f7f88$ph5$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have been testing with Net Express for about 1 week and I have encountered
a problem that I am assuming to be an ws alignment problem. I have
succesfully called a C program with a COBOL program. I have later (in a
process of cleaning it up) removed an unused ws item, recompiled and it would
fail. I have then put the unused ws item back in, recompiled and it would
work again. I have also recompiled the next day and it would fail until I
modified the size of some unused ws item. I will vary the size of the field
and it works for some sizes and not for others.

I assumed it had something to do with alignment. I have tried several
different compile directives (e.g. align, ibmcomp) and sync left and right on
fields but nothing seems to fix the problem.

Does anyone have any ideas or solutions?

thanks.

## Thread: RM COBOL - Win Programming? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** dbmartin@onramp.net (David)
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37180521.17374622@news.onramp.net>`

```
Does anyone use RM COBOL  to produce Windows programs?  How well does
the WIN GUI system work and how hard is it to interface the RM COBOL
language to GUI development

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fankc$lso$1@news.imp.ch>`
- **References:** `<37180521.17374622@news.onramp.net>`

```
Hello David

I use RM Cobol 6.51 for Windows since October 98. Before I was using RM
Cobol for DOS 5.32.
There are many factors to consider.
The main reason for me was to use the 32Bit OS (Win 95/98 or NT) and to be
compatible for the future.
After changing and compiling about 350 Programs within about 3 months I
would say it was worth it. I don't have limits anymore with programs that
have more than 25'000 lines of source code.
Important things to consider (but there can be many more):
Forget old self written routines in Assembler for Windows managing etc. But
you can recplace them with
the built in Windows Pop up Manager or new written DLL's . If you use
external routines for ex. in C consider, there could be some problems like
with finding the right links to the DLL's.
The System is so stable as the DOS one. I never had Cobol Program under 6.51
going down. Many other
Window Programms did.
I still use the ISAM File System, the File can be used without any changes
if you define it in the
configuration record.
Don't expect the Windows 6.51 to work as event programming. You can use
mouse events
use boxes and and and but you can recompile your original sources and they
run under Windows 6.51.
You can define many properties by default or by program and they are managed
it the Registry per machine.
or by default for all machines.
For example you can tell the 15 inch display machine to use Arial 13 and the
17 inch machine to use Arial 15.
Forget the BLINK option if used.
The next version 7 which was announced for about April this year should
bring the possibilty to use Windows
printer drivers. So start forgetting the built in printer codes and the
handling of them in your program!
If you use RM Cobol for Dos you can send me a source (not 25'000 lines!) and
I will check it
for ev. traps or problems. If you have specific questions, make them.
Greetings
John@Casagrande.ch

David <dbmartin@onramp.net> schrieb in im Newsbeitrag:
37180521.17374622@news.onramp.net...
> Does anyone use RM COBOL  to produce Windows programs?  How well does
> the WIN GUI system work and how hard is it to interface the RM COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: RM COBOL - Win Programming?

- **From:** phil@the-dock.freeserve.co.uk
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371b9eb8.10100509@news.easynet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<37180521.17374622@news.onramp.net>`

```
David,

I have been using RM Cobol 6.51 with Cobol-WOW ActiveX version for a
GUI front end. I have had some problems with WOW as it is a new
product. You have all the standard controls(tabs,bitmaps etc...) with
in it and a large selection of API calls. 

I have written several controls in VB 6 to handle JPEG's, control
network connections, access DUN connections, print images, any true
type font that is available in any colour and frames.

The rmcobol runtime is one of the most stable peices of windows
software I have ever come across even under 98.

Regrads

Philip Pike

phil@the-dock.fresserve.co.uk

## Thread: How to clean up a program etc.: Automate it! — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to clean up a program etc.: Automate it!

- **From:** John Hicks <jonsjob@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37194F62.F38DD62A@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <ec$pfiUh#GA.305@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
An interesting post by Mr. Pritchett and ensuing thread by all the
regulars...

It's curious that no one has mentioned automating the clean-up process.
I've heard tell that there are these new-fangled machines out now that
can be programmed to read an input file, apply certain logical
operations to the data, and write the results to an output file. If this
is really true, then perhaps they could be programmed to transform COBOL
source using the logic Mr. Pritchett outlines. 

Trouble is, I hear these fancy new machines require very specialized
technical experts to program them. And I'm told these experts are all
very busy maintaining old and very messy "legacy" programs. 

So I guess fate decrees that we must spend the rest of our days cleaning
up COBOL source by hand. ;)

But seriously folks... I know I've played around writing such cleanup
programs, and I have a hunch many of the rest of you have too. Any
finished products out there? Or should I polish up my code and publish
it?

--John Hicks

"Robert M. Pritchett" wrote:
> 
> OK, here we go. In an ideal world, e.g. new clean development without old
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> working from the outermost levels inward. Rearrange groups of code as

snip snip
> 
> Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Re: How to clean up a program etc.: Automate it!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371a031f.145278511@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <ec$pfiUh#GA.305@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <37194F62.F38DD62A@ibm.net>`

```
On Sat, 17 Apr 1999 23:20:02 -0400, John Hicks <jonsjob@ibm.net>
wrote:


>But seriously folks... I know I've played around writing such cleanup
>programs, and I have a hunch many of the rest of you have too. Any
>finished products out there? Or should I polish up my code and publish
>it?

There is a tool out there called "COBOL Transformer" or something
close.  On of our members is responsible for it.  The Engine behind it
is what converts MicroFocus code to Fujitsu.  It does several other
things, but the important thing is that it does it by ANALYZING the
code and rewriting it.  It does NOT just change words around.  (If I
understand it correctly).  So, such an animal does exist!
```

#### ↳ Re: How to clean up a program etc.: Automate it!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3719E2A6.73C34AF3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7el8st$o6a$1@news.cerf.net> <ec$pfiUh#GA.305@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <37194F62.F38DD62A@ibm.net>`

```
John Hicks wrote:
> So I guess fate decrees that we must spend the rest of our days cleaning
> up COBOL source by hand. ;)
> 

This is the reason I started the parser project.  I have started
the reformatter for working storage code to wet the appetite for
the project.

It is a big project and I think that I will probably take about
one year to get somewhere with this (being realistic).

OK question time:

How would you create this:

  OCCURS 1 to 21 times

from:

01 occurs-nmbr PIC 9(04) comp
01 occurs-to   PIC 9(04) comp

Occurs-to may be zero in which case the 'to 21' is removed.

My main problem is if I string a PIC zzz9 I get the extra spaces. 
I don't want the extra blanks.

Ken
```

## Thread: Help with Tables — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with Tables

- **From:** "C.Lussier" <clussier@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net>`

```
I am a student working with Micro Focus Cobol. I have an assignment to
do that requires that I write a program that will read 4 input files and
write to 2 output files. The first 3 files are used to upload data into
arrays. The arrays will then be used to validate the transactions coming
from the fourth input file (text file).
I am stuck on the uploading of the data into arrays. The following is a
small test program I created to try the uploading but I get the error: 
Error Accessing file: A\PROV
Description: Wrong open mode or access mode for read/start

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. FINALEX.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT PROV-FILE
           ASSIGN TO "A:\PROV"
           ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

       FD  PROV-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
       01  PROV-RECORD.
           05  PROV-CODE       PIC X(3).
           05  PROV-DESC       PIC X(15).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           05  PRINT-CODE      PIC X(3).
           05  FILLER          PIC X(3)    VALUE SPACE.
           05  PRINT-DESC      PIC X(15).

       77  EOF                 PIC X.

       01  TABLE-PROV-RECORD OCCURS 2 TIMES
           INDEXED BY PROV-INDEX.
           05  TABLE-PROV-CODE PIC X(3).
           05  TABLE-PROV-DESC PIC X(15).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       0000-MAIN.

           SET PROV-INDEX TO 1.
           PERFORM 0700-PRINT-PROV-FIELDS
               UNTIL PROV-INDEX > 3 OR
                   TABLE-PROV-RECORD(PROV-INDEX) = SPACE.

       9999-PROGRAM-END.
           STOP-RUN.

       0100-OPENING-PROCEDURE.
           OPEN I-O PROV-FILE.
           PERFORM 0200-LOAD-PROV-TABLE.
           CLOSE PROV-FILE.
       0200-LOAD-PROV-TABLE.
           PERFORM 0300-CLEAR-TABLE.
           SET PROV-INDEX TO 1.
           PERFORM 0400-READ-NEXT-RECORD.
           PERFORM 0500-LOAD-ONE-PROV-RECORD
               UNTIL EOF = "Y" OR
                   PROV-INDEX > 2.

       0300-CLEAR-TABLE.
           PERFORM 0350-CLEAR-ONE-ROW
               VARYING PROV-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
                   UNTIL PROV-INDEX > 2.


       0350-CLEAR-ONE-ROW.
           MOVE SPACE TO TABLE-PROV-RECORD(PROV-INDEX).

       0500-LOAD-ONE-PROV-RECORD.
           MOVE PROV-CODE TO TABLE-PROV-CODE(PROV-INDEX).
           MOVE PROV-DESC TO TABLE-PROV-DESC(PROV-INDEX).
           PERFORM 0400-READ-NEXT-RECORD.
           IF EOF NOT = "Y"
               SET PROV-INDEX UP BY 1
               IF PROV-INDEX > 2
               DISPLAY "TABLE FULL".


       0700-PRINT-PROV-FIELDS.
           PERFORM 0800-PRINT-THE-RECORD.
           SET PROV-INDEX UP BY 1.

       0800-PRINT-THE-RECORD.
           MOVE TABLE-PROV-CODE(PROV-INDEX) TO PRINT-CODE.
           MOVE TABLE-PROV-DESC(PROV-INDEX) TO PRINT-DESC.
           MOVE DETAIL-LINE TO PROV-RECORD.

       0400-READ-NEXT-RECORD.
           READ PROV-FILE NEXT RECORD
               AT END MOVE "Y" TO EOF.

       0600-CLOSING-PROCEDURE.
           CLOSE PROV-FILE.

Thank you for your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Tables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fba8j$b03@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net>`

```
I surprised by the error that you report - in relationship to the program
that you show us.

If I look at your 000-MAIN routine, The only thing that you do before you
STOP RUN is to set an index and to PERFORM a routine which does some MOVEs -
but never tries to WRITE anything.

Your logic never seems to get to any OPEN, WRITE, etc.

Another POSSIBLE issue is the fact that you use a "\" rather than a "/" in
the ASSIGN clause.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Tables

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YZnS2.3053$_a6.139910@paloalto-snr1>`
- **References:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net>`

```
To use READ ... NEXT you need ACCESS IS DYNAMIC in the SELECT. (And a START,
which will fail on a sequential file anyway).

Just use READ PROV-FILE
                AT END
                   MOVE "Y" TO EOF-FLAG.


For extra style points, open PROV-FILE for INPUT rather than I-O, as you
don't need to write to it.

MCM

C.Lussier wrote in message ...
>I am stuck on the uploading of the data into arrays. The following is a
>small test program I created to try the uploading but I get the error:
>Error Accessing file: A\PROV
>Description: Wrong open mode or access mode for read/start
>
```

           05  SALARY-MIN        PIC 9(5).
           05  SALARY-MAX        PIC 9(5).

       FD  TRANSAC-FILE

       01  TRANSAC-RECORD.

           05  CIVIL-STAT-CODE   PIC 9.
           05  SALARY            PIC 9(5).

           05  EMPLOYEE-CODE     PIC X(5).

       FD  REJECT-FILE

           05  RPROVINCE-CODE    PIC X(3).
           05  RSALARY           PIC 9(5).
           05  REJECT-REASON     PIC X(3).

       FD  VALID-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
       01  VALID-RECORD.
           05  VGIVEN-NAME       PIC X(10).
           05  VLAST-NAME        PIC X(10).
           05  VSALARY-CODE      PIC 9.
           05  VPROVINCE-DESC    PIC X(15).
           05  VCIVIL-STAT-DESC  PIC X(10).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

               INDEXED BY PROV-INDEX.
           05  TABLE-PROV-CODE   PIC X(3).
           05  TABLE-PROV-DESC   PIC X(10).

      *This table will be used to upload data into arrays

               INDEXED BY CIVIL-INDEX.
           05  TABLE-CIVIL-CODE  PIC 9.
           05  TABLE-CIVIL-DESC  PIC X(10).

      *This table will be used to upload data into arrays
       01  TABLE-SALARY-RECORD OCCURS 20 TIMES
               INDEXED BY SALARY-INDEX.
           05  TABLE-SALARY-CODE PIC 9.
           05  TABLE-SALARY-MIN  PIC 9(5).
           05  TABLE-SALARY-MAX  PIC 9(6).
           05  TABLE-SALARY-DESC PIC X(15).

       77  FILE-AT-END           PIC X VALUE "N".
       77  END-TABLE             PIC X VALUE "N".
       77  NB-REC-READ           PIC 9(4) VALUE 0.
       77  NB-CIV-REJECTS        PIC 9(4) VALUE 0.
       77  NB-PRV-REJECTS        PIC 9(4) VALUE 0.
       77  NB-SAL-REJECTS        PIC 9(4) VALUE 0.
       77  SALARY-VALIDATE       PIC 9(6) VALUE 0.
           88  MIN-SAL           VALUE 0 THROUGH 25000.
           88  MAX-SAL           VALUE 25001 THROUGH 200000.

           OPEN INPUT PROV-FILE.

           OPEN INPUT SALARY-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT TRANSAC-FILE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with Tables

- **From:** John Trifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371A2B78.5178B8F3@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <YZnS2.3053$_a6.139910@paloalto-snr1> <371A1306.49821A03@istar.ca>`

```
I have reworked some of  the code and inserted comments to assist in understanding
my reasoning.  You  never reset your 'end-table' flag, so that after the first
table was loaded, no subsequent table was loaded, as the flag was still set to an
end-of-file condition. Also,  I believe the reason you got the message 'no current
record defined for sequential read' is that you dropped though your paragraphs.
when referencing table elements you must usea subscript, and SEARCH the table for
find if the elements existed. As  I don't have a compiler here to test the code
any further (including a syntax check), pls forgive any syntax errors.

Regards,
John

000200 PROGRAM-ID. ASSIGN5.
000300
000400 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000500 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000600 FILE-CONTROL.
000700
000800     SELECT PROV-FILE
000900         ASSIGN TO 'A:/PROV.TXT'
001000         ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.

001400         ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.
001500

001900

002200         ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.
002300

011100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

011300 0000-MAIN.
011400* USE ONLY 1 OPEN STATEMENT RATHER THAN 6.
011500     OPEN INPUT PROV-FILE
011600                CIVIL-FILE
011700                SALARY-FILE
011800                TRANSAC-FILE
011900          OUTPUT REJECT-FILE
012000                 VALID-FILE.
012100*
012200     PERFORM 0100-INITIALIZE-PROV-TABLE
012300         VARYING PROV-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
012400         UNTIL END-TABLE = 'Y' OR
012500             PROV-INDEX > 20.
012600*
012700*   HAVE TO INITIALIZE END-TABLE FIELD AGAIN, OR THIS WILL
012800*   NEVER BE EXECUTED.
012900     MOVE 'N' TO END-TABLE
013000*
013100     PERFORM 0200-INITIALIZE-CIVIL-TABLE
013200         VARYING CIVIL-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
013300         UNTIL END-TABLE = 'Y' OR
013400             CIVIL-INDEX > 20.

013700*   NEVER BE EXECUTED.
013800     MOVE 'N' TO END-TABLE
013900*
014000     PERFORM 0300-INITIALIZE-SALARY-TABLE
014100         VARYING SALARY-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
014200         UNTIL END-TABLE = 'Y' OR
014300             SALARY-INDEX > 20.
014400*
014500     PERFORM 0400-READ-FILES.
014600     PERFORM 0500-PROCESS-RECORDS
014700         UNTIL FILE-AT-END = 'Y'.
014800*
014900*    ADDED THE PERFORM OF THE DISPLAY PARA.
015000     PERFORM 0600-DISPLAY-RECORDS.
015100*
015200* USE ONLY 1 CLOSE STATEMENT RATHER THAN THE 5 IN ORGINAL.
015300*  (TRANSAC-FILE MISSING FROM ORGINAL SOURCE)
015400*
015500     CLOSE      PROV-FILE
015600                CIVIL-FILE
015700                SALARY-FILE
015800                TRANSAC-FILE
015900                REJECT-FILE
016000                VALID-FILE.
016100*
016200*   NO RETURN TO OPERATING SYSTEM - ORIGINAL FALLS THROUGH
016300*   TO NEXT PARA, AND ATTEMPTS READ AFTER END OF FILE.
016400*>  GOBACK ADDED.
016500*
016600     GOBACK.
016700
016800 0100-INITIALIZE-PROV-TABLE.
016900     READ PROV-FILE INTO TABLE-PROV-RECORD(PROV-INDEX)
017000         AT END
017100             MOVE 'Y' TO END-TABLE.
017200
017300 0200-INITIALIZE-CIVIL-TABLE.
017400     READ CIVIL-FILE INTO TABLE-CIVIL-RECORD(CIVIL-INDEX)
017500         AT END
017600             MOVE 'Y' TO END-TABLE.
017700
017800 0300-INITIALIZE-SALARY-TABLE.
017900     READ SALARY-FILE INTO TABLE-SALARY-RECORD(SALARY-INDEX)
018000         AT END
018100             MOVE 'Y' TO END-TABLE.
018200
018300 0400-READ-FILES.
018400*    THIS COMMENTED OUT FILES ALREADY READ TO END OF FILE.
018500*    READ PROV-FILE
018600*        AT END MOVE 'Y' TO FILE-AT-END.
018700*    READ CIVIL-FILE
018800*        AT END MOVE 'Y' TO FILE-AT-END.
018900*    READ SALARY-FILE
019000*        AT END MOVE 'Y' TO FILE-AT-END.
019100     READ TRANSAC-FILE
019200         AT END MOVE 'Y' TO FILE-AT-END
019300         ADD 1 TO NB-REC-READ.
019400
019500 0500-PROCESS-RECORDS.
019600*  be aware that this code will only give you the last
019700*  reason the record was rejected. you may want to enhance this
019800*  to be able to show all the reasons the record was rejected.
019900*
020000     MOVE SPACES TO REJECT-REASON
020100     SET PROV-INDEX
020200         CIVIL-INDEX
020300         SALARY-INDEX  TO 1.
020400     SEARCH TABLE-CIVIL-RECORD
020500        END
020600           ADD    1   TO NB-CIV-REJECTS
020700        WHEN TABLE-CIVIL-CODE (CIVIL-INDEX) = CIVIL-CODE
020800           CONTINUE.
020900*
021000     SEARCH TABLE-PROV-RECORD
021100        END
021200            MOVE 'PRV' TO REJECT-REASON
021300            ADD    1   TO NB-PRV-REJECTS
021400        WHEN TABLE-PROV-CODE (PROV-INDEX) = PROV-CODE
021500           CONTINUE.
021600*
021700     SEARCH TABLE-SALARY-RECORD
021800        END
021900            MOVE 'SAL' TO REJECT-REASON
022000            ADD    1   TO NB-SAL-REJECTS
022100        WHEN TABLE-SALARY-CODE (SALARY-INDEX) = SALARY-CODE
022200           CONTINUE.
022300*
022400     IF REJECT-REASON = SPACES
022500        PERFORM 0550-WRITE-VALID-RECORD
022600     ELSE
022700        PERFORM 0560-WRITE-REJECT-RECORD.
022800*
022900*  YOU NEED TO KEEP PROCESSING ALL RECORDS IN THE FILE. I HAVE
023000*  LEFT THE ORGINAL PARAGRATH NUMBERING ALONE FOR CLARITY, BUT
023100*  YOU SHOULD MOVE THE PERFORM'D PARA BELOW THE PERFORMING PARA.
023200*
023300     PERFORM 0400-READ-FILES.
023400*
023500 0550-WRITE-VALID-RECORD.
023600* FILL IN THE BLANKS....
023700
023800
023900
024000
024100 0560-WRITE-REJECT-RECORD.
024200* FILL IN THE BLANKS....
024300
024400
024500
024600
024700 0600-DISPLAY-RECORDS.
024800     DISPLAY 'NUMBER OF RECORDS READ' NB-REC-READ.
024900     DISPLAY 'NUMBER OF REJECTS CIVIL' NB-CIV-REJECTS.
025000     DISPLAY 'NUMBER OF REJECTS PROVINCE' NB-PRV-REJECTS.
025100     DISPLAY 'NUMBER OF REJECTS SALARY' NB-SAL-REJECTS.


"C.Lussier" wrote:
.....

> no current record defined for sequential read.
>

......
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with Tables

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bZNS2.5194$qx1.115727@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net> <YZnS2.3053$_a6.139910@paloalto-snr1> <371A1306.49821A03@istar.ca>`

```

C.Lussier wrote in message <371A1306.49821A03@istar.ca>...
>I thank all of you who responded to my question. Although it has become
>a little clearer, I have attempted to change the code to try and put all
>this together. I am still getting errors when running the program:
>
>no current record defined for sequential read.



no current record usually means that you are referencing an IO area which
either has not been opened, or has not been read at least once. If true,
this means you are skipping your READ statements and trying to reference the
values contained therein without there being an initial value.

I personally never use indexes, but you are not initializing the indexes you
are using, therefore they could have garbage in them. If true, chances are
they will have a value greater than 20, thus falling through your loops
which load the tables. Try:

           SET PROV-INDEX TO ZERO
           SET CIVIL-INDEX TO ZERO
           SET SALARY-INDEX TO ZERO

in your MAIN paragraph after your OPEN statements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Tables

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B3108.327AEDD4@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<ih9S2.87$UU2.478459@NewsRead.Toronto.iSTAR.net>`

```
Boy you're confused.

With a quick glance, I notice that you have open and close paragraphs
which you do not perform.  I also see that you have a table with two
occurrences.  There is no relationship between the number of files
you're reading and the size of the table.  A table of size 2 seems
silly.  

I'm guessing you read your multiple files, putting fields from each
record in your table.  The table is large enough so that you can read a
complete file.  If the files have 300 records, the table should at least
OCCUR 300.  I guess that each file has different fields which get
populated in the table.  But you haven't really explained why we need a
table.  From what you said, it's more likely that you read from each
file, check them, then write to the appropriate output file.  In that
case, no table is needed.

I can't guess without understanding your requirements.

C.Lussier wrote:
> 
> I am a student working with Micro Focus Cobol. I have an assignment to
…[97 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you for your help.
```

<a id="t-955fbfc721"></a>
## Thread: Intrinsic Function Range — 5 msgs

### Intrinsic Function Range

- **From:** Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37182681.49B2@coredcs.com>`

```
I'd like to get a better understanding of how the intrinsic function
range works.  Let's assume that I have a single character field in a
record that I'd like to test against a range of values.  This range of
values is variable and could be obtained externally either from a
calling program or from a single record control file.  How could I
verify that the single character field falls within the specified range
of values using a FUNCTION RANGE construct?  Yes I know I could just
compare my field to the high and low range values to verify that it did
not fall outside the range but I'd like to know how the range function
is or can be used.

Thanks
================================
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
================================
```

#### ↳ Re: Intrinsic Function Range

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990417092655.23536.00001497@ng117.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37182681.49B2@coredcs.com>`

```
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com> writes ...

>I'd like to get a better understanding of how the intrinsic function
>range works.  Let's assume that I have a single character field in a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is or can be used.

RANGE is for numeric data items. It subtracts the smallest value in the list
from the largest value in the list, returning a single value. It has nothing to
do with seeing if a character is "in the range".

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Intrinsic Function Range

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37195012.99434435@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37182681.49B2@coredcs.com>`

```
On Sat, 17 Apr 1999 02:13:21 -0400, Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
wrote:

>I'd like to get a better understanding of how the intrinsic function
>range works.  Let's assume that I have a single character field in a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is or can be used.

When writing Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 hours, I made sure to put
special emphasis on the Intrinsic functions.  These are a very
important and powerful part of the current standard.  I cover EACH
function, with examples.  While some of the functions can be used with
numeric and alphanumeric arguments, Range is only valid for numbers.
You might be better of setting up a new CLASS in the Special Names
section.  Interestingly, MIN and MAX can be used with alphanumeric
fields.  I remember writing code to search a table for the highest
value -- Sure wish I had MAX available then!
```

Comments on developing for Fujitsu Cobol V4+ for Windows 95 GUI
programs.  Also, does anyone have a good feeling for developing with
Fujitsu vs. Visual Basic 5 or 6.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Development - Good, Bad, or ?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37194f52.99242598@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<371a0582.17471544@news.onramp.net>`

```
On Sat, 17 Apr 1999 03:54:10 GMT, dbmartin@onramp.net (David) wrote:

>Comments on developing for Fujitsu Cobol V4+ for Windows 95 GUI
>programs.  Also, does anyone have a good feeling for developing with
>Fujitsu vs. Visual Basic 5 or 6.

Developing with PowerCOBOL is like developing with Visual Basic.  If a
Visual Basic programmer, wanted to do COBOL (Without any prior COBOL
experience) I think PowerCOBOL is the most like what they are used to.

Personally, I use several different COBOL compilers, but a common
interface tool - COBOL sp2 from Flexus.   It's not like developing in
Visual basic at all.  It's much easier!
```

<a id="t-934368f79a"></a>
## Thread: help needed — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### help needed

- **From:** "grimor" <fguzman@clientes.euskaltel.es>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
I need, if possible, an easy and free way to convert cobol data files (.dat)
to an MS Access legible format, or any other way to handle this data files.
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: help needed

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fabp6$io8$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
1. The presence of a "DAT" extension is meaningless - the programmer
could name the file - and its extension - anything he wished.
2. There does not exist a conversion routine to change an arbitrary COBOL
file
to anything known to God or man.
3. The file may contain virtually any sized records each containing a
variable number of fields each of which can be in a variety of renditions
(binary, ASCII, EBCDIC, graphics, etc.). Further, the file itself may be
arranged in a proprietary format (ISAM, etc.). And there does not exist
an external or internal clue as the the arrangement or format of any
of the above.
4. If all you have is the file, this is the only job you will every have.

grimor wrote in message <7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>...
>I need, if possible, an easy and free way to convert cobol data files
(.dat)
>to an MS Access legible format, or any other way to handle this data files.
>Thanks
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: help needed

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec5S2.165$Sb.12396@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
grimor <fguzman@clientes.euskaltel.es> wrote in message
news:7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es...
> I need, if possible, an easy and free way to convert cobol data
files (.dat)
> to an MS Access legible format, or any other way to handle this data
files.
> Thanks
>
>


Use the COBOL product that produced the .dat file to create a
display-readable .txt file, then use MS Access97's Import feature, and
viola, you have your data.

If you do not have the COBOL product that produced the file, then head
over to www.dejanews.com and do a PowerSearch on this forum on the
keywords COBOL, Conversion, and ODBC.  This topic has been mentioned
in the past, and a product capable of granting ODBC access to the
COBOL files produced by various products was mentioned.  I would tell
you the product name myself, but since it didn't interest me at the
time, I didn't remember it; and anyway.
```

#### ↳ Re: help needed

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37195173.99788136@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7f9tuj$iij12@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
On Sat, 17 Apr 1999 14:13:26 +0200, "grimor"
<fguzman@clientes.euskaltel.es> wrote:

>I need, if possible, an easy and free way to convert cobol data files (.dat)
>to an MS Access legible format, or any other way to handle this data files.
>Thanks
>
>

Let me explain.  

This is like asking - "Can someone provide a
tool/utility/procedure/... to convert a RDBS data file to plain
ASCII-text".  Which one?  

Try to convert a Oracle database to ASCII, without using Oracle!
Try to convert a Microsoft Access database (.mdb) file, without using
Access!
There are numerous brands of COBOL on numerous systems - SOME use
proprietary, vendor invented, file strutures.  Others use the native
file structure of the platform involved.  

We need more information.  Companies such as LIANT provide ODBC
drivers to access many different type of COBOL data files.  There is a
company called "Data Junction" that makes a very good tool for
converting various data formats, take a look at that.  Tell us which
COBOL version, brand and platform and we'll try to help further.
```

<a id="t-7732f801cb"></a>
## Thread: How are records born? — 17 msgs

### How are records born?

- **From:** trout59727@aol.com (Trout59727)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
I'm just learning Cobol and we're given our record descriptions to work with.
How do these come about in the real world? Is there someone in the computer
room who says "Thou shalt be an 80 byte variable-length record", or does
management come up with the kind of information they need and design the record
themselves? 

Thanks,
Curious RR
```

#### ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** Dave Polzin <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371A6BB0.2593@coredcs.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
Trout59727 wrote:
 > 
 > I'm just learning Cobol and we're given our record descriptions to
work with.
 > How do these come about in the real world? Is there someone in the
computer
 > room who says "Thou shalt be an 80 byte variable-length record", or
does
 > management come up with the kind of information they need and design
the record
 > themselves?
 > 
 > Thanks,
 > Curious RR

That's what a programmer/analyst (or analyst/programmer) does.  Based on
the requirements of the particular system or subsystem, record
definition is part of the design phase.

I get the feeling that either your instructor didn't explain things very
well or you slept through one too many classes.

================================
Dave Polzin  <dlpolzin@coredcs.com>
================================
```

#### ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fdtqm$ojh@sjx-ixn9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
This is an interesting question.  The simple answer is that there is almost
a different answer for every file.

Certainly the most common place is that MOST files that you work with are
actually either input files that already exist (so the definition is already
defined) or output files (print or disk/tape) that someone else tells you
what they need/want.

HOWEVER, somewhere there must be someone who is figuring out what new files
look like.  I can think of three (common but not universal) ways that these
are designed:

1) Print files - the "record size" is usually determined by the printer(s)
to which it will be sent.  The actual layout is usually designed by an
"analyst" in consultation with the end user.  (The analyst MAY be a
programmer/analysts - so it may be you.)

2) Databases (of the relational or hierarchical types) - There are entire
methodologies established on how to design these.  In most (but not all)
cases, the efficiency of the database engine has almost as much to do with
the user/program needs in how these are designed.  The term "DBA" refers to
a database analyst who will usually work with a senior programmer/analyst to
figure out how to set these out.  It is important to understand when
designing these that the same database will (almost certainly) be used by
multiple applications - therefore, the design may include fields that your
program/application will never use - and the design may be other than what
you would expect for your specific program.

3) Traditional COBOL output files (sequential, indexed - and rarely IMHO -
relative) - mostly these go thru the same process as databases listed above.
However, it is more common for these to be specific to a single application
(or even program).  Therefore, it is more common for the individual
programmer/analyst to set out their layout.
```

#### ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ff66m$qoi$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```
As a rule, management would not know a record length from a word processing
document.  Whoever designs the software designs the files as well.

Trout59727 wrote in message
<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>...
>I'm just learning Cobol and we're given our record descriptions to work
with.
>How do these come about in the real world? Is there someone in the computer
>room who says "Thou shalt be an 80 byte variable-length record", or does
>management come up with the kind of information they need and design the
record
>themselves?
>
>Thanks,
>Curious RR
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990419085132.20551.00004044@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7ff66m$qoi$1@news.igs.net>`

```
we normally base our records on the paper documents the information originally
come on.  Then all the blank spaces are deleted, the numbers are compressed 
(to protect the innocent) and viola -  a record format.

Seriously, all record formats come directly from the innoucous mind of God.

TW
```

#### ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** "jeroen crabbe" <jeroen.crabbe@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>`

```

Trout59727 wrote in message
<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com>...
>I'm just learning Cobol and we're given our record descriptions to work
with.
>How do these come about in the real world? Is there someone in the computer
>room who says "Thou shalt be an 80 byte variable-length record", or does
>management come up with the kind of information they need and design the
record
>themselves?
>
>Thanks,
>Curious RR

This probably has nothing to do with the question, but I'm curious :

I've seen a lot of files arround which have length 80 (now allready in 3
different mainframe using companies), even when they just as well could have
been 70 or 90 bytes wide (with a filler in the end). I once heard this
psychological tendancy to have files with length 80 comes from the fact that
in the early days (before I was born) mainframes worked with cards as input.
These cards had holes in them to show where there was a bit, and it was
possible to put 80 bits on a row on such a card. Can anyone say me if this
theory is right ?

By the way, last week our account manager went on retirement. In his
farewell speech he mentioned that the first mainframe he had to work with
(somewhere in the sixties I guess) had 4K memory. Incredible, huh ?!

Greetz, Jeroentje
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371bb8eb.257372044@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 01:48:08 +0200, "jeroen crabbe"
<jeroen.crabbe@skynet.be> wrote:


>I've seen a lot of files arround which have length 80 (now allready in 3
>different mainframe using companies), even when they just as well could have
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sounds good to me.  When the company I worked for then, went
"Cardless" the reader could still read only 80 column cards.  So we
used 80 byte input records - so they could be included inline with the
JCL in the job run.  Later, our particular OS allows 128 byte cards -
so we started using 80 or 128.  This was Software Pursuits' MVT/VSE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7FD4.7144A346@worf.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be> <371bb8eb.257372044@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:


> >These cards had holes in them to show where there was a bit, and it was
> >possible to put 80 bits on a row on such a card. Can anyone say me if this
> >theory is right ?
 
> Sounds good to me.  When the company I worked for then, went
> "Cardless" the reader could still read only 80 column cards.  So we
> used 80 byte input records - so they could be included inline with the
> JCL in the job run.  Later, our particular OS allows 128 byte cards -
> so we started using 80 or 128.  This was Software Pursuits' MVT/VSE.

Almost right.  IBM cards, the most common, were designed
around the EBCDIC system for representing character data. 
Each column in a card represented a single character (not a
bit).  There are 12 rows on each card.  The top two rows are
called "zone" punches; the remaining 10 rows are "numeric"
punches - each row representing a particular digit. Digits
are encoded by punching the correct digit.  Alphabetic
characters and punctuation marks are punching a hole in a
zone row and one in the numeric row.  One source that
provides more detailed description than this is Austing and
Cassel "File Organization and Access" published in 1988 (see
page 106).  

I have never heard of 128 byte cards - anybody know how
these were formatted?  Smaller holes, bigger card, different
standard?  I do know that other manufacturers used other
different sized cards - though I do not know which
manufacturer, I remember seeing one that used a 3"x3" square
format.  One of the reasons IBM was so successful as a
computer company was that they were using 80-column "IBM
Cards" in their mechanical accounting machines long before
they began making computers.  When a company was ready to
install a computer, they could use the same data they had
always used - i.e., the IBM cards.  No conversion was
necessary to a new format card.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371d03e2.342111148@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be> <371bb8eb.257372044@news1.ibm.net> <371C7FD4.7144A346@worf.netins.net>`

```
On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 09:23:32 -0400, Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote:


>I have never heard of 128 byte cards - anybody know how
>these were formatted?  

They were virtual.  Software Pursuits allowed then because the in line
reader data restriction of 80 bytes was, well, restrictive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371e8c7a.5238966@netnews>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be> <371bb8eb.257372044@news1.ibm.net> <371C7FD4.7144A346@worf.netins.net>`

```
'Twas Tue, 20 Apr 1999 09:23:32 -0400, when Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Almost right.  IBM cards, the most common, were designed
>around the EBCDIC system for representing character data. 

Punch cards were invented by Herman Hollerith in 1884, and the 80 by 12
format was standardized by the 1890 U S Census.

EBCDIC was introduced in 1963.  EBCDIC was designed around the cards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jack R. Rose" <mbreath.ppp@indy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3728A8F6.6B05@indy.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be> <371bb8eb.257372044@news1.ibm.net> <371C7FD4.7144A346@worf.netins.net> <371e8c7a.5238966@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Tue, 20 Apr 1999 09:23:32 -0400, when Floyd Johnson
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> R B |\  Randall Bart

The 128 byte format was also started by IBM.  This is/was the format
used in their first generally available floppy disk entry unit, the
3741.  This was an 8-inch floppy with 78 tracks (1 sided), 26
record/track, and 128 bytes per record.

The 3741 was also used as a primary input unit to the System/3 series of
IBM mid-range systems along with the 3x3 card format that had 3 lines of
32 columns with 6 rows per column.  (The card format died because it
wasn't capable of holding all possible values for a byte.)

IBM JCL (OCL on the System/3) was kept at 80 bytes due to inertia (there
are still companies out there running 1401 emulators), but I think that
the latest version of OS390 allows different sizes, but I've not seen it
otherwise (more inertia).

Jack R. Rose
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371BBA18.6CAE@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
There is an advantage to using LRECL=80 in any simple MVS business
application.  JCL(Job Control Language) is processed as 80 byte
"cards".  If my input file is defined as LRECL=80, then the following
JCL option may be used to feed in my hand-keyed test data:
	//MYINFILE DD *

This is much more convienent than having to allocate a separate file and
populating it with my test data.  If my data requirements are less than
80, I will always pad it out to 80 for the above reason.

Pete
(Been doing this MVS stuff for 20 years)



jeroen crabbe wrote:
> 
> Trout59727 wrote in message
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greetz, Jeroentje
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7D4D.87935736@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be> <371BBA18.6CAE@saif.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is still right but there is actually no need to keep JCL in
80 bytes since MVS-ESA.  The standard sticks and it will be an
unusual shop that breaks the convention.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7fgnel$qmc$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
jeroen crabbe wrote in message <7fgbo2
>This probably has nothing to do with the question, but I'm curious :
>
>I've seen a lot of files arround which have length 80 (now allready in 3
>different mainframe using companies), even when they just as well could
have
>been 70 or 90 bytes wide (with a filler in the end). I once heard this
>psychological tendancy to have files with length 80 comes from the fact
that
>in the early days (before I was born) mainframes worked with cards as
input.
>These cards had holes in them to show where there was a bit, and it was
>possible to put 80 bits on a row on such a card. Can anyone say me if this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well the 80 columns is definitely a holdover from cards, though you might
keep in mind that a standard screen is also 80 columns, as were most
printers (either 80 or 132 for printers).

When I started in this business, 4k bytes of memory rented in the over 4000
dollars per month range. I have programmed lots of machines of that size,
though not in Cobol.  The PDP-8, the first mini that I ever worked on, maxed
out at 12 bits of address.  After that you had to get into page swapping
using an output port.  I have a full accounting system here that is 4k.
That includes the help system.  It will run on a single sided 160k floppy
quite nicely.  It only took 2700 hours to write(in assembler).  I could do
the same thing in Cobol in three to four weeks, but I'll bet it would go 2-3
megs under windows.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fjhg5$34t$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```

jeroen crabbe wrote in message <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>...
>
>This probably has nothing to do with the question, but I'm curious :
>
>I've seen a lot of files arround which have length 80 (now allready in 3
>different mainframe using companies), even when they just as well could
have
>been 70 or 90 bytes wide (with a filler in the end). I once heard this
>psychological tendancy to have files with length 80 comes from the fact
that
>in the early days (before I was born) mainframes worked with cards as
input.
>These cards had holes in them to show where there was a bit, and it was
>possible to put 80 bits on a row on such a card. Can anyone say me if this
>theory is right ?
>

Good Lord! A programmer who has never punched his own deck of cards!
(Or probably even paper tape for that matter). Since he's unsure of whether
the 80-character convention came from cards (also 80-character lines on
screens), he's probably unaware that the size of a punch card was exactly
the same size as 1890 currency (dollar bills and the like) because so much
equipment already existed for currency (boxes, trays, counting devices - no,
not hole punches) and that Hermann Hollerith - director of the U.S. Census -
was the 'inventor' of the punch card system. Not really, he took most of
his ideas from the weaving industry which used punched cards to drive
looms.

But, what the hey. I saw recently where the last Morse Code station in the
world just shut down. Another part of my education rendered obsolete
(I tried translating the Bible into Morse Code, but could find no publisher
willing to take a chance).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How are records born?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7DC7.C1513F4A@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418200914.29269.00002637@ng-ft1.aol.com> <7fgbo2$o0m$1@news1.skynet.be>`

```
> I've seen a lot of files arround which have length 80 (now allready in 3
> different mainframe using companies), even when they just as well could have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> theory is right ?

Pretty much.  But there are some other leftover effects.  My COBOL
source code is in a PDS (a library) which is set up for 80 column wide
data, because that's the COBOL standard and because it fits nicely on my
screen.  TSO will allow 133 columns also, but that's hard to read and
not applicable for editing source code.

With this in mind, it's relatively easy to create test data 80 columns
wide.  It's easy to have an 80 column data file which is editable and/or
viewable by users.  If the data only warrant 40 columns, the overhead of
wasting the other 40 columns isn't significant for small files, such as
those which users hand edit.  

But large files are almost always the size necessary to hold the data. 
This is only coincidentally 80 columns.
 
> By the way, last week our account manager went on retirement. In his
> farewell speech he mentioned that the first mainframe he had to work with
> (somewhere in the sixties I guess) had 4K memory. Incredible, huh ?!
> 
> Greetz, Jeroentje

Been there, done that.
```

- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ffj1a$f0a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd087$37i@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```

  "William M. Klein" <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com> wrote:
> Look at the intrinsic function -
>    UPPER-CASE

Bill,

How about the standard? Does FUNCTION UPPER-CASE have to respect locale
characters like the french '����' or the german ���?

IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM 2.1.1 leaves them 'asis' which is not at all
correct.
