###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ffk79$9f3@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd087$37i@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <7ffj1a$f0a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
The current ('85 with '89 Intrinsic Function) Standard thinks that only
"un-accented" characters are alphabetic.  Therefore, UPPER-CASE and similar
functions will NOT work (or may not work - depending on the implementation
extensions) correctly.  The draft of the next Standard *does* add support
for "locales" - which includes the ability to work correctly with
accented-characters.

NOTE: For IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (mentioned in this note), I am checking
into whether there are already LE routines that will do this.  I think that
CEESTXF may work in this area - but I am not certain.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgrg8$407@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd087$37i@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com> <7ffj1a$f0a$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7ffk79$9f3@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```


> NOTE: For IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (mentioned in this note), I am
checking
> into whether there are already LE routines that will do this.  I think
that
> CEESTXF may work in this area - but I am not certain.
>
>

I have checked with my "usually reliable sources" and there is currently no
LE (CEE) callable routine for doing Upper-Case/Lower-Case conversion based
on "locale" (i.e. including European - and other - accented characters).  I
have passed this on to the project leader of the SHARE "COBOL & LE" project
for consideration as a new requirement.  My guess is that this is something
that would get a BETTER reply if submitted by the European GUIDE or SHARE
groups - but I will see if it can also be progressed in the US.  If any
reader of this NG is active in the European group, please let me know
(private email is OK) and we can see if this can get progressed.

As stated earlier, the NEXT Standard will include support for this - but the
most optimistic estimate is that it will be come official in 2002 - and when
(if?) IBM would/will ever implement it is another question.

P.S. I think that the CEESTXF callable service IS usable for determining if
an upper-case and lower-case letter ARE equivalent (even with accents) - but
it can't be used to do the actual conversion.
```

#### ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
inspect stringname converting "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" to
"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".

Alberto Santamaria Garcia wrote in message
<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>...
>HOW CAN I CONVERT ANY STRING IN COBOL TO A STRING WITH ONLY CAPITALS
>CHARACTERS?
>
>
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
>
>Alberto Santamaria Garcia wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".

The advantage of Donald's method is that it also works in COBOL 74 as
well as the current draft standard (at least the last draft I saw :).
Some of my clients still use COBOL 74, and I must write a lot of code
which works across versions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B6EAC.AE4BA05B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia>`

```
> >inspect stringname converting "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" to
> >"ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> which works across versions.

On the other hand, it doesn't work with ANSI 68, where EXAMINE works. 
TRANSFORM works for both, but doesn't work for modern COBOLs, but
upper_case works.  It appears that nothing works for all COBOLs.   I
have no idea why syntax has been futzed around so much.  What was wrong
with EXAMINE and TRANSFORM?  I'm guessing they were extensions which
lost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CCE2F.5ABC@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <371B6EAC.AE4BA05B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> > >inspect stringname converting "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz" to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > well as the current draft standard (at least the last draft I saw :).

No it does not.  It works in COBOL 85, not COBOL 74.  See below.

> > Some of my clients still use COBOL 74, and I must write a lot of code
> > which works across versions.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> lost.

EXAMINE (which was in the '68 standard) was replaced by INSPECT in the 
'74 standard.  EXAMINE was too limited and rather than changing the 
syntax of EXAMINE it was decided to replace the statement entirely.  I 
wasn't part of this, but I think it was felt that replacing the 
statement worked better.  Implementors could keep EXAMINE as an 
extension (which almost all of them did) until it faded away.

INSPECT in the '74 standard did not have a CONVERTING option.  That 
was added to the '85 standard.  Primarily because of the IBM 
extension, TRANSFORM.  It was decided that rather than adding a new 
statement we could easily change one that was already in the standard 
that does that kind of thing anyhow.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgrtm$4c9@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I wouldn't use "weird" - but I would say that if a character is not in the
ISO 646 "portable" character set (and notice this is an ISO Standard, not an
ANSI one) - then the character is at least "non-universal in Roman scripts".

FYI - (Plug for next Standard)
   The advantage of using the enhancements in the next Standard over using
the INSPECT verb is that you need not "hard code" the conversion at compile
time.  Instead, you (the COBOL run-time) automatically gets these
equivalency values at run-time.  This is particularly important for cases
where one "language" uses one set of values as valid lower-cases for a
specific upper-case graphic - and another language uses another set of
value.  It is also nice in that the exact same "object code" can run in
France with one (locale-based) result, in Germany with another, and Sweden
with still another.

FINALLY,  you might want to check with your specific vendor to see if they
already provide a facility for doing this.  (I have checked and IBM COBOL
for OS/390 & VM does *not* support this - but I think that Micro Focus'
"NLS" support might provide this already.)
```

Cheesle wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>Should we take offense that you did not capitalize 'English'?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>this matter.

I'm sure you are correct.  On the other hand, Americans get tired of
being whacked on by people, especially when they do it using technology
we created, often with funds we gave them, okay. :-)  Such is life, we
are all most interested in our own backyards.  We Americans don't sit
around and plot how we can offend the rest of the world. ;-)  We, just
like you, are simply tending to our own interests.  After all, if it
were not for the huge American market, would companies in other countries
be concerned about American interests?  Of course not, they are looking
after their own interests too.  The United States has been blessed in
many ways, and currently has enormous worldwide influence because of it.
It wasn't always this way, and surely won't always be this way.  It is
human nature that this affects how U.S. companies and citizens view their
country, the world, and themselves, and how others view us.  So would it
affect your country or any other.  We are all human here.  But there is
nothing malevolent in it on our part.  How many countries can you name,
other than your own, that you would sleep better if the U.S. was gone and
they assumed the position that the U.S. has now?  If your country had a
terrible natural disaster or was attacked by somebody, from where would
you most expect help to come?  We're not the 'bad guys', just give us a
break, okay? :-)
```

Judson McClendon wrote in message <_u8T2.1207$oH.399439@news3.mia>...
>I'm sure you are correct.  On the other hand, Americans get tired of
>...

Generally speaking; well said. You made good points and I have to agree,
except for:

>How many countries can you name, other than your own, that you would sleep
better if the U.S. was >gone and they assumed the position that the U.S. has
now?

I think I have most Norwegian with me in saying that we sleep pretty well
the way US is today. Both countries are a part of the western culture and
are politically the same. There certainly are other countries that would
want US down and away, but for Norway? No way.

>If your country had a terrible natural disaster

I would be surprised if we were to get any help at all. Being one of the
richest countries in the world (way more rich pr. man than the US) we would
fix a natural disaster on our own.\

>was attacked by somebody, from where would you most expect help to come?

England, France, Germany, US and others. That's why we're a part of NATO.

>We're not the 'bad guys', just give us a break, okay? :-)

Nor did I say you were, calm down. As I have stated before english is not my
native tongue, hence my speeches may become more offending than expected,
for that case I apologize. It may not have been clear but I targeted the
American Standard committee and in particular IBM. Although both are from
the US and have a lot of influence I was not targeting US. What's most
important; There was made a mistake once, and I just want that i won't
happen again.

BTW: Regarding technological inventions, did you know that the very first
Object Oriented programming language was from Norway (Simula), that the
research around that was the basics that made Xerox' do what later on became
Apple Lisa? In these days of war, it may make sense to mention that the hand
granate also were invented by a Norwegian :-)

Hm, this is way of the subject of this NG :-)

Have a sunny day!

Cheesle

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371e3eef.30849606@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <aBOS2.269$_N.107537@news3.mia> <7fi4tq$b9o$1@news.cerf.net> <_u8T2.1207$oH.399439@news3.mia> <7fkres$et5$1@news.cerf.net> <371E09D2.21CF488C@NOSPAMhome.com> <7fl7fh$ive$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote in message <371E09D2.21CF488C@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>>Yes.  And it wasn't developed because of some style or theory about
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Cheesle:

I'm not sure if I agree 100%.  I think that human thought can and does
occur in many different forms, depending upon the specific
circumstances.  I also believe that humans generally adapt their
thinking to the specific circumstance at hand.

I believe that the way a soldier thinks under fire (reactionary) is a
much different thought process than when a person is following
directions to a strange new place (very procedural..ergo "top down").

There are many differences in the two scenarios.  I consider the
soldier to be thinking in more of an "event" type mode...in other
words, he doesn't really know what will happen next, so whatever
happens next, he will react to that specific event.

But the person following directions is thinking in more of a
procedural approach.

1.  Go down this road first.
2.  Turn left at second stop light.
3.  etc.

Just my opinion.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 16)_

such as Russian.  At least that is the sort of situation I was referring to.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fi5uo$bkv$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net>...
>I consider weird to be any language where upper and lower case do not
match,
>such as Russian.  At least that is the sort of situation I was referring
to.

Then it is time for school :-)

Example:
Norwegian has three special letters, which in the early days where not found
to have demand enough to be offered a place in the sunshine of the American
Standard comitee. Which indeed makes sense, as long as there is just above 4
million of us in the world. The fact that the ignorant attitude of the
American Standard comitee also affected millions of French, German,
Portugeese, Spanish and many more is another story.

What Norwegian developers had to do, to fit their special letters into the
127 available characters was to replace other, indeed useful characters.
Thus, [\] and {|} became the special ��� and ��� (I hope you are able to
read them).
Fair enough as long as one was inside Norway. But, you may imagine how many
hilarious experiences you get, when the system switch (\ for windows/dos) is
used.

Though, IBM knew their responsibility, being an international corporation
they set up an extended ASCII set in order to make a solution to this. Which
would have been great, if it wasn't for the bad work they performed. If you
look once more at the special characters, you notice they are represented in
both lowercase and uppercase. As we all know, uppercase letters are supposed
to be sorted before lowercase letters, not so with IBM extended, in their
lack of knowledge, they placed the letters quite random, thus causing a
simple sorting by value impossible to perform. Unfortunately, this became a
standard.

As for EBCDIC I don't know, I am not familiar with that set.

Luckily, though I may say a lot of bad things about Microsoft, character
sets is something they have done proper. Although the letters are put in
randomly, they are in correct order, such that upper case will sort before
lowercase in sense of values.

You may say that it is not the business of US software developers to pay
attention to this subject, I cannot argue with you on that. What I would
make a point of here, is that the weird positioning of letters in the ASCII,
IBM charactersets are done by US developers /commitees. Not by the affected
countries themself.

Finally, it is not my intention to start a war here, if anything is to be
considered offending of what I have written it is due to the fact that
english is not my native tongue. I just wanted to attempt to describe what a
problem this really is.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CD2D9.AC1D90BA@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net> <7fi5uo$bkv$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
> Luckily, though I may say a lot of bad things about Microsoft, character
> sets is something they have done proper. Although the letters are put in
> randomly, they are in correct order, such that upper case will sort before
> lowercase in sense of values.

Is there any historical reason for upper case going before lower case? 
Or where non-alphabetic characters should sort?  Or how accented
characters should sort?  

I certainly don't think users would think the following are in order:

Abc
CBA
abc

In real life every variation of A should go before any variation of B. 
Check your dictionary - it doesn't have all capitalized words before all
lower case words.

Let's say we are using Unicode and have five different symbols which can
be placed over the letter "e".  How do we sort the data using COBOL so
that they appear in the correct order to users?  We probably have to
train them to our way of handling non-alpha characters, but we should
adjust to their existing concepts of alpha.
```

Good question! I don't know, may be just a coincident?

>In real life every variation of A should go before any variation of B.
>Check your dictionary - it doesn't have all capitalized words before all
>lower case words.

Agree.

>Let's say we are using Unicode and have five different symbols which can
>be placed over the letter "e".  How do we sort the data using COBOL so

will fix that.

Cheesle

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fj1qg$qc0@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net> <7fi5uo$bkv$1@news.cerf.net> <371CD2D9.AC1D90BA@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Interesting to read this note today.  I was just reading some comments on
the proposed (looks like it needs more work) ISO Standard on character
ordering.  The entire document that I was reading was too LONG to post here,
but I thought you might want to read a section on sorting upper-/lower-case,
(If you are interested in the WHOLE thing - contact your national body
organization for ISO and ask for information on,
 "FCD 14651 - Method for Comparing Character Strings and Description of a
Common Tailorable Ordering Template"

Interesting quote (semi-long) follows,

"> 3. The third decomposition breaks ties for quasi-homographs different
> only because upper-case and lower-case characters are used. This time,
> the tradition is well established in English and German dictionaries,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> along with the actual syntax for ordering caps before smalls. "
```

standard.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fimut$9q6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net> <7fi5uo$bkv$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote in message
<large snippage>

>You may say that it is not the business of US software developers to pay
>attention to this subject, I cannot argue with you on that. What I would
>make a point of here, is that the weird positioning of letters in the
ASCII,
>IBM charactersets are done by US developers /commitees. Not by the affected
>countries themself.


(I am not in the U.S., BTW)  I chose the wrong adjective in using "weird",
as it could be contrued offensive.  I am sorry, as that was not my intent.
The real point of the message is that the inspect translating method can be
used with any character set, not just the English one.  Theoretically, the
upper case function should be smart enough to handle all of the character
sets available, but I doubt that they do.
>
>Finally, it is not my intention to start a war here, if anything is to be
>considered offending of what I have written it is due to the fact that
>english is not my native tongue. I just wanted to attempt to describe what
a
>problem this really is.


I have been aware of the problem for quite some time, as I also have
programmed in French. My other languages (Hebrew, Latin and Greek), I have
never written code for.  In fact, I don't think I've even read anything in
any of them that was written after about the year 300. ;<)  I am glad of
that, as handling *those* character sets ... I would not know where to
start. I think Hebrew would be the worst ... I've noticed one poster in here
from Israel, how do you handle points?

Certainly, I never took offense at your post ... I wasn't sure if you were
annoyed or not, so I just answered it factually.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fks29$f66$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es> <7fd809$i66$1@news.igs.net> <IyJS2.686$km2.205729@news2.mia> <7fg65u$gb8$1@news.igs.net> <7fg7p4$fuc$1@news.cerf.net> <7fgnit$qv7$1@news.igs.net> <7fi5uo$bkv$1@news.cerf.net> <7fimut$9q6$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote in message <7fimut$9q6$1@news.igs.net>...
>Theoretically, the upper case function should be smart enough to handle all
of the character
>sets available, but I doubt that they do.

Upper/Lower is a difficult task indeed. In my opinion it should be a
function of the host (OS) not of the language itself, both because of its
complex nature but also because it would ensure strings equal between
applications developed in the different languages.

I prefer the INSPECT REPLACING statement as it is very dynamic, may be too
dynamic. Your suggestion of adding 0x20 although very common is strictly
limited to the ASCII.

>Certainly, I never took offense at your post ...

I pleased to hear that.

>I wasn't sure if you were annoyed or not

A little bit may be :-) But more as a result of dealing with the sorting
throughout the years than your statement in particular.

Have a sunny day!

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: string mixed to string in CAPITALS

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B345A.340E4B75@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fcs5t$jj39@eui1nw.euskaltel.es>`

```
Alberto Santamaria Garcia wrote:
> 
> HOW CAN I CONVERT ANY STRING IN COBOL TO A STRING WITH ONLY CAPITALS
> CHARACTERS?


With a modern compiler use the UPPER-CASE function.  With older
compilers, the INSPECT, EXAMINE, or TRANSFORM verbs can be used.

BTW, newsgroups work better with mixed case.  Upper case is yelling, or
in this NG, is used to indicate COBOL code (as in my example above).
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The best course I ever took was from Verhoef many, many years ago. They
taught a modular coding technique that is extremely easy to write, read, and
maintain. However, I have only seen a few sites that actually use or accept
the technique because they feel it is inefficient (OLD SCHOOL). With compiler
optimizers this is definitely not true, but it is hard to change mind sets. I
don't know if Verhoef still exists or has courses, a few internet searches
should find them if they still exist. If not, look for a book called
something like 'Advanced CICS Techniques' by Summerville. The book is at
home, otherwise, I could give you the exact title.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

```

  cesaralt@blockbuster.com.pe (Cesar Altamirano Arbayza) wrote:
> Hi friends,
> Will you please tell me where to find more information
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Number: SC33-1687-01', online at:

#### ↳ Re: COBOL CICS

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B4872.50C9E34C@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<37194262.6255514@news.unired.net.pe>`

```

Cesar

I'm assuming you're asking this question as a COBOL programmer who wants
to know a little more about COBOL programming under CICS.

In that light, you might also appreciate the CICS Application
Programming Primer at -
 http://www.s390.ibm.com:80/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHZP104/CCONTENTS?SHELF=DFHHSH21

As a CICS Systems Programmer (albeit with COBOL experience) I found it a
very useful way of learning what my applications colleagues were up to.
The important thing about this book, compared with the Application
Programmers Guide (APG) or Application Programmers Reference (APR) is
that it explains how to write a CICS application from the ground up.
Assuming you complete the exercises in the book, by the time you reach
the end, you've written a passable CICS application and its modular
approach means you've got a library of reusable procedures. From my
point of view this is important because it means you're not going to try
and run batch type code under CICS.
```

- **From:** casey <sarvec@n-link.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<371A5184.137D@n-link.com>`

```
Are walkthroughs still done?
Are they productive or does management use them as a tool?
Are they popular or something that is to be avoided?
I am interested in any stories but good and bad that anyone has.
Thanks,
Casey.
sarvec@n-link.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Walkthroughs

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#vi5zgpi#GA.309@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<371A5184.137D@n-link.com>`

```
They can be part of a great set of procedures if done right, or part of a
terrible group of practices by a company that hasn't a clue about how
software should be developed and how people should be treated (funny how
these 2 seem to go hand in hand). At one client, a "walkthrough" was more
of a group attack criticizing every possible thing where if the
participants had been so willing to spend that much time earlier explaining
the company's arcane middleware and strange coding practices and the
screwed up logic in the program in the first place, the programmer would've
been able to make the needed changes more easily without having to add lots
of extra defensive code just in case.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA

that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!

#### ↳ Re: Walkthroughs

- **From:** lawmom1001@aol.comsiscom (LawMom1001)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990418221207.17295.00002481@ng153.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<371A5184.137D@n-link.com>`

```
they're still done where i work.  the value seems to depend on who attends. 
i'm new at the company and have only gone to 2 so far.  the first one was very
helpful.  i had a copy of the load module well ahead of time, looked at some of
the includes and feeds in and out of the program and had a pretty good idea of
what the program was supposed to do when i got there.  the people there had
good feedback and suggestions for the programmer that had made the coding
changes (maintenance).
    the second waltkthru was nothing like the first one.  it seemed everyone
was just there to rubberstamp.  although the program was well-written, i had
still expected more discussion.
Carol a/k/a  Lawmom1001@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Walkthroughs

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990419085507.20551.00004045@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19990418221207.17295.00002481@ng153.aol.com>`

```
I wish more companies would do formal walkthroughs, perhaps the cycle of what
the programmer produces is curiously not exactly what the user wanted, or the
user changed their mind somewhere downstream, and "just" forgot to tell
anybody.

"...the cycle will be unbroken, bye and bye"

TW
```

#### ↳ Re: Walkthroughs

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B2746.16436942@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<371A5184.137D@n-link.com>`

```
casey wrote:
> 
> Are walkthroughs still done?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> sarvec@n-link.com

Walkthroughs are an exceptional tool *if* they are done at clean
compile time.  Often they are done far to late and become a rubber
stamp exercise.  Done correctly they pay themselves back 3 times
(1 hour in takes out three hours testing) on average.

Walkthroughs of a skilled programmers work for a junior programmer
should still happen.  The review by a peer is always useful and
second the junior will gain knowledge about why things are
attacked a certain way.  Ask lots of questions and get under the
skin of these reviews.  Spend at least two hours before the review
understanding what is going on and ask if you can't figure it
out.  The bottom line is that the others are probably not thinking
and you can really change that.

If you think that the guru's code was that well done tell him to
post it and see what happens :-)

Good luck,
Ken
```

<a id="t-77187f30d8"></a>
## Thread: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software — 13 msgs

### Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** Demirtas Enver <edemirtas@bluewin.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch>`

```
Hello everybody,

I am very much impressed by the current trend for open source software
led by Linux operating system as well as a multitude of application
software, commercialized with the source code at an unbeatable price.

I have a general accounging software, written in COBOL. During the
last ten years, I have sold, an average of three or for copies per
year at a price of 1200.-- Swiss Francs. It has always remained a
marginal activity in my business since I have realized the major part
of my revenue in custom-tailored business applications and other related
computer services and consultancy.

Now I am at a crossread, ready to take a decision as to what to do with
my accounting software. Open the source code to the entire planet and
sell it at an unbeatable price of say, 50 U.S. dollars, just enough to
cover the costs of recording it on a CD and the shipping costs, leaving
me a small margin of profit? Or to continue to content myself of the
modest sales of two or three copies per year, hoping that one day I
shall have, by some miracle, enough funds to invest for promoting
and marketing it, as dictated by business rules, in an already crowded
competition environment.

So, I will appreciate very much your opinion on this subject.

Here is a a brief historics and description of my accounting software.

It was initially developed on a Tany Model III, more than 15 years ago,
before the entry of IBM on the PC market, using the interpreted BASIC,
the only computer programming language then available on this platform.
After the entry of IBM in the PC market, I have acquired an IBM PC AT 
and rewritten it in COBOL, first using the Microsoft COBOL compiler and
later, using the Microfocus COBOL compiler. I have, afterwards, moved it
from MS-DOS platform to MS Windows 3.1, 3.11, Windows 95, Windows 98 and
Windows NT 4.00. I have also rendered it a multiuser, multi-currency
software running under NOVEL network operating system.

It is a general accounting software, which has proved to be solid like
rock during its uitilization of more than ten years by my few but
happy clients. It is simple to use, ideal for small businesses as
well as single-person businesses. The source code is split to a dozen
of modules, each one corresponding to one or more relevant accounting
functions. Altogether, that makes about thirty five thousand lines of
source code. The program is is written, using a structured programming
method, having some similarities to the method of Jackson, but having
after all my own personal method of structured programming. In the
entire source code, there does not exist a single GO TO instruction.
I have given priority to maintainablility of the source code rather
than to optimization for program speed. By carefully choosing the
constant names, the data names and the paragraph names, and by making
them as long as required, upto 30 characters, if necessary, I have
auto-documented it. I have largely used the concept of top-down
programming technique and devided the code to small logical blocks,
placed within the paragraphs. Despite all this, astonishingly, I have
had no speed penalty. My program executes at an honorable speed even
with old slow PCs. The whole source code is written, using only
English language for constant names, data names and paragraph names
except for the user interface elements since it was written to address
the needs of French-speaking clients. However, it would be very
easy to convert the user interface to English or to any other language.

So, please give me your opinion. 

Many thanks in advance.

E. DEMIRTAS
```

#### ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#lSsMjpi#GA.56@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch>`

```
Sounds very interesting. I really need something that does most all of what
Quicken and QuickBooks do (except inventory) but with decent support for
multiple business (including nesting). I need to be able to define multiple
entities and the relationships between them and then be able to enter
inter-entity transactions just ONCE and have it flow automatically to both
sides, each from the appropriate entity's point of view, e.g. company A
loans money to company B, it's an asset to A but a liability to B; C
invests in D, it's an investment account to C but an equity account in D,
etc. In Quicken I have to keep each business in a separate file (account
group) and manually enter the same transaction a different way in each one.
Is there any chance your program can handle this? I don't have time to
write my own.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA

```

- **Message-ID:** `<7fg6do$gk8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I do not think that open source means low price, particularly.  Frankly, I
would avoid dropping the price unless you are planning on spending a lot of
money marketing.  Open source should make it *more* valuable, not less.

Demirtas Enver wrote in message <371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch>...
>Hello everybody,
>
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7FC5.B00FB7E8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch> <7fg6do$gk8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> 
> I do not think that open source means low price, particularly.  Frankly, I
> would avoid dropping the price unless you are planning on spending a lot of
> money marketing.  Open source should make it *more* valuable, not less.
> 

The ups and the down sides.  The source can and will be lifted and
used for nothing by people.  There is nothing you can (or should)
do about this.

The product being open source you will get interested parties who
are professional programmers fixing your problems and extending
your software for nothing.  Marketing is about flexible software
after all.

Demirtas has already stated that he was consulting more than he
was selling.  It is a smart principle to stick with a strength. 
If you look at the MYSQL model you will find that the product is
free but support has a variety of options.  This varies from (I
think) one week email turn around to instant response.

If you want to go consulting encourage a minimal supporting fee so
that the people (that will steal it anyway) would rather pay than
have something illegal on their system.

I use Quicken (mentioned elsewhere) it cost me $120 with support
and manuals.  I want to move to Linux and a good accounting
package that I can support is better than anything else I can buy.

The choice is yours,
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** John Leach <bbosware@vic.bigpond.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371DC032.42B47C37@vic.bigpond.net.au>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch>`

```
I often get asked about accounting software under Linux. There is a need
for Open Source accounting software. Although there are quite a few
other initiatives underway from what I have read they are either huge
SAP competing products, or single-user PC competitors. If your product
is multi-user, I would say there is a place for it. I'd like to have a
look at it if you do decide to go OS with it.
There are various Open Source licences - from the 'GPL' to the
'Artistic' etc.
The main problem for any OS Cobol product under Linux is that as far as
I know all the Cobol useable runtimes and compilers for Linux are
commercial. Whereas if you code in another language under Linux there
are multiple free languages you can choose from: numerous free
compilers, and run-times are free too generally. No new programmer has
any incentive to pick up a Cobol manual because there is so much for
free out there. 
For comparison, check out the San Francisco project by IBM. They give
the source code away for free but charge a small royalty on every sale.
(this is written in Java).
Regards
John Leach
AcuCobol/Linux consultant
www.bosware.com.au




Demirtas Enver wrote:
> 
> Hello everybody,
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
> E. DEMIRTAS
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fksqv$97d$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch> <371DC032.42B47C37@vic.bigpond.net.au>`

```
In article <371DC032.42B47C37@vic.bigpond.net.au>,
  John Leach <bbosware@vic.bigpond.net.au> wrote:
> The main problem for any OS Cobol product under Linux is
that as far as
> I know all the Cobol useable runtimes and compilers for
Linux are
> commercial.

Presently your only real choice for COBOL under Linux is
PerCOBOL.  This is because it is the only one that does not
charge a runtime fee.

When RM does their Linux port, there will still be a
runtime fee.  I can't sell $50-75 software with a $50-100
runtime fee.  I cannot compete.  Linux and open source
could very well be the "end of the line" for COBOL.  I do
NOT MIND purchasing a Linux COBOL compiler - but I need one
that does NOT require a per copy runtime license fee for
the distributed product.


-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** emilne@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fm4gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch>`

```
Here are some issues that you might want to think about.

1) Intention and Motivation What is your intention in this?  Is it just to
put out another software product that happens to include the source code or
is it to give free software to the world like Linux?

What is your motivation?  Linus Torvald had a passionate belief that software
should be free.  This belief not only motivated him; it attracted many other
people to contribute to his project.  If you are only looking for the best way
for you to make some money, that is an entirely different matter.

2) Copyright, Copyleft or Public Domain
The rights to Linux are controlled by a copyleft.  Any one who modifies Linus
code is required to make their modifications public.

I don�t think that this is practical for business applications.  Specialising
an application for a specific business usually means including their business
policies and procedures in the programs.  This is all information that the
business would not want to make public.

If you were just thinking about including source code as part of the product
that you sell, then a normal copyright would work.  Otherwise, I think
putting the source code into the public domain is the only practical option. 
This implies that other people could take your source code, make their own
private modifications and sell the result as a commercial product.  Could you
live with this?

3) Role
Linux is not just some software.  It is an ongoing development project.  If
Linux is your model, what sort of role do you want for yourself - project
manager, contributor, exploiter?

4) Tools
My choice of tools would be the Fujitsu v3 COBOL.  If is a full scale ANSI 84
COBOL development environment that is free for the downloading
(www.adtools.com) and does not require any runtimes.

Ed Milne

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net

- **Message-ID:** `<7fnb9q$g38$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch> <7fm4gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <7fm4gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  emilne@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> Here are some issues that you might want to think about.

> What is your motivation?  Linus Torvald had a passionate
belief that software
> should be free.

Here Linus and I disagree.  Software should not be free.
How will programmers make a living if the fruits of their
labors are given away?

> 4) Tools
> My choice of tools would be the Fujitsu v3 COBOL.  If is
a full scale ANSI 84
> COBOL development environment that is free for the
downloading
> (www.adtools.com) and does not require any runtimes.
>
> Ed Milne

Ed,

If the target platform is to INCLUDE Linux - then Fujitsu
is not the answer - it won't run under Linux!!!

I think that the first compiler vendor to come up with a
reasonobly priced COBOL compiler for Linux (say < $500.00)
and with no runtime distribution fees, will win the game.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** emilne@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fpddq$clf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch> <7fm4gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fnb9q$g38$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```

>> What is your motivation?  Linus Torvald had a passionate belief that software
>> should be free.
> Here Linus and I disagree.  Software should not be free.
> How will programmers make a living if the fruits of their
> labors are given away?

There is money to be made from free (or more accurately "open source")
software but it is made indirectly rather than directly.  There are many
companies who would pay a significant premium to be able to claim that Linus
Torvald is one of their employees.  For application software, companies would
pay to have the applications modified to meet their specific needs.  They
would probably pay a premium to have an expert (say an acknowledged
contributor) make such changes.

Even if there is no charge for the base software, I believe that companies
will spend the same amount on software development.  They will just spend
more on specialising and enhancing the software.

>> My choice of tools would be the Fujitsu v3 COBOL.

> If the target platform is to INCLUDE Linux - then Fujitsu
> is not the answer - it won't run under Linux!!!

I don't think that environments in which a tools runs is the issue.  The
important things are that:
- there is no charge for the tool so it is as easy as possible for people to
contribute.
- the tool is usable by the largest number of people (which in practice means
that it runs on a PC under Windows).
- the resulting code is ANSI standard and thus portable.

Ed Milne

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3720fdad.172832355@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<371B0495.9368C28@bluewin.ch> <7fm4gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fnb9q$g38$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fpddq$clf$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Fri, 23 Apr 1999 09:10:20 GMT, emilne@my-dejanews.com wrote:


>There is money to be made from free (or more accurately "open source")
>software but it is made indirectly rather than directly.  There are many
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Good points.  I've been thinking about this for a while and I have
some additional thoughts.

I've been part of "open source"  for a while.  My first encounter was
with the BBS software WWIV.  Registered users were given the source
code, and they could modify or "Mod" it in order to customize their
BBS.  Other sysops published their Mods and allowed others to install
them.  Distribution of the source code was prohibited - and while
pirate copies existed, for the most part this was successful.
Periodically the author would release new versions of the entire code
base - and the whole customization cycle would begin again.  

Toward the end (and it's still actually going, but the dial up BBS is
dead) there were professional modification services available for
those who did not want to do the modificaitons themselves.

This is not PURE open source, as it is currently considered, but it is
something similar.  

Now, the PROBLEM  (until recently) with COBOL projects was the sheer
COST of the compiler.  There are few hobbiest COBOL programmers - most
are professionals working as COBOL programmers.  With Linux, WWIV and
others, you have a lot of part timers and hobbiests.  The compiler is
free and easy to get.  

With Fujitsu v3 being free (as long as it is available) it makes a
fair platform.  Fair only because 4.2 is so much better than v3 (not
that v3 is bad). 

To be truly popular, an open source COBOL project would need to be
cross system compatible - at least in the root code base.  For an
Operating system  this is no big deal, but for an application this is
a major headache.  Designed properly perhaps not to large a headache.
I would isolate the user interface and File I/O from the rest of the
system.  

Those are just some preliminary thoughts.  I've got more running
around in my head, but I can't get them out right now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFAvqEF.4Ho@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fnb9q$g38$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net writes:

>I think that the first compiler vendor to come up with a
>reasonobly priced COBOL compiler for Linux (say < $500.00)
>and with no runtime distribution fees, will win the game.

I haven't seen a response that presents an alternative plan.

What if a sterile (no contamination from the commercial version)
runtime were produced under under the Linux/FSF/OS umbrella?  
A runtime that could run the p-code produced by, for instance,
AcuCobol.  

AcuCobol p-code, regardless of the machine that produced it, 
will run on any platform with an AcuCobol runtime.  Produce 
an open source runtime which can be distributed with Linux.  

A Developer then buys a personal version of AcuCobol and
distributes the p-code it produces.  I think the licensing
restrictions are on the runtime not the p-code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Your opinion on Open Source Accounting software

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37279e0a.284992219@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<gl$ehk$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fnb9q$g38$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <dbryantFAvqEF.4Ho@netcom.com>`

```
On Wed, 28 Apr 1999 03:15:51 GMT, dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
wrote:

>redsky@ibm.net writes:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>restrictions are on the runtime not the p-code.

AcuCOBOL presently provides a runtime (and PERHAPS a full development
environment) for Linux.  My MAIN objection is NOT to the cost of the
development environment - really - but to the end user cost of the
RUNTIME.  Yes, the development tool SHOULD be inexpensive, so that the
hobbiest developer can afford it.

Hobbiest COBOL programmers don't really seem to exist.  It's really
too bad, and something I would like to do something about.  PERSONALLY
I think the main reason is the cost of the development tools then the
subsequent cost of deployment.

Any other opinions?
```

<a id="t-64c5a58b30"></a>
## Thread: Valid Values — 7 msgs

### Valid Values

- **From:** Harish Murthy <worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Hi,

Suppose the variable is defined as below,

01 TEMP-SWITCH        PIC 9
     88 FIRST-VALID VALUE 0
     88 SECOND-VALIE VALUE 1.

Is there any method in COBOL where i can get the name that corresponds
to the particular valid value.

example:: when the variable TEMP-SWITCH gets the value 0 , i want to
know that
                 FIRST-VALID is the value in TEMP-SWITCH.
i wud like to display "FIRST-VALID" rather than a 0 .

Any other method than

                                IF FIRST-VALID IN TEMP-SWITCH
                                THEN
                                       DISPLAY "FIRST-VALID"
                                END-IF.

or an equiv EVALUATE statement

??


Thanks
Harish
```

#### ↳ Re: Valid Values

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B28DB.4C3A137A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Harish Murthy wrote:
> 
> Is there any method in COBOL where i can get the name that corresponds
> to the particular valid value.
> 

The answer is possibly use tables.  for example read in a table
containing:

1,fred
2,joe

When you read 'fred' you search the table and get 1 when you want
to revert it your read the number 1 and get 'fred'.

Other than that NO
```

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```

hard-coding it in the Proc. Div.

#### ↳ Re: Valid Values

- **From:** twymer@aol.com (Twymer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990419084246.20551.00004037@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```

you could say:

if first-valid
   display "first- valid"
else
   if second-value
       display "second-value"
   else
        display "invalid value"
   end-if
end-if.

or

Evaluate temp-switch
   when 0
      display "first-value"
   when 1
      display "second-value"
   otherwise
      display "invalid value"
end-evaluate.
```

#### ↳ Re: Valid Values

- **From:** colm@skynet.csn.ul.ie (An Fear Ar An nGealach)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FALoBp.GMv@ul.ie>`
- **References:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```
Like this.

01 TEMP-SWITCH PIC 9.
	88 FIRST-VALID	VALUE 0.
	88 SECOND-VALID VALUE 1.

then

01 TEMP-SWITCH-TABLE.
	02	FIRST-ONE 	PIC X(12) VALUE "FIRST-VALID".
	02	SECOND-ONE	PIC X(12) VALUE "SECOND-VALID".

then

01 OTHER-TS-TABLE	REDEFINES TEMP-SWITCH-TABLE.
	02	VALID-STRING	PIC X(12) OCCURS 2 TIMES.

then in the procedure div

	DISPLAY VALID-STRING (TEMP-SWITCH).

Presumably if you don't want to be doing loads of if statements, you have
a lot more than two valid values, otherwise this would be a lot of
unnecessary work.


Harish Murthy (worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com) wrote:
: Hi,

: Suppose the variable is defined as below,

: 01 TEMP-SWITCH        PIC 9
:      88 FIRST-VALID VALUE 0
:      88 SECOND-VALIE VALUE 1.

: Is there any method in COBOL where i can get the name that corresponds
: to the particular valid value.

: example:: when the variable TEMP-SWITCH gets the value 0 , i want to
: know that
:                  FIRST-VALID is the value in TEMP-SWITCH.
: i wud like to display "FIRST-VALID" rather than a 0 .

: Any other method than

:                                 IF FIRST-VALID IN TEMP-SWITCH
:                                 THEN
:                                        DISPLAY "FIRST-VALID"
:                                 END-IF.

: or an equiv EVALUATE statement

: ??


: Thanks
: Harish
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Valid Values

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990423221704.14335.00002932@ng-cl1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<FALoBp.GMv@ul.ie>`

```
>Harish Murthy (worm@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com) wrote:
>: Hi,
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 
How does TEMP-SWITCH get valued? If the program is setting it based on certain
conditions, you could go this route:
01  TEMP-SWITCH PIC X(12).
    88  FIRST-VALID VALUE 'FIRST-VALID'
    88  SECOND-VALID VALUE 'SECOND-VALID'

IF some condition
    SET FIRST-VALID TO TRUE.

IF some other condition
    SET SECOND-VALID TO TRUE.

Then TEMP-SWITCH would contain the desired literal.
```

#### ↳ Re: Valid Values

- **From:** smayer@t-online.de (Sebastian Mayer)
- **Date:** 1999-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3724EC39.9BA749C3@t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<371B06E7.321BFA04@SPAM-NO.thewatercooler.com>`

```


Harish Murthy schrieb:
> 
> Hi,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Hi Harish,

this isnï¿½t probably what you are looking for, but perhaps you can use
it. In other replies the way to do it by "hand" was shown. An
alternative may be to use my source code generator PROGEN (itï¿½s beta
running under Win95 and above and can be downloaded at
http://www.maysoft.de/progen.htm). The main advantage is, that it can
handle several hundred 88-values at once and that he is flexible in the
way that if you change/add an 88-value the acording display statement
will follow. Before I show you how you can do it with progen here are
some remarks:

- Lines begining at col 1 are control lines (meta language) for the
generator (like "loop", "saveas" ..) 
- Variables starting with an "$"

*--------------------- Source code file for generator ------------
scan "cbldata"		    *> scan a cobol data structure
  01 TEMP-SWITCH        PIC 9
       88 FIRST-VALID VALUE 0
       88 SECOND-VALIE VALUE 1.
end-scan
saveas "outpout.pro"        *> Put the generated lines in file
"output.pro"
loop tabUsed($datVarName)   *> Loop over all scaned data fields
         evaluate true
if $datVarLevel = 88        *> data field is an 88 value
            when $datVarName
               display "$datVarName"
end-if   
         end-evaluate
end-loop
*--------------------- End of source code file -------------------

If you run progen with a file containing these lines it will produce a
file "output.pro" containing these lines:

         evaluate true
            when FIRST-VALUE
               display "FIRST-VALUE"
            when SECOND-VALUE
               display "SECOND-VALUE"
         end-evaluate

You can put the data structure in a file:

scan "cbldata"		    *> scan a cobol data structure
copy "temp.swi"
end-scan

to be more flexible. So any time you change "temp.swi" you can invoke
progen and the file "output.pro" gets adapted.

For more info see progen.htm or the whole package progen.zip at
www.maysoft.de
The generator is free for non comercial use (btw it is writen in
oo-cobol)

regards
Sebastian
```

### help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** tnngo@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hi,

I've been learning Cobol for the past 2 days just to convert a small program
written in Pascal from VAX to HP-UX.  I have to write in Cobol because
there're only Cobol libraries available for use...

To make the long story short, I can't seem to implement a linked-list in
Cobol. I just couldn't find anywhere in this thick Cobol Unleashed book
regarding data structure in Cobol besides simple records and tables.

My question is: Is there pointer in Cobol?  What the program does is reading a
text file, parse data and place them in a linked-list structure.

I know C and Pascal, but Cobol is just simply strange to me.  I would really
appreciate any help or pointer to any resources that I can use as example.

thanks in advance,
terry

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YgOS2.5195$qx1.116166@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
I have only done what you suggest once in 4 years, and even then it was in
my training and was not used in production.

A pointer is just a large library number, or as you suggest a field of type
POINTER. you give it a value by using the SET statement. You reference a
fields pointer by using the ADDRESS OF clause. There is no built in way to
allocate memory from the HEAP in COBOL. The method I used called a
subprogram called GETMAIN and FREEMAIN to allocate space for each NODE in
the LINKED-LIST/TREE/STACK. Then using the SET statement with the ADDRESS OF
phrase to traverse the LINKED-LIST I implemented. Such as:

4000-INIT-NODE.
    CALL 'GETMAIN' USING NEW-NODE
    .
4100-APPEND-NEW-NODE.
    SET    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF CURRENT-NODE
        TO ADDRESS OF NEW-NODE
    SET    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF NEW-NODE
        TO LOW-VALUES
    .
4200-MOVE-TO-NEXT.
    IF NEXT-NODE OF CURRENT-NODE = LOW-VALUES THEN
       SET END-OF-LIST TO TRUE
    ELSE
       SET    CURRENT-POINTER
           TO ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF CURRENT-NODE
       SET    ADDRESS OF CURRENT-NODE
           TO CURRENT-POINTER
    END-IF
    .


Good luck on finding documentation for GETMAIN and FREEMAIN. I assume they
are assembler routines.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgmtg$qa4$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <YgOS2.5195$qx1.116166@storm.twcol.com>`

```

though I have used it dozens of times as a file methodology.

>my training and was not used in production.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

OF
>phrase to traverse the LINKED-LIST I implemented. Such as:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3q6T2.5455$qx1.123374@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <YgOS2.5195$qx1.116166@storm.twcol.com>`

```
>A pointer is just a large library number, or as you suggest a field of type
>POINTER. you give it a value by using the SET statement. You reference a


I was wrong here. A simple MOVE does the trick.

>    SET    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF CURRENT-NODE
>        TO ADDRESS OF NEW-NODE
>    SET    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF NEW-NODE
>        TO LOW-VALUES

should be:
    move    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF CURRENT-NODE
        TO ADDRESS OF NEW-NODE
    move    ADDRESS OF NEXT-NODE OF NEW-NODE
        TO LOW-VALUES



A further qualification would be that I have done this on an IBM MVS
mainframe.

Although dynamic memory allocation would be good, using a table-implemented
linked-list or possibly implementing it in a RANDOM/INDEXED file would
probably be more practical. Pointers in the TABLE would simply be the index
of the row of the table the node is contained in. Whereas in a file it would
be the record key where the node is located.

The whole idea behind DATA STRUCTURES is for them to be platform
independent. Whether you implement it using dynamic memory, tables/arrays,
or files only the low level IO routines should change. This will be true if
the routines are properly modularized. Once done transporting them from
system to system should be relatively painless.

One thing I did in COBOL to overcome a memory limitation was to do something
like:

01 table-data-1.
   05 filler pic x(143000).
01 table-data-2.
   05 filler pic x(143000).
01 table-data-3.
   05 filler pic x(143000).   <--- some number representing
                                   the maximum size of a field.
66 linked-list redefines table-data-1 thru table-data-3.
   05 first-node      pic 9(18) usage comp.
   05 curr-node       pic 9(18) usage comp.
   05 last-node       pic 9(18) usage comp.
      88 full-list    value some-rediculously-large-number.
   05 max-node-index  pic 9(18) usage comp
                      value some-rediculously-large-number.
   05 node-count      pic 9(18) usage comp.
      88 empty-list             value zero.

   05 node occurs 1 to some-rediculously-large-number.
      10 data-field   .......
      10 next-node    pic 9(18) usage comp.
         88 no-next-node        value zero.
      10 prev-node    pic 9(18) usage comp.
         88 no-prev-node        value zero.


Then you simply use first-, curr-, last- nodes as pointers. Your logic
should then be easily adapted to COBOL. Mind you the 143000 number was off
the top of my head. That number will definitely differ from system to system
depending on the maximum field size which the compiler allows.
```

#### ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ewOS2.260$_N.105049@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
tnngo@my-dejanews.com wrote:
>
>I've been learning Cobol for the past 2 days just to convert a small program
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>appreciate any help or pointer to any resources that I can use as example.

Some COBOL compilers support pointers, perhaps some support dynamic
memory allocation and deallocation.  But I suggest you loosen up your
thinking a bit. :-)  Many younger programmers who have finished school
since C has become popular have been taught a somewhat limited view of
what 'linked list' means.  Programmers were using linked lists long
before dynamic memory allocation and pointers were available other
than in assembly language.  While dynamic memory allocation, pointers
and linked lists work well together, like ham and eggs, pointers are
not necessary to implement a linked list.  Many linked lists are in
data structures on disk, and the 'pointers' are record numbers.  In
memory, just create an array ('table' in COBOLspeak) and use subscripts
instead of pointers.  Works just as well, is far less error-prone, the
only real disadvantage for most uses is that the size is not dynamic.
But as abundant is RAM is these days, that's usually no big deal. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fi6d4$bqb$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ewOS2.260$_N.105049@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>But as abundant is RAM is these days, that's usually no big deal. :-)

I partly agree with you. I did however once make a spreadsheet equalence
where the user could do simple arithmetics and create his own formulas which
where possible to be nested. Although this could have been solved using
tables and/or files it would have implied a huge preallocation (tables) and
a significant performance loss. I would dare to say that my application
would have suffered significantly without the ability to use dynamic memory.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eV7T2.1139$oH.376925@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <ewOS2.260$_N.105049@news3.mia> <7fi6d4$bqb$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
>
>I partly agree with you. I did however once make a spreadsheet equalence
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>would have suffered significantly without the ability to use dynamic memory.


No doubt.  That's why all computer languages are good only for certain
kinds of tasks, with much overlap of course.  You *can* write a word
processor or spreadsheet in COBOL, but much better languages for those
tasks are available.  In my opinion, it is just as senseless to write a
system with heavy money handling in C/C++, as it is to write a word
processor in COBOL.  In both cases you would be using tools poorly
suited to the task.  Unfortunately, many programmers today can only see
the problem in one direction.  They see the deficiencies in COBOL for
certain kinds of tasks, but they are often unaware, or ignore, the
deficiencies of the 'language du jour' relative to COBOL for the tasks
COBOL is well suited for.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgqfe$349@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <371bc504@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif,
  I was fascinated (and a little skeptical) about your claim of being able
to do a linked list in "portable (run any-where) Cobol" <sic for COBOL>.

I haven't studied your web page in detail, but as soon as I saw

    02  EMPTY-PTR               PIC S9(3)  COMP  VALUE ZERO.

I knew where the "fallacy" was.  Although there are many (possibly - but not
even certainly - most) systems where memory addresses are stored in "USAGE
COMP" format, this is NOT a reasonable "run any-where" assumption.  (Clearly
most 16-bit INTEL systems do *not* use "USAGE COMP" for memory addresses -
if the USAGE COMP is big-endian binary data.)

Personally, I can't imagine any "portable" way to do linked lists UNTIL
"usage POINTER" is added to the Standard.  The entire reason that the
internal format of the Standard USAGE POINTER is "implementor defined" is
that memory addresses are not portable.

As I say, I haven't gone thru your web page in detail - but if you don't
explicitly address this issue somewhere, I would suggest that you do.
```

program

> My question is: Is there pointer in Cobol?  What the program does is
reading a
> text file, parse data and place them in a linked-list structure.
>

As pointed out elsewhere, ANSI/ISO (current) COBOL does NOT support pointers
(although the next Standard will).  However, I think that most of the COBOLs
that run on HP-UX do.  I know that Micro Focus does - so it depends on what
COBOL product you are using (and so will the application logic to do a
linked-list).
```

```
There is a pointer data type, starting with VS COBOL 2, if you don't have a
VS COBOL 2 compiler or above , "old COBOL" didn't have the pointer feature.
Besides the pointer data type, you would also need to find out how to use
the type, such as moving addresses to the pointer ( SET ADDRESS OF).
Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: help: pointer, is there any?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C8373.7DC03FAF@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7fg85k$3gl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
tnngo@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Done it in simple Cobol (listen up Bill cuase this is probably
what Leif did).

What is a linked list:  It is the ability to locate a random entry
into a list from the preceding element. The technique is simple
but must be on like elements (which they mostly are).

01 element-free      PIC 9(04) COMP VALUE 1.
01 element-first     pic 9(04) COMP VLAUE 0.

01 element occurs X times
   05  element-details.
     10 .....
   05  element-next  PIC 9(04) COMP.

Element next points to the next element but is really a simple
subscript.   Initialise the array with each element-next =
subscript + 1 and there is your free list.

The element list starts at element-first and finishes when it hits
a zero (initialy empty).  As you add to the list you can either
add to the end or figure out an insertion for element-next.

Pretty simple huh!

Yes I know it is not a 'real' linked list but it performs the same
functions.  I use it in the cobol parser to link the subordinate
working storage to it's group, which in turn points to it's group,
the '01' level has a zero.

Note I use 9(04) COMP because it is the most efficient subscript
for MVS.  Results may vary (as the advertisement says).

Ken
```

---

## Thread: Help: Problem when linking to db2gmf16.LIB(SQLGMF16) — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help: Problem when linking to db2gmf16.LIB(SQLGMF16)

- **From:** Patrick Wong <dbadmin@wawanesa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371B5E55.409BBCF7@wawanesa.com>`

```
I was testing the bldmfcob.cmd in the \sqllib\sample\cobol_16 directory
in OS/2 Warp, DB2 UDB with FP 9043 and MicroFocus COBOL, everything goes
fine until the link step, I got the error message

C:\SQLLIB\LIB\db2gmf16.LIB(SQLGMF16) : error L2029 :
'PC_SET_ABNORMAL_CALLBACK' : unresolved external

Any idea. Thank you in advance.



db2 prep updat.sqb bindfile target mfcob16

LINE    MESSAGES FOR updat.sqb
------
--------------------------------------------------------------------
        SQL0060W  The "COBOL" precompiler is in progress.
        SQL0091W  Precompilation or binding was ended with "0"
                  errors and "0" warnings.



link /ST:32000 /PM:VIO /NOI
updat.obj+checkerr.obj,,,coblib+os2286.lib+sqldyn16+db2gmf16;

Microsoft (R) Segmented-Executable Linker  Version 5.10.005
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1984-1989.  All rights reserved.


C:\SQLLIB\LIB\db2gmf16.LIB(SQLGMF16) : error L2029 :
'PC_SET_ABNORMAL_CALLBACK' : unresolved external


There was 1 error detected
```

#### ↳ Re: Help: Problem when linking to db2gmf16.LIB(SQLGMF16)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fhqls$5ol@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<371B5E55.409BBCF7@wawanesa.com>`

```
The reply that I got from my "usually reliable sources" was,

"This routine ('PC_SET_ABNORMAL_CALLBACK') was added in the (Micro Focus
16-bit) 3.4 product for the IBM DB2 software to call. My guess is that this
customer is maybe using 3.2 rather than the (latest) 3.4 product."

If you *are* using the Micro Focus 3.4 product (or higher), then please tell
me exactly what release you are using and I will follow up further.  If not,
then, I think you will need to upgrade to that version of the compiler to
use that version of DB2.
```
