### how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** "gritche" <gritche@france-mail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
Help me please !!!

Sorry for my english. I'm french
I hope you will understand my questions!

We have a VSAM KSDS file (variable length) which is used as a
"log file" for our apllications (CICS the day and BATCH the night)
The length is variable (from 134 to 4000 byte).
key is 60 byte length
Usually we have about 3000 records daily and no problem with R/W.
(we use also BLSR)

This week a job will run and will do about 1 Million "add" record in this
file.
300 000 records of 580 bytes
300 000 records of 1580 bytes
440 000 records of  380 bytes
300 000 records of 150 bytes.

My questions are :
1- what are the best parameters  "idcams" to minimize the space occupied by
this file ?
2- what are the best parameters "BLSR" to minimise the I/O and reduce the
time of this job ?

or where can i find "formulas" to determine these parameters?

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** wthompson@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fg29g$trl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
Generally the answer is in your need for recoverability.  A high degree of
recoverability would mandate smaller CIs and update/insert mode processing.
Less recoverability could allow larger CIs and mass insert/load mode
processing.

If you could avoid the requirement of having the file keyed at load time, you
could just flat file it out and sort it into load after the day's (or week's)
processing.  This would enhance thruput.

Last time I looked, the rule-of-thumb was large allocations of data CIs, even
numbers up to (one or two) CAs in length and as many index CIs as you have (or
expect) in the index (including and expecially the highest and intermedate
levels).


>
> This week a job will run and will do about 1 Million "add" record in this
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371BFBE9.2AF70D00@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr> <7fg29g$trl$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
CI size should be chosen based on how the KSDS will be used after it is
created, not on recoverability.  Should you have a major
hardware/software disaster during the creation process using large
number of buffers, the asynchronous nature of the physical writes means
you have an unpredictably large number of CI's in doubt, not just a
single CI; and the only way to have complete confidence in you file
would be to rebuild, regardless of CI size.  Predominantly random access
would probably favor a 4K CISIZE, while a majority of sequential or
skip-sequential access would perhaps favor a 8K or 16K CISIZE.

The total amount of data involved appears to be around 0.8 GB, so unless
you are talking about an Extended Format file (which allows going over 4
GB), you are either talking about replacing a very significant portion
of the file, or possibly building a completely new file from scratch. 
In either case, a general ROT is that sequential update techniques
(placing records to be retained along with records to be added into a
flat file, doing an external sort on the key field, and then loading an
empty KSDS with the sorted records) can outperform doing the adds via
random updates even if as little as 5% of the records are being
replaced/added, and in your case the percentage would appear much higher
than 5%.  Adding the equivalent of 25-100% of the file randomly, even
with many buffers, even with Batch LSR, you cannot avoid an incredible
number of CI/CA splits, which will greatly add to the overhead of the
process.  Loading these records into an empty file in sorted order would
also eliminate the need or rationale for specifying any CI/CA free space
and minimize the space required by the file.

BLSR is optimized for random access and does not transfer multiple CI's
per I/O.  Doing the updates randomly using BLSR buffering, requesting
DEFERRED WRITE, and supplying enough INDEX and DATA buffers will at best
give you one physical write for each INDEX and DATA CI.  For sequential
updates, BLSR performs more poorly than standard BUFNI BUFND buffer
techniques.  If building the file sequentially,  with sufficient buffers
for your input flat file and sufficient DATA buffers for the KSDS file,
the build can be done with as little as one write per INDEX CI and one
write for all DATA CI's in the same CA  -- considerably less MVS
overhead and probably less DASD time as well.

If you have no choice but to perform these updates randomly, then the
large number of updates pretty much makes any defined FreeSpace values
irrelevant -- you would need to plan on allocating at least twice as
much space as the number of records would suggest, and should consider
reorganizing the file after the update process if a significant number
of records will be accessed afterwards.

Tuning considerations for subsequent reading of the file after its
creation are a separate issue (i.e, if the creation and usage of the
file are two separate phases in a single program, different DDNAMEs
should be referenced to permit different tuning techniques for each type
of usage).

wthompson@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> Generally the answer is in your need for recoverability.  A high degree of
> recoverability would mandate smaller CIs and update/insert mode processing.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgr2a$3ju@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
This is a question that would probably get a better answer in
     bit.listserv.ibm-main

Many of the comp.lang.cobol newsgroup participants are quite familiar with
VSAM, but I think you would get the best "performance tuning" information
from the IBM-MAIN group.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** "Sarah W" <sworspamblk@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01be8b41$c4815d20$d704938c@mmga1127.loc.gov>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
You don't say what your keys are.  Here's something to think about:

If your records will be presented to the process adding to the file in
ascending key sequence (for example, if your log records have date and time
as their primary key) then if you use normal one-at-a-time add processes,
you will end up needing at least 4 times the amount of space that the
records will occupy (probably more) because of the CI and CA split
processes.  When a CI or CA gets full, VSAM will split it and  leave about
half of the split CI or CA empty to accommodate future adds.  But if your
keys only go up, the unused space that was left will remain empty until the
cluster is reorganized.  

Good luck,

Sarah W.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C80BB.CC6681D4@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
gritche wrote:
> 
> Help me please !!!
> 
> This week a job will run and will do about 1 Million "add" record in this
> file.

The non-obvious answer.

Don't use VSAM directly:

Create sequential file,
unload VSAM cluster
reload VSAM cluster from sorted version of unload and sequential
file.  No VSAM insertions and very tidy VSAM cluster for the next
day.

Good if it can work this way,
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: how to optimise VSAM KSDS file : Space and I/O

- **From:** wtsquid@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fi6vc$qnj$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>`

```
All the responses I have read are excellent options and alternatives,
especially handling the data sequentially rather than randomly. However, if
randomly is your only processing method, try "pre-formatting" your data space
to reduce your CI and CA splits. You must know a good deal about your keys
and insertions, so that you can estimate the number of records and range of
keys to be uniformly added to your file.

The least complex method is to process sequentially.

Good Luck.


In article <7ffqms$aqa$1@front3.grolier.fr>,
  "gritche" <gritche@france-mail.com> wrote:
> Help me please !!!
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

I realise that this may not be the right forum for this question, but I have
noticed quite a few people exhibiting MVS knowledge.

My question is this;
I have an input file that is defined as follows
   dcb=(recfm=vb,lrecl=363)
my output file is defined as follows
   dcb=(recfm=vb,lrecl=377)

I want to read in the file and only select certain fields to output.

Easytrieve Plus lets me read it in fine but the output seems to contain part
of the next record ??

I have included the jcl used.

where am I going wrong.

yes I do know I can use cobol but the stipulation for this project is to use
easytrieve plus.

Thanks in advance
G


source follows

//EZY      JOB (22YR0T,CP03),'EZY',MSGCLASS=X,
//      CLASS=C,NOTIFY=EHTGTQ,RESTART=REPORT00
//*
/*JOBPARM  SYSAFF=ANY
//***************>>>>>>> START OF JOB <<<<<<<<<**************
//DELETE    EXEC PGM=IEFBR14

//DD02       DD  DSN=YRTEST.COMPUWRE.NBSUBSET.XTROLD2.D,
//     DISP=(,CATLG,DELETE),SPACE=(TRK,(100,10),RLSE),UNIT=SYSDA,
//     DCB=(RECFM=VB,LRECL=377)
//*
//***************>>>>>>> START OF JOB <<<<<<<<<**************
//REPORT00  EXEC PGM=EZTPA00
//STEPLIB    DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=AMPS.SW.EASYPLUS.LOAD
//*
//MACROS     DD   DSN=YRTEST.P.CCPROD.EASYMAC,DISP=SHR
//SYSPRINT   DD   SYSOUT=*
//SYSUDUMP   DD   SYSOUT=*
//SYSOUT     DD   SYSOUT=*
//NBOLD2     DD   DUMMY,DISP=SHR,DSN=YRTEST.COMPUWRE.NBSUBSET.XTROLD2
//NBOLD2D    DD   DUMMY,DISP=SHR,DSN=YRTEST.COMPUWRE.NBSUBSET.XTROLD2.D
//NBNEW2     DD   DISP=SHR,DSN=YRTEST.COMPUWRE.NBSUBSET.XTRNEW2
//NBNEW2D    DD   DISP=SHR,DSN=YRTEST.COMPUWRE.NBSUBSET.XTRNEW2.D
//SYSIN      DD   *
*
LIST ON
*
FILE NBNEW2  VB (0 0)
   NEW-TYPE         1     1 A
   NEW-NUM          2     3 N, 0
   NEW-TABLE       33    18 A
   NEW-DATA         5   219 A
*

FILE NBNEW2D VB (0 0)
   NEW2D-TABLE      1    18  A
   NEW2D-DATA      19   359  A
*

FILE NBOLD2 VB (0 0)
   OLD-TYPE         1     1 A
   OLD-NUM          2     3 N, 0
   OLD-TABLE       33    18 A
   OLD-DATA         5   359 A
*
FILE NBOLD2D VB (0 0)
*  OLD2D-REC        1   377  A
   OLD2D-TABLE      1    18  A
   OLD2D-DATA      19   359  A

*
   WS-NEW-TBL W 18   A  OCCURS 20
   WS-OLD-TBL W 18   A  OCCURS 20

   WS-COUNT   W  3   N

JOB INPUT NBNEW2
*
    IF NEW-TYPE = 'H'
        DISPLAY NEW-NUM NEW-TABLE
        WS-NEW-TBL(NEW-NUM) = NEW-TABLE
    ELSE
      IF NEW-TYPE = 'T'
         STOP
      ELSE
          IF NEW-TYPE = 'D'
            WS-COUNT = WS-COUNT + 1
            IF WS-COUNT > 10
               STOP
            END-IF
            NEW2D-TABLE = WS-NEW-TBL(NEW-NUM)
            NEW2D-DATA  = NEW-DATA
            PUT NBNEW2D
          END-IF
      END-IF
    END-IF

#### ↳ Re: Help with Easytrieve

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>

```
From the code below it doesn't seem like your output file needs to be 4
bytes longer. Shorten it.

Geussing at how EZ stores blocked files, It is probably blocking up the
input records in sequential storage, then moving the input record for the
length of the output record to the output record area. Thus grabbing the
first few bytes of the next record. Sort of a table overflow problem.

>*
>LIST ON
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Easytrieve

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371BB798.4ADF@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<371ad5a7@199.186.16.51>`

```
I think you have things set up just fine, but with one minor problem; 
how does your EZPLUS program behave when the incoming record is shorter
than its maximum possible length?

The answer to this is that if your incoming record is physically shorter
than max and you don't code for this condition, then the remaining area
is actually looking forward in the buffer and gives you the header
section of the next record.

I have used two different solutions:
====================================
1) Use the WORKAREA parameter on the input file.  However, this requires
you to "blank out" this workarea before each read, or EZPLUS leaves data
from previous reads in the right-most area.

2) Re-compute the correct LRECL for the output file right before writing
the record.
In this case, I think the instruction would something like this:
	RECORD-LENGTH(OUTFILE) = RECORD-LENGTH(INFILE) + 14
	PUT OUTFILE

Let me know if this works.
mailto:petwir@saif.com

> 
> I realise that this may not be the right forum for this question, but I have

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990419084543.20551.00004038@ng-fi1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<371ad5a7@199.186.16.51>`

```
you need to specify the length of the record being output based on the length
of the input record.  You may want to intialize the output record everytime you
read in a new record.  EZtrieve does not do this for you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with Easytrieve

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ffih8$egm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371ad5a7@199.186.16.51>`

```
In article <371ad5a7@199.186.16.51>,
  "gee" <grant_englebrecht@nospam.compuware.com> wrote:
> I realise that this may not be the right forum for this question, but I have
> noticed quite a few people exhibiting MVS knowledge.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> I have included the jcl used.

<snip the remainder>

Easy holds the 'System-Defined File Fields' RECORD-LENGTH, RECORD-COUNT and
FILE-STATUS. These are somehow similar to the COBOL special registers.

In the Reference Guide of the release 6.2, you can read at page 11-4:
'RCORD-LENGTH is a two-byte binary field used for all file types to determine
or establish the length of the current data record....<snip>... For
variable-length files, assign the length fo the record to the RECORD-LENGTH
field before the PUT or WRIE statement is executed.'

So do the following:

MOVE 363 to RECORD-LENGTH
WRITE myoutputfile

Or even better, move the input lenght to the output length:

FILE INFILE
INFIELD 1 100 A
FILE OUTFILE
OUTFIELD 1 200 A
JOB INPUT INFILE
 MOVE INFILE:RECORD-LENGTH TO OUTFILE:RECORD-LENGTH
 PUT OUTFILE FROM INFILE

Daniel

------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<371ad5a7@199.186.16.51> <7ffih8$egm$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

G

#### ↳ Re: Help with Easytrieve

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4vHS2.4744$s_5.219452@dfiatx1-snr1>`
- **References:** `<371ad5a7@199.186.16.51>`

```
There is an MVS-Specific web-based message board at http://www.mvshelp.com

Oh, your specific problem? Well, I don't know EZTrieve, but with both
SyncSort and QuickJob I *often* forget to account for the four-byte length
word at the start of each variable-length record (i.e., the data starts at
position 5, not position 1). Maybe you have a similar situation?

MCM

## Thread: How do I determine the COBOL database type. — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I determine the COBOL database type.

- **From:** John Comfort <jcomfort@deltanet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371D3CCC.15915DAA@deltanet.com>`

```
I'm not a COBOL programmer, but I've already read Mattias' cobolcvt
document on data structures. I do code in Perl though.

Here's the info I DO know:
    1. The program name is TruckPro and it is running on SCO unix.
    2. The extensions on the related files are: .cob, .i85, .idx, .dat
    3. There are various utilities that suggest it is AcuCOBOL like:
vutil, cblutil.

Now, I've tried using vutil to no avail. There is an extensive document
on the internet describing the use of this utility, but it didn't help
either. My objective is to extract the data from these files into an
ascii delimited file or another type of db like oracle.

Does anyone know how to yank the data out, or to at least determine what
the FD is?
```

```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fkp05$58p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <371D5A22.E5279ADC@deltanet.com>,
  John Comfort <jcomfort@deltanet.com> wrote:
> The .cob files are the object code, NOT the source. If it
was the source I
> would have been able to find the FD field descriptors.
>

Ok.  There is nothing in the data itself that will give you
a clue as to the FD.  I have helped a friend that sells
software to Waste Disposal companies convert data such as
this.  The method is crude, but it does get the job done.

He prints all the reports he can generate from the system -
to a serial port, and captures this data.  He then analyzes
the reports and uses a parser he wrote to generate
convertable data for his system.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I determine the COBOL database type.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)

- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFAvp95.2HI@netcom.com>`

```
redsky@ibm.net writes:

>He prints all the reports he can generate from the system -
>to a serial port, and captures this data.  He then analyzes
>the reports and uses a parser he wrote to generate
>convertable data for his system.

Sure sounds like Monarch, doesn't it?

I've found myself in the situation of having a production system
without source code.  The way we used to provide custom features
was to grab the standard report printfiles and scrape the data
off of them as your friend does (except for the serial stuff.
we just grabbed it while it was in the print queue)

Then along came Monarch.  I guess somebody got tired of doing
the same kind of report scrapping.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I determine the COBOL database type.

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fkqhk$elu$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371D3CCC.15915DAA@deltanet.com>`

```
John Comfort wrote in message <371D3CCC.15915DAA@deltanet.com>...
>    3. There are various utilities that suggest it is AcuCOBOL like:
>vutil, cblutil.

To determine if your data files are of vision data type, you may do the
following:

vutil -info <filename>

This should report an error if the particular file is not a vision file.

To export data from the file:

vutil -unload -v -t <input filename> <output filename>

Cheesle
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371E87C1.AF1595D0@deltanet.com>`
- **References:** `<371D3CCC.15915DAA@deltanet.com> <7fkqhk$elu$1@news.cerf.net>`

any other ideas? :-)

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I determine the COBOL database type.

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.no>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fnbef$c97$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<371D3CCC.15915DAA@deltanet.com> <7fkqhk$elu$1@news.cerf.net> <371E87C1.AF1595D0@deltanet.com>`

```
John Comfort wrote in message <371E87C1.AF1595D0@deltanet.com>...
>I have tried this suggestion and I get the same error: Incorrect file type.
>
>any other ideas? :-)

There is two options:

a) It is not a vision filetype. Acucobol does also support B-Trieve, if you
have access to any B-Trieve utility, you may give it a shot.

b) It is not indexed files but relative files. In that case vutil will do no
good because it is intended for indexed files.

In either case, you definitely should have someone experienced with Acucobol
to look at it. At least someone who is experienced with Cobol in general.
Trying to figure out on your own without knowing what to look for is at best
a lottery.

Have you tried to look at the .DAT file in a binary fileviewer? It may give
you an idea if you could hack the data.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-76910f04bd"></a>
## Thread: DYNAM or NODYNAM? — 9 msgs

### DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** Jeff Breslen <jbresle@cvps.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>`

```
If anyone has any info if when compiling COBOL programs under COBOL for
OS/390 if you should use DYNAM or NODYNAM I would love to hear it.  We
have used NODYNAM due to CA stating that with IDMS we should use NODYNAM

but IBM appears to let it be up to the client.  We haven't confirmed
CA's opinion but have over 1000 regular NON IDMS cobol programs that we
can go either way.

Any ideas from anyone?

Thanks!

jbresle@cvps.com
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CC464.4676@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>`

```
Jeff Breslen wrote:
> 
> If anyone has any info if when compiling COBOL programs under COBOL for
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> jbresle@cvps.com


It is my opinion that DYNAM should be used whenever possible;
1) Your LOADLIB member will be smaller.
2) So that you can upgrade application subroutines and have them take
effect without having to re-compile every application that calls them.

There are cases where DYNAM is not the right thing to do:
1) I believe IBM still says CICS programs are to be NODYNAM.  We follow
this rule, and if an application subroutine is involved, we add it to
the PCT, and have the calling application call it via a working storage
variable to cause it to be called dynamically.
2) We use NODYNAM in all application subroutines so they may be used
from other environments. (e.g. Easytrieve Plus)

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#v7DOWFj#GA.221@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>`

```
Philosophically as well as practically, I've always prefered the Dynam
approach. Otherwise, whenever a subroutine is changed, all the programs
that call it have to be relinked, which usually means they have to be
recompiled because they weren't saved in unlinked format anywhere because
Cobol shops (unlike PL/1 shops) don't usually think of that.

CICS automatically inserts a compile option card with NoDynam, I'm not sure
what other subsystems and preprocessors do; anyway in cases where NoDynam
is needed, it should be used. I'd rather see it handled as an exception
(even a blank automatic one for e.g. CICS) and Dynam be the default.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fk1vh$hcr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>`

```
In article <371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>,
  Jeff Breslen <jbresle@cvps.com> wrote:
> If anyone has any info if when compiling COBOL programs under COBOL for
> OS/390 if you should use DYNAM or NODYNAM I would love to hear it.  We
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The compiler option DYNAM causes every CALLed program to be loaded at runtime.
The programmer cannot make difference between modules or stubs that have to be
linked to the application and modules that can/must be called dynamically.

CICS- and DB2-stubs MUST be linked to the application, it will abend if you
do not. So, if your programmer have to deal with both batch and CICS or DB2,
you'd better leave the compiler default to NODYNAM. Then they know that they
allways call 'string' for static and call variable for dynamic calls.

There are other aspects you have to consider when dealing with dynamic calls:
From the point of view of QA, linking is much better: WYTIWYHP, what you test
is what you have in production. If you copy a dynamically called module in
production, EVERY application that calls that specific module gets the new
version, even they NEVER did a test with this new version. From the point of
view of the person who does the production upgrade, DYNAM is preferred,
because he never has to find out in what programs a specific module is linked
to. From the point of view of the sysprog staff, either option is equal.
DYNAM causes more directory blocks in loadlibraries, but less space
allocation and the load time is not a big problem if you use VLF/LLA or a
similar utility.

Daniel

------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fk6o4$bsm@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com> <7fk1vh$hcr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Daniel's comments are "right on" - but I thought that I would add three more
things into the "mix" for making the DYNAM/NODYNAM decision

1) If you use the NODYNAM compiler option, you can still use the CALL
identifier format (rather than CALL 'literal) format to force a specific
CALL to be made "dynamically".

2) As IBM documents, the NODYNAM compiler option is *not* ANSI/ISO
conforming.  The reason that this is true (which is more important than just
whether your program is conforming) is that when you use the NODYNAM
compiler option, the CANCEL verb ends up as a "no-op".  This applies
particularly to freeing up memory - but also (in some? most? all?) cases to
getting a program in "last-used" versus "initial" state after a CANCEL.
This can be an application design issue.

3) If you still have some pre-31-bit addressing programs (AMODE or RMODE =
24), you most definitely want to call them dynamically - or otherwise, your
entire applications is "put below the line".  Obviously, you also want to
use the DATA(24) compiler option if you are trying to PASS data to a 24-bit
program, but this won't help unless you call it dynamically.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fkpek$5j3$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com> <7fk1vh$hcr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fk6o4$bsm@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```

  "William M. Klein" <wmklein at ix dot netcom dot com>
wrote:
> 1) If you use the NODYNAM compiler option, you can still
use the CALL
> identifier format (rather than CALL 'literal) format to
force a specific
> CALL to be made "dynamically".
>

I have made use of this.  See next comment.

> 2) As IBM documents, the NODYNAM compiler option is *not*
ANSI/ISO
> conforming.  The reason that this is true (which is more
important than just
> whether your program is conforming) is that when you use
the NODYNAM
> compiler option, the CANCEL verb ends up as a "no-op".
This applies
> particularly to freeing up memory - but also (in some?
most? all?) cases to
> getting a program in "last-used" versus "initial" state
after a CANCEL.
> This can be an application design issue.
>

I also ran into this. I BUT- by using the convention in
point #1 above, I could make the call dynamic and the
cancel DOES put it back in it's initial state.  I think
that is implied from these two points, but I just wanted to
state it explicitly.

http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371DD18F.1AA0F12B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com> <7fk1vh$hcr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In most shops I've worked at, the standard was to use COBOL code to
decide that a call was dynamic.  Instead of calling a constant, it calls
a variable.

Unless compilers "outsmart" coders, this guarantees a dynamic load no
matter how the program is compiled.  But I don't know of any way to
guarantee a compile-time load.

Sometimes the migration process uses a slightly different set of compile
options as was used earlier.  And sometimes a particular program needs
to be different from the company standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAM or NODYNAM?

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<57.27.a12L0xF67wq4IuAt4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<371CC085.44FC1773@cvps.com>`

```
"JB" == "Jeff Breslen" writes:

JB> If anyone has any info if when compiling COBOL programs under
JB> COBOL for OS/390 if you should use DYNAM or NODYNAM I would love
JB> to hear it.  We have used NODYNAM due to CA stating that with
JB> IDMS we should use NODYNAM
JB> 
JB> but IBM appears to let it be up to the client.  We haven't
JB> confirmed CA's opinion but have over 1000 regular NON IDMS cobol
JB> programs that we can go either way.
JB> 
JB> Any ideas from anyone?
JB> 
JB> Thanks!
JB> 
JB> jbresle@cvps.com
    I just wanted to add my 2� worth to this thread.  I agree here
    with the opinion that one should always try too use Dynam
    wherever possible, however, I'd like to offer a "caveat emptor". 
    At the last place I was consulting at, there was this woman
    across from me that couldn't understand the unbelievable slowness
    she experienced when she added a CALL to a canned program.  She
    struggled for a week trying to determine what could be the
    problem with the routine.  I had a very similar experience years
    before this and could take it no longer.  I usually mind my own
    business, but I felt so bad for her overhearing her for a whole
    week, I excused myself & said that I heard her struggling and
    maybe she would like to hear my opinion.  She was curious, having
    run out of ideas.  What I had suggested was happening ultimately
    turned out to be the problem.  What was happening was that this
    particular subprogram needed to be CALLed with the NODYNAM option
    because it was DYNAMically being CALLed everytime & getting
    CANCELed out of memory, therefore causing a tremendous amount of
    I/O in just loading in this subprogram.  Once this change was
    made, run times went way down.

Jay
```

## Thread: Asking help with International (military) Time converted to pay. — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Asking help with International (military) Time converted to pay.

- **From:** y2kbyten@aol.com (Y2KByten)
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990420100557.02348.00002738@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
This is has been a challenging task, and I still think there has to be a
shorter
(cleaner) way to code.

Requirement :  (In military time)  Start-Time minus Stop-Time.  Then times
Pay-Rate for Period-Earnings.

Ex:

Start   1400  (2pm)
Stop - 1700  (5pm)
________________
    - 300  x -.01  = 3 hrs  then x Pay-Rate  
Pretty Simple until you toss in 1/2 hour (or other)  increments.

Start   1430  (2:30pm)
Stop  - 1700  (5:00pm)
__________________
- 270  x -.01 = 2.7  x Pay-Rate WRONG  the .7 should be .5 for 1/2 hour

Is there a coversion possible with out rounding down the result of
-270 x -.01 = 2.7  ?

Otherwise this is the only way I can see to convert military time when
computing pay.  I think the problem occurs because the military time
jumps 100 in 60 units ( min increments). 

Ex:

0159  (1:59 am)  Does not turn into 1:60am, it turns into 0200.  When you
subtract
start / stop times you are back into the system based on tenths.  Giving you
not a clean .5 for 1/2 hour but a .7.

Any input appreciated....    I am also interested is how may diff. ways
this can be done.  (Using COMPUTE and rounding anywhere)

Thank you,
Jake
```

```

#### ↳ Re: Asking help with International (military) Time converted to pay.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fidqo$r6m$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<19990420100557.02348.00002738@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
1. Convert the hours and minutes to minutes only.
2. Adjust the stop time if crossing midnight.
3. Calcuate the difference in minutes by subtracting the
    start time minutes from the stop time minutes.
4. Convert the difference in minutes to hours and hundredths.
5. Multiply this difference by the hourly rate.

as in:

    Compute start-minutes = (start-time-hour * 60) + start-time-minute
    Compute stop-minutes = (stop-time-hour * 60) + stop-time-minute
    If stop-minutes < start-minutes
        Add 1440 to stop-minutes
    End-if
    Subtract start-minutes from stop-minutes
        giving minutes-difference
    Divide minutes-difference by 60
        giving hours-hundredths-difference
    Multiply hours-hundredths-difference by hourly-rate
        giving period-earnings
------------------
Rick Smith

Y2KByten wrote in message <19990420100557.02348.00002738@ng-cr1.aol.com>...
>This is has been a challenging task, and I still think there has to be a
>shorter
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pretty Simple until you toss in 1/2 hour (or other)  increments.
[...]
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Asking help with International (military) Time converted to pay.

- **From:** Arn Burkhoff <arnb@myself.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3720DF5D.9DD9C31F@myself.com>`
- **References:** `<19990420100557.02348.00002738@ng-cr1.aol.com> <7fo82k$rhq$1@oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Try this.
03 start-time.
   05  start-hh pic 99.
   05  start-mm pic 99.
03 end-time.
   05  end-hh   pic 99.
   05  end-mm   pic 99.
03 pay-amount   pic s9(5)v99 comp-3.

 
*  check if working 11pm to 7AM 
if end-hh < start-hh     
    add 24 to end-hh 
end-if     	 
compute pay-amount = (((start-hh * 60 + start-mm) - (end-hh * 60 + end-mm)) /
60) * pay-rate-perhour

#### ↳ Re: Date Calculation

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZG6T2.5459$qx1.123553@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl>`

```
>Could someone give me some tips for date-routines,
>f.i. calculating a weeknumber, day of week etc..
>My current routine soesn;t seem to work ok after Febr. 29 of the year 2000.


If you post your example I am sure we would all like to tell you what is
wrong with it.

Otherwise, if you are using a newer compiler, OS370 mainframe, chances are
there are some nice ones implemented as intrinsic functions.

I have written some in REXX as well as COBOL and one thing that always helps
is to convert all dates to a BINARY type of date, number of days, then do
your operations, then convert back. This method works nicely when just
converting date formats. It also easily generates algorithms for number of
weeks between and of course number of days between. Of course if you don't
have a BINARY date function this could be a little difficult. In that case
JULIAN makes a good substitute. But again you need a good JULIAN date
function or your program will get rather complicated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Date Calculation

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371d0577.342516663@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl>`

```
On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 22:00:43 +0200, "Cor Bruntink"
<Cor.Bruntink@wxs.nl> wrote:

>Hi everybody,
>
>Could someone give me some tips for date-routines,
>f.i. calculating a weeknumber, day of week etc..
>My current routine soesn;t seem to work ok after Febr. 29 of the year 2000.

Current COBOL?  

Compute Integer-First-Date = Function Integer-Of-Date (Date-YYYYMMDD)

Compute Remainder-Days = (Function Rem (Integer-First-Date 7) + 1)

Use a table based on Remainder-Days - 1 = Sunday, 7 = Saturday.

From page 380 - Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours.
```

- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eFlT2.922$JC1.37713@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl>`

```
Cor Bruntink wrote in message <7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl>...
>Could someone give me some tips for date-routines,
>f.i. calculating a weeknumber, day of week etc..
>My current routine soesn;t seem to work ok after Febr. 29 of the year 2000.

The following does the conversion without a table using the fact there are
two cycles of months with lengths 31-30-31-30-31 starting in March.
SUB-FACTOR is 9(01).


000000     IF BEG-MO > 2
000000         COMPUTE SUB-FACTOR = BEG-MO * .4 + 2.3
000000         MOVE BEG-YEAR TO ADD-FACTOR
000000     ELSE
000000         MOVE ZERO TO SUB-FACTOR
000000         COMPUTE ADD-FACTOR = BEG-YEAR - 1.
000000     DIVIDE 4 INTO ADD-FACTOR.
000000     COMPUTE BEG-DAYS = (BEG-YEAR * 365) + ((BEG-MO - 1) * 31)
000000                      +  BEG-DA + ADD-FACTOR - SUB-FACTOR.

That is part of a days-between-dates calculation and can't convert days back
into a date the same way.  I'm sure something could be worked out to divide
the julian days after February by 153, but it would be more trouble than
just using a table.
```

helpful.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date Calculation

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HjFT2.985$JC1.42860@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<eFlT2.922$JC1.37713@axe.netdoor.com> <19990421181348.13785.00000097@ng-fx1.aol.com>`

```
Twymer wrote in message <19990421181348.13785.00000097@ng-fx1.aol.com>...
>does this work for 02/29/2000 also?


A little more quoting of the original message would have been helpful, but
it appears you were responding to my post.

Yes.  A calculation for changing a date into a count of days since a given
baseline which includes Y * 365 + Y / 4 (ignoring months and days for the
moment) would assume all years divisible by 4 were leap years and such logic
would work from 1901 until 2099.  If the calculation was Y * 365 + Y / 4 - Y
/ 100 + Y / 400 , it would work for any Gregorian year.  Only if Y / 400 was
missing would the program not treat 2000 as a leap year.
Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Date Calculation

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371DEC4C.D19F85B0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl> <eFlT2.922$JC1.37713@axe.netdoor.com>`

```
I don't recommend using this type of "trick" in COBOL programs.  I want
people doing maintenance to be able to read the code and quickly
understand what's being done.  Don't take advantage of off-screen
patterns.  Make your code as direct and clear as possible.

Warren Porter wrote:
> 
> Cor Bruntink wrote in message <7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl>...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> just using a table.
```

```

Fortunately I can't claim credit for the above and if I hadn't read an

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Date Calculation

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7flmrn$fkp$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7fim9i$mh1r$1@reader3.wxs.nl> <eFlT2.922$JC1.37713@axe.netdoor.com> <371DEC4C.D19F85B0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Here is my library copy routine, complete with warts from year 2000.  The
only thing tricky is that it converts all dates to make the years start on
March 1st before the tables are used.  That makes leap years day always the
last day of the year, so that adding it in is easy.

The base date is from March 1, 1980.

 01 DATE-WORKING-AREA.
  02 SUBSCRIPT  PICTURE 99.
  02 DATETEMP1  PICTURE S9(6).
  02 DATETEMP2  PICTURE 9.
  02 YEAR-TABLE.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 0.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 365.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 730.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 1096.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 1461.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 1826.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 2191.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 2557.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 2922.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 3287.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 3652.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 4018.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 4383.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 4748.
                   04 FILLER            PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 5113.
                   04 FILLER            PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 5479.
                   04 FILLER            PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 5844.
                   04 FILLER            PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 6209.
                   04 FILLER            PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 6573.
  02 YEAR-TABLE-TWO REDEFINES YEAR-TABLE.
                   04 YEAR-START        PICTURE 9(6) OCCURS 19 TIMES.
  02 MONTH-TABLE.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 0.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 31.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 59.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 90.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 120.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 151.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 181.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 212.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 243.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 273.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 304.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 334.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE 9(6) VALUE 365.
  02 MONTH-TABLE-TWO REDEFINES MONTH-TABLE.
     04 MONTH-START PICTURE 9(6) OCCURS 13 TIMES.
  02 MONTH-LITERAL-TABLE.
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "January*  ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "February* ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "March*    ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "April*    ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "May*      ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "June*     ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "July*     ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "August*   ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "September*".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "October*  ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "November* ".
     04 FILLER PICTURE X(10) VALUE "December* ".
  02 MONTH-LITERAL-TABLE-TWO REDEFINES
     MONTH-LITERAL-TABLE.
     04 MONTH-LITERAL PICTURE X(10) OCCURS 12 TIMES.
  02 DAY-LITERAL-TABLE.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Sunday   ".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Monday   ".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Tuesday  ".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Wednesday".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Thursday ".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Friday   ".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(9) VALUE "Saturday ".
  02 DAY-LITERAL-TABLE-TWO REDEFINES DAY-LITERAL-TABLE.
     04 DAY-LITERAL PICTURE X(9) OCCURS 7 TIMES.
  02 DAY-LITERAL2-TABLE.
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Sun.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Mon.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Tue.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Wed.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Thu.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Fri.".
     04 FILLER  PICTURE X(4) VALUE "Sat.".
  02 DAY-LITERAL2-TABLE-TWO REDEFINES DAY-LITERAL2-TABLE.
     04 DAY-LITERAL2 PICTURE X(4) OCCURS 7 TIMES.
 01 DATE-CONVERSION-DATA.
                02 NEW-SYSTEM-DATE.
                   04 NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR   PICTURE 9999.
                   04 XXX REDEFINES NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR.
                      06 SYSTEM-CENTURY PICTURE 99.
                      06 OLD-SYSTEM-YEAR PICTURE 99.
     04 SYSTEM-MONTH PICTURE 99.
     04 SYSTEM-DAY PICTURE 99.
                02 NEW-JULIAN-DATE.
                   04 NEW-JULIAN-YEAR   PICTURE 9999.
     04 JULIAN-DAY PICTURE 999.
  02 BASE-DATE  PICTURE 9(6).
  02 DAY-WEEK-NUMB PICTURE 9.
  02 DAY-WEEK-LITERAL PICTURE X(10).
  02 MONTH-YEAR-LITERAL PICTURE X(10).
  02 FANCY-DATE  PICTURE X(25).
  02 FANCY-DATE2  PICTURE X(35).
  02 FANCY-DATE3  PICTURE X(25).

 CONVERT-FROM-STANDARD.
                MOVE ZERO TO NEW-JULIAN-YEAR, JULIAN-DAY, BASE-DATE.
  MOVE 1 TO DAY-WEEK-NUMB.
                IF NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR IS LESS THAN 1986
                        MOVE 1986 TO NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR.
  IF SYSTEM-MONTH IS LESS THAN 1
   MOVE 1 TO SYSTEM-MONTH.
  IF SYSTEM-MONTH IS GREATER THAN 12
   MOVE 12 TO SYSTEM-MONTH.
  IF SYSTEM-DAY IS LESS THAN 1
   MOVE 1 TO SYSTEM-DAY.
  IF SYSTEM-DAY IS GREATER THAN 31
   MOVE 30 TO SYSTEM-DAY.
  PERFORM SYSTEM-TO-BASE.
  PERFORM BASE-TO-JULIAN.
  PERFORM BASE-TO-DAY-WEEK-NUMB.


 SYSTEM-TO-BASE.
                SUBTRACT 1985 FROM NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR GIVING SUBSCRIPT.
  ADD YEAR-START (SUBSCRIPT), BASE-DATE
   GIVING BASE-DATE.
  ADD MONTH-START (SYSTEM-MONTH), BASE-DATE
   GIVING BASE-DATE.
  ADD SYSTEM-DAY, BASE-DATE GIVING BASE-DATE.
                IF NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1988
   MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
  ELSE
                IF NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1992
   MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
  ELSE
                IF NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1996
   MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
                ELSE
                IF NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 2000
                        MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
  ELSE
   MOVE ZERO TO SUBSCRIPT.
  IF SYSTEM-MONTH IS LESS THAN 3
   MOVE ZERO TO SUBSCRIPT.
  ADD SUBSCRIPT, BASE-DATE GIVING BASE-DATE.


 BASE-TO-JULIAN.
  MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT.
  PERFORM BASE-TO-JULIAN-TWO.
  MOVE BASE-DATE TO DATETEMP1.
  SUBTRACT YEAR-START (SUBSCRIPT) FROM DATETEMP1
   GIVING JULIAN-DAY.
                ADD 1985, SUBSCRIPT GIVING NEW-JULIAN-YEAR.


 BASE-TO-JULIAN-TWO.
  MOVE BASE-DATE TO DATETEMP1.
  SUBTRACT YEAR-START (SUBSCRIPT) FROM DATETEMP1.
  IF DATETEMP1 IS GREATER THAN ZERO
   ADD 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
   GO TO BASE-TO-JULIAN-TWO.
  SUBTRACT 1 FROM SUBSCRIPT.


 JULIAN-TO-BASE.
  MOVE ZERO TO BASE-DATE.
                IF NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS LESS THAN 1986
   NEXT SENTENCE
  ELSE
                        SUBTRACT 1985 FROM NEW-JULIAN-YEAR
                                GIVING SUBSCRIPT
   ADD YEAR-START (SUBSCRIPT), BASE-DATE
    GIVING BASE-DATE
   ADD JULIAN-DAY, BASE-DATE GIVING BASE-DATE.


 BASE-TO-DAY-WEEK-NUMB.
  DIVIDE BASE-DATE BY 7 GIVING DATETEMP1
   REMAINDER DAY-WEEK-NUMB.
  IF DAY-WEEK-NUMB IS LESS THAN 5
   ADD DAY-WEEK-NUMB, 3 GIVING DAY-WEEK-NUMB
  ELSE
   SUBTRACT 4 FROM DAY-WEEK-NUMB.
  MOVE MONTH-LITERAL (SYSTEM-MONTH)
   TO MONTH-YEAR-LITERAL.
  MOVE DAY-LITERAL (DAY-WEEK-NUMB) TO DAY-WEEK-LITERAL.


 CONVERT-TO-STANDARD.
  PERFORM BASE-TO-JULIAN.
  PERFORM JULIAN-TO-SYSTEM.
  PERFORM CONVERT-FROM-STANDARD.


 JULIAN-TO-SYSTEM.
                MOVE NEW-JULIAN-YEAR TO NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR.
  MOVE 1 TO SUBSCRIPT.
  PERFORM BASE-TO-SYSTEM-TWO.
  MOVE SUBSCRIPT TO SYSTEM-MONTH.
  SUBTRACT MONTH-START (SUBSCRIPT) FROM JULIAN-DAY
   GIVING SYSTEM-DAY.
  IF SUBSCRIPT IS LESS THAN 3
   NEXT SENTENCE
  ELSE
                IF      ((NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1988)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1992)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1996)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 2000))
    SUBTRACT 1 FROM SYSTEM-DAY.


 BASE-TO-SYSTEM-TWO.
  MOVE JULIAN-DAY TO DATETEMP1.
  SUBTRACT MONTH-START (SUBSCRIPT) FROM DATETEMP1.
  IF SUBSCRIPT IS LESS THAN 3
   NEXT SENTENCE
  ELSE
                IF      ((NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1988)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1992)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 1996)
    OR
                        (NEW-JULIAN-YEAR IS EQUAL TO 2000))
    SUBTRACT 1 FROM DATETEMP1.
  IF DATETEMP1 IS GREATER THAN ZERO
   ADD 1 TO SUBSCRIPT
   GO TO BASE-TO-SYSTEM-TWO.
  SUBTRACT 1 FROM SUBSCRIPT.


 CREATE-FANCY-DATE.
  MOVE ALL "*" TO FANCY-DATE,
   FANCY-DATE2, FANCY-DATE3.
  IF SYSTEM-DAY IS GREATER THAN 9
   STRING MONTH-LITERAL (SYSTEM-MONTH)
                        SYSTEM-DAY, ",*", NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR, "*"
   DELIMITED BY SPACE
   INTO FANCY-DATE
  ELSE
   MOVE SYSTEM-DAY TO DATETEMP2
   STRING MONTH-LITERAL (SYSTEM-MONTH)
                        DATETEMP2, ",*", NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR, "*"
   DELIMITED BY SPACE
   INTO FANCY-DATE.
  STRING DAY-LITERAL (DAY-WEEK-NUMB), ",*"
   MONTH-LITERAL (SYSTEM-MONTH)
                        SYSTEM-DAY, ",*", NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR, "*"
   DELIMITED BY SPACE
   INTO FANCY-DATE2.
  STRING DAY-LITERAL2 (DAY-WEEK-NUMB), ",*"
   MONTH-LITERAL (SYSTEM-MONTH)
                        SYSTEM-DAY, ",*", NEW-SYSTEM-YEAR, "*"
   DELIMITED BY SPACE
   INTO FANCY-DATE3.
  INSPECT FANCY-DATE REPLACING ALL "*" BY " ".
  INSPECT FANCY-DATE2 REPLACING ALL "*" BY " ".
  INSPECT FANCY-DATE3 REPLACING ALL "*" BY " ".


Howard Brazee wrote in message <371DEC4C.D19F85B0@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>I don't recommend using this type of "trick" in COBOL programs.  I want
>people doing maintenance to be able to read the code and quickly
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >My current routine soesn;t seem to work ok after Febr. 29 of the year
2000.
>>
>> The following does the conversion without a table using the fact there
are
>> two cycles of months with lengths 31-30-31-30-31 starting in March.
>> SUB-FACTOR is 9(01).
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>> That is part of a days-between-dates calculation and can't convert days
back
>> into a date the same way.  I'm sure something could be worked out to
divide
>> the julian days after February by 153, but it would be more trouble than
>> just using a table.
```

<a id="t-7626340967"></a>
## Thread: What is the future of Cobol? — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu>`

```
What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
Will there still be jobs for Cobol programmers? In short, is the future bright
for cobol or are we approaching the sunset days of Cobol?

Gilbert
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371D14FC.3AC0@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu>`

```
gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com wrote:
> 
> What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gilbert

This depends on how COBOL grows with new demands and technology. It 
should be THE Common Business-Oriented Language "by definition". So it 
should now have assimilated all the fourth- and fifth-generation 
principles required for business. This has happened in some areas, such 
as OO (though done at too technical and basic a level and without much 
thought for business) and in its fourth-generation data-oriented 
features. Unfortunately, there are some who seek to extend the language 
as though it were a purely third-generation language, so that many of the 
new features proposed are just a wish-list of what COBOL might have had 
in the 60s and 70s. For example, the new error recovery feature is 
unstructured even though structured techniques were prevalent long before 
the last standard.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu> <371D14FC.3AC0@compuserve.com>`

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CBF4D.508CA7A0@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu>`

```
gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com wrote:
> What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
> Will there still be jobs for Cobol programmers? In short, is the future bright
> for cobol or are we approaching the sunset days of Cobol?
> Gilbert

Here are a few guesses, just to get the discussion flowing:

Right now and for the short term, the demand for COBOL programmers is
declining, since all the Y2K projects have begun and many are wrapping
up. (And new COBOL projects are being postponed until after the crisis
has passed.)

There will be an uptick after the first of the year as postponed
projects begin, but much of the programmer supply will come from Y2K
projects wrapping up.

In the medium run, the demand will be slightly reduced from pre-Y2K days
due to the fact that Y2K precipitated the conversion of some COBOL
systems to other languages.


In the longer run:

--the demand for mainframe COBOL programmers will increase, albeit as
slowly as pre-Y2K, since the large mainframe systems that survived Y2K
aren't going away.

--demand for PC/Unix COBOL programmers may grow significantly. Many of
the Y2K conversions were from mainframe COBOL to PC-based COBOL. The
success of such projects may help to confirm the wisdom of using COBOL
on the newer platforms. 


Any significant growth in demand will come from the PC/Unix platforms.
Success here will depend on several factors:

--Vendor support. Micro-Focus seems to be holding its own and growing.
IBM seems to be hanging in there. The advent of the Fujitsu compiler is
a very good sign. Syncronix's PerCOBOL (COBOL/Java) is an even better
sign. Computer Associates seems to be the one vendor that is dropping
the ball (with their Realia products).

--Academic support for business systems. In the past, new programmers
have come from computer science departments, which tend to be adjuncts
of math departments. COBOL's word-based symbols don't look like math, so
academicians have tended to put it down. (Academicians tend to put down
that which they don't understand.) 

--Alternatives (i.e. competition from other languages). Complex business
systems development will gravitate toward a language that is
self-documenting, is highly structurable, and allows the easy hiding of
complexity. This is not C++. This is probably not Java. This could be
COBOL. Or a variant of COBOL. (If a good open-source COBOL ever gains
recognition, COBOL may rapidly mutate into variants.) Or it could be a
new language entirely. 

So what do *you* think?

--John Hicks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371f2362.827394@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu> <371CBF4D.508CA7A0@ibm.net>`

```
On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 13:54:21 -0400, John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net>
wrote:

>gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com wrote:
>> What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Agreed.  All of my work is slated to be implemented in production in
1st Qtr 2000.

>There will be an uptick after the first of the year as postponed
>projects begin, but much of the programmer supply will come from Y2K
>projects wrapping up.

Agreed, but don't forget that the Y2k people will also be fixing the
patch work and work arounds for problems found after the rollover.
And some programs will run for the first time in 2000 after the
rollover, such as Weeklys, Monthlys, Quarterlys.  The Y2k people's job
won't be over quite yet.

>
>In the longer run:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>aren't going away.

The role of the mainframe has changed quite dramatically, probably
always will.  20yrs ago, it just about had a monoply on serious
business data processing.  10yrs ago, everybody thought it was a
dead-end, and moved to client/server.  But now, mainframes are big
servers on the client/server, acting as a secured data repository,
having client/server apps act as feeder systems to the mainframe.

Mainframes are faster, more scallable, and much more secure.
Microsoft does a lot of things right, but they always blunder at
security.

Mainframes support LAN, WAN, internet, and ERP.

And COBOL is the application language of choice on mainframes by a
wide margin.  The BCD format data which is still the backbone of
enterprise data.  COBOL supports BCD, most other new application
languages like Java, C, C++, and VB don't.

http://www.solutionsintegrator.com/882567030008e4a6/65f563c01d13349288256704006a7f14/c828a65a300b5fcc8825673e000d9eec?OpenDocument
http://www.infoworld.com/cgi-bin/displayArchive.pl?/98/19/t16-19.12.htm
http://www.infoworld.com/cgi-bin/displayStory.pl?/features/970818mainframe.htm

They are also used as internet servers:
http://www.computerworld.com/home/print.nsf/all/9901258B66
http://www.computerworld.com/home/print9497.nsf/all/SL4web317C1A

Companies are finding them have much better price/performance ratios
than high-end UNIX.  Distributed systems cost don't apply to
mainframes, such as multiple site and staff costs.
http://www.computerworld.com/home/print.nsf/all/9901258BD2

BTW, I telecommute all of my work from home, over the internet, to one
client/server (NT), and three mainframes in four different states,
sometimes simultaneously, using a VPN, RUMBA and email.

>
>--demand for PC/Unix COBOL programmers may grow significantly. Many of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Much of that wisdom, is based on moving large chunks of that COBOL
code to the new platform.  Many times, mission critical systems have
spent years invested in building secure and reliable code for their
specific needs.  Moving to another language also means starting from
scratch.

>
>Any significant growth in demand will come from the PC/Unix platforms.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the ball (with their Realia products).

Not only COBOL vendor support, but vendor support and improvements of
products that work very well with COBOL, or can make COBOL a better or
more viable product, such as DB2, CICS, and NetExpress.

>
>--Academic support for business systems. In the past, new programmers
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>that which they don't understand.) 

Let's just face it.  This is not going to happen.  Never has, never
will.  Academia has an "anything but COBOL" mentality.  They prefer
algorhythmic based languages.  COBOL isn't fancy, isn't pretty, isn't
sexy.  They prefer to rave about the "sportscars" in computer
languages, than even consider the dependable "truck" that COBOL is.

But, lets also not forget that academia tends to shun the real world
and live and thrive in a world of their own.




Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
Take the Y2k Quiz
http://www.teo-computer.com/dev/quiz.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371f9339.31923436@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu> <371CBF4D.508CA7A0@ibm.net> <371f2362.827394@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
John:

>On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 13:54:21 -0400, John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net>

>>--Vendor support. Micro-Focus seems to be holding its own and growing.
>>IBM seems to be hanging in there. The advent of the Fujitsu compiler is
>>a very good sign. Syncronix's PerCOBOL (COBOL/Java) is an even better
>>sign. Computer Associates seems to be the one vendor that is dropping
>>the ball (with their Realia products).

Actually, Computer Associates has made some recent improvements to
tyheir compiler which indicate to me that they are not going to
abandon the PC as a viable platform for Realia COBOL.

They have improved their Windows debugger dramatically and have added
ODBC support for Windows applications.

These recent developments appear to me as though the company is truly
interested in supporting the PC.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CCE31.6EA5@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu>`

```
gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com wrote:
> 
> What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gilbert

Heck, every time we write a new client/server application here with the
Mainframe as the server, we have to write new COBOL code to support it. 
And it is Mainframe/Cobol that performs all of our massive batch runs. 
PC servers and networks just can't handle the load yet.

Mainframe/Cobol is so well entrenched, it will be with us as long as the
Mainframe is.  And the Mainframe is the only tool for very large
corporation work loads.

Analogy: The trains never went away just because trucks were invented,
did they?

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371CD39C.21E1E02C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu>`

```
gilbert@reaper.bean-net.com wrote:
> 
> What will the future in Cobol be like after the Y2k problem is absorbed/solved?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gilbert


If I had to guess which existing languages will be in widest use in 20
years, COBOL would be right at the top.  But it is also likely that
whatever we will be using then won't look a lot like what is existing
now.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of Cobol?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%L8T2.1236$oH.408974@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7fhu8b$p7v$1@infoserver.bgsu.edu> <371CD39C.21E1E02C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>If I had to guess which existing languages will be in widest use in 20
>years, COBOL would be right at the top.  But it is also likely that
>whatever we will be using then won't look a lot like what is existing
>now.

I wouldn't bet on it.  What we have now sure looks a lot like what we
had 20 years ago. :-)  The success of OO itself, and particularly the
proposed new OO COBOL is far from sure.  I don't think it will go away,
but I seriously doubt that it will storm the field.
```

## Thread: export DAT file cobol HELP!!! — 4 msgs

### export DAT file cobol HELP!!!

- **From:** marlon95@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fgi8g$chc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Hello,
we have a database application running on a runtime version
of cobol.  The programmer has left and cannot be found.
We want to import the datafiles (tables) into a spreadsheet or
 another database program. I suppose i've to import the
*.dat files. It is my first time with cobol so i don't know what to do
. Is there any way or
trick to read or import these .DAT files into excel, acces or paradox?
Thanks for any help.
Marlon
marlon95@hotmail.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: export DAT file cobol HELP!!!

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371bf793.273414421@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7fgi8g$chc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
On Tue, 20 Apr 1999 00:37:37 GMT, marlon95@hotmail.com wrote:

>Hello,
>we have a database application running on a runtime version
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>trick to read or import these .DAT files into excel, acces or paradox?

Let me explain.  

This is like asking - "Can someone provide a
tool/utility/procedure/... to convert a RDBS data file to plain
ASCII-text".  Which one?  

Try to convert a Oracle database to ASCII, without using Oracle!
Try to convert a Microsoft Access database (.mdb) file, without using
Access!
There are numerous brands of COBOL on numerous systems - SOME use
proprietary, vendor invented, file strutures.  Others use the native
file structure of the platform involved.  

We need more information.  Companies such as LIANT provide ODBC
drivers to access many different type of COBOL data files.  There is a
company called "Data Junction" that makes a very good tool for
converting various data formats, take a look at that.  Tell us which
COBOL version, brand and platform and we'll try to help further.

Do you have the source code?  Given that, and the development
environment, any one of us can convert the data into something else.
Or we can maintain/enhance the existing code and eliminate the need to
move to something else.
```

#### ↳ Re: export DAT file cobol HELP!!!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371C7B47.5489F963@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fgi8g$chc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
marlon95@hotmail.com wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> trick to read or import these .DAT files into excel, acces or paradox?

No.  Those three applications require specially formatted data.  Unless
the COBOL program wrote its output particularily designed for one of
those, you will have to reformat the output.  The easiest way to do this
is to change the COBOL to delimit fields in a way your program will
understand.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: export DAT file cobol HELP!!!

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zQkT2.43$Rb5.25955@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<7fgi8g$chc$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <371C7B47.5489F963@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <371C7B47.5489F963@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>marlon95@hotmail.com wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>understand.

Not quite true.  I believe that all three applications are ODBC enabled,
thereby allowing an ODBC driver for COBOL-managed data to provide the
capability needed.  Relativity is such a product (see www.liant.com).

Do you have the COBOL source (at least the COBOL record descriptions), or
did that leave with the programmer?

Regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

>i need help writing a program for my second semester cobol class   it is on 
>control breaks, and has some calcultions in it.  any one willing to help

Go to the library or book store and look at "Sams Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours".  I paid particular attention to trying to explain
control breaks so that they would be easy to grasp and understand.
Turn to hour 20 and everything should become clear.  If this helps
you, and you happen to be in the bookstore and not the library, you
might want to buy the book.  Thanks!

Hi all, I asked this question earlier and didn't really give enough info. on
source and target machines/OS's

I want to know if it's easy to do a Cobol conversion between these two
environments and what would be involved, what tools/services are available,
etc.  Ideally the source code would be taken from the Mainframe, recompiled
on the Unix box and then everything would work perfectly (!)  I doubt if its
that simple.

On the mainframe :

The Cobol version is IBM VS Cobol II Release 4.0

The operating system version is MVS/ESA Version 5 Release 2.2

The version of UNIX on the IBM box is Version M-11/16/88f

Cobol to be used, based on your advice.

Any thoughts/ideas?

Thanks in anticipation!
```

Al Niï¿½o wrote:
> 
> Hi all, I asked this question earlier and didn't really give enough info. on
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that simple.

You can use our tool called IBM2FSC.
It would convert IBM COBOL dialects
OSVS, VSII and likely SAA to Fujitsu Cobol.
Please check it out at http://www.siber.com/sct/convs/

If you want to play with conversions
and create your own converter, based
on a set of transformations that we sell,
then you can use CobolTransformer
which is a general toolkit for building
Cobol converters and analyzers.
http://www.siber.com/sct/

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

LINK EXTERNL,,,COBLIB+COBAPI;

## Thread: VisualBasic and Cobol... — 3 msgs

### VisualBasic and Cobol...

- **From:** "tommy" <tommynilsen@yahoo.ocm>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1YhT2.135$Wi2.340@news1.online.no>`

```

In Visual Basic there is a datatype called Single,
could anyone please tell me the corresponding datatype in Cobol ....

Thanks.

Tommy Nilsen
```

#### ↳ Re: VisualBasic and Cobol...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aZFT2.11112$s_5.648189@dfiatx1-snr1>`
- **References:** `<1YhT2.135$Wi2.340@news1.online.no>`

```
Single in Visual Basic is a 16-bit IEEE real, or float.

In COBOL it is usually COMP-1.

MCM

tommy wrote in message <1YhT2.135$Wi2.340@news1.online.no>...
>
>In Visual Basic there is a datatype called Single,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: VisualBasic and Cobol...

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371e944a.7236533@netnews>`
- **References:** `<1YhT2.135$Wi2.340@news1.online.no>`

```
'Twas Wed, 21 Apr 1999 12:42:24 +0200, when "tommy"
<tommynilsen@yahoo.ocm> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>In Visual Basic there is a datatype called Single,
>could anyone please tell me the corresponding datatype in Cobol ....

SINGLE (like DOUBLE) is a floating point representation.  In the draft of
the next standard there is a data usage called FLOAT-SHORT which is the
same as SINGLE, which has been implemented in some newer compilers.  There
is no floating point in the current (1985) standard but many compilers
implement floating point under the name COMP-n (where n is an integer thru
9 which varied from one implementation to the next).  What compiler are
you using?
```

<a id="t-a46ba956f2"></a>
## Thread: Creating Files On PCs in COBOL — 3 msgs

### Creating Files On PCs in COBOL

- **From:** Andrew Spiers <andrew.spiers@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371E1FFF.33CBC511@virgin.net>`

```
Hi,

My background is COBOL on MVS mainframes and was wondering if you could
help me with a question about COBOL on PCs.

Basically, the question is : Is it possible to create files from within
a COBOL program on a PC ?

The scenario is that I have an input file which contains a list of
people with personal details for each person, in the following format:

NAME:ADDRESS1:ADDRESS2:ADDRESS3
NAME:ADDRESS1:ADDRESS2:ADDRESS3
NAME:ADDRESS1:ADDRESS2:ADDRESS3
NAME:ADDRESS1:ADDRESS2:ADDRESS3

What I would like to do is read each line in turn and create a file for
each record with the NAME field used as the filename. So in this example
I would end up with three files each containing
NAME:ADDRESS1:ADDRESS2:ADDRESS3.

Does it make sense ?

Many thanks,

Andrew
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Files On PCs in COBOL

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7flg7h$sja$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<371E1FFF.33CBC511@virgin.net>`

```
In article <371E1FFF.33CBC511@virgin.net>,
  andrew.spiers@virgin.net wrote:

> Basically, the question is : Is it possible to create
files from within
> a COBOL program on a PC ?
>

From your scenario - yes it is possible with any of the PC
compilers I am familiar with.  But this means running a
COBOL program ON THE PC.  I'm not sure if that was what you
were asking -- it could be read as: Can the a COBOL program
running on the mainframe create a file on the PC, and that
is a different animal.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Creating Files On PCs in COBOL

- **From:** John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371E43A7.FDE9F272@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<371E1FFF.33CBC511@virgin.net>`

```
> Basically, the question is : Is it possible to create files from within
> a COBOL program on a PC ?

Yes! It's a piece of cake. And welcome to the wonderful world of PC
programming. (What took you so long?)

The key is in the SELECT statement: the ASSIGN can point to a field
where you store the filename you wish to use. In Realia COBOL, it goes:

    SELECT MY-OUTPUT-FILE 
        ASSIGN TO VARYING MY-OUTPUT-FILE-NAME

You can then loop for each input record:

    READ MY-INPUT-FILE 
       (Exit loop on end of file)
    MOVE NAME OF MY-INPUT-REC TO MY-OUTPUT-FILE-NAME 
       (& check for valid file name and allow for duplicates)
    OPEN OUTPUT MY-OUTPUT-FILE (& check file status for error)
    WRITE MY-OUTPUT-REC FROM MY-INPUT-REC (& check for error)
    CLOSE MY-OUTPUT-FILE (& check for error)
    (Repeat loop to read input again)

There's no need to reserve or allocate disk space outside of the
program. If the name you assign is a simple file name (without
directories), it's stored in the current directory. You are free to
override that by including a drive/directory path as part of the
filename.

Go to it!

--John Hicks


Andrew Spiers wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Andrew
```

```
Please forgive the ignorance as I am not a programmer, but I really need to
know at what phase of a Y2K remediation project would the following tools be
needed?  Abend-AID/XLS, Xpediter/CICS,	and CICS Abend-aid/FX.

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: Tool question

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fjsng$c5p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`
- **References:** `<7fjg7p$264$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
In article <7fjg7p$264$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
  billtx2@my-dejanews.com wrote:
> Please forgive the ignorance as I am not a programmer, but I really need to
> know at what phase of a Y2K remediation project would the following tools be
> needed?  Abend-AID/XLS, Xpediter/CICS,	and CICS Abend-aid/FX.
>

XLS simulates a specific 'current' date for a specific transaction while all
other transactions remain their real current date unchanged. Works witch DB2
too, but not witch the time, helas. --> Test phase

Xpediter allows you to walk through a running COBOL, Assembler or PL/I
program (main or routine) and to peek and change the variable contents. The
transaction is running while you debug the module, but you can select a
specific terminal- and/or userid so all other transactions that call that
routine run unchanged. --> Debug phase (before and after the test phase)

AbendAid interprets the dumps when an abend occured and removes duplicate
dumps (same abendcode, same PSW). --> All phases from programming to
production

Daniel

------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tool question

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371E200C.2B91@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7fjg7p$264$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fjsng$c5p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Daniel Jacot wrote:
> 
> In article <7fjg7p$264$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>,
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own



Footnote about Xpediter/BATCH and Xpediter/CICS: In order to use this
tool, the developer must do an additional compile phase for Xpediter,
which results in storing an Xpediter "listing" into a dataset.  This
listing is required for Xpediter to be able to show you the application
source as you are stepping thru the code.  

We have found that this Xpediter compile phase can in some instances
double your compile time!  This is not an issue for us because we have
MIPs to burn, but for some shops, this could be a killer.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tool question

- **From:** billtx2@my-dejanews.com
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7fjg7p$264$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <7fjsng$c5p$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <371E200C.2B91@saif.com>`
