```
 Anyone has experience implementation of Language Environment run time
 in applications with mixed COBOL II and PL/I programs
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL II, PLI V2.3 and Language environment runtime

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7flj3g$n84@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<371DF637.5794E9D9@mail.com>`

```
Normally, I don't like lots of "cross-posting" - but the attached question
was put out in the comp.lang.cobol newsgroup.  I was thinking that some of
the people in IBM-MAIN *might* have more information on this and could
provide input.   If you do want to reply, please note that the COBOL
product is VS COBOL II (not COBOL for OS/390 & VM) and the PL/I release is
2.3.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL II, PLI V2.3 and Language environment runtime

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fm77q$4al@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<371DF637.5794E9D9@mail.com> <7flj3g$n84@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In answer to the general question of whether VS COBOL II and PL/I V2.3 ILC
is supported with the LE run-time library, the following is the reply that I
got from my "usually reliable sources".

"  They sure are!  They should be link-edited with the PL/I Migration
aid (OS PL/I V2) first, then they can run under LE or pre-LE.  If the
OS PL/I V2 library is up to date (latest service) and the application
has been re-linkedited since the Migration Aid was added (it first
shipped in 1993), then no relink is necessary.  Oh, I just remembered,
if the PL/I is using multitasking, then there are some restrictions,
but PL/I multitasking use is not too common."

This doesn't answer the question of who has had experience with this, but it
at least give you the "official" answer.
```

## Thread: comp-3 conversion — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### comp-3 conversion

- **From:** Dave Cunitz <dcunitz@my-dejanews.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fo072$4i8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Has anyone heard of a good/easy way to convert a number to a format on a PC
that will become a comp-3 field once it's uploaded to a mainframe and
converted from ASCII to EBCDIC?  Any direction or suggestions would be
greatly appreciated!

Thank you!
Dave

-----------== Posted via Deja News, The Discussion Network ==----------
http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own
```

#### ↳ Re: comp-3 conversion

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371F8D4E.4716@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7fo072$4i8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Dave Cunitz wrote:
> 
> Has anyone heard of a good/easy way to convert a number to a format on a PC
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.dejanews.com/       Search, Read, Discuss, or Start Your Own


I think your up a creek without a paddle on this one.
The ASCII to EBCDIC conversion at upload time will reek havoc with your
data.
We just convert the data on the host after upload.

If someone else has a solution, I'll be impressed!

Pete
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: comp-3 conversion

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fo3ht$coq@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fo072$4i8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <371F8D4E.4716@saif.com>`

```
Micro Focus has a "nifty" tool for doing this - called "WFL".  What it does,
is let you convert character data from ASCII to EBCDIC or vice versa - but
doesn't change COMP-n data (binary, packed-decimal).  Other PC or UNIX
vendors may have similar utilities.   The bottom-line is that you need
SOMETHING (utility, program, whatever) that can be "feed" your record layout
and convert character data but not binary or packed data.

The alternative is to have your program (on the original platform) write out
everything as DISPLAY.  For *all* numeric data you should use

    USAGE DISPLAY SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE

Then if you use the CODE-SET clause of the FD, you can create "totally
portable" files.
```

#### ↳ Re: comp-3 conversion

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFAry82.62q@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7fo072$4i8$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
Dave Cunitz <dcunitz@my-dejanews.com> writes:

>Has anyone heard of a good/easy way to convert a number to a format on a PC
>that will become a comp-3 field once it's uploaded to a mainframe and
>converted from ASCII to EBCDIC?  Any direction or suggestions would be
>greatly appreciated!

I don't know how many hundred little throwaway conversion
programs I've written to unpack COMP'd fields.  So when faced
with another batch of 30 I said "Enough!".  I started to 
think of how I could come up with a utility program that
would do it all for me.

I have it all spec'd out and have started writing it.  I have
to get it finished this week at which time I'll toss it out
for public consumption.

Handles 1, 2, & 4 byte binary and packed decimal fields.
Also  ASCII <--> EBCDIC text conversions.


Film at 11:00.
```

<a id="t-d46bad4e25"></a>
## Thread: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!! — 4 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** "DEDALO S.a.s." <dedalo@mtrade.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
Dear friends,
i ask you for this questions?

I have a sequential file created in Cobol2 on IBM Mainframe platform, this
file has PIC S9(9) fileds, i transfer it with IND$FILE command on Microsoft
NT Server machine with ASCII conversion options,
this file transferred are read by a Cobol program on NT that dumping
reading this s9(9) field - "Not numeric"!!!!!.
Where is the problem?
Is a transfer problem o is a conversion error To Charset EBCDIC-ASCII?.
Help Me!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Thank you all!!!
Bye Gianluca
```

#### ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** pitchl@tdbank.ca (Lew Pitcher)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371f1c58.5521064@news21.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
On Thu, 22 Apr 1999 14:03:54 +0200, "DEDALO S.a.s."
<dedalo@mtrade.com> wrote:

>Dear friends,
>i ask you for this questions?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Help Me!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!


Many COBOL formats are neither ASCII nor EBCDIC. If the field is
defined as USAGE IS DISPLAY, then characterset conversion may be used,
otherwise you have to cope with binary and/or packed decimal numbers
which cannot be altered by characterset conversion.

Additionally, NT natively interprets binary numbers differently from
the interpretation IBM mainframes use. Look up "big endian", "little
endian", and "endianness" before you do anything. NT doesn't have
native support for COMP-3 (PACKED DECIMAL) numbers at all, so you'll
have to accomodate that as well.

You don't say what tool will use the file on the NT platform; if you
are processing the file through a COBOL program on NT, there is a good
chance that the COBOL program can be written and/or compiled to a
mainframe compatability mode, which will process the data correctly.

Otherwise, the simplest way to fix the problem is to get the mainframe
file regenerated without the binary/packed fields, either by dropping
that data, or by converting it to DISPLAY prior to writing it to file.
This would permit EBCDIC-to-ASCII conversion to work properly (such as
it is), and should give you a better looking file. Remember,
characterset conversion is incomplete (there are characters in EBCDIC
that don't exist in ASCII, and vice-versa), so even with changes like
these, you still may wind up with an unreadable file.



Lew Pitcher
System Consultant, Development Services
Toronto Dominion Bank

(Opinions expressed are my own, not my employers')
```

#### ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371F27E9.16C0A471@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
DEDALO S.a.s. wrote:
> 
> Dear friends,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bye Gianluca

EBCDIC x'F0'  becomes a } on the PC, etc after ascii ebcdic
translation.

Make your signs separate -9(9) and download that.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File conversion from IBM Mainframe to Windows NT platform!!!Help Me!!!!!!!!!!

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3732c4ec.24274044@news.netvision.net.il>`
- **References:** `<7fn2q3$6um$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <371F27E9.16C0A471@zip.com.au>`

```
On Thu, 22 Apr 1999 23:45:13 +1000 Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

:>> Dear friends,
:>> i ask you for this questions?
 
:>> I have a sequential file created in Cobol2 on IBM Mainframe platform, this
:>> file has PIC S9(9) fileds, i transfer it with IND$FILE command on Microsoft
:>> NT Server machine with ASCII conversion options,
:>> this file transferred are read by a Cobol program on NT that dumping
:>> reading this s9(9) field - "Not numeric"!!!!!.
:>> Where is the problem?
:>> Is a transfer problem o is a conversion error To Charset EBCDIC-ASCII?.
:>> Help Me!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
 
:>> Thank you all!!!
:>> Bye Gianluca

:>EBCDIC x'F0'  becomes a } on the PC, etc after ascii ebcdic
:>translation.

No, it does not.

x'F0' is a character zero.

The issue probably is that the last digit of the number is signed so that
instead of being 0-9 (x'F0'-x'F9') is x'C0'-X'C9' for positive numbers (first
unprintable, rest being A-I) or x'D0'-x'D9' for negative numbers (first
unprintable, rest being J-R).

You can probably write a routine to edit the numbers. If the last digit is
A-I, you can safely convert to 1-9 and know the number is positive. If the
last digit is J-R you can safely convert to 1-9 and know the number is
negative. Some research will be required to determine what x'C0' and x'D0'
look like when downloaded in ASCII.

:>Make your signs separate -9(9) and download that.

A good approach if you can massage the data on the mainframe.
```

---

<a id="t-32eeb546e6"></a>
## Thread: Day of Week — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Day of Week

- **From:** colm@skynet.csn.ul.ie (An Fear Ar An nGealach)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```
How can you calculate the Day of the week, given the date.

I am using VAX COBOL, and have been looking at LIB$DAY_OF_WEEK.
Unfortunately the help doesn't tell you how to call it.

the following program doesn't work, it just displays 

Call Status:
Day:

(as if the fields were blank, but both are defined as numeric)
-=-=-=-=-=

<snip>

01      THE-DATE        PIC 9(6).
01      DAY-NUMBER      PIC 9.
01      CALL-STATUS     PIC 9(9)        COMP.
.
. <snip>
.
        MOVE 200499 TO THE-DATE
        MOVE 0 TO DAY-NUMBER

        CALL "LIB$DAY_OF_WEEK" USING
                BY REFERENCE THE-DATE
                BY REFERENCE DAY-NUMBER
                GIVING CALL-STATUS

        IF CALL-STATUS NOT = 1 
                DISPLAY "ERROR" 
        END-IF
                
        DISPLAY "Call Status: " CALL-STATUS
        DISPLAY "Day: " DAY-NUMBER.

        STOP RUN.
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-

I have also tried setting THE-DATE to 0 which, according to the help, is
supposed to return the current day, but that doesn't work either. (Same as
above)


Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Day of Week

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fnkka$a31@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```
There were several answers to this same question within the last few weeks -
or you can check DejaNews and the question gets answered every few months.
The bottom-line (IMHO) is that if you have a fully "current" COBOL compiler,
use the INTEGER-OF-DATE intrinsic function and divide by 7.  The remainder
gives you the day of the week (the same value as the ACCEPT FROM DAY-OF-WEEK
statement - except you get a 0 instead of 7).

If you don't have access to the COBOL intrinsic functions, then multiple
routines have been posted - or provided via ETK.
```

#### ↳ Re: Day of Week

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371fa6d8.85054360@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```
On Thu, 22 Apr 1999 16:43:39 GMT, colm@skynet.csn.ul.ie (An Fear Ar An
nGealach) wrote:

>How can you calculate the Day of the week, given the date.
>
>I am using VAX COBOL, and have been looking at LIB$DAY_OF_WEEK.
>Unfortunately the help doesn't tell you how to call it.

If it's REALLY VMS COBOL you can use the intrinsic functions as
previously described.  Intrinsic funcitons did not make it into the
VAX COBOL as far as I can see.
```

#### ↳ Re: Day of Week

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7frfrp$dqk@journal.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```

  05 DAY-OF-THE-WEEK      PIC 9.
  05 CALENDAR-DATE        PIC 9(8). (YYYYMMDD FORMAT).

  COMPUTE DAY-OF-THE-WEEK =
    FUNCTION MOD (FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE (CALENDAR-DATE), 7) + 1.

  Result is 1=Sunday 2=Monday and so on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Day of Week

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Wu_T2.6975$L61.2209335@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```
An Fear Ar An nGealach wrote:
>How can you calculate the Day of the week, given the date.

The following is good for any date in the Gregorian calendar, through
9999/12/31.

       01  DATE-WORK-AREA.
           03  DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD            PIC  9(08).
           03  DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD-R          REDEFINES DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD.
               05  DW-WORK-CCYY                PIC  9(04).
               05  DW-WORK-MM                  PIC  9(02).
               05  DW-WORK-DD                  PIC  9(02).
           03  DW-WEEKDAY                  PIC  9(01).
           03  DW-TEMP-CCYY                PIC  9(04).
           03  DW-TEMP-MM                  PIC  9(04).
           03  DW-TEMP1                    PIC  9(08).
           03  DW-TEMP2                    PIC  9(08).

      *            C A L C U L A T E   D A Y   O F   W E E K
      *
      *    WHEN GIVEN ANY DATE THIS ROUTINE RETURNS A NUMBER FROM
      *    1 TO 7 INDICATING THE DAY OF WEEK ON WHICH THE DATE FALLS.
      *
      *    USAGE:  MOVE <DATE> TO DW-WORK-CCYYMMDD
      *            PERFORM CALC-DAY-OF-WEEK
      *
      *    RESULT: DW-WEEKDAY = DAY OF WEEK
      *
      *    THIS ROUTINE USES ZELLER'S CONGRUENCE, EXPRESSED BY THE
      *    FOLLOWING FORMULA:
      *
      *        <WEEKDAY> = (INT(13 * MONTH + 3) / 5)
      *                   + DAY
      *                   + YEAR
      *                   + INT(YEAR / 4)
      *                   - INT(YEAR / 100)
      *                   + INT(YEAR / 400)
      *                   + 1) MOD 7) + 1
      *
      *    WHERE: WEEKDAY = 1 TO 7: 1=SUN, 2=MON, ..., 7=SAT
      *           DAY     = DAY OF THE MONTH
      *           MONTH   = MONTH OF THE YEAR (JAN AND FEB COUNT AS
      *                      MONTHS 13 AND 14 OF THE PREVIOUS YEAR)
      *           YEAR    = FOUR DIGIT YEAR (YEAR - 1 IF MONTH IS
      *                      JAN OR FEB)
      *           INT(...) MEANS TAKE THE INTEGER PART (NO ROUNDING)
      *           X MOD Y  MEANS THE REMAINDER AFTER DIVIDING Y
      *                      INTO X
      *
       CALC-DAY-OF-WEEK.
           MOVE DW-WORK-CCYY TO DW-TEMP-CCYY.
           MOVE DW-WORK-MM   TO DW-TEMP-MM.
           IF (DW-WORK-MM < 03)
               ADD 12 TO DW-TEMP-MM
               SUBTRACT 1 FROM DW-TEMP-CCYY
           END-IF.
           COMPUTE DW-TEMP1 = (13 * DW-TEMP-MM + 3) / 5.
           ADD DW-WORK-DD DW-TEMP-CCYY TO DW-TEMP1.
           DIVIDE DW-TEMP-CCYY BY 4 GIVING DW-TEMP2.
           ADD DW-TEMP2 TO DW-TEMP1.
           DIVIDE DW-TEMP-CCYY BY 100 GIVING DW-TEMP2.
           SUBTRACT DW-TEMP2 FROM DW-TEMP1.
           DIVIDE DW-TEMP-CCYY BY 400 GIVING DW-TEMP2.
           ADD DW-TEMP2 TO DW-TEMP1.
           ADD 1 TO DW-TEMP1.
           DIVIDE DW-TEMP1 BY 7 GIVING DW-TEMP2 REMAINDER DW-WEEKDAY.
           ADD 1 TO DW-WEEKDAY.
```

#### ↳ Re: Day of Week

- **From:** johnw@stones.com (John Whistler)
- **Date:** 1999-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B3494EC99668E1FC2@192.168.1.7>`
- **References:** `<FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>`

```
In article <FALnsr.G98@ul.ie>,
colm@skynet.csn.ul.ie (An Fear Ar An nGealach) wrote:

>Subject: Day of Week
>From: An Fear Ar An nGealach
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You've had a couple of answers showing the Cobol way of doing this. If you
still want to use lib$day_of_week then you need to change the definition of
your variables.

According to VMS help (help RTL LIB LIB$DAY_OF_WEEK), the first parameter
should be an unsigned quadword, and the second an unsigned longword. In VMS
Cobol terms this translates as:

01      THE-DATE        PIC 9(18)   COMP.
01      DAY-NUMBER      PIC 9(9)    COMP.

Try this and see what happens.

John Whistler.
```

## Thread: See how you compare against the Top 10 list of COBOL programmers. — 6 msgs

### See how you compare against the Top 10 list of COBOL programmers.

- **From:** Ludlow@NewsReader.Com (Ann Marie Ludlow)
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990421230447.831$Px@newsreader.com>`

```
Dear COBOL Programmer,

Through April, TekMetrics has extended its free service of COBOL Programmer
certifications conducted through it's internet-based CAT engine.  Simply go
to http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/certtop.html to order your free
certification.  You can either elect to take the test right away, or come
back later, using the PIN you are provided when you order.

To qualify as a Certified COBOL Programmer, you must pass the examination
with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master COBOL
Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.  At the end of the
test, your score will be provided (with a scoring range of 1-5).  Once
certified, you can add the logo associated with the test (standard or
master logo, depending on how well you do) to your resume or website.
Furthermore, those that pass prior to 4/19 will be mailed a certificate,
once again, at no cost (after which time their may be a nominal fee for the
shipping of the certificates). There's no catch.  We're simply trying to
prove a concept during a beta trial period.  Thus far, the feedback has
been extremely positive!

Why bother getting certified by TekMetrics?

1)  More and more corporations recognize TekMetrics Certifications as an
indication not only of the technical workers knowledge level, but also as
an indicator that they were willing to demonstrate their skills to them.

2)  It's free and it's fun.  They won't be free forever, but they are now.
So take advantage of them.  Furthermore, those who have completed the tests
generally indicate that they enjoyed the experience and considered both the
delivery of the test and the items themselves to be of high quality.

3)  Over 4000 technical workers from around the world are ordering our
certifications each week, and that number is steadily rising.

4)  Strut your stuff.  See if you are good enough to make the top 10 list.

5)  Find out both your strengths and weaknesses in COBOL.  At the end of
the test, you can request specifics on how you did, by subtopic.  And yes,
that too is free.

All we ask for in return is your feedback at the end of the exam.  We will
be continually focusing on the improvement of the delivery of the test and
quality of the questions.  Any feedback you can provide, positive or
negative, will help us greatly.

Another FAQ is, "How do you know the person who supposedly took the test
was actually the same person?  What if it was his hotshot COBOL Programmer
brother, sister or friend?  In other words, what is to prevent him from
cheating?"  Our solution:  we allow employers to spotcheck the technical
workers with a short test at no cost at their corporate locations, if they
so choose.  Corporations have unanimously applauded this solution.

Lastly, data you provide in the certification process is not used without
your permission other than to store your certification record and verify to
valid employers at their request.  We do, however, publish names on our
site in a Top Ten Scores list for each category, but no addresses, phone
numbers, email, etc.. are shown.  For more information, see our privacy
statement at http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/privacy.html

Well, that's pretty much it!  We're trying to establish a new
cost-effective and convenient alternative to today's certification
environment.  During this beta trial period, you will be the benefactor of
this fun and challenging experience.

If you have any questions/concerns regarding the COBOL certification,
please contact Deborah Manley at <mailto:deborah.manley@tekmetrics.com>.

Good luck and thank you.

Ann Marie Ludlow
TekMetrics, Inc.
www.tekmetrics.com <http://www.tekmetrics.com>
```

#### ↳ Re: See how you compare against the Top 10 list of COBOL programmers.

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<371F6F34.482A@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<19990421230447.831$Px@newsreader.com>`

```
Ann Marie Ludlow wrote:
> 
> Dear COBOL Programmer,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
(snip)


This thing ain't any good.  I just tried taking your COBOL-II test.  I
quit after about 4 or 5 of these multiple choice questions because there
was no correct answer available for one of the questions!

The question I'm refering to asks "how many times will A0200 be
executed?". Module A0200 is executed like this:
     PERFORM A0200-CALC-INTEREST
       UNTIL W-BANK-BALANCE IS GREATER THAN W-BALANCE-LIMIT

But the question also states that neither of these variables are ever
modified by A0200, and right before the PERFORM, the balance limit is
set to the current balance times two.  Therefore, this loop will be
infinate. ("infinate" was not an available choice.)

Based upon the poor quality of this "certification", I must assume its
really just a way to get my name, number, and address (which were
REQUIRED to get to the questions) for marketing purposes.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: See how you compare against the Top 10 list of COBOL programmers.

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37238de4.6452259@netnews>`
- **References:** `<19990421230447.831$Px@newsreader.com>`

```
'Twas 22 Apr 1999 03:04:48 GMT, when Ludlow@NewsReader.Com (Ann Marie
Ludlow) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>Through April, TekMetrics has extended its free service of COBOL Programmer
>certifications conducted through it's internet-based CAT engine.  Simply go
>to http://www.tekmetrics.com/cert/certtop.html to order your free
>certification.

>Thus far, the feedback has
>been extremely positive!

Well let me give you a good dose of negative feedback:

This is not sour grapes.  I scored 4.55 and was thereby judged to be "a
Certified Master COBOL II Programmer".  My score is all the more
outstanding when you consider that I have never used nor studied COBOL II.

COBOL II is IBM's name for their implementation of COBOL-85 (ANSI
X3.23-1985, ISO 1989-1985).  If you mean this to be a test of COBOL II
proficiency then you should label it "IBM COBOL II" and include some
questions specific to IBM extensions.  The test as presented should be
labeled a test for COBOL-85.

The user is allowed three minutes (180 seconds) to answer each question.
For most simple multiple choice questions this is ample time, but for
questions where the choices are complex it can take more than that amount
of time to determine the differences.  I timed out on Q.2 and Q.30, and
nearly timed out on Q.21.  Though the instructions specify this three
minute limit, it doesn't tell me this:  If I select one of the radio
buttons and then it times out, do I get credit for my answer?  I suggest a
five minute limit for the questions with lots of code to read.

The following are problems with the specific questions.  I have striven
not to reveal your actual questions and answers.

Q.2:  None of the answers are right.  The PERFORM will continue
indefinitely; the condition will never be met.  (Actually this code should
get a syntax error, because there are two extraneous END-PERFORMs.  It's
possible this is COBOL II specific.  Some compilers allow END-PERFORM on
PERFORM procedure-name, because X3.23-1985 defined this poorly.)

Q.6:  The input data is all upper case, yet the expected output is mixed
case.

Q.13:  The data fields are separated by FILLERs with PIC X(15) yet you
squeeze out all the spaces in the data layout.

Q.15:  This is essentially the same question as Q.4.

Q.16:  Two of the answers are correct.  I chose the one with more detail.

Q.20:  The value of A-COUNT1 cannot be determined, because it was never
initialized.  I assumed it was initialized to zero.

Q.25:  The first definition of the table doesn't match the redefinition.
Each X(30) should be X(20), or you need to add 05 FILLER PIC X(10) to the
end of the table definition.

Q.26:  Your asking for code to produce MM/YY but there's no place for the
/ so you'll only get MMYY.

Q.27:  If the problem is that line X ought to come after line Y, which is
wrong, line X or line Y?

Q.29:  The "correct" answer assumes that the table was initialized to
zeroes, which was not stated.

Q.33:  The task is to change the table definition to do a binary search,
yet none of the answers say put the table in order (and it isn't already).

Q.36:  One of the errors is where indicated, but the other error is in the
given code that is presumably correct.

Q.39:  The "error" in this case is bad style and probably a logic error
(the two go hand in hand) but it is not strictly an error.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: See how you compare against the Top 10 list of COBOL programmers.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7g23bo$ji8@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990421230447.831$Px@newsreader.com> <37238de4.6452259@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote in message
  <snip>
>
> This is not sour grapes.  I scored 4.55 and was thereby judged to be "a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Actually, as has been pointed out to the people who offer this test,

1) The name of the IBM product is *NOT* (and never has been) "COBOL II" - It
is "VS COBOL II".

2) This is an OLD (non-strategic) IBM product - that has been replaced by 3
subsequent products (IBM SAA  AD/Cycle COBOL/370 - followed by IBM COBOL for
MVS & VM followed by IBM COBOL for OS/390).    VS COBOL II is an ANSI
High-level '85 compiler - but is NOT a FIPS High-level compiler (because it
doesn't include intrinsic function support - which was added in the later
products).

3) The code in the sample questions was (while still in betas) pointed out
to be INVALID and clearly would NOT compile on any VS COBOL II compiler.
(The example of erroneous END-PERFORM statements for out-of-line PERFORMs
was pointed out about a year ago).  It is pretty clear that the test
examples were never compiled and the answers never verified.

4) Generally, as the people offering this test seem to have NO interest in
offering a correct (or corrected) test - my assumption remains that they are
"trolling" for names and not in determining VS COBOL II - much less current
IBM mainframe COBOL - competency.
```

```
*  *  *  *  * Please reply to Cindy@ottinf.com*  *  *  *  *  *

JOB TITLE: Test Team Leader

REQUIREMENTS:
- Good working knowledge of structured Testing for the Mainframe and Client
Server environment
- Experience n defining testing strategy and the systems test plan including
resource requirements, approach schedule and checkpoints
- Experience in defining testing requirements for the given scope and goals
of the project
- Experience in defining system implementation plan
- Proven ability to co-ordinate with the development team and other
supportive participants for the testing activities, such as, test data
population and conversion, execution, discrepancy reporting, defect/problem
resolution, dry run, and off-site testing
- Proven ability to ensure supportability of the converted systems,
including the hand over to the Support team
- Excellent organizational and communication skills
```

<a id="t-e595bca949"></a>
## Thread: Internationalization in draft of next COBOL Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Internationalization in draft of next COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fnqoq$pnj@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
There have been several recent posts (questions, answers, and more
questions) about how to handle "internationalization" issues in COBOL (for
example, upper-/lower-case conversion of European accented letters,
alphabetization of upper and lower-case letters, etc).  The draft of the
next Standard provides some "nifty" solutions to these problems which I
think that many of this NG's readers might be interested in.  Therefore, I
have put out on the web an excerpt from the "Concepts" section of draft 1.4
of the next Standard.

  NOTE WELL:  Draft 1.5 is expected "momentarily" and there are changes -
both in details and facilities - in this draft for internationalization (and
other features).  Therefore, this concept section should only be read for
"general over-view" NOT as details that you should expect to see "as is".

Please look at the section at:
   http://pw2.netcom.com/~wmklein/INTER.HTM
```

<a id="t-707b00ac88"></a>
## Thread: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability — 20 msgs

### Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** endearment <endear@tig.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au>`

```
Howdy all!

If I wish to pass an item of data through a number of tests where a fail
in any of those tests is fatal for that item of data AND I wish to react
differently to each type of fail, is it better to have all the nested
loops in one routine or to parcel some of them out in called routines?

Here is a pseudocode example:

Read dessert-suggestion TILL no more suggestions
AT END
    MOVE true to no-more-suggestions
    SELECT dessert-suggestion from list-of-yummy-things
NOT AT END
   IF dessert-suggestion has ice-cream
       think about it...
      IF dessert-suggestion has chocolate topping
         think about it hard...
         IF dessert-suggestion is for two people
             think about it really hard...
            IF dessert-suggestion has no bananas
               Imagine what it tastes like...
               ADD dessert-suggestion to list-of-yummy-things
            ELSE
               SAY ï¿½This dessert has bananas!ï¿½
            END-IF
         ELSE
            SAY ï¿½I want something for two people!ï¿½
         END-IF
      ELSE
         SAY ï¿½I want chocolate topping!ï¿½
      END-IF
   ELSE
      SAY ï¿½I want ice cream!ï¿½
END-READ


Each of those tests might have one or two extra lines of processing in
either the THEN or ELSE section.

a) IF I keep the tests all in one routine I have potentially hard to
read code

b) IF I parcel some of those tests into called routines and use boolean
flags to signify whether a test has been passed, my code might be more
readable but I also increase the length of my code and incur the extra
processing costs of those extra method calls and boolean flag handling
techniqes, particularly if I am dealing with thousands of data items
(thatï¿½s LOT of desserts!)..


Is efficiency of greater importance than readability here?

*smile*

Robert.
```

#### ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-J4BnWbT1jdlM@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au>`

```
On Fri, 23 Apr 1999 09:12:29, endearment <endear@tig.com.au> wrote:
.. snippo...

How about something simpler.

01  Error-Flag Pic X Value Space.
      88  Some-Error Value "X" "Y" "Z".
      88  Error-X Value "X".
      88  Error-Y Value "Y".
      88  Error-Z Value "Z".
      88  No-Error Value Space.

Evaluate ... Some Condition...
  When First-Thing
        Set Error-X to true
   When Second-Thing
        Set Error-Y to true
   When Third-Thing
        Set Error-Z to true
   When Other
         Set No-Error To True
End-Evaluate

If Some-Error
   Perform Error-Routine
End-if

Error-Routine.
    Evaluate True
         When Error-X
               Do Something
         When Error-Y
               Do Somethign else
         When Error-Z
               Do somethign different still
    End-Evaluate
-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

Check out a really great search engine http://www.google.com
(Thanks COBOL Report!)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** endearment <endear@tig.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37212117.5987A29C@tig.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-J4BnWbT1jdlM@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
I *like* this idea a lot and have used compound boolean flags often..
*smile*

When I think about, I have one advantage and one disadvantage.

Advantage is in error reporting: an item of data that would fail might
fail one test or two or three or all of them . If I use compound flags I
could report errors more completely.

The disadvantage is the original reason I went for a nested loop - each
item of data will still pass through some sort of test for *each*
error.. adding overheads..
*thinking still*

Robert.

In this case I do not think it is a better solution

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> On Fri, 23 Apr 1999 09:12:29, endearment <endear@tig.com.au> wrote:
> .. snippo...
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Thanks COBOL Report!)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37221732.244912200@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-J4BnWbT1jdlM@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <37212117.5987A29C@tig.com.au>`

```
On Sat, 24 Apr 1999 11:40:39 +1000, endearment <endear@tig.com.au>
wrote:

>
>In this case I do not think it is a better solution
>

I listed the simplest implementation.  You could use multi-byte flags
and ranges for errors, thus handling the more complex conditions - and
avoiding the heavy nested if... else.. structures.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** mrbiueskyz@aol.comatose (MrBIueSkyz)
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990423215651.14335.00002893@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
> How about something simpler.

If this is acceptable, you don't need the switches at all.  Just code

Evaluate ...
Some Condition...
When First-Thing
 Do Something
When Second-Thing
 Do Something else
When Third-Thing
 Do something different
End-Evaluate

However, the original pseudo code included additional processing if a test was
true. E.g.

IF dessert-suggestion has ice-cream
--> think about it... <--
    IF dessert-suggestion has chocolate topping

This logic doesn't do that.
```

#### ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uS3vIDgj#GA.250@nih2naac.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au>`

```
Sometimes sacrificing some efficiency for functionality is better. An
example might be to check for as many errors as possible on a data entry
screen so that operators can correct as many as possible before pressing
enter again.


Loop
  Error-count = 0
  If error-cond-1
    queue error-msg-1, highlight bad field, etc.
    add 1 to error-count
  Endif
  If error-cond-2
    queue error-msg-2, highlight bad field, etc.
    add 1 to error-count
  Endif
  If error-cond-3
    queue error-msg-3, highlight bad field, etc.
    add 1 to error-count
  Endif
Exit if error-count = 0
  Beep and display accumulated error messages and error-count at bottom
  Reset highlighting and read screen again
Endloop


This is inefficient in that every condition is tested, but that's because
that's what you want to do in this case. It does avoid excess nesting, and
you still get a nice summary go/nogo condition at the end.

There are probably better examples, such as a compiler which should
diagnose as many errors as possible, since another compilation might be
more wasteful of system resources (and more importantly of highly paid
human time) than getting only one error message each time you press enter
on a data entry screen with lots of bad fields.

This example could be turned around, in case you want to quit testing and
fail immediately at the first error, by adding "and error-count = 0" to
each if. Inefficient in repeating that test, but in some cases it might be
better than more nesting. However in this kind of case it's usually much
better to use a case structure like Evaluate. As others have pointed out,
that's the best way to avoid excessively nested IFs. However, this is not
always possible if testing for some conditions requires more complicated
code and can only be done safely if other conditions have already been
ruled out, then some nesting is unavoidable. However, this can usually be
broken down into groups, e.g.

Evaluate True
  When safe-to-test-now-error-cond-1
    Response-1
  When safe-to-test-now-error-cond-2
    Response-2
  When safe-to-test-now-error-cond-3
    Response-3
  When Other
    code needed to set up tests for more complicated conditions
    (where this code might have been invalid in the other cases)
    Evaluate True
      When cond-4
        Response-4
      When cond-5
        Response-5
      When cond-6
        Response-6
      When Other
        etc.
    End-Evaluate
End-Evaluate


Each Other case might be suitable for a Perform as appropriate.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!
```

#### ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fpprd$n4l@sjx-ixn10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au>`

```
This gets into the "exit immediate" situation that comes up often in this
NG.  Given the current extension in some compilers which will be in the next
Standard, let me give you at least one alternative set of logic (using "exit
immediate" instead of EXIT Perform/Paragraph/Section which is the actual
syntax).  My "re-write" is based on the assumption that you really DID want
to avoid any subsequent tests - if an item has failed the previous test.
(In other words, if such-and-such a condition is true/false - then do NOT do
the rest of the tests in this routine.)

NOTE: FYI, I actually like the "deeply nested" approach - but do see (some
times) the advantages of avoiding such code - for maintainability.

P.S.  I have kept your logic - even if I LIKE desserts with banans!

> Read dessert-suggestion TILL no more suggestions
> AT END
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>       SAY "I want ice cream!"
          Exit Immediate
      End-IF
>    IF dessert-suggestion has chocolate topping
>          think about it hard...
>     ELSE
>          SAY "I want chocolate topping!"
             Exit Immediate
>     END-IF
>     IF dessert-suggestion is for two people
>              think about it really hard...
>      ELSE
>           SAY "I want something for two people!"
             Exit Immediate
>       END-IF
>       IF dessert-suggestion has no bananas
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-READ
```

```
<19B0C69644B4C792.517199234C28ED36.B6F5D8A30E1471D6@library-proxy.airnews.net
>,  "George Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airfoce.net> wrote: <snip
much text>

>
> Personally I come down on development in the majority of cases as being:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net

I agree!

We teach our programmers that maintainability is really most important. The
real bottleneck is mostly physical I-O, not the logic of your program. Don't
read a file twice, try not to force your database to search sequentially,
that's the essential! Don't waste your time with single expressions (except
when you write a utility in ASSEMBLER language). And as you mentioned, a well
structured program is quickly improved by a experienced programmer.

Daniel

------------------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at http://www.winterthur.com

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<924882806.11296.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au> <19B0C69644B4C792.517199234C28ED36.B6F5D8A30E1471D6@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7fq2br$vfr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com>`

```
One important part of maintainability is writing to the sites standards.
You can write the most elegant and beautifully crafted code that would win
prizes but if the programmers on site are not used to working with that
standard/structure then they become prone to erroneous maintenance.

At our site (with hundreds of modules) we have a few highly complex 'well
written' modules that were written by a 'consultant' who would not use our
standards.

These cost us the most to maintain and are the ones that we are most
reluctant to change..

In fact if we were designing our standards now we would probably incorporate
some of these features in our standards (but definitely not all) but in fact
this is bad code as far as I am concerned.

Regards
Rick

it
>> is to understand, explain and defend.  It allows the more experienced

minimum
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Standards

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#8YT72fj#GA.226@nih2naac.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au> <19B0C69644B4C792.517199234C28ED36.B6F5D8A30E1471D6@library-proxy.airnews.net> <7fq2br$vfr$1@nnrp1.dejanews.com> <924882806.11296.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
I can sympathize with this stance to some extent but many clients seem to
carry it to extremes and standards become a way of enforcing the lowest
common denominator of program quality by stressing conformance and
consistency to the exclusion of good coding practices. This only propagates
the continuing degradation of code with each change, resulting in
unmaintainable code that should be thrown away.

The correct purpose and application of standards is to raise the quality of
the code by encouraging good coding practices, and any resulting similarity
of code is a useful but secondary byproduct. As better coding techniques
become available they should be phased into use in some reasonable manner
at every opportunity, even as changes in old programs. This will tend to
leave each program in better shape, rather than worse, with each change.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
```

- **From:** endearment <endear@tig.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
To all who responded..

Thank you all for the great replies!
It is hard to know where to strike the balance - especially since I do not know
how much data is expected to flow through the program.

Personally, coming from a java background, I wince a bit at the exit solution
*smile* and firmly believe that the nested loops are the most efficient
solution. The actual code will only involve six  tests, not much more
complicated than the pseudocode.

The end solution will come from a more in-depth examination of our standards in
this case.

Thank you again!

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Efficiency and Nested Loops VS Readability

- **From:** endearment <endear@tig.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37210B62.47EA2AA9@tig.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3720397C.C1A82E13@tig.com.au> <924877307.795.83@news.remarQ.com> <37210A38.B0BF3C19@tig.com.au>`

```
Howdy all!

Thank you for the suggestion Desmond - I posted it to the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup and got a  number of great replies!

The best that came were:

a) use sequential IF-THEN-ELSE statements and use the EXIT command to
break from the routine if a data item fails a test. Apparently EXIT will
become part of the 9X standard (which better be finished quick or it
will be the 0X standard!).

b) refer to the programming standards of whichever site/company etc you
are working for.

If anyone would like to read the replies (they are long) and can't
access comp.lang.cobol, feel free to e-mail me and I can e-mail them to
you.

Robert.
```

#### ↳ Re: Unisys to PC

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-04-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l7.J7.a12m0ZX67wq4boQv4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<7fprcg$99c$1@oxygen.technet.net>`

```
"AY" == "AY" writes:

AY> I want Conver my Cobol programs on the Unisys Micro A to PC.
AY> Which PC Cobol Compiler can I use for this?
AY> 
AY> Thank

I don't understand the question.  What exactly do you mean?  Unisys
Micro A is already the Unisys Series A (nee Burroughs Large Systems)
running on a PC where the Unisys Corp ported it to the PC.  Do you
mean you're looking to migrate from it to a /*different*/ PC COBOL
compiler?

Jay
```

```
Define "video database."  For that matter, define "database."  Is this
database a catlog of your collection of video tapes?  Or is it a collection
of digitized video images?  Is "database" a relational database management
system like DB2 or Oracle?  A hierarchical system like IMS?  Or are you using
the term in a more amorphous sense to mean "a collection of data" without
specifying the structure or access method?  Any sample Cobol code would have
to be specific to the answers to these questions.
```

<a id="t-69963976a0"></a>
## Thread: Call statement - performance discussion — 8 msgs

### Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** mdaugl235@aol.com (Mdaugl235)
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```

Here is some of my opinions, and I would also like some other opinions on the
following scenario:

Using the call statement 
ProgA -> Calls -> ProgB
If ProgB can return 2 fields
Field1 is 10 bytes
Field2 is 16k bytes
The linkage section of ProgB will contain both fields, plus a parm to tell what
it needs to return, but in 90% of the cases, it will only return the 10 bytes. 
Many programs may call ProgB, as ProgB is a driver program to return either 10
bytes or 16,010 bytes depending upon the parm it receives.

These calls are pass by reference, and I'm curious to know the best performance
if this is getting called continuously by many different programs.  I assume
that performance would not be affected by having the additional 16k in the
linkage section even if 90% of the calls won't use it, as it is just a pointer
to the calling program and won't be using/passing any information if Prog A
doesn't pass it any fields other the a parm and the 10 bytes.  (opinions?)
I have thought about making 2 different drivers (i.e. 2 diff progb's) as one
could contain just 10 bytes, the other 16k, and the calling program could
choose what they need.  This may help performance, but I don't think it would
because I don't think performance is affected because the calling program isn't
passing the additional 16k parm if it isn't needed (as the prev paragraph
states).
I would like to see if anyone has any opinions/experience with this scenario
and if you agree or disagree. (?).   
I am also curious to know if it would help to do a "cancel' from each calling
program after calling progb and getting data back, as cancel will free the
memory since the progb is only needed once in each program that calls it.
Would pass by content instead of reference have any performance advantages?
I don't believe it would, but I have always used pass by reference on the
calls.
Opinions anyone?

                  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** Don Nelson <don.nelson@compaq.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3720AB66.5416@compaq.com>`
- **References:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Mdaugl235 wrote:
> 
> Here is some of my opinions, and I would also like some other opinions on the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't pass it any fields other the a parm and the 10 bytes.  (opinions?)

All the parameters are passed as addresses.  10 bytes or a zillion 
bytes.  It makes no difference. The only performance hit is that it 
has to pass an extra address, but I doubt if anyone could ever measure 
any difference.

> I have thought about making 2 different drivers (i.e. 2 diff progb's) as one
> could contain just 10 bytes, the other 16k, and the calling program could
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> states).

You would probably take more of a performance hit in figuring out 
which one to call than that for the extra parameter.  Remember, the 
16k is not passed.  Its address is.

> I would like to see if anyone has any opinions/experience with this scenario
> and if you agree or disagree. (?).
> I am also curious to know if it would help to do a "cancel' from each calling
> program after calling progb and getting data back, as cancel will free the
> memory since the progb is only needed once in each program that calls it.

No memory is taken up by the parameters.  There is normally lots of 
overhead in CANCEL (it all depends on the implementation).  There is 
no reason to do this.

> Would pass by content instead of reference have any performance advantages?

Lots of disadvantages because the compiler has to allocate space for 
the parameter, move the data to it, and then pass the address of the 
area.  And, you cannot pass data back to the caller in a parameter 
passed by content.

> I don't believe it would, but I have always used pass by reference on the
> calls.

Which is what you should do in this case.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** "ThinkAgain" <1day@atime.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<924888784.839.49@news.remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Giving that progB is called by other prgms and the calling pgms know
before calling progB the length of the linkage fieldB, it seems that
creating a clone of progB to handle the 16K would help if we are talking
1000's of calls. If you know that 90% of the time your using 10bytes, then
this should help in your performance since your not always passing 16K on
each call.  If your processing 1000's of calls
you should see a performance improvement. With some small changes to ProgB
and ProgB-clone you might reclaim .xxxx nanoseconds.<grin>
```

#### ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-04-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<924882079.10914.0.nnrp-10.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
Normally COBOL just passes the address not the field so it doesn't matter
what the field size is on the call.

Of course if you have 100s of programs that call ProgB and they all have 16K
defined, but only 1 actually needs this size then you're wasting a lot of
memory.

Regards

Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372123CB.80C9D5A8@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
There is actually an overhead of having large passareas in
IBM COBOL. It generates a base address per 4k of size in each
linkage area. So for a 4mb area, you get 1024 address resolutions
(at about three instructions per resolution). This is because of
the 4k offset limit in the 390 instruction set.

But even 16k would only generate 4 base registers which should
not be a problem.

Tim Josling
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** mdaugl235@aol.com (Mdaugl235)
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990424113426.13290.00000999@ng99.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<372123CB.80C9D5A8@melbpc.org.au>`

```
>There is actually an overhead of having large passareas in
>IBM COBOL. It generates a base address per 4k of size in each
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

However if this program is getting called many times simultaneously (a driver
program), it could have more of an impact.  I guess that would explain why
performance has decreased when we created other drivers for hitting against
db2, rather than using native code in the progs themself.  :)
Thanks for the info.  
Does anyone know if "canceling" after a call statement has any benefits on
performance overall, if the driver is getting called many times by multiple
progs?  In other words canceling the call in the calling prog after it called
the driver will free the memory as this will only be called once in each
program. I would be curious to know if this is worth the time or would have any
benefit.  
(This is all referring to IBM Mainframe OS390/cobol).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-04-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372223E7.6CB429D2@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<372123CB.80C9D5A8@melbpc.org.au> <19990424113426.13290.00000999@ng99.aol.com>`

```
I'm not aware of any useful performance benefits from using
CANCEL. 

It can save memory: in a dynamic environment it can result in the
program being unloaded, thus saving memory if it is not used
again.

On the other hand if it is used again, you are up for a very
expensive reload operation.

In a non-dynamic environment, it has to reinitialize the working
storage, which is expensive.

On the mainframe, a CALL is itself a very expensive operation,
around 100-200 instructions plus about 3 for each paramater, plus
another 3 per 4k of size of the declared linkage area. Dynam
calls are worse again, maybe another 50% on the call itself.

The overhead is much reduced, I am told, if you use nested
procedures - that way it does not have to go through all the
language environment slop every time.

Tim Josling	

<personal opinions only>

Mdaugl235 wrote:
> 
> >There is actually an overhead of having large passareas in
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> (This is all referring to IBM Mainframe OS390/cobol).
```

#### ↳ Re: Call statement - performance discussion

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YmmU2.9599$L61.3075378@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990423113404.19044.00000468@ng-cf1.aol.com>`

```
If you are calling one program from a number of others, then it is being
loaded and dynamically linked at runtime.  Depending on the architecture
and compiler, the overhead for this type of call can be far greater than,
and make completely negligible, the effect of number of arguments passed.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990423102811.09928.00000487@ng144.aol.com>`

```
I would like to use the delete command instead
of doing the following:
move spaces to bookauthor
                        booktitle
                        bookprice

I also get an error because bookprice has an PIC 9(4) not an PIC X(4).


***********************************
-CPR--subscribe@onelist.com

PCHelpDesk-subscribe@onelist.com

```

#### ↳ Re: delete

- **From:** Simon Martens <smart@sunshine.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<372205FA.324BBD1A@sunshine.nl>`
- **References:** `<19990423102811.09928.00000487@ng144.aol.com>`

```
Hello,

You asked two questions, so I'll give you two answers.

First: you want to delete fields in stead of initializing them.

Is there any good reason for deleting them? By moving spaces
into them or by stating "INITIALIZE BOOKAUTHOR, BOOKTITLE,
BOOKPRICE" you erase their contents, to be able to use them
again, and that is okay and superfluous.

It is okay, because it is safe programming. It is superfluous
for experienced programmers who do not need this kind of
strong protection against logical errors any more. If you
move "P.P. Cooper" into a field BOOKAUTHOR that has p.e.
PIC X(20), then after Cooper's name the computer will fill
spaces for you, so that the name that was there before and
that maybe contained 13 characters, will be totally erased.

The command DELETE can only be used for records, not for fields.
This command destroys the container, not only the contents!
So if you would succeed in deleting your 3 fields, after that
the programm would't be able to use them any more. Is that what
you want the programm to do? Then code your PROCEDURE DIVISION
in such a way that after having used these fields, the program
avoids them.

Second: how to move SPACES to a field with PIC 9(4)? Move ZERO
unless the output file is a printed report. If it is a report,
in your DATA DIVISION create a group item for BOOKPRICE as
follows:
     01  BOOKPRICE-X.
         05  BOOKPRICE           PIC 9(4).
After that, in your PROCEDURE DIVISION code this:
         MOVE SPACES TO BOOKPRICE-X
That is all there is to it.

Good luck!

- Simon -


Dupavoy wrote:
>

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-fe43c0f7ce"></a>
## Thread: Labels .. Pros and Cons — 30 this month / 40 total

### Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pascal.borland
- **Message-ID:** `<OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan>`

```
Impossible? No. Learning structured programming is like learning a new
language, you have to think in that language, you have to think structured.
Unstructured programmers are thinking in terms of where to go to do what
needs to be done, structured programmers are thinking directly in terms of
what needs to be done, it's really much simpler once you get the hang of
it. A large part of the key is modularity and top-down design and coding.

Top-down does NOT mean that the entire program flows from the top to the
bottom of the huge listing or source file. It means that it's designed in
conceptual levels from top to bottom, e.g. first express the program in the
most general terms (using appropriate structures of course), then each of
the very general pseudo-statements becomes (at least conceptually) an
invocation of a more detailed procedure which is defined separately, each
procedure is further broken down recursively until the whole program is
coded as a modular set of nicely structured procedures.

Most languages now provide Break's or other means of helping to avoid
GOTO's, even when they don't, I never use GOTO's in code I get to write
from scratch. Even the OLD Cobol before Cobol II, which didn't have End-If
and the period killed all your If's, had just barely enough support for
structured programming that you could write an entire old Cobol program
with NO GOTO's. Modern structured extensions just make it that much easier.
Pascal of course was designed structured to begin with. So if a structured
programming fanatic like myself can write pure GOTO-less code even in OLD
Cobol, surely you can learn to do it in Pascal. Good luck. It's worth it.

Oh, if you want a really severe example, try Lisp. It's been years since I
had Lisp in school and I never really used it; although there are some GOTO
type of things (which I don't like), everything is basically sort of like a
function call, if's and case's were handled by a function call to a special
function that worked like a case expression, and there's not even a while
statement; all looping was done entirely by recursion. Maybe inefficient
but it sure imposes discipline on your programming approach! If you could
learn to program like that, then regular structured programming in any
language would be a breeze.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmpcp@pobox.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


fmb wrote in message <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan>...

if I had not been able to use the goto command
>writing it would have been impossible.
```

#### ↳ Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons

- **From:** Richard Maine <maine@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pascal.borland
- **Message-ID:** `<ueeml7uyyx.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan> <OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com>`

```
"Robert M. Pritchett" <NewsCSIbus@rmpcp.com> writes:

> A large part of the key is modularity and top-down design and coding.
  etc.

Yep.  Good stuff.  Agree, etc.

> Most languages now provide Break's or other means of helping to avoid
> GOTO's, ...
> Pascal of course was designed structured to begin with. So if a structured
> programming fanatic like myself can write pure GOTO-less code even in OLD
> Cobol, surely you can learn to do it in Pascal.

Of course, some of my favorite examples of (what I consider to be) bad
code structure come from Pascal.  I'm (also) a big fan of structured code.
Its just that I don't make the identification that you have good
structure if and only if you have no goto's.  Someone who's name I
forget once said (approximately - I paraphrase, having forgotten the
exact words) that a sufficiently obstinate programmer can write
spaghetti code in any language and with any set of constructs.
Good structure cannot be boiled down to a set of statements to use.

My favorite (and certainly not original to me, but its one I see a lot)
"Pascalism" is how to code something where you need an exit from the
middle of a loop (the F90 exit statement is great for the purpose).
You often see a strange abomination done with little reason that I can
ascertain except  to force the code into a preconcieved notion that
good structure means using "do while".

You rotate the code around in the loop so that the exit condition is
at the top, where the "do while" needs it.  Then, before the loop
you add a second copy of the first part of the code to patch things
up so that the loop will work when messed up like that.  (Yes,
very prejudicial wording on my part).  Giving something like

    [extra copy of first part of loop body]
    do while [inverse of the exit condition]
       [second part of loop body]
       [first part of loop body]
    end

You see this a lot in things like processing data from file of
unknown length.  In that case, the 2 parts in their "natural" order
are "read some data from the file" and "process the data you just read".
The exit condition is when there is no more data in the file, which
you typically don't discover until after you try to read it.  For that
kind of case, the above proto-code looks like

   [read some data from the file]
   do while [did not hit an end of file]
     [process the data read in the previous loop cycle]
     [read some more data from the file
   end

I cannot by any stretch of the imagination consider this to be
naturally structured.  This certainly doesn't match how "I" think
of the problem.  I think of a loop that involves reading data,
then processing the data just read.  The only way I'd ever
come up with something like that is by saying "here's what
I need to get done - now how can I contort it to fit this set
of allowed constructs".

The business about having to have 2 copies of the "read some data"
code is a huge red flag of bad structure.  That violates one of the
major principles of good coding - don't write the same thing twice,
particularly if it is important that the 2 parts match.  And
again, I can't imagine any "natural" mental model that starts out with
2 copies of the same stuff.

The above construct is so common that Pascal contorts its notion of
input in order to accomodate it.  (A get doesn't read data from a file
and return that data - instead it returns data from its buffer and
then pre-fetches data for the next read).  This contortion has spawned
a series of hacks to work around its unsuitability to some
applications.  Look into the term "lazy i/o", for example.

But the contortion falls apart anyway when the data input
isn't done by the twisted "get" construct - for example if the
data is input by calling some 3rd party library procedure.
You end up thrown back to the above loop construct with the
loop written "backwards" and part of its code duplicated.
....either that or you go look up how to use a goto in Pascal.
It is possible, although some variants might disallow it, and
it always seemed to me that the language went out of its way
to make the goto awkward.

As is probably evident from the above tirade, I personally don't
consider Pascal well suited to structured coding at all.  Yes,
its possible to code anything in Pascal - I've done a fair amount
of it in my time.  Its even possible to code Pascal in a well-structured
manner; but the language doesn't do what I consider a good job of
encouraging/supporting good code structure -  and I'm not so sure
that much of my own Pascal code was well structured; there were just
too many times that I "gave in" and wrote garbled code because that
was easier than writing it well.

F90/f95 supports the above kind of problem excellently (in my opinion).
It turns into

  data_loop: do
    [read some data]
    if ([hit an end of file]) exit data_loop
    [process the data]
  end do data_loop

I consider that good structure.  Also happens that it doesn't use goto's.

But my main thesis is that the number of goto's is not a plausible
"metric" of good structure.  It is sometimes a reasonable first
approximation, but like so many "metrics", when people go overboard
and chase the metric, it can become counter-productive.  I have seen
people take well-structured code and turn it into spaghetti in the
name of "structured coding", by which they meant nothing more and
nothing less than getting rid of goto's.

Perhaps appropriate to mention again the Fortran compiler (hi, Bob)
that actually implemented the "comefrom" statement as a joke, thus
allowing people to write "well-structured" code by the simple expedient
of replacing all the goto's by corresponding comefroms.

P.S. The above is admitedly highly opinionated.  I threw in my
opinions on it.  But I won't argue about it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pascal.borland
- **Message-ID:** `<3724B946.10355A2B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan> <OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com> <ueeml7uyyx.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov>`

```
Richard Maine wrote:
>    [read some data from the file]
>    do while [did not hit an end of file]
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> naturally structured. 

But it is extremely common in COBOL, and IMHO, better than having a GO
TO exit in the perform paragraph.  It also has an advantage in that it
can be written without saving switches, if you have some type of
integrated file-status switch showing end-of-data.  (the file might be a
database).

Certainly the "fix", replacing the read with PERFORM 9999-READ-ROUTINE
does nothing useful.

I haven't seen maintenance problems with this logic, and I have seen
lots of maintenance problems caused by GO TO exit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons

- **From:** kaz@ashi.FootPrints.net (Kaz Kylheku)
- **Date:** 1999-04-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.misc,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pascal.borland
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn7i9gvq.iir.kaz@ashi.FootPrints.net>`
- **References:** `<7fg2f3$1oe8@enews3.newsguy.com> <01be8b0a$09868fe0$LocalHost@bunyan> <OAJUXPqj#GA.312@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com> <ueeml7uyyx.fsf@altair.dfrc.nasa.gov> <3724B946.10355A2B@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Mon, 26 Apr 1999 13:06:46 -0600, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
>Richard Maine wrote:
>>    [read some data from the file]
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>database).

It's also the C++ STL way for iterating containers:

	iter = container.begin();

	while (iter != container.end()) {
		// process element indexed by iter
		iter++;
	}

which can be condensed into:

	for (iter = container.begin(); iter != container.end(); iter++) {
		//
	}

When processing a sequence, fetching the first item is a special case that
is different from fetching the next item. Therefore it requires different
logic.  This is evident even in simple loops that increment an integer:

	i = 0;	/* get first value of i */

	while (i < N) {
		/* process value of i set in previous increment */
		i++;	/* get next value of i */
	}

The reasons that we use this logic in these circumstances is that firstly, the
increment step is independent from data retrieval. That is, I can use the value
pointed at by the iterator without causing that iterator to automatically
increment. Secondly, the increment step never fails. To ensure this, we reserve
a special value that the iterator can take on if it is incremented after the
last iteration; the value container.end() or the value N in the case of the
integer loop.

In the case of file input, we do not have these two conditions. 
The iteration variable is the file position indicator. It is initialized
when the file is opened or reset to the beginning. And it is incremented
when data is fetched. The acts of fetching data and the incrementing of
the position indicator are inseparable. Furthermore, this operation
can fail, unlike the increment of iter or i in the above two loops.

Therefore, a different structure makes more sense:

	/* open file (position marker = 0) */

	for (;;) {
		/* fetch data, (position_marker += X, if successful) */

		if (/* error test */)
			break;

		/* process data */
	}

There is no point in doing the data read in two places unless you are using
some crippled programming language that doesn't have structured breaks.

Furthermore, you may be able to test the result of the fetching operation
in the loop guard, resulting in something patterned after the widely
used idiom:

	while ((ch = getchar()) != EOF) {
		/* process ch */	
	}
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Labels .. Pros and Cons
